blocks:
Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ وَخَلَقَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا وَبَثَّ مِنْهُمَا رِجَالًا كَثِيرًا وَنِسَآءً ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى تَسَآءَلُونَ بِهِۦ وَٱلْأَرْحَامَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَيْكُمْ رَقِيبًا (١)
O mankind! Be wary of your Lord who created you from a single soul, and created its mate from it, and from the two of them scattered numerous men and women. Be wary of Allah, in whose Name you adjure one another and [of severing ties with] blood relations. Indeed Allah is watchful over you. (1)

Commentary

The Struggle Against Discrimination

يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اتَّقُوا رَبَّكُمُ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ وَاحِدَةٍ The address in the first verse of this Surah is directed toward all human beings, because the contents of this Surah are, in truth, the very issues that every individual requires in their life.

It then calls for Taqwa (piety and God-wariness), which is the primary root of programs for the healthy development of society. Fulfilling one another's rights, the just distribution of inheritance, supporting orphans, and observing family rights—and things of this nature—are all matters that will lead nowhere without the backing of Taqwa and piety. Therefore, He begins this Surah, which contains all these issues, with a call to Taqwa.

To introduce the God who monitors all human actions, He refers to one of His attributes that is the root of human social unity: الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ وَاحِدَةٍ "The God who created all of you from a single human being." Consequently, the imaginary distinctions and honors that various groups have created for themselves—such as racial, linguistic, regional, or tribal distinctions and the like, which today are the source of a thousand types of afflictions in societies—should not exist in an Islamic society. This is because everyone originates from a single source, they are the children of one father and mother, and they are of one essence in creation.

Considering that the society of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله era was a tribal society in every sense of the word clarifies the importance of this struggle. Similar expressions exist in other parts of the Holy Quran, which will be mentioned in their proper places.

Now let us see who is meant by نَفْسٍ وَاحِدَةٍ (a single soul)?

Does "single soul" refer to a specific individual or a generic unit (i.e., the male gender)? There is no doubt that the apparent meaning of this expression refers to a specific individual and points to the first human, whom the Quran introduces by the name of Adam, the father of today's humans. The expression "Children of Adam" (Bani Adam), which appears in numerous verses of the Quran, also refers to this. The possibility that it refers to a generic unity is very far from the apparent meaning of the verse.

وَخَلَقَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا This phrase says: "Adam's spouse was created from him." Some commentators have understood from this expression that Adam's spouse, "Eve" (Hawwa), was created from Adam's body, taking certain unreliable narrations—which say Eve was created from one of Adam's ribs—as evidence (this meaning is also explicitly stated in the second chapter of Genesis in the Torah).

However, by considering other verses of the Quran, any ambiguity in the interpretation of this verse is removed, and it becomes clear that the meaning is that Allah created his spouse from his own kind (the human species). In Surah Ar-Rum, verse 21, we read: وَمِنْ آيَاتِهِ أَنْ خَلَقَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْوَاجًا لِتَسْكُنُوا إِلَيْهَا "And of His signs is that He created for you from yourselves mates that you may find tranquility in them." And in Surah An-Nahl, verse 72, He says: وَاللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْوَاجًا "And Allah has made for you from yourselves mates."

It is clear that when we read in these verses "He made for you mates from yourselves," it means He made them from your own species, not from the members of your body.

According to a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام quoted in Tafsir al-Ayyashi, the creation of Eve from one of Adam's ribs is strongly refuted, and it is explicitly stated that Eve was created from the remainder of the clay from which Adam was made.

How did the children of Adam marry?

وَبَثَّ مِنْهُمَا رِجَالًا كَثِيرًا وَنِسَاءً This phrase says: "Allah brought forth from Adam and his spouse many men and women." From this expression, it is inferred that the multiplication of the generation of Adam's children took place solely through Adam and his spouse, and no third entity was involved.

The implication of this statement is that Adam's children (brothers and sisters) must have married one another; for if they had married other races or spouses, the term مِنْهُمَا (from those two) would not be applicable.

This subject has also appeared in several hadiths, and it is not very surprising, because according to the reasoning mentioned in some hadiths narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, these marriages were permissible because the ruling prohibiting the marriage of brother and sister had not yet been revealed. Obviously, the prohibition of an act depends on it being forbidden by Allah. What is to prevent certain necessities and interests from making something permissible at one time and then prohibiting it later?

However, in other hadiths, it is explicitly stated that the children of Adam never married each other, and those who believe they married one another are strongly attacked.

If we are to prefer those among conflicting hadiths that agree with the apparent meaning of the Quran, we must choose the first group of hadiths, as they agree with the above verse.

There is another possibility here: it could be said that Adam's children married the survivors of previous humans. According to some narrations, Adam was not the first human on earth. Modern scientific studies also suggest that the human species has likely lived on Earth for several million years, whereas not much time has passed since the emergence of Adam. Therefore, we must accept that other humans lived on Earth before Adam and were in the process of extinction at the time of Adam's emergence. What is to prevent Adam's children from marrying the survivors of one of the previous generations? But as we said, this possibility is not very consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse above. (This discussion requires more dialogue, which is beyond the scope of this exegetical discussion).

وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ الَّذِي تَسَاءَلُونَ بِهِ وَالْأَرْحَامَ The importance that Taqwa holds in building the foundation of a healthy society has caused Him to again invite the people to piety and Taqwa at the end of the verse. However, here He adds a phrase, saying: "Fear the God who is Great in your eyes, and whose name you invoke when you want to ask something of one another." [^1] Then He says: وَالْأَرْحَامَ (and the wombs/kinship). This word is conjoined to "Allah," and therefore, in the well-known recitation, it is read in the accusative case (mansub). Thus, the meaning becomes:

"And fear [severing ties with] the kinsfolk." Mentioning this subject here is, firstly, a sign of the extraordinary importance the Quran places on Silat al-Rahim (maintaining family ties), to the extent that the name of the kinsfolk appears after the name of Allah. Secondly, it is an allusion to what was mentioned at the beginning of the verse: that you are all from one father and mother, and in truth, all children of Adam are kinsfolk to one another. This bond and connection require that you show affection toward all humans of every race and tribe, just as you do toward your own family relatives.

إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَيْكُمْ رَقِيبًا Raqib originally refers to someone who monitors a situation from a high place, and subsequently, it has come to mean a protector and guardian of something, because guardianship is a requirement of monitoring. The height of the Raqib's position might be physical—standing on a high place to monitor—or it might be spiritual.

In the above phrase, He says: "Allah is watchful over you," and He sees and knows all your actions and intentions, and furthermore, He is your guardian against accidents. (The use of كَانَ in the above phrase, which is a past tense verb, is for emphasis).

[^1]: "Tasa'alun" is from the root "Tasa'ul," meaning to ask one another. "Tasa'ul bi-Allah" means that when people want something from one another, they say "I ask you by Allah..." (For God's sake). This is a sign of Allah's greatness in their eyes.

Ayah

وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰٓ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَتَبَدَّلُوا۟ ٱلْخَبِيثَ بِٱلطَّيِّبِ ۖ وَلَا تَأْكُلُوٓا۟ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَمْوَٰلِكُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ حُوبًا كَبِيرًا (٢)
Give the orphans their property, and do not replace the good with the bad, and do not eat up their property [by mingling it] with your own property, for that is indeed a great sin. (2)

Occasion of Revelation

A man from the tribe of "Bani Ghatafan" had a wealthy brother who passed away. He took possession of his brother's property as the guardian of his orphaned nephews. When the nephew reached the age of maturity, the uncle refused to hand over his rightful property. The matter was brought before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the first verse was revealed. Upon hearing it, the usurping man repented and returned the property to its owner, saying: "اعوذ باللَّه من الحوب الكبير" (I seek refuge in Allah from being tainted by a great sin).

Commentary

Treachery Regarding the Property of Orphans is Forbidden

In every society, due to various events, fathers pass away and leave behind small children. However, in unhealthy societies plagued by internal wars—such as the Arab society during the Age of Ignorance—the number of such orphaned children is much higher. These children must be placed under the protection of the Islamic government and every individual Muslim. In the above verse, three important commands are given regarding the property of orphans:

  1. وَ آتُوا الْيَتامى‌ أَمْوالَهُمْ – In this phrase, He commands: "Give the orphans their property when they reach maturity." This means your possession of this property is only as a trustee, supervisor, and agent, not as an owner.

  2. وَ لا تَتَبَدَّلُوا الْخَبِيثَ بِالطَّيِّبِ – "And do not substitute the pure [property] of theirs with your impure and inferior property." This command is actually to prevent the misappropriations that guardians of orphans sometimes committed under the pretext that exchanging the property was for the orphan's benefit, or that there was no difference between them, or that the property would perish if left alone. They would take the good and choice property of the orphans and leave their own bad and low-quality property in its place.

  3. وَ لا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَهُمْ إِلى‌ أَمْوالِكُمْ – "And do not consume their property into your own property." This means do not mix the orphans' property with your own in such a way that the result is the appropriation of everything, or do not mix your bad property with their good property such that the rights of the orphans are trampled (the word ila in the above phrase is actually in the sense of ma'a [with]).

[What is "Hub"? (Huban Kabira)]

إِنَّهُ كانَ حُوباً كَبِيراً – At the end of the verse, to emphasize and establish the importance of the matter, He says: Such transgression and encroachment upon the property of orphans is a great sin. Raghib says in the book Al-Mufradat:

"Al-Hubah" originally means a need or necessity that drives a person toward sin. Since the transgressions of guardians against the property of orphans often occur due to need or under the pretext of necessity, the word "Hub" is used in the above verse instead of the word "Ithm" (sin), to point toward this reality.

An analysis of various verses of the Quran shows that Islam places extraordinary importance on this matter. It threatens those who betray the property of orphans with severe punishments in the strongest terms and invites guardians to exercise complete care over the orphans' property with firm and decisive language. The details of this will follow in several verses later in this same Surah, as well as under verse 152 of Surah Al-An'am and verse 34 of Surah Al-Isra.

The severe tone of these verses was so effective in the hearts of Muslims that they even feared preparing a shared meal for themselves and the orphans. For this reason, they would separate the orphans' food from the food of themselves and their own children, which caused hardship for both parties. Therefore, in verse 220 of Surah Al-Baqarah, they were instructed that if their intention in mixing the property or food of the orphans with their own was for goodwill and improvement, there is no objection to it. [1]

[^1]: Refer to the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 220.

Ayah

وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تُقْسِطُوا۟ فِى ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ فَٱنكِحُوا۟ مَا طَابَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ مَثْنَىٰ وَثُلَـٰثَ وَرُبَـٰعَ ۖ فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا۟ فَوَٰحِدَةً أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَعُولُوا۟ (٣)
If you fear that you may not deal justly with the orphans, then marry [other] women that you like, two, three, or four. But if you fear that you may not treat them fairly, then [marry only] one, or [marry from among] your slave-women. That makes it likelier that you will not be unfair. (3)

Circumstances of Revelation

A specific circumstance of revelation has been narrated for this verse: before Islam, it was customary for many people in the Hejaz to take orphan girls into their homes under the guise of guardianship and care. Subsequently, they would marry them and take possession of their property. Since they held all the power, they would even set their dowries lower than usual, and at the slightest sign of displeasure, they would easily abandon them. In reality, they were not even willing to treat them as ordinary wives.

At this time, the above verse was revealed, commanding the guardians of orphans to marry orphan girls only if they observed justice towards them completely; otherwise, they should overlook them and choose their spouses from other women.

Commentary

وَ إِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تُقْسِطُوا فِي الْيَتامى‌ فَانْكِحُوا ...

Following the instruction given in the previous verse regarding the protection of the property of orphans, this verse points to another of their rights, stating: "If you fear that you will not observe right and justice regarding the marital rights and property of orphan girls when marrying them, then refrain from marrying them and seek other women."

In light of what has been said, the interpretation of the verse is perfectly clear, and the answer to the question of why the beginning of the verse is about orphans and its end is about marriage—two subjects that seemingly do not align—becomes evident. Both the beginning and the end of the verse concern marriage; however, the start of the verse says: if you cannot combine marriage to orphans with the principles of justice, it is better to forgo it and then seek women who are not orphans.

Although commentators have said much on this subject, what is derived from the verse itself is what was mentioned above. That is, the address in the verse is directed at the guardians of orphans, to whom various instructions were given in the preceding verse for protecting the orphans' property. In this verse, it speaks of marriage with orphans, stating that just as you must observe justice regarding their property, you must also exercise the utmost care for their best interests regarding marriage with orphan girls; otherwise, refrain from marrying them and choose other women.

Among the evidences that clarify the above interpretation is Verse 127 of this same Surah, which explicitly mentions the issue of observing justice regarding marriage with orphan girls, the explanation of which will come under that verse.

The narrations cited under this verse in various books also attest to this interpretation [1]. As for the narration attributed to the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, suggesting that a large portion of the Quran existed between the beginning and end of this verse and was deleted, it holds no credibility whatsoever in terms of its chain of transmission. Such hadiths that suggest the distortion (tahrif) or omission of parts of the Quran are either fabrications by the enemies of Islam and hypocrites to make the Quran appear unreliable, or they are the result of individuals who, failing to perceive the connection between the beginning and end of the verse, imagined there was an omission and gradually presented it in the form of a narration. This is while we have seen that the sentences of the verse are perfectly connected and linked to one another.

مَثْنى‌ وَ ثُلاثَ وَ رُباعَ‌

"Mathna" linguistically means "two by two," "thulath" means "three by three," and "ruba‘" means "four by four." Since the address in the verse—

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 438, and Tafsir al-Manar under the mentioned verse.

—is to all Muslims, the meaning of the verse becomes: to avoid committing injustice against orphan girls, you may refrain from marrying them and instead marry women whose social and family status does not allow you to oppress them. You may choose two, three, or four of them as wives. However, because the audience is all Muslims, the expression "two by two" and the like is used.

Otherwise, there is no doubt that the maximum limit for polygyny (and that too, only when specific conditions are met) is no more than four.

It is also necessary to note that the "and" (waw) in the above phrase means "or" (aw); it does not mean that you can choose two wives plus three wives plus four wives, totaling nine. If that were the intention, the number nine would have been explicitly mentioned, rather than in this fragmented and complex manner. Furthermore, in Islamic jurisprudence, it is a matter of necessity that exceeding four wives is absolutely prohibited.

In any case, the above verse is explicit proof of the permissibility of polygyny, albeit with conditions that will soon be mentioned.

فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا فَواحِدَةً: Then it immediately says: this is in the case of maintaining perfect justice; however, if you cannot observe justice, suffice with only one wife so that you remain free from oppression and injustice toward others.

أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ‌: Or, instead of choosing a second wife, make use of a bondwoman you own, as their conditions are lighter (although they too must enjoy their rightful rights).

ذلِكَ أَدْنى‌ أَلَّا تَعُولُوا: This (choosing one wife or a bondwoman) better prevents oppression, injustice, and deviation from equity. (Regarding the issue of slavery and

Islam's view on this matter, we will provide sufficient discussion under the appropriate verses).

What is Meant by Justice Regarding Spouses?

Now, before understanding the philosophy of the ruling on polygyny in Islam, it is necessary to examine what is meant by the "justice" mentioned as one of the conditions for having multiple wives.

Does this justice pertain to the affairs of life, such as shared nights, living facilities, and comfort, or does it also mean justice within the realm of the heart and human emotions?

There is no doubt that "justice" in internal heart-felt love is beyond human power. Who can fully control their love, the factors of which lie outside their being? For this reason, Allah has not made observing this type of justice obligatory. In Verse 129 of this same Surah (An-Nisa), He says: "وَ لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَ لَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ‌": "You will never be able to maintain [perfect] justice between your wives [in terms of heart-felt inclination], no matter how much you desire to do so."

Therefore, internal affections are not prohibited as long as they do not lead to favoring some wives over others in practical aspects. What a man is duty-bound to do is observe justice in practical and external aspects.

From this explanation, it becomes clear that those who have tried to conclude that polygyny is absolutely prohibited in Islam—by combining the above verse (فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا فَواحِدَةً) with Verse 129 (وَ لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَ لَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ‌)—are gravely mistaken. They argue that the first verse makes it conditional upon justice, while the second verse deems justice impossible for men in this regard.

This is because, as noted, the justice that is beyond human power—

—is justice in heart-felt inclinations, and this is not among the conditions for polygyny. What is a condition is justice in practical aspects.

Evidence for this is the end of Verse 129 of this Surah, where it says:

"فَلا تَمِيلُوا كُلَّ الْمَيْلِ فَتَذَرُوها كَالْمُعَلَّقَةِ": meaning, "Now that you cannot observe perfect equality in love between your wives, at least do not turn away entirely [from one] so as to leave her suspended [as if she has no husband]."

The conclusion is that those who have taken one part of this verse and forgotten the other have fallen into such an error regarding the issue of polygyny that it is surprising to any researcher.

Beyond this, in Islamic jurisprudence and its various sources among both the Shi'a and Sunnis, the issue of polygyny with its conditions is beyond debate and is considered one of the necessities of Islamic law.

Now let us return to the philosophy of this Islamic ruling (polygyny).

Polygyny is a Social Necessity

The above verse permits polygyny (with heavy conditions and within certain limits). Here, we encounter the objections and attacks of opponents who, with superficial study and uncalculated emotions, have risen against this Islamic law. Westerners, in particular, criticize us more in this regard, claiming that Islam has allowed men to build "harems" for themselves and take wives without limit.

However, Islam has neither allowed the formation of a harem in the sense they imagine, nor has it made polygyny unconditional and unlimited.

To explain: by studying the conditions of various environments before Islam, we—

—reach the conclusion that unlimited polygyny was a normal occurrence. Sometimes, idolaters would have more than ten wives or fewer at the time of becoming Muslim. Therefore, polygyny is not one of the proposals or innovations of Islam; rather, Islam limited it within the framework of the necessities of human life and imposed heavy restrictions and conditions upon it.

Islamic laws revolve around the real needs of humanity, not superficial propaganda and unguided emotions. The issue of polygyny is also examined from this angle in Islam, for no one can deny that men are at greater risk of destruction in various life events than women, and they constitute the primary victims in wars and other incidents.

Nor can it be denied that the span of sexual life for men is longer than for women, as women lose their sexual readiness at a certain age, whereas this is not the case for men.

Furthermore, women are practically sexually restricted during their monthly cycles and part of the period of pregnancy, while these restrictions do not exist for men.

Above all, there are women who lose their husbands for various reasons and usually cannot be considered as a first wife by men. If the issue of polygyny were not an option, they would have to remain without a husband forever. As we read in various publications, this group of widows complains about the instability of their lives when polygyny is restricted and views the prevention of it as a kind of cruel sentimentality toward themselves.

Considering these realities, in cases where the balance between men and women is disrupted for various reasons, we are forced to choose one of three paths:

  1. Men content themselves with only one wife in all cases, and the surplus women remain without husbands until the end—

—of their lives, suppressing all their natural needs and internal desires.

  1. Men have only one legal wife but establish free and illegitimate sexual relations with women who have remained husbandless in the form of mistresses.

  2. Those who have the ability to manage more than one wife—and for whom no "physical," "financial," or "moral" problems arise, and who have the power to implement perfect justice among their wives and children—be allowed to choose more than one wife for themselves. Certainly, there is no path other than these three.

If we wish to choose the first path, we must fight against human nature, instincts, and spiritual and physical needs, and ignore the emotions and feelings of such women. This is a battle in which there is no victory, and even if this plan were feasible, its inhuman aspects are hidden from no one.

In other words, the issue of multiple wives in cases of necessity should not be examined solely through the eyes of the first wife; it must also be studied through the eyes of the second wife. Those who cite the problems of the first wife in the event of polygyny are those who look at a three-sided issue from only one angle. The issue of multiple wives must be studied from the perspective of the man, the first wife, and the second wife, and we must judge based on the collective interest.

If we choose the second path, we must recognize prostitution. Furthermore, women who are exploited sexually as mistresses have neither security nor a future, and their personality is effectively trampled upon. These are not matters that any rational person would permit.

Therefore, only the third path remains, which both responds positively to the natural desires and instinctive needs of women and remains free from the dire consequences of prostitution and the instability of these women's lives, pulling society out of the whirlpool of sin.

Of course, it must be noted that while the permissibility of polygyny is a social necessity in some cases and is considered one of the established rulings of Islam, fulfilling its conditions today differs greatly from the past. Life in the past was simple and basic, and thus observing perfect equality between wives was easy and within the capability of most individuals. But in our age and time, those who wish to use this law must be mindful of all-encompassing justice and take such action only if they have the power to do so. Fundamentally, taking this step should not be out of whim and desire.

It is noteworthy that the very people who oppose multiple wives (such as Westerners) have encountered events throughout their history that have made their need for this issue perfectly clear. For example, after World War II, a severe need was felt in war-torn countries, especially Germany, for this subject. This prompted a group of their thinkers to reconsider the prohibition of multiple wives to find a remedy and solve the problem. They even requested the Islamic polygyny program from "Al-Azhar" University and studied it. However, they were forced to stop this program in the face of harsh attacks from the Church. The result was the same horrific prostitution and widespread sexual promiscuity that engulfed all the war-torn countries.

Beyond all this, the inclination of some men toward multiple wives cannot be denied. If this inclination is based on whim, it is not worth considering. But sometimes, due to the wife's infertility and the man's intense desire to have a child, this inclination becomes logical. Or sometimes, due to intense sexual desires and the first wife's inability to fulfill this instinctive demand, the man finds himself forced into a second marriage. Even if—

—it is not done through legitimate means, he may resort to illegitimate ones. In such cases, the logic of the man's demand cannot be denied. Therefore, even in countries where multiple wives are legally prohibited, in many cases, relationships with multiple women are fully prevalent in practice, and a man may have illegitimate relationships with several women simultaneously.

The famous French historian Gustave Le Bon considers the Islamic law of polygyny, which is limited and conditional, as one of the advantages of this faith. When comparing it to the free and illegitimate relationships of men with several women in Europe, he writes:

"In the West, despite the fact that neither the climate nor the natural state necessitates such a custom (polygyny), monogamy is something we see only in law books! Otherwise, I do not think it can be denied that in our actual social life, there is no trace of this custom! Truly, I am bewildered and do not know what the legitimate and limited polygyny of the East lacks compared to the hypocritical polygyny of the West? Rather, I say that the former is better and more worthy in every respect than the latter." [1] Of course, it cannot be denied that some nominal Muslims, without observing the Islamic spirit of this law, have abused it and established shameful harems for themselves, violating the rights of their wives. But this flaw is not in the law, and their actions should not be attributed to the instructions of Islam. What good law is there that profit-seekers have not exploited illegitimately?

Question

Here, some ask: is it possible that the conditions and circumstances mentioned above could arise for a woman or women? In that case, can she be allowed to—

[1]: History of Islamic and Arab Civilization, translated by Fakhr Gilani, p. 509.

—choose two husbands for herself?

Answer

The answer to this question is not very difficult.

Firstly (contrary to what is common among the laypeople), sexual desire in men is far greater than in women. Among the ailments mentioned in scientific books regarding sexual issues for most women is the issue of "frigidity," whereas in men, the subject is the opposite. Even among other living creatures, it is always seen that sexual displays usually begin from the male.

Secondly, multiple spouses in the case of men creates no social or legal problems, whereas for women, if they were to choose two husbands, numerous problems would arise. The simplest of these is the issue of the child's unknown parentage, as it would not be clear which of the two husbands it belongs to. Certainly, such a child would not be supported by either man. Some scientists even believe:

A child whose father is unknown will be less loved by the mother. Thus, such children would be in absolute emotional deprivation and their legal status would be completely ambiguous.

And perhaps it does not need mentioning that resorting to means of contraception through pills or the like is never fully reliable and cannot be a definitive reason for not having a child. There are many women who have used these means or made mistakes in their use and have conceived. Therefore, no woman can submit to multiple husbands relying on that.

Based on these points, multiple husbands for women cannot be logical, while—

—in the case of men, considering its conditions, it is both logical and practical.

Ayah

وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلنِّسَآءَ صَدُقَـٰتِهِنَّ نِحْلَةً ۚ فَإِن طِبْنَ لَكُمْ عَن شَىْءٍ مِّنْهُ نَفْسًا فَكُلُوهُ هَنِيٓـًٔا مَّرِيٓـًٔا (٤)
Give women their dowries, handing it over to them; but if they remit anything of it of their own accord, then consume it as [something] lawful and wholesome. (4)

Commentary

The word "Nihlah" (نحلة) linguistically means a debt or obligation, and it also means a gift or bestowal. Al-Raghib states in his book Al-Mufradat: "In my opinion, this word is derived from the root 'Nahl' (meaning honeybee), because a gift or bestowal bears a resemblance to the work of honeybees in providing honey."

Saduqatihinna (صَدُقاتِهِنَّ) is the plural of Sadaq (صداق), which means dowry (Mahr). Following the discussion in the previous verse regarding the choice of a spouse, the above verse refers to one of the established rights of women and emphasizes: "Give women their dowries fully as a debt." This means that just as you are careful not to diminish anything when paying other debts, you must maintain the same attitude regarding the payment of the dowry (this is if we take "Nihlah" to mean debt). If it is taken to mean a gift or bestowal, the interpretation of the verse would be: "Pay the dowry—which is a Divine bestowal and something God ordained so that women may have greater rights in society and their relative physical weakness may be compensated through this means—in its entirety."

فَإِنْ طِبْنَ لَكُمْ عَنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ مِنْهُ نَفْساً فَكُلُوهُ هَنِيئاً مَرِيئاً At the beginning of the verse, to protect the rights of women, it explicitly commands that the entire dowry must be paid to them. However, at the end of the verse, to honor the feelings of both parties, strengthen heart-to-heart bonds, and attract affection, it says: "But if they, of their own good pleasure, remit any part of it to you, then enjoy it with satisfaction and good cheer." This ensures that the marital environment is not governed solely by dry legal regulations, but that parallel to them, affection and love also prevail.

The Dowry: A Social Security for Women

In the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), since women were not valued, the dowry—which was the woman's established right—was often placed at the disposal of her guardians, who considered it their own absolute property. Sometimes, the dowry of one woman was made the marriage of another; for instance, a brother would marry off his sister to someone on the condition that the other person marries his sister to him, and the dowry for these two women was simply this exchange.

Islam drew a line of cancellation through all these oppressive customs and designated the dowry as an established right belonging to the woman. In the verses of the Quran, men are repeatedly advised to fully observe this right.

In Islam, no specific amount has been fixed for the dowry; it depends on the agreement of the two spouses. Although numerous narrations emphasize not making the dowry heavy, this is not an obligatory ruling but rather a recommended (Mustahabb) one.

Now the question arises: since both man and woman benefit equally from marriage and the marital union is a bond based on mutual interests, what is the reason for the man to pay a sum, small or large, as a dowry to the woman? Furthermore, does this not damage the woman's personality and give marriage the appearance of a purchase and sale?

It is for these reasons that some strongly oppose the issue of dowry. In particular, the fact that dowries are not customary among Westerners fuels this thought for those infatuated with the West. However, not only does removing the dowry not enhance a woman's personality, but it also jeopardizes her situation.

To explain: it is true that both man and woman benefit equally from marital life, but it cannot be denied that in the event of separation, the woman will suffer greater loss because:

Firstly, according to specific physical aptitude, the man usually has more influence and dominance in society. Although some wish to deny this clear reality when speaking, the state of human social life that we see with our own eyes—even in European societies where women supposedly enjoy full freedom—shows that the initiative for high-income activities is mostly in the hands of men.

Additionally, men have more opportunities for remarriage, whereas widowed women—especially as a portion of their lives passes and they lose the capital of youth and beauty—have fewer opportunities for choosing a new spouse.

Considering these points, it becomes clear that the opportunities and capital a woman loses through marriage are more than what the man loses. In reality, the dowry is a form of "compensation for loss" for the woman and a means to secure her future life. Furthermore, the issue of the dowry usually acts as a "brake" against a man's inclinations toward separation and divorce.

It is true that according to Islamic laws, the dowry becomes a liability upon the man as soon as the marriage contract is established, and the woman has the right to demand it immediately. However, since it usually remains as a debt upon the man, it is considered both a reserve for the woman's future and a support for preserving her rights and preventing the marital bond from disintegrating (of course, there are exceptions to this, but what we have said holds true in most cases).

If some have misinterpreted the dowry and perceived it as a kind of "price for the woman," it has no connection to Islamic laws. In Islam, the dowry in no way has the aspect of a price or the value of a commodity. The best evidence for this is the marriage contract formula (Sighah) itself, in which the "man" and "woman" are officially considered the two fundamental pillars of the marriage pact, and the dowry is something additional and peripheral. For this reason, if no mention is made of the dowry in the contract formula, the contract is not void, whereas in buying, selling, and transactions, if the price is not mentioned, it will certainly be void. (Of course, it should be noted that if no dowry is mentioned in the marriage contract, the husband is obliged to pay Mahr al-Mithl—a dowry equivalent to that of women of her status—in the event of sexual intercourse).

From what has been said, we conclude that the dowry has the aspect of "compensation for loss" and "support for respecting the woman's rights," not a price or value. Perhaps the expression "Nihlah" meaning "bestowal" in the verse refers to this aspect.

Ayahs

وَلَا تُؤْتُوا۟ ٱلسُّفَهَآءَ أَمْوَٰلَكُمُ ٱلَّتِى جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ قِيَـٰمًا وَٱرْزُقُوهُمْ فِيهَا وَٱكْسُوهُمْ وَقُولُوا۟ لَهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا (٥)
Do not give the feeble-minded your property, which Allah has assigned you to manage: provide for them out of it and clothe them, and speak to them honourable words. (5)
وَٱبْتَلُوا۟ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغُوا۟ ٱلنِّكَاحَ فَإِنْ ءَانَسْتُم مِّنْهُمْ رُشْدًا فَٱدْفَعُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَأْكُلُوهَآ إِسْرَافًا وَبِدَارًا أَن يَكْبَرُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن كَانَ غَنِيًّا فَلْيَسْتَعْفِفْ ۖ وَمَن كَانَ فَقِيرًا فَلْيَأْكُلْ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ فَإِذَا دَفَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فَأَشْهِدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ حَسِيبًا (٦)
Test the orphans when they reach the age of marriage. Then if you discern in them maturity, deliver to them their property. And do not consume it lavishly and hastily lest they should grow up. As for him who is well-off, let him be abstemious, and as for him who is poor, let him eat in an honourable manner. And when you deliver to them their property, take witnesses over them, and Allah suffices as reckoner. (6)

Commentary

The above verses complement the discussions regarding orphans that were mentioned in the preceding verses.

وَ لا تُؤْتُوا السُّفَهاءَ أَمْوالَكُمُ‌:

"Do not entrust your properties and wealth to the foolish," and let them attain maturity in economic matters so that your wealth is not exposed to risk and destruction.

Who is a Fool (Safiih)?

"Raghib" states in the book Al-Mufradat: Safah (on the weight of tabah) originally refers to a kind of lightness of body such that balance is not maintained while walking. For this reason, a rein that is unbalanced and constantly in motion is called "safih." Subsequently, by analogy, it has been used for individuals who lack intellectual maturity, whether the lightness of their intellect is in material or spiritual matters.

However, it is clear that the meaning of "foolishness" (safahah) in the above verse is the lack of sufficient maturity specifically regarding financial matters, such that the person cannot take charge of their own property or secure their interests in financial exchanges—in common parlance, they are easily cheated. The evidence for this is the second verse which says:

"فَإِنْ آنَسْتُمْ مِنْهُمْ رُشْداً فَادْفَعُوا إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوالَهُمْ‌": "If you find them to be mature, then hand over their properties to them."

Therefore, although the above verse discusses orphans, it contains a general and universal rule for all cases: that a person should never, under any circumstances, entrust properties under their guardianship—or upon which their life depends in some way—to individuals of weak intellect and lack of maturity. In this regard, there is no difference between private property and public property (the wealth of the Islamic government). The evidence for this, besides the breadth of the verse's concept and specifically the word "safiih," are the narrations quoted from the leaders of Islam in this field.

For example: In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "A person named Ibrahim ibn Abd al-Hamid says: I asked the Imam for the commentary of the verse 'وَ لا تُؤْتُوا السُّفَهاءَ أَمْوالَكُمُ‌'. He replied: 'Wine-drinkers are foolish; do not entrust your wealth to them!'" [^1] In another narration, appointing a "drinker of wine" as a trustee for financial matters is prohibited. In short, the description of a wine-drinker as "foolish" is frequently seen in narrations. This expression is perhaps because the wine-drinker loses both his material capital and his spiritual capital. What foolishness is greater than a person paying money to give away his intellect and consciousness and purchase madness, exhausting his various physical powers in this pursuit and causing immense social harm?! In another narration, all individuals who are unreliable for any reason are called "safiih," and entrusting wealth (personal or public) to them is forbidden. "Yunus ibn Ya'qub" says: I asked Imam Ja'far al-Sadiq عليه السلام for the commentary of the verse 'وَ لا تُؤْتُوا السُّفَهاءَ أَمْوالَكُمُ‌'. He said: Man la tathiqu bihi—"A fool is one who is not trustworthy." [^2] From these narrations, it is understood that "safiih" has a broad meaning, and entrusting public and private wealth to them is prohibited. However, this prohibition is in the sense of "Haram" (forbidden) in some cases, and in cases where the degree of foolishness is not severe, it means "Makruh" (disliked).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, under the verse being discussed. [^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, under the verse being discussed; also in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn.

A question arises here: if the verse is about the property of orphans, why does it say "amwalakum" (your wealth) instead of "amwalahum" (their wealth)?

The point of this expression may be to state an important social and economic principle: that Islam considers all members of society as one, such that the interest and benefit of one individual cannot be separated from the interests of others; likewise, the loss of one individual is the loss of the entire society. Therefore, for this reason, the "second-person pronoun" (your) is used instead of the "third-person pronoun" (their). That is, this wealth, in reality, does not belong only to the orphans but is also related to you; if any harm comes to it, it indirectly affects you. Thus, you must exercise complete care in its preservation.

There is another interpretation for this expression: that "your wealth" refers to the wealth of the guardians themselves, not the orphans. That is, if you wish to help orphans who have not yet reached sufficient maturity, you might, under the influence of uncalculated emotions, hand over wealth to them or assign them tasks they are not capable of. Instead of this irrational act, it is better to provide for their food, clothing, and housing until they become mature and sensible.

In fact, this is a great social lesson that the Quran gives us: do not assign "incapable and weak" individuals to tasks they lack the power to perform just to help them personally. For even if this act has a minor benefit for them, it may result in major losses for society. Rather, they should be supported through unconditional aid and light, small tasks.

From this, it becomes clear that when some short-sighted people choose weak and incapable individuals for missionary and religious posts as a way of helping or showing favor to them, it is one of the most harmful and unwise actions.

الَّتِي جَعَلَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ قِياماً:

In this phrase, the Quran uses a beautiful expression regarding wealth and properties, saying:

"These capitals of yours, which Allah has made a means of support for your life and society and without which you cannot stand upright, do not entrust to the foolish and the extravagant." From this expression, the importance that Islam places on financial and economic matters becomes clearly evident. Contrary to what we read in the current Gospel [^1]—that "a rich person shall never enter the kingdom of heaven"—Islam says a nation that is poor can never stand upright. It is strange where they have reached with those incorrect teachings, and at what stage we are wandering despite these sublime teachings. In truth, they distanced themselves from those superstitions and reached somewhere, while we remained distant from these sublime teachings and became so bewildered.

وَ ارْزُقُوهُمْ فِيها وَ اكْسُوهُمْ وَ قُولُوا لَهُمْ قَوْلًا مَعْرُوفاً: At the end of the verse, it gives two important instructions regarding orphans:

First: "Provide for their food and clothing through their wealth" so that they grow up with dignity until they reach maturity. It is interesting that in this verse, the expression used is "fiiha" (in their wealth) rather than "minha" (from their wealth). The concept of this expression is that you should provide for the orphans' lives from the income of their properties and capital. For if it had said provide for their lives from their capital, it would mean gradually taking from the principal capital, and naturally, by the time they reached maturity, they might have lost a significant portion of their capital. But the Quran, by this subtle change in expression, advises guardians to strive to generate profit and income for the orphans' property, at least equal to their needs, so that their original capital remains preserved.

[^1]: Gospel of Matthew, Chapter 19, Verse 23.

Second: The verse says: "Speak to the orphans in a fitting manner." That is, with pleasant and appropriate words, both address their psychological needs and assist in their "intellectual growth" so that they possess sufficient intellectual maturity by the time they reach adulthood. In this way, the program of building their personality is also part of the guardians' duties.

وَ ابْتَلُوا الْيَتامى‌ حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغُوا النِّكاحَ‌: Here, another instruction is given regarding orphans and the fate of their property, stating:

"Test the orphans until they reach the age of marriage," and if at that time you find in them sufficient maturity to manage their own property, return their wealth to them. There are several points in this verse that must be noted:

  1. From the use of the word "hatta" (until), it is inferred that the testing of orphans must be carried out before they reach maturity and in a repeated and continuous manner, so that when they are on the threshold of maturity, their status regarding intellectual growth for managing financial affairs is completely clear. Additionally, it is inferred that the purpose of the test is the gradual training of the orphans; that is, do not wait for them to reach maturity and then take action to hand over their property, but rather prepare them for independent life through practical programs before maturity.

As for how the orphans should be tested, the way is to place some property at their disposal and let them engage in buying, selling, and trade, but their actions should be under the supervision of the "guardian" in a way that does not strip them of their independence. When it becomes clear that they can handle this task and are not deceived in transactions, their property must be handed over to them; otherwise, through continuous training and upbringing, they must be prepared so that they can take the reins of their own lives in the future.

  1. The expression "إِذا بَلَغُوا النِّكاحَ‌" (when they reach marriage) indicates that they reach a stage where they have the capacity for marriage. It is clear that one who has the capacity for marriage will have the capacity to form a family, and such a person cannot reach their goals without capital. Therefore, the beginning of married life is accompanied by the beginning of independent economic life. In other words, their wealth is given to them when they reach both physical maturity—and their need for wealth becomes intense—and intellectual maturity, possessing the ability to preserve the wealth.

  2. The expression "آنَسْتُمْ مِنْهُمْ رُشْداً" (you perceive maturity in them) indicates that their maturity must become completely certain, for "anastum" is from the root "iinas," meaning to observe and see. This root is derived from the word "insan" (human), one of the meanings of which is the pupil of the eye (in fact, during sight and observation, help is taken from the "insan," i.e., the pupil of the eye, and for this reason, observing is expressed as iinas).

وَ لا تَأْكُلُوها إِسْرافاً وَ بِداراً أَنْ يَكْبَرُوا: Then, it emphasizes once more to the guardians that they should not squander or waste the orphans' property under any pretext, and they should not destroy their capital before they grow up.

وَ مَنْ كانَ غَنِيًّا فَلْيَسْتَعْفِفْ وَ مَنْ كانَ فَقِيراً فَلْيَأْكُلْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ‌:

That is, if the guardians of the orphans are well-off and wealthy, they should not use the orphans' property under any pretext. But if they are poor and needy, they may take their wages from the orphans' property in exchange for the efforts they endure to preserve that property, while observing justice and fairness.

In this regard, narrations have also been recorded explaining the content of the verse as stated. Among them, we read in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

Fadhalika rajulun yahbisu nafsahu 'an al-ma'ishati fala ba'sa an ya'kula bil-ma'rufi idha kana yuslihu lahum fa'in kana al-malu qalilan fala ya'kul minhu shay'an

"This refers to a man who keeps himself from his own livelihood [to manage the orphan's affairs]; in that case, there is no harm if he consumes from it in a fitting manner if it is in their best interest. However, if the orphan's wealth is little (and naturally, its guardianship does not occupy much time), then he should not take anything from it."

فَإِذا دَفَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوالَهُمْ فَأَشْهِدُوا عَلَيْهِمْ‌:

This is the final ruling mentioned in this verse regarding the guardians of orphans: "When you wish to hand over their properties to them, take witnesses," so that no room remains for accusation, dispute, or argument.

وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ حَسِيباً:

But know that the real Reckoner is Allah, and the most important thing is that your account be clear before Him. It is He who, if any betrayal occurs on your part and remains hidden from the witnesses, will call it to account.

Ayah

لِّلرِّجَالِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا تَرَكَ ٱلْوَٰلِدَانِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبُونَ وَلِلنِّسَآءِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا تَرَكَ ٱلْوَٰلِدَانِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبُونَ مِمَّا قَلَّ مِنْهُ أَوْ كَثُرَ ۚ نَصِيبًا مَّفْرُوضًا (٧)
Men have a share in the heritage left by parents and near relatives, and women have a share in the heritage left by parents and near relatives, whether it be little or much, a share ordained [by Allah]. (7)

Occasion of Revelation

In the era of Arab Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), the custom was such that only men were recognized as heirs. They believed that anyone who lacked the power to carry weapons, fight, and defend the sanctity of life—and occasionally engage in plundering—was not entitled to inheritance. For this reason, they deprived women and children of inheritance and distributed the deceased's wealth among distant male relatives.

This continued until one of the Ansar named "Aws ibn Thabit" passed away, leaving behind daughters and young sons. His cousins, named "Khalid" and "Arfatah," came and divided his property among themselves, giving nothing to his wife and young children. His wife complained to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and until that time, no ruling had been revealed in Islam regarding this matter. At this moment, the above verse was revealed, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله summoned those two individuals and commanded them not to interfere in the said property in any way, and to leave it for the first-degree survivors—namely the children and the wife—until the manner of its distribution among them became clear in the light of the subsequent verses.

Commentary

Another Step Toward Protecting Women's Rights

This verse is, in fact, another step toward combating the wrong customs and traditions that deprived women and children of their definite rights. Therefore, it complements the discussions that passed in the previous verses. Since the Arabs, with their erroneous and oppressive custom, deprived women and young children of the right to inheritance, the verse drew a line of cancellation over this false law and stated: "For men there is a share of what parents and close relatives leave, and for women there is a share, whether it be little or much." Therefore, no one has the right to usurp the share of another (لِلرِّجالِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا تَرَكَ الْوالِدانِ وَ الْأَقْرَبُونَ وَ لِلنِّساءِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا تَرَكَ الْوالِدانِ وَ الْأَقْرَبُونَ مِمَّا قَلَّ مِنْهُ أَوْ كَثُرَ).

Then, at the end of the verse, to emphasize the matter, He says: "This is a share ordained and obligatory to be paid," so that no doubt remains in this discussion (نَصِيباً مَفْرُوضاً). Furthermore, as we see, the above verse mentions a general rule for all cases; therefore, those who think that if Prophets possess wealth, it does not reach their relatives as inheritance, are acting contrary to the above verse (of course, the intention is the personal property of the Prophet; otherwise, the property of the Public Treasury (Bayt al-Mal), which belongs to the Muslims, will be spent in its proper cases according to the laws of the Public Treasury).

Likewise, from the generality of the above verse and other verses that we will read later regarding inheritance, it becomes clear that adhering to Ta'sib—meaning "allocating a portion of the property to men who are related to the deceased through the father in certain cases, as the scholars of the Ahl al-Sunnah believe"—is also contrary to the teachings of the Quran. This is because its consequence is the deprivation of women from inheritance in some instances, and this is a type of Ignorant discrimination that Islam has negated with the above verse and its likes (Observe carefully!).

Ayah

وَإِذَا حَضَرَ ٱلْقِسْمَةَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينُ فَٱرْزُقُوهُم مِّنْهُ وَقُولُوا۟ لَهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا (٨)
And when the division is attended by relatives, the orphans and the needy, provide for them out of it, and speak to them honourable words. (8)

Commentary

An Ethical Ruling

وَ إِذا حَضَرَ الْقِسْمَةَ أُولُوا الْقُرْبى‌ وَ الْيَتامى‌ وَ الْمَساكِينُ فَارْزُقُوهُمْ مِنْهُ‌ This verse was certainly revealed after the law of inheritance distribution, for it states: "And when the relatives, the orphans, and the needy are present at the [time of] division, give them something therefrom." Therefore, the content of the verse is an ethical and recommended (mustahabb) ruling regarding those classes of people who are deprived of inheritance due to the presence of closer relatives. The verse says: "If at the gathering for the distribution of inheritance, a group of second or third-degree relatives, as well as some orphans and the needy, are present, give them something from the property." In this way, prevent the provocation of their envy and resentment, which might be ignited due to being deprived of the inheritance, and thereby strengthen your bonds of kinship and humanity.

Although the words "orphans" (الْيَتامى‌) and "the needy" (الْمَساكِينُ) are mentioned in an absolute sense, it appears that the intention refers to the orphans and needy within the family. This is because, according to the law of inheritance, the presence of closer tiers deprives the more distant tiers from inheriting. Therefore, if they are present in such a gathering, it is fitting that a suitable gift (the amount of which depends solely on the will of the heirs and shall be taken from the portion of the adult heirs) be given to them.

A group of commentators have considered the possibility that "orphans and the needy" in the verse refers to any orphan or needy person, whether they are relatives of the deceased or not. However, this possibility seems unlikely, as strangers usually have no way into these private family gatherings.

Some commentators also believe that the verse expresses an obligatory (wajib) ruling rather than a recommended one, but this also seems unlikely. If they had an obligatory right, it would have been necessary to define its amount and limits, whereas it has been left to the discretion of the actual heirs.

وَ قُولُوا لَهُمْ قَوْلًا مَعْرُوفاً At the end of the verse, it commands: "And speak to them words of appropriate kindness." This means that in addition to the aspect of material aid, use your ethical capital to win their hearts so that no distress remains in their souls. This command is another indication that the aforementioned ruling is recommended.

From what we have discussed, it also becomes clear that there is no reason to claim that the ruling of this verse has been abrogated by the verses that define the fixed shares of inheritance, as there is no contradiction whatsoever between those verses and this one.

Ayah

وَلْيَخْشَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا۟ مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعَـٰفًا خَافُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ فَلْيَتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلْيَقُولُوا۟ قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا (٩)
Let those fear [the result of mistreating orphans] who, were they to leave behind weak offspring, would be concerned on their account. So let them be wary of Allah, and let them speak upright words. (9)

Commentary

Appealing to Emotions Toward Orphans

وَ لْيَخْشَ الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا ...

To stir the emotions of the people regarding the condition of orphans, the Quran points to a reality that people sometimes overlook, which is: treat the orphans of others in the same way you would wish your own orphans to be treated in the future. Consider the sight of your own defenseless and guardianless children placed under the care of a cold-hearted and treacherous person who neither responds positively to their feelings nor observes justice regarding their property. To what extent does this painful sight distress you, and how much do you care about the future of your own children? Be concerned to that same extent regarding the children and orphans of others, and be pained by their suffering. Therefore, the meaning of the verse is: "Those who fear for the future state of their own children must fear betraying or mistreating orphans."

Fundamentally, social issues always spread like a tradition from today to tomorrow, and from tomorrow to the distant future. Those who establish an oppressive tradition in society—for example, by making the "mistreatment of orphans" a common practice—are in reality the very agents causing the same to happen to their own children in the future. Thus, they not only wrong the children of others but also pave the way for oppression against their own children.

فَلْيَتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ لْيَقُولُوا قَوْلًا سَدِيداً "Since this is the case, the guardians of orphans must fear disobeying Allah's commands and speak to orphans with gentle language and expressions full of human affection," so that their inner distress and the wounds of their hearts may be healed through this means.

This sublime Islamic instruction mentioned in the above phrase points to a psychological point regarding the upbringing of orphans that deserves the utmost attention: the needs of an orphaned child are not limited to food and clothing; rather, responding to their emotions and heart-felt feelings is more important and is extraordinarily effective in building their future. This is because an orphaned child is a human being like any other and must be nourished emotionally as well. They must benefit from the love and affection that a child receives in the embrace of parents. They are not like a lamb that goes to the pasture with the flock in the morning and returns at sunset; rather, in addition to physical care, they must be satisfied psychologically. Otherwise, they will grow up to be cold-hearted, broken, lacking in personality, and dangerous.

Necessary Clarification

One of the companions of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام narrates that one day the sixth Imam عليه السلام said: "Whoever commits an injustice against someone, Allah will cause someone to dominate him who will commit the same injustice and oppression against him and his children." I said to myself in my heart: "How strange! The father is the oppressor, yet the child must face the consequences of his oppression?!" Before I could voice my thought, the Imam recited: "وَ لْيَخْشَ الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعافاً خافُوا عَلَيْهِمْ" [1].

The same question that occurred to the narrator of the Hadith occurs to many: How can Allah allow the punishment for one person's action to fall upon another? And fundamentally, what sin have the children of an oppressor committed to be subjected to oppression?!

The answer to this question can be found in the explanation provided above: the actions that individuals commit in society gradually take the form of a tradition and are passed on to future generations. Therefore, those who lay the foundation of injustice and oppression against orphans in society will eventually find that this wrong innovation will one day affect their own children. In reality, this matter is one of the "existential" (takwini) and consequential effects of their actions. If it is attributed to Allah, it is because all existential effects and the properties of cause and effect are attributed to Him; it does not in any way imply that an injustice or oppression is being committed by Allah against anyone. In short, once the door to injustice and oppression is opened in society, it will eventually catch up with the "oppressor" and his children as well.

[1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 346.

Ayah

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوَٰلَ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ ظُلْمًا إِنَّمَا يَأْكُلُونَ فِى بُطُونِهِمْ نَارًا ۖ وَسَيَصْلَوْنَ سَعِيرًا (١٠)
Indeed those who consume the property of orphans wrongfully, only ingest fire into their bellies, and soon they will enter the Blaze. (10)

Commentary

The Inner Reality of Our Deeds

إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوالَ الْيَتامى‌ ظُلْماً إِنَّما يَأْكُلُونَ فِي بُطُونِهِمْ ناراً At the beginning of this Surah, we stated that its verses were revealed for the purpose of establishing a healthy society. For this reason, it first eradicates the remnants of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) and the wrongdoings of that era—which still existed in the hearts of some new Muslims—to prepare the ground for a healthy society.

And what foul act is worse than consuming the property of orphans? Therefore, at the beginning of this Surah, severe expressions are seen regarding the unlawful seizure of orphans' wealth, the most explicit of which is the verse above.

This verse says: "Indeed, those who unjustly consume the property of orphans are in fact consuming fire."

A similar expression is found in only one other place throughout the entire Holy Quran. That is regarding those who gain benefits by concealing truths and distorting Divine verses, about whom He says: "إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْتُمُونَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَ يَشْتَرُونَ بِهِ ثَمَناً قَلِيلًا أُولئِكَ ما يَأْكُلُونَ فِي بُطُونِهِمْ إِلَّا النَّارَ": "Indeed, those who conceal what Allah has sent down of the Book and exchange it for a small price—those consume into their bellies nothing but fire" (Al-Baqarah: 174).

وَ سَيَصْلَوْنَ سَعِيراً "Sayasla" is originally derived from the root "Salay" (rhyming with 'dard') meaning to enter the fire and burn, and "Sa'ir" means a blazing fire.

In this sentence, the Quran says: In addition to the fact that they are actually consuming fire in this world, they will soon enter a blazing fire in the next world that will burn them severely.

From this verse, it is understood that our deeds, in addition to their outward appearance, have a real inner face that is hidden from us in this world. However, these inner faces will manifest in the next world and constitute the principle of the "Embodiment of Deeds."

The Quran says in this verse: "Those who consume the orphan's wealth, although the outward appearance of their action is enjoying delicious and colorful food, the real face of this food is burning fire, and it is this face that will be revealed on the Day of Resurrection."

The real face of a deed always has a specific correspondence with the outward quality of that deed. Just as consuming an orphan's wealth and usurping his rights burns his heart and torments his soul, the real face of this deed is burning fire.

Paying attention to this subject (the real faces of deeds) is the best deterrent against committing wrongdoings for those who believe in these truths. Can anyone be found who would pick up pieces of fire with his own hand, put them in his mouth, and swallow them? Likewise, it is impossible for people of faith to unjustly consume the orphan's wealth. And if we see that the men of God did not even allow the thought of sin to enter their minds, one reason was that, due to the power of knowledge, faith, and moral cultivation, they saw the real faces of deeds and never thought of doing a bad deed.

An ignorant and uninformed child might be attracted by the beautiful glow of a burning flame and thrust his hand into it, but how could a wise person, who has repeatedly tested the burning nature of fire, even entertain such a thought?

The Hadiths and narrations condemning the violation of orphans' property are numerous and shocking, and even the slightest transgression against the property of orphans is introduced as being subject to this ruling:

In a Hadith narrated from Imam al-Baqir or Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, someone asked: "For what amount of usurping an orphan's wealth is this punishment of fire?" He replied: "For [as little as] two Dirhams!" [1]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, under the noble verse.

Ayahs

يُوصِيكُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِىٓ أَوْلَـٰدِكُمْ ۖ لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ ۚ فَإِن كُنَّ نِسَآءً فَوْقَ ٱثْنَتَيْنِ فَلَهُنَّ ثُلُثَا مَا تَرَكَ ۖ وَإِن كَانَتْ وَٰحِدَةً فَلَهَا ٱلنِّصْفُ ۚ وَلِأَبَوَيْهِ لِكُلِّ وَٰحِدٍ مِّنْهُمَا ٱلسُّدُسُ مِمَّا تَرَكَ إِن كَانَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ وَلَدٌ وَوَرِثَهُۥٓ أَبَوَاهُ فَلِأُمِّهِ ٱلثُّلُثُ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَهُۥٓ إِخْوَةٌ فَلِأُمِّهِ ٱلسُّدُسُ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصِى بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ ۗ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ وَأَبْنَآؤُكُمْ لَا تَدْرُونَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ لَكُمْ نَفْعًا ۚ فَرِيضَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١١)
Allah enjoins you concerning your children: for the male shall be the like of the share of two females, and if there be [two or] more than two females, then for them shall be two-thirds of what he leaves; but if she be alone, then for her shall be a half; and for each of his parents a sixth of what he leaves, if he has children; but if he has no children, and his parents are his [sole] heirs, then it shall be a third for his mother; but if he has brothers, then a sixth for his mother, after [paying off] any bequest he may have made or any debt [he may have incurred]. Your parents and your children—you do not know which of them is likelier to be beneficial for you. This is an ordinance from Allah. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (11)
۞ وَلَكُمْ نِصْفُ مَا تَرَكَ أَزْوَٰجُكُمْ إِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّهُنَّ وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَهُنَّ وَلَدٌ فَلَكُمُ ٱلرُّبُعُ مِمَّا تَرَكْنَ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصِينَ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ ۚ وَلَهُنَّ ٱلرُّبُعُ مِمَّا تَرَكْتُمْ إِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّكُمْ وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَكُمْ وَلَدٌ فَلَهُنَّ ٱلثُّمُنُ مِمَّا تَرَكْتُم ۚ مِّنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ تُوصُونَ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ ۗ وَإِن كَانَ رَجُلٌ يُورَثُ كَلَـٰلَةً أَوِ ٱمْرَأَةٌ وَلَهُۥٓ أَخٌ أَوْ أُخْتٌ فَلِكُلِّ وَٰحِدٍ مِّنْهُمَا ٱلسُّدُسُ ۚ فَإِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَكْثَرَ مِن ذَٰلِكَ فَهُمْ شُرَكَآءُ فِى ٱلثُّلُثِ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصَىٰ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ غَيْرَ مُضَآرٍّ ۚ وَصِيَّةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَلِيمٌ (١٢)
For you shall be a half of what your wives leave, if they have no children; but if they have children, then for you shall be a fourth of what they leave, after [paying off] any bequest they may have made or any debt [they may have incurred]. And for them [it shall be] a fourth of what you leave, if you have no children; but if you have children, then for them shall be an eighth of what you leave, after [paying off] any bequest you may have made or any debt [you may have incurred]. If a man or woman is inherited by siblings and has a brother or a sister, then each of them shall receive a sixth; but if they are more than that, then they shall share in one third, after [paying off] any bequest he may have made or any debt [he may have incurred] without prejudice. [This is] an enjoinment from Allah, and Allah is all-knowing, all-forbearing. (12)

Circumstances of Revelation

"Abd al-Rahman ibn Thabit al-Ansari," the brother of "Hassan ibn Thabit," the famous poet of early Islam, passed away, leaving behind a wife and five brothers. The brothers divided Abd al-Rahman's inheritance among themselves and gave nothing to his wife. She presented the matter to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and complained about them. At this moment, the above verses were revealed, and in them, the inheritance of spouses was precisely determined.

It is also narrated from "Jabir ibn Abd Allah," who said: "I had fallen ill, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله came to visit me. I had lost consciousness. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked for water, performed ablution with some of it, and sprinkled the rest on me. I regained consciousness and said: 'O Messenger of Allah! What will happen to my property after I am gone?' The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله remained silent. Not long after, the above verses were revealed, and the shares of the heirs were determined therein."

Inheritance is a Natural Right

Before we proceed to the commentary of the above verses, it is necessary to point out several points:

First, many might imagine that it would be better if, upon someone's death, their property became part of public property and was placed at the disposal of the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal). However, upon careful consideration, it becomes clear that this is completely far from justice. This is because the issue of "inheritance" is a completely natural and logical matter. Just as parents transfer a portion of their physical and spiritual traits to future generations according to the law of natural heredity, why should their property be an exception to this law and not be transferred to the next generation?

Furthermore, the legitimate property of every individual is the result of their efforts, struggles, and endeavors, and in reality, it represents their accumulated energy. For this reason, we recognize everyone as the natural owner of the fruits of their labor; this is an innate (fitri) judgment.

Therefore, at the time of death, when a person's hand is cut off from their property, the most just way is for this property to belong to those who are the closest individuals to them, and in fact, the existence of those persons is considered a continuation of that person's existence.

For this reason, many people, even though they have sufficient capital for their lives until the end of their days, do not stop striving and working for more production, and their goal is to secure the future of their children. That is, the law of inheritance can give more movement and momentum to the economic wheels of a country. If everyone's property were completely severed from them after death and became part of public property, a significant portion of economic activities might be extinguished.

A witness to this statement is an event that occurred in France: It is said that some time ago, members of the French Parliament abolished the law of inheritance and instead passed a law that whatever remains of a person should be seized as public property and used for public expenses, such that none of the person's relatives would have a share. However, after some time, the undesirable economic effects of this law became apparent. It was observed that it had a profound effect on the country's exports and imports and significantly reduced economic effort. This issue worried economic authorities, and they identified the "abolition of the law of inheritance" as the main factor, and they were forced to revise it.

Therefore, it cannot be denied that the law of inheritance, in addition to being a natural and innate matter, also has a profound effect on the expansion of economic efforts.

Inheritance Among Past Nations

Since the law of inheritance has innate roots, it is seen in various forms among past nations.

Among the Jews, although some claim that no law of inheritance existed, by referring to the Torah, we see that this law is explicitly mentioned in the Book of "Numbers," where it says:

"And speak unto the children of Israel, saying, If a man die, and have no son, then ye shall cause his inheritance to pass unto his daughter. And if he have no daughter, then ye shall give his inheritance unto his brethren. And if he have no brethren, then ye shall give his inheritance unto his father's brethren. And if his father have no brethren, then ye shall give his inheritance unto his kinsman that is next to him of his family, and he shall possess it: and it shall be unto the children of Israel a statute of judgment, as the Lord commanded Moses." 1

1: Numbers 27:8-11.

From the above sentences, it is understood that inheritance among the Children of Israel revolved only around the issue of lineage (nasab), as no mention of a spouse is made therein.

In the religion of Christ عليه السلام as well, this same law of the Torah must be valid, because it is narrated in the existing Gospels that Christ said: "I have not come to change anything from the laws of the Torah." Therefore, we do not find a discussion about inheritance in their existing religious books and epistles; only in a few cases is the derivative of the word "inheritance" mentioned, all of which concern spiritual or otherworldly inheritance. 1 However, among the Arabs before Islam, inheritance was through one of three ways:

  1. Lineage (by lineage, they meant only sons and men; children and women were deprived of inheriting).
  2. Adoption (Tabanni)—meaning a child rejected by one family was attributed to another family and became an "adopted son." In this case, inheritance was established between this adopted son and his adoptive father.
  3. Covenant and Pledge—meaning two people would pledge to defend each other during their lifetime and inherit from each other after death.

Islam purified the innate and natural law of inheritance from the superstitions that had become mixed with it. It abolished the oppressive discriminations that existed between men and women on one hand, and adults and children on the other. It summarized the sources of inheritance into three things, which had no precedent in this form until then:

  1. Lineage (Nasab): In its broad sense, meaning any connection created through birth between two people at various levels, including men, women, adults, and children.
  2. Marriage (Sabab): Meaning the connections created between individuals through marriage.
  3. Wala' (Succession/Affiliation): Meaning other connections found between two people through means other than kinship (Sabab and Nasab).

1: Bible Dictionary, page 903.

Such as "Wala' al-Itq" (Succession of Manumission)—meaning if someone frees their slave, and that slave leaves behind no relatives by lineage or marriage after death, their property goes to the one who freed them (and this is itself a type of encouragement and reward for freeing slaves). And "Wala' Dhaman al-Jarirah" (Succession by Guarantee of Injury), which was a specific pact established between two people by their own will, where both parties committed to defending each other in various cases and inheriting from each other after death (provided there are no relatives by lineage or marriage). And finally, "Wala' al-Imamah" (Succession of the Imam)—meaning if someone passes away and has no heir by lineage, marriage, or otherwise, their inheritance goes to the Imam عليه السلام, or in other words, to the public treasury of the Muslims.

Of course, each of the above categories has conditions and rulings that are detailed in books of jurisprudence (Fiqh).

Commentary

يُوصِيكُمُ اللَّهُ فِي أَوْلادِكُمْ لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ In this verse, the ruling for the first category of heirs (children, fathers, and mothers) is stated. It is obvious that no kinship relationship is closer than the relationship between child and parent; therefore, the Quran has prioritized them over other categories of inheritance.

In the first sentence, It says: "Allah instructs you concerning your children: for the male, what is equal to the share of two females."

It is noteworthy that in terms of phrasing and style of expression, the inheritance of daughters is made the basis, and the inheritance of sons is determined as a derivative and by comparison to it. This is because It says: "males take twice the share of females," which is a type of emphasis on the inheritance rights of daughters and a challenge to the traditions of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) that deprived them entirely. (The philosophy behind the difference in inheritance between these two will be explained shortly).

فَإِنْ كُنَّ نِساءً فَوْقَ اثْنَتَيْنِ فَلَهُنَّ ثُلُثا ما تَرَكَ "But if there are [only] women [daughters], more than two, for them is two-thirds of what he [the deceased] left."

وَ إِنْ كانَتْ واحِدَةً فَلَهَا النِّصْفُ "And if there is only one, for her is half."

Question

A question arises here: the Quran says in this verse "above two" (fawqa ithnatayn), meaning if the daughters are more than two, two-thirds of the property belongs to them. Therefore, the verse is silent regarding the ruling for two daughters; rather, it has only stated the ruling for one daughter and several daughters.

Answer

By paying attention to the first sentence of the verse, the answer to this question becomes clear. That is, the share of two daughters is understood implicitly from the phrase: لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ ("for the male is the like of the share of two females"). For if the survivors of a deceased person are only one son and one daughter, the daughter's share is one-third and the son's share is two-thirds. Therefore, the share of two daughters according to this sentence would be two-thirds. Perhaps for this reason, the subsequent sentence refrained from mentioning the share of two daughters and only referred to the share of several daughters, which also does not exceed two-thirds. (Reflect on this). This matter also becomes clear by referring to the last verse of Surah An-Nisa, for in that verse, the share of one sister is set at half (the same as the share of one daughter), then It says: "If there are two sisters, they shall have two-thirds of what he left." From this ruling, we understand that for two daughters as well, two-thirds of the property is intended.

Furthermore, this expression is seen in Arabic literature where they sometimes say "above two" and mean "two and above."
