Beyond all this, the aforementioned ruling is certain from the perspective of Islamic jurisprudence and Hadith sources. If, hypothetically, there were ambiguity in the above sentence, it is resolved by referring to the Sunnah (Hadith sources).

Why is the man's inheritance twice that of the woman?

Although apparently the man's inheritance is twice that of the woman, upon closer inspection, it becomes clear that from one perspective, women's inheritance is twice that of men! This is because of the protection Islam has provided for women's rights.

To explain: certain duties have been placed upon men, due to which half of men's income is practically spent on women, while no such burden is placed upon women. A man must pay the living expenses of his wife according to her needs—including housing, clothing, food, and other necessities—and the living expenses of young children are also his responsibility. Meanwhile, women are exempt from any payment of expenses, even for themselves. Therefore, a woman can save her entire share of inheritance, while a man is forced to spend it on himself, his wife, and his children. The practical result is that half of the man's income is spent on the woman and half on himself, while the woman's share remains intact.

For further clarification, consider this example: Suppose the total wealth existing in the world is equivalent to 30 billion Tomans, which is gradually divided through inheritance among the women and men of the world (daughters and sons). Now, let us calculate the total income of men versus the total income of women through inheritance. We see that from this amount, 20 billion is the share of men and 10 billion is the share of women. However, as is customary, women marry, and their living expenses will be on the shoulders of men. For this reason, women can save their 10 billion and will practically be partners in the 20 billion share of men, because it is also spent on them and their children.

Therefore, in reality, half of the men's share—which is 10 billion—will be spent on women. By adding this amount to the 10 billion they saved, they will collectively have control over 20 billion—two-thirds of the total money in the world—while men practically consume no more than 10 billion for themselves.

The conclusion is that the real share of women, in terms of consumption and utilization, is twice the real share of men. This difference is because their power to produce wealth is usually less. This is a type of logical and just protection that Islam has exercised for women, making their true share greater, even if apparently their share is half.

Coincidentally, by referring to Islamic traditions, we find that the above question was in people's minds from the very beginning of Islam, and they occasionally asked the leaders of Islam about it. The answers given by these great leaders (the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام) to this question are mostly of the same theme: "Allah has placed the living expenses and the payment of the dowry (Mahr) upon men; for this reason, He has made their share greater."

In the book "Ma'ani al-Akhbar," it is narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Ridha عليه السلام that in response to this question, he said: "The reason the share of women is half the share of men in inheritance is because when a woman marries, she receives something, and the man is forced to give something. Furthermore, the living expenses of women are on the shoulders of men, while the woman has no responsibility for the living expenses of the man or herself."

Inheritance of Parents

As for the inheritance of fathers and mothers, who are also part of the first category and on par with children, it is what has been stated in the above verse, and it has three states:

First state: The deceased has a child or children. In this case, the father and mother each receive one-sixth (وَ لِأَبَوَيْهِ لِكُلِّ واحِدٍ مِنْهُمَا السُّدُسُ مِمَّا تَرَكَ إِنْ كانَ لَهُ وَلَدٌ).

Second state: There is no child, and the heirs are only the father and mother. In this case, the mother's share is one-third of the total property (فَإِنْ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ وَلَدٌ وَ وَرِثَهُ أَبَواهُ فَلِأُمِّهِ الثُّلُثُ). The reason we see no mention of the father's share here is that his share is clear—meaning "two-thirds." Additionally, sometimes the deceased might have a spouse; in this case, the spouse's share is deducted from the father's share, and therefore the father's share in the second state is variable.

Third state: The heirs are only the father and mother and there is no child, but the deceased has brothers from the same father and mother, or only from the father's side. In this case, the mother's share decreases from one-third to one-sixth. In reality, the brothers, although they do not inherit, prevent the mother from receiving the extra amount of inheritance; for this reason, they are called "Hajib" (Excluders) (فَإِنْ كانَ لَهُ إِخْوَةٌ فَلِأُمِّهِ السُّدُسُ).

The philosophy of this ruling is clear, because the existence of multiple brothers increases the burden of the father's life, as the father must pay their expenses until they grow up, and even after they grow up, they still have expenses for the father. For this reason, the brothers who cause the reduction of the mother's share are those from the side of the father and mother or only from the father's side. However, brothers who are only from the mother's side and place no burden on the father do not act as excluders.

Question

A question is raised here: the Quran uses the plural term "ikhwah" (brothers) in this verse, saying فَإِنْ كانَ لَهُ إِخْوَةٌ ("if he has brothers"), and we know that the minimum for a plural is three. However, all Islamic jurists believe that even two brothers can act as excluders and cause the reduction of the mother's inheritance.

Answer

The answer to this question becomes clear by referring to other verses of the Quran. It is not necessary for the plural term to be used for three or more in all cases; rather, in some instances, it is applied to two people as well, such as verse 78 of Surah Al-Anbiya: وكُنَّا لِحُكْمِهِمْ شاهِدِينَ ("And We were witnesses to their judgment").

This verse relates to the judgment of "David" and "Solomon," and the Quran uses the plural pronoun (hum - their) for these two people. From this, it becomes clear that sometimes the plural term can be used for two people. However, this matter requires evidence and context, and in the verse under discussion, the evidence is the consensus of Muslims and the evidence provided by the leaders of Islam. For in this matter, all Islamic scholars, both Shi'a and Sunni (except for Ibn Abbas), have considered two brothers to be included in the ruling of the verse.

Inheritance is After the Will and Debt

مِنْ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصِي بِها أَوْ دَيْنٍ:

Then the Quran says: The heirs can divide the property among themselves only when the deceased has not made a will or does not have a debt upon him. Therefore, if he has made a will or has debts, they must first be fulfilled. (Of course, as stated in the chapter on wills, a person can only make a will regarding one-third of his property; if he wills more than that, it is not valid unless the heirs permit it).

آباؤُكُمْ وَ أَبْناؤُكُمْ لا تَدْرُونَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ لَكُمْ نَفْعاً:

In this sentence, He says: "Your fathers and your sons—you do not know which of them is nearer to you in benefit." This means the law of inheritance is based on the real interests of humanity, and the discernment of these interests is in the hand of Allah. For man cannot everywhere discern what pertains to his good and benefit. Some might imagine that fathers and mothers respond more to his needs and therefore should be prioritized over children in inheritance, while a group might think the opposite. If the law of inheritance were in the hands of the people, a thousand types of chaos, dispute, and conflict would occur. But Allah, who knows the realities of matters as they are, has placed the law of inheritance on a stable system in which the good of humanity lies.

فَرِيضَةً مِنَ اللَّهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً:

"[This is] an ordinance from Allah. Indeed, Allah is ever Knowing and Wise." This sentence is to emphasize the previous points, so that no room for bargaining remains for the people regarding the laws related to inheritance shares.

Inheritance Shares of Spouses from Each Other

وَ لَكُمْ نِصْفُ ما تَرَكَ أَزْواجُكُمْ إِنْ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُنَّ وَلَدٌ:

In the previous verse, the share of children and parents was mentioned, and in this verse, the manner of inheritance of husband and wife from each other is explained. The verse says: "The man inherits half of his wife's property if she has no child. But if she has a child or children (even if from another husband), the husband inherits only one-fourth of the property" (فَإِنْ كانَ لَهُنَّ وَلَدٌ فَلَكُمُ الرُّبُعُ مِمَّا تَرَكْنَ).

Of course, this division is also after the payment of the wife's debts and the fulfillment of her financial wills, as He says: مِنْ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصِينَ بِها أَوْ دَيْنٍ.

As for the inheritance of women from the wealth of their husbands, if the husband has no child, it is one-fourth of the original property, as He says: وَ لَهُنَّ الرُّبُعُ مِمَّا تَرَكْتُمْ إِنْ لَمْ يَكُنْ لكم وَلَدٌ. But if the husbands have a child (even if this child is from another wife), the women's share becomes one-eighth, as He says: فَإِنْ كانَ لَكُمْ وَلَدٌ فَلَهُنَّ الثُّمُنُ مِمَّا تَرَكْتُمْ.

This division, like the previous one, is after the payment of the husband's debts and the fulfillment of his financial will: مِنْ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ تُوصُونَ بِها أَوْ دَيْنٍ.

It is noteworthy that the shares of husbands and wives are reduced to half if the deceased has a child, and that is to respect the situation of the children.

The reason the husbands' share is set at twice the wives' share is the same as what was explained in detail in the previous discussion regarding the inheritance of sons and daughters.

It is also necessary to note that the share determined for women (whether one-fourth or one-eighth) is not exclusive to one wife; rather, if the man has multiple wives, the said share will be divided equally among all of them, and the appearance of the above verse also indicates this.

Inheritance of Brothers and Sisters

وَ إِنْ كانَ رَجُلٌ يُورَثُ كَلالَةً ...

In this sentence of the verse, we encounter a new term that is seen in only two places in the Quran: one in the verse under discussion and the other in the last verse of this same Surah An-Nisa, and that is the word "Kalalah."

What is gathered from dictionaries is that "Kalalah" originally has an infinitive meaning and means "Kalal," which is the loss of strength and ability. 1 But later it was applied to the sisters and brothers who inherit from a deceased person. Perhaps the connection is that brothers and sisters are part of the second category of inheritance and only inherit in the absence of parents and children. Such a person who has no parents or children is certainly in distress and has lost his strength and ability; hence they are called "Kalalah." Raghib says in the book "Al-Mufradat":

"Kalalah" is applied to those who inherit from the deceased while they are not his parents, children, or grandchildren.

However, from a narration reported from the Prophet of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, it is understood that "Kalalah" is a title for the person who has passed away while having neither parents nor children.

There is no obstacle to the title "Kalalah" being applied both to the "deceased" person and to "this group of relatives" (as he has explicitly stated in his book).

As for why the Quran chose this expression (Kalalah) instead of naming brother and sister? Perhaps it is so that such individuals who have neither parents nor children may be mindful that their property will reach those who are signs of their weakness. Therefore, before others use it, they themselves should spend it in more necessary and essential cases, in the way of helping the needy and preserving social interests.

Now let us return to the commentary of the verse

وَ إِنْ كانَ رَجُلٌ يُورَثُ كَلالَةً أَوِ امْرَأَةٌ وَ لَهُ أَخٌ أَوْ أُخْتٌ فَلِكُلِّ واحِدٍ مِنْهُمَا السُّدُسُ:

The verse says: "If a man dies and brothers and sisters inherit from him, or a woman dies and has a brother or a sister, each of them inherits one-sixth of the property."

This is in the case where one brother and one sister remain from the deceased. But if they are more than one, they collectively take one-third; meaning they must divide one-third of the property among themselves (فَإِنْ كانُوا أَكْثَرَ مِنْ ذلِكَ فَهُمْ شُرَكاءُ فِي الثُّلُثِ). Then It adds: مِنْ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصى بِها أَوْ دَيْنٍ. This is provided that the will is performed beforehand and debts are removed from it, "without harm" (ghayra mudarrin)—meaning that the will and also the debt should not have the aspect of causing harm to the heirs. This means he should not will more than one-third, because according to narrations received from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, a will for more than one-third is "harm" (idrar) to the heirs and its validity is conditional upon their consent. Or that he should not confess to debts and liabilities while not being a debtor, in order to deprive the heirs and cause them harm.

At the end, for emphasis, He says: وَصِيَّةً مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَلِيمٌ. "This is an ordinance from Allah, and Allah is Knowing and Forbearing." This is a divine recommendation that must be respected, for Allah is aware of your benefits and interests for which He has established these rulings, and He is also aware of the intentions of the testators. At the same time, He is "Forbearing" (Halim) and does not immediately punish those who act contrary to His command.

Here, it is necessary to point out several matters:

  1. What has come from the above verse regarding the inheritance of brothers and sisters, although apparently absolute and including full brothers and sisters, paternal only, and maternal only, yet by paying attention to the last verse of this same Surah An-Nisa—the commentary of which will come soon—it becomes clear that this verse refers only to the maternal brothers and sisters of the deceased (those who are connected to him only through the mother). Meanwhile, the last verse of Surah An-Nisa is about full brothers and sisters or paternal ones only. (We will explain the evidence for this, God willing, under that same verse). Therefore, although both verses discuss the inheritance of "Kalalah" (brothers and sisters) and apparently seem inconsistent, by reflecting on the content of the two verses, it becomes clear that each speaks about a specific group of brothers and sisters, and there is no contradiction between them.

  2. It is clear that the inheritance of this category is in the case where no heir from the first category—namely parents and children—is present. The witness to this is the verse: وَ أُولُوا الْأَرْحامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلى بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتابِ اللَّهِ ("And blood relatives are nearer to one another in the decree of Allah") (Al-Anfal: 75). Also, the numerous reports received in this regard are another witness to the determination of inheritance categories and the prioritization of some over others.

  3. From the expression فَهُمْ شُرَكاءُ فِي الثُّلُثِ ("they are partners in one-third"—referring to maternal brothers and sisters if they are more than one), it is understood that they divide the one-third among themselves equally, and man and woman have no difference here, because the concept of absolute partnership implies equality of shares.

  4. From the above verse, it is well understood that a person has no right to stage a scene against the heirs through a will or confession to a debt that is not upon his conscience, thereby wasting their rights. He is only duty-bound to mention his real debts at the last opportunity and has the right to make a just will, which in reports is expressed as the amount of one-third.

In the narrations of the leaders of Islam in this regard, severe expressions are seen, including a Hadith where we read: ان الضرار فى الوصية من الكبائر "Causing harm in a will [to the heirs] is among the major sins." 1 Islam, in truth, through this instruction, wants both to benefit the person from a portion of his property even after death, and also the heirs, lest malice and complexes arise in their hearts and the bond of love, which should remain even after death, becomes weakened.

1: Majma' al-Bayan.

Ayahs

تِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (١٣)
These are Allah’s bounds, and whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle, He shall admit him to gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever]. That is the great success. (13)
وَمَن يَعْصِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَيَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَهُۥ يُدْخِلْهُ نَارًا خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا وَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (١٤)
But whoever disobeys Allah and His Apostle and transgresses the bounds set by Allah, He shall make him enter a Fire, to remain in it [forever], and there will be a humiliating punishment for him. (14)

Commentary

"Hudud" (حدود) is the plural of "hadd" (حد), which originally means to prevent or restrain. Subsequently, it is applied to anything that creates a distance between two things and distinguishes them from one another. For example, the "hadd" (boundary) of a house, a garden, a city, or a country refers to the points that separate them from other areas.

In the above verse, using the word "tilka" (these), it refers to the laws of inheritance mentioned in the preceding verses and states: "These are the limits and boundaries of Allah, and crossing or transgressing them is forbidden; those who step beyond these boundaries and transgress are considered sinners and criminals."

This expression (تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ) appears in several instances in the Holy Quran, and all of them occur after the explanation of a series of social rulings and regulations. For example, in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 187, after announcing the prohibition of sexual intercourse during I'tikaf and rulings regarding fasting; in Al-Baqarah, verses 229 and 230, and Surah At-Talaq, verse 1, after explaining part of the rulings on divorce; and in Surah Al-Mujadila, verse 4, after explaining the expiation (kaffarah) for Zihar. In all these cases, there are rulings and laws whose transgression is forbidden, and for this reason, they are recognized as Divine boundaries. [^1]

وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّاتٍ...

After referring to this portion of Divine limits and boundaries, He says: "Those who obey Allah and the Prophet and respect these boundaries shall reside eternally in gardens of Paradise, beneath whose trees water never ceases to flow." At the end of the verse, He says: "This is the great success and victory" (وَذَلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ).

وَمَنْ يَعْصِ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَيَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَهُ يُدْخِلْهُ نَارًا خَالِدًا فِيهَا...

This section describes the opposite of those mentioned in the previous verse: "Those who disobey Allah and His Prophet and transgress His limits shall be in the Fire eternally."

Of course, we know that mere disobedience to Allah (even if it is a major sin) does not necessarily result in khulud (eternal punishment). Therefore, the above verse refers to those who, out of rebellion, defiance, enmity, and denial of Divine verses, trample upon Allah's command and, in reality, have no faith in Allah and the Last Day. Considering that "limits" (hudud) is plural and encompasses all Divine laws, this meaning does not seem far-fetched, because someone who breaks all Divine laws usually does not believe in Allah; otherwise, they would respect at least some portion of them.

It is noteworthy that in the previous verse, regarding the inhabitants of Paradise, "khalidina fiha" (they shall be eternally in Paradise) is used in the plural form, whereas in this verse, regarding the inhabitants of Hell, "khalidan fiha" is used in the singular form. This difference in expression in two consecutive verses perhaps suggests that the people of Paradise have their own social gatherings, which is itself considered one of the heavenly blessings for them, while the people of Hell are so preoccupied with themselves and immersed in their own suffering that they do not attend to others and are practically alone. This matter even holds true in this world regarding individualistic and autocratic people compared to united and communal people; the latter are in a paradise in this world, while the former are in a hell.

وَلَهُ عَذَابٌ مُهِينٌ:

"They shall have a humiliating punishment mixed with insult." In fact, the previous phrase reflected the physical aspect of Divine punishment, and in this phrase, where the issue of humiliation is raised, it refers to its spiritual aspect.

[^1]: A more detailed discussion regarding the commentary of "Hudud Allah" can be found in Volume 2 of this Tafsir, page 121.

Advantages of the Islamic Law of Inheritance

There are advantages in the law of inheritance in general, and in the Islamic law of inheritance in particular, some of which are mentioned below:

  1. In the Islamic system of inheritance, none of the relatives of the deceased are deprived of inheritance based on the hierarchy of ranks. What was common among the pre-Islamic Arabs or in some countries—where women or children were deprived of inheritance due to their inability to carry weapons and participate in the battlefield, and the wealth of the deceased was given to more distant individuals—does not exist in Islam. All individuals are included in the laws of inheritance in proportion to their relationship with the deceased.

  2. This law provides a positive response to the natural and legitimate needs of human beings, as humans always desire to see the fruit of their labor in the hands of those who are considered part of their own being, and whose lives are, in reality, a continuation of their own. Therefore, we see that in this law, the share of children is the greatest, while at the same time, parents and other relatives also have a significant share in their own right.

  3. This law encourages individuals to exert more effort and strive toward the production of wealth and the turning of economic wheels. When a person sees the results of a lifetime of labor benefiting their loved ones, they are encouraged to work regardless of age or circumstances, and no pause or stagnation occurs in their activities. As we mentioned, the law of inheritance was abolished in some countries and the property of the deceased was placed at the disposal of the state, but soon the negative effects of this law appeared in the economic environment of those countries as an obvious stagnation; for this reason, they were forced to abolish said law.

  4. The Islamic law of inheritance prevents the accumulation of wealth, because in this system, after every generation, wealth is divided fairly among multiple individuals, and in this way, it assists in the equitable distribution of wealth. It is noteworthy that this distribution of wealth is not like some forms of wealth distribution in today's world, which are often accompanied by social unrest; rather, it is such that everyone accepts it with open arms.

  5. The Islamic law of inheritance is not organized solely based on the nature of the relationship with the deceased, but the actual needs of the heirs are also taken into account. For example, if we see that the inheritance of sons is apparently twice that of daughters, or the inheritance of the father in some cases is more than that of the mother, it is because in Islamic laws, men have numerous financial responsibilities and the cost of living for women is upon their shoulders; therefore, their financial need is greater than that of women.

What are "Awl" and "Ta'sib"?

In the book of Islamic inheritance, we encounter two important discussions regarding the issues of "Awl" and "Ta'sib." The source of this discussion begins from the fact that the shares of inheritance, as explained in the previous verses, are sometimes less than the total estate and sometimes more.

For example: If the heirs are only two sisters (paternal and maternal) and a husband, the inheritance of the two sisters is two-thirds of the estate, and the husband's inheritance is one-half. The sum of these two is 7/6, meaning it is 1/6 more than the total estate. Here, the discussion arises: should the 1/6 be reduced fairly and proportionately from all heirs? Or should it be reduced from specific individuals? The well-known view among Sunni scholars is that it must be reduced from everyone, and jurists call this "Awl" (because Awl literally means increase, rise, or height).

In the above example, they say: the extra 1/6 must be reduced from both in proportion to their shares [^1]. Similarly, in other cases, they essentially treat the shareholders of the inheritance like creditors where the debtor is unable to pay all their claims and is, so to speak, "bankrupt." We know that in such a case, the deficit is reduced proportionately from all creditors.

However, in the view of "Shi'a jurists," the deficit always falls upon specific individuals. In the above example, they apply the deficit only to the two sisters and say: as stated in the Hadith, "It is impossible for Allah, who keeps account of everything, even the sands of the desert, to set the shares of inheritance in a way that there is a deficit." Surely, Allah has established a law in such cases whereby no deficit is conceivable. That law is that among the heirs, some have a fixed share mentioned in the Quran in terms of "minimum" and "maximum," such as the share of the husband, wife, father, and mother; but others are not like this, such as "two sisters" and "two daughters." From this, we understand that the deficit must always fall upon those whose minimum and maximum shares are not specified—meaning they are subject to change and fluctuation. Therefore, in the above example, no deficit affects the husband, and only the extra 1/6 must be reduced from the share of the two sisters (observe carefully).

And sometimes, conversely, the sum of the shares is less than the total estate, and something extra remains. For example, if a man dies and only one daughter and a mother remain, we know that the mother's share in this case is 1/6 and the daughter's is 3/6 (1/2) of the estate, the sum of which is 4/6, meaning 2/6 remains extra. Sunni scholars and jurists say: this extra must be given to the "Asabah" (the male relatives of the next level [^2], such as the brothers of the deceased in this example), and this is technically called "Ta'sib." However, Shi'a jurists believe that all of it must be divided between the two (mother and daughter) in a 1:3 ratio, because as long as the previous level exists, it does not reach the next level. Furthermore, giving the extra amount to the men of the next level resembles the laws of the era of ignorance (Jahiliyyah) which deprived women of inheritance without reason. (The above discussion is a complex scholarly debate, the summary of which is provided here; for more detail, refer to books of jurisprudence).

[^1]: The method of calculation is that from the fractional numbers 4/6 (the share of two sisters) and 3/6 (the share of the husband), the extra 1/6 must be reduced proportionately; that is, the 1/6 is divided between the two in a 4:3 ratio. According to the rule of "proportional sharing" in mathematics, we conclude that 4/42 is deducted from the sisters' share and 3/42 from the husband's share. [^2]: Men who are related to the deceased directly or through another male.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّـٰتِى يَأْتِينَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةَ مِن نِّسَآئِكُمْ فَٱسْتَشْهِدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ أَرْبَعَةً مِّنكُمْ ۖ فَإِن شَهِدُوا۟ فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ فِى ٱلْبُيُوتِ حَتَّىٰ يَتَوَفَّىٰهُنَّ ٱلْمَوْتُ أَوْ يَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُنَّ سَبِيلًا (١٥)
Should any of your women commit an indecent act, produce against them four witnesses from yourselves, and if they testify, detain them in [their] houses until death finishes them, or Allah decrees a course for them. (15)
وَٱلَّذَانِ يَأْتِيَـٰنِهَا مِنكُمْ فَـَٔاذُوهُمَا ۖ فَإِن تَابَا وَأَصْلَحَا فَأَعْرِضُوا۟ عَنْهُمَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ تَوَّابًا رَّحِيمًا (١٦)
Should two among you commit it, chastise them both; but if they repent and reform, let them alone. Indeed Allah is all-clement, all-merciful. (16)

Commentary

[The Punishment for the Deviation of Married Women]

وَ اللَّاتِي يَأْتِينَ الْفاحِشَةَ ...

The word "fāḥishah," as we have previously mentioned, originally means an extremely ugly act or speech. Its use in the context of "adultery" (zinā) and acts contrary to chastity is for this very reason. This word appears 13 times in the Holy Quran, sometimes referring to "adultery," sometimes to "homosexuality," and sometimes to shameful and ugly deeds in general.

As most commentators have understood from this noble verse, it refers to the punishment of married women who become tainted with "indecency" (fāḥishah). It states: "If your wives become tainted with 'adultery,' call four people as witnesses to this act. If four people testify to this matter, confine them in houses until death overtakes them."

The evidence that the above verse refers to "adultery by a married person" (zinā al-muḥṣanah)—in addition to the context found in the following verse—is the expression "min nisā’ikum" (from your women/wives). This expression is repeatedly used in the Quran to refer to wives. Therefore, the punishment for an act contrary to chastity for married women in this verse is determined as "life imprisonment."

However, it immediately says: "أَوْ يَجْعَلَ اللَّهُ لَهُنَّ سَبِيلًا" (or Allah ordains for them another way). Thus, the punishment of imprisonment must continue for them until their death, or until a new law is determined for them by Allah.

From this expression, it is understood that this ruling was a temporary one, and it was announced from the very beginning that in the future (after the environment and public thought were prepared), a new ruling regarding them would be revealed. At that time, women who were subject to this law and were still alive would naturally be released from prison, and no other punishment would be implemented regarding them. Their release from prison is due to the cancellation of the previous ruling, while the non-implementation of the new punishment regarding them is because the penal law does not include cases committed before the law came into effect. In this way, whatever the future law might be, it is a way for the salvation of these prisoners; however, this new law will certainly apply to all those who commit the act in the future (reflect on this).

As for the possibility suggested by some that the phrase "أَوْ يَجْعَلَ اللَّهُ لَهُنَّ سَبِيلًا" means that Allah opened a way for their freedom through the future command regarding the stoning of such individuals, this is incorrect. This is because it is not consistent with the expression "lahunna sabīlā" (a way in their favor), as execution is not a way of salvation.

We know that the law subsequently established in Islam for those committing adultery while married was the law of "rajm" (stoning). (This law is definitively established in the ḥadīths of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, even if it is not explicitly mentioned in the Quran).

From what we have said above, it becomes clear that the above verse has never been "abrogated" (naskh), because abrogation applies to rulings that are stated in an absolute manner from the beginning, not those stated as temporary and limited. The above verse mentions the ruling of "life imprisonment" as a limited and temporary ruling. If we observe that in some narrations it is explicitly stated that the above verse was abrogated by the rulings regarding the punishment for acts contrary to chastity, the meaning is not "abrogation" in the technical sense. This is because in the language of narrations, "abrogation" is used for any kind of restriction (taqyīd) or specification (takhṣīṣ) of a ruling (reflect on this).

Additionally, it should be noted that the command to confine such women in houses is a ruling that, on one hand, is to their benefit, as it is far better than being confined in public prisons. On the other hand, experience has shown that public prisons have a profound effect on polluting society, as these centers usually turn into large schools of vice where criminal individuals share their experiences with one another during constant association and ample time.

وَ الَّذانِ يَأْتِيانِها مِنْكُمْ‌ ...

Then, in this verse, it explains the ruling for adultery and acts contrary to chastity for "unmarried persons" (ghayr al-muḥṣanah), stating: "And the two among you (a man and a woman who are unmarried) who commit this ugly act, punish (harass) them."

Although there is no explicit mention of "unmarried adultery" in this verse, since it follows the previous verse and the punishment mentioned for adultery here differs from and is lighter than the punishment in the previous verse, it is inferred that this ruling concerns those perpetrators of adultery who were not included in the previous verse. Since the previous verse, by the context mentioned, is specific to "adultery by married persons," we conclude that this verse explains the ruling for "unmarried adultery."

It is also clear that the punishment mentioned in this verse is a general punishment, and verse 2 of Surah An-Nur, which states the legal penalty (ḥadd) for adultery as one hundred lashes for each party, can be considered an interpretation and explanation for this verse. For this reason, this ruling has also not been abrogated.

In the "Tafsir al-Ayyashi," it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding this verse that he said: "يعني البكر اذا اتت الفاحشة التي اتتها هذه الثيب فاذوهما" Meaning: "This verse refers to the unmarried man and woman; if they commit an act contrary to chastity, they must be harassed (and punished)."

Based on what we have said, although the word "al-ladhān" is a masculine dual pronoun, it refers to both the man and the woman, following the linguistic rule of "taghlīb" (predominance).

A group of commentators have suggested the possibility that this verse concerns the ugly act of "homosexuality" (liwāṭ), and considered the previous verse to be related to "lesbianism" (musāḥaqah). However, considering that the pronoun in "ya’tiyānihā" refers back to the word "fāḥishah" mentioned in the previous verse, it is understood that the type of act contrary to chastity mentioned in this verse is the same as the type in the previous verse. Therefore, considering one to be about "homosexuality" and the other about "lesbianism" is contrary to the apparent meaning (even though both types share a general category, i.e., same-sex acts). Thus, both verses are about "adultery."

Furthermore, we know that the punishment for "homosexuality" in Islam is "execution," not merely harassment or flogging, and there is no evidence that the ruling of the verse under discussion has been abrogated.

فَإِنْ تابا وَ أَصْلَحا فَأَعْرِضُوا عَنْهُما إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ تَوَّاباً رَحِيماً.

At the end of the verse, it refers to the issue of repentance, pardon, and forgiveness for such sinners, stating: "If they truly repent and reform themselves and make amends for the past, then leave them alone (cease their punishment), for Allah is Ever-Relenting, Most Merciful."

This command, in reality, opens the door of return for such wrongdoers, so that in the case of repentance and reform, the Islamic society accepts them with open arms and they do not remain as outcasts of society.

However, of course (as stated in books of jurisprudence), repentance is valid only if it occurs before the crime is proven in an Islamic court, before witnesses are presented, and before the court's verdict is issued. Otherwise, repentance that occurs after the verdict has been issued will have no effect [on the legal penalty].

From this ruling, it is also inferred that those who have repented should never be blamed for their past sins. Where the legal punishment and the religious penalty are dropped through repentance, it is even more appropriate that people overlook their past. Similarly, those upon whom this penalty is carried out and who subsequently repent should be included in the forgiveness of Muslims.

The "Easy yet Difficult" Method of Islamic Penal Laws

From time to time, on various occasions, people ask: Why has Islam established such hard and grueling penal and criminal laws? For example, for being tainted once by married adultery, life imprisonment was initially ordained and then the death penalty was set. Would it not have been better to set milder punishments for such acts so that a balance between "crime" and "punishment" is established?!

However, it must be noted that although Islamic penal laws are seemingly harsh and severe, in contrast, the way of proving a crime in Islam is not so easy. Conditions have been set for it such that, usually, until the sin becomes public, those conditions are not met.

For instance: increasing the number of witnesses to four, as mentioned in the above verse, is so heavy that only reckless and brazen individuals might be found guilty. It is obvious that such persons must face the severest punishment to serve as a lesson to others and to cleanse the environment from the pollution of sin. Furthermore, conditions have been set for the testimony of witnesses, such as actual sighting, not sufficing with circumstantial evidence, and consistency in testimony, which makes proving the crime even more difficult.

In this way, Islam has placed the possibility of an exceptionally severe punishment before such sinners, and this very possibility—even if weak—can affect the morale of most individuals. Yet, it has made the way of proving it difficult so that, in practice, such violence is not carried out on a wide scale. In reality, Islam intended to preserve the deterrent effect of this penal law without many individuals being subject to execution.

The result is that the Islamic method in "determining punishment" and the "way of proving the crime" is a method that has the maximum effect in saving society from the pollution of sin, while the individuals subject to these punishments are not many in practice. For this reason, we have described this method as the "easy yet difficult" (sahl wa mumtani‘) method.

Ayahs

إِنَّمَا ٱلتَّوْبَةُ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلسُّوٓءَ بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِن قَرِيبٍ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَتُوبُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١٧)
[Acceptance of] repentance by Allah is only for those who commit evil out of ignorance and then repent promptly. It is such whose repentance Allah will accept, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (17)
وَلَيْسَتِ ٱلتَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَهُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ قَالَ إِنِّى تُبْتُ ٱلْـَٔـٰنَ وَلَا ٱلَّذِينَ يَمُوتُونَ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٨)
But [acceptance of] repentance is not for those who go on committing misdeeds: when death approaches any of them, he says, ‘I repent now.’ Nor is it for those who die while they are faithless. For such We have prepared a painful punishment. (18)

Commentary

Conditions for the Acceptance of Repentance

إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللَّهِ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ In the previous verse, the issue of the dropping of the prescribed punishment (hadd) and the penalty for those who commit acts contrary to chastity was explicitly stated in the light of repentance. At its conclusion, with the phrase إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ تَوَّاباً رَحِيماً ("Indeed, Allah is ever-accepting of repentance and Merciful"), a reference was made to the acceptance of repentance by the Lord. In this verse, the issue of repentance and some of its conditions are explicitly stated, saying: "Repentance is only for those who commit a sin out of ignorance (jahalah)."

Now let us see what is meant by "ignorance" (jahalah). Does it mean actual ignorance and being unaware that the act is a sin, or does it mean a lack of awareness regarding its sinister effects and painful consequences?

Although the word "ignorance" (jahl) and its derivatives are used with various meanings, it can be inferred from the context that in the verse under discussion, it refers to the outburst of instincts, the dominance of rebellious desires, and their triumph over the power of intellect and faith. In such a state, although a person's knowledge of the sin does not vanish, it falls under the influence of those rebellious instincts and becomes practically ineffective. When knowledge loses its effect, it is practically equivalent to ignorance and lack of knowledge.

However, if a sin is not due to such "ignorance" but is committed out of denial of the Lord's command, stubbornness, and enmity, such a sin indicates disbelief (kufr). For this reason, repentance for it is not accepted unless the person turns back from this state and abandons the stubbornness and denial.

In fact, this verse expresses the same reality that Imam Sajjad عليه السلام explained with more detail in the Du'a of Abu Hamzah, where he says:

“O my God, I did not disobey You when I disobeyed You while being a denier of Your Lordship, nor out of belittling Your command, nor seeking Your punishment, nor being indifferent to Your threat, nor undervaluing Your warning; rather, it was a mistake that presented itself to me, my soul enticed me, and my desires overcame me...”

ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِنْ قَرِيبٍ In this phrase, another condition of repentance is mentioned: "Then they repent soon after."

Regarding what is meant by "soon" (qarib - a near time), there is discussion among commentators. A large group takes it to mean "before the signs of death appear," and they consider the subsequent verse—which says repentance is not accepted after the appearance of the signs of death—as evidence for this. Thus, the expression "soon" might be because, fundamentally, the life of this world, whatever its length, is short and its end is near.

However, others have taken it to mean a time close to the sin itself; that is, the person should soon regret their action and return to Allah. This is because perfect repentance is that which completely washes away the effects and residues of sin from the human soul and spirit, leaving not the slightest trace of it in the heart. This is only possible if one repents at a close interval before the sin takes root in the person's being and becomes a "second nature." Otherwise, the effects of sin will often remain in the corners of the human heart and soul. Therefore, perfect repentance is that which is performed quickly, and the word "soon" (qarib) is also more linguistically and conventionally appropriate for this meaning.

It is true that repentance after a long time is also accepted, but it is not "perfect" repentance. Perhaps the expression عَلَى اللَّهِ (a repentance that Allah has made incumbent upon Himself to accept) also refers to this meaning, as this expression appears only in this verse of the Quran. Its implication is that the acceptance of such repentances is among the rights of the servants, whereas the acceptance of distant repentances by Allah is a type of grace (tafaddul), not a right.

فَأُولئِكَ يَتُوبُ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً After mentioning the conditions of repentance, He says: "Allah accepts the repentance of such people, and Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise."

وَ لَيْسَتِ التَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ ... In this verse, He refers to those whose repentance is not accepted, saying: "Those who find themselves on the brink of death and say, 'Now I repent of my sin,' their repentance will not be accepted."

The reason for this is clear: during the throes of death and on the verge of passing, the veils are removed from before a person's eyes, and a different vision is granted to them. They see with their own eyes part of the realities related to the next world and the results of the deeds they performed in this life, and matters take on a sensory nature. It is obvious that in such a case, every sinner would regret their evil deeds, just as someone who sees a flame of fire near them would flee from it.

It is certain that the basis of religious obligation (taklif) and the Lord's trial is not based on such observations, but rather on faith in the unseen (al-ghayb) and observation through the eye of intellect and wisdom.

For this reason, we read in the Holy Quran that when the first signs of worldly punishment appeared to some of the previous nations, the door of repentance was closed to them. In the story of Pharaoh, we read: حَتَّى إِذا أَدْرَكَهُ الْغَرَقُ قالَ آمَنْتُ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا الَّذِي آمَنَتْ بِهِ بَنُوا إِسْرائِيلَ وَ أَنَا مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ آلْآنَ وَ قَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ وَ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ (Yunus: 90-91): "Until, when drowning overtook him, he said, 'I believe that there is no deity except that in whom the Children of Israel believe, and I am of the Muslims.' [It was said], 'Now? And you had disobeyed before and were of the corrupters!?'" For this reason, his repentance was not accepted.

From some verses of the Quran (such as verse 12 of Surah As-Sajdah), it is inferred that sinners will regret their actions on the Day of Resurrection upon seeing the divine punishment, but their regret will be of no use. Such people are exactly like criminals who, when their eyes fall upon the gallows and they feel the rope around their neck, regret their actions. It is clear that this regret is neither a virtue, nor an honor, nor a perfection, and for this reason, such repentance is ineffective.

Of course, this verse does not contradict the narrations that say repentance is accepted until the last breath, because those narrations refer to moments when one has not yet observed the definitive signs of death and has not, so to speak, acquired "purgatorial vision" (did-e barzakhi).

The second group of those whose repentance is not accepted are those who leave the world in a state of disbelief. In the above verse, He says regarding them: وَ لَا الَّذِينَ يَمُوتُونَ وَ هُمْ كُفَّارٌ: "Nor [is repentance accepted for] those who die while they are disbelievers."

This reality has been reiterated in several other verses of the Holy Quran [1]. Now the question arises: when do such people who die in a state of disbelief repent such that their repentance is not accepted?

Some have suggested that their repentance is not accepted in the other world, and some have suggested that "repentance" here does not refer to the repentance of the servants, but rather the "repentance of Allah," meaning His return to forgiveness and mercy. However, it appears that the verse pursues another goal, saying: "Those who repented of their sins while in a state of health, safety, and faith, but did not leave the world with faith at the time of death, their past repentances are also rendered ineffective."

To explain: we know that one of the conditions for the acceptance of a person's good deeds is "consistency in faith" (muwafat), meaning leaving the world with faith. Those who are disbelievers at the moment of the end of life, their past deeds (even the good deeds they performed while in a state of faith) are, according to the explicit text of Quranic verses [2], nullified (habt). Their repentances from sin, even if performed while in a state of faith, will also be nullified in such a case.

In summary, the conditions for the acceptance of repentance are two things: first, that it be before observing the signs of death, and second, that the person leaves the world with faith.

Additionally, it is inferred from this verse that a person should not delay repentance, for death may arrive suddenly and the doors of repentance may be closed to them. It is noteworthy that delaying repentance—which is referred to as taswif—is placed in the above verse alongside "death in a state of disbelief," and this is a sign of the importance the Quran gives to this subject.

أُولئِكَ أَعْتَدْنا لَهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً At the end of the verse, He says: "For both these groups, We have prepared a painful punishment." It need not be mentioned that repentance, in addition to what has been said, has other conditions that will be mentioned in appropriate verses.

[1]: Surah Al-Imran: 91, Al-Baqarah: 161, Al-Baqarah: 217, Muhammad: 34. [2]: Surah Al-Baqarah: 217.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَن تَرِثُوا۟ ٱلنِّسَآءَ كَرْهًا ۖ وَلَا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ لِتَذْهَبُوا۟ بِبَعْضِ مَآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ إِلَّآ أَن يَأْتِينَ بِفَـٰحِشَةٍ مُّبَيِّنَةٍ ۚ وَعَاشِرُوهُنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ فَإِن كَرِهْتُمُوهُنَّ فَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تَكْرَهُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَيَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ فِيهِ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا (١٩)
O you who have faith! It is not lawful for you to inherit women forcibly, and do not press them to take away part of what you have given them, unless they commit a gross indecency. Consort with them in an honourable manner; and should you dislike them, maybe you dislike something while Allah invests it with an abundant good. (19)

Occasion of Revelation

In the commentary Majma' al-Bayan, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that this verse was revealed regarding those who would keep their wives without treating them as a wife should be treated, waiting for them to die so they could take possession of their property.

It is also narrated from Ibn Abbas that the above verse was revealed regarding individuals whose wives had heavy dowries (mahr); despite having no desire to continue the marriage, they were unwilling to divorce them due to the weight of the dowry. Consequently, they would place them under pressure so that the wives would forfeit their dowry to obtain a divorce.

A group of commentators has cited another occasion of revelation for the above verse which does not align with this verse, but rather with Verse 22, which we shall mention under that verse, God willing.

Commentary

Further Defense of Women's Rights

يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَرِثُوا النِّسَاءَ كَرْهًا At the beginning of the Surah, we mentioned that the verses of this Surah combat many of the improper practices of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah). In the verse under discussion, several unpleasant customs of that era are pointed out, and Muslims are warned not to become tainted by them:

1- Do not imprison women for their wealth – As mentioned in the occasion of revelation, one of the oppressive behaviors of men during the Age of Ignorance was that they would marry wealthy women who lacked beauty, then leave them in a state of limbo. They would neither divorce them nor treat them as a wife, hoping that death would overtake them so they could inherit their wealth. The above verse says: "O you who have believed, it is not lawful for you to inherit women by compulsion [and by creating distress]," thus condemning the aforementioned practice.

2- Do not pressure women to forfeit their dowry – Another of their reprehensible customs was to pressure women through various means to forfeit their dowry and seek a divorce. This was especially common when the woman had a heavy dowry. The above verse, with the phrase وَلَا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ لِتَذْهَبُوا بِبَعْضِ مَا آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ, prohibits this act and says: "And do not distress them in order to take back part of what you have given them."

However, this ruling has an exception mentioned in the phrase إِلَّا أَنْ يَأْتِينَ بِفَاحِشَةٍ مُبَيِّنَةٍ, which is that if they commit a lewd and shameful act, husbands may pressure them to forfeit their dowry and obtain a divorce. In reality, this act is a form of punishment and akin to taking compensation for the improper actions of this group of women.

Does "manifest lewdness" (fahishah mubayyinah) in the above verse refer specifically to acts contrary to chastity, or to any form of severe discord? There is discussion among commentators, but in a tradition narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, it is explicitly stated that it includes any severe opposition, disobedience, and incompatibility from the wife [1]. (Of course, this does not mean every minor disagreement, as the concept of the word "fahishah" implies significance and extraordinariness, and the mention of the word "mubayyinah" [manifest] further emphasizes this).

3- Consort with them in a fitting manner – With the phrase وَعَاشِرُوهُنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ, He issues the command for honorable consorting and appropriate humane behavior toward women. Following that, He adds: فَإِنْ كَرِهْتُمُوهُنَّ فَعَسَى أَنْ تَكْرَهُوا شَيْئًا وَيَجْعَلَ اللَّهُ فِيهِ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا, meaning that even if for some reasons you are not fully satisfied with your spouses and they appear unpleasant to you due to certain matters, do not immediately decide on separation or ill-treatment. Exercise forbearance as much as you are able, "for perhaps you are mistaken in your judgment, and that which you dislike, Allah has placed within it much good, blessing, and benefit." Therefore, as long as the situation has not become unbearable, it is proper not to abandon honorable consorting and worthy behavior. This is especially true because spouses often fall prey to groundless suspicions and baseless likes and dislikes toward one another, and their judgments in such states are often incorrect, to the point that virtues appear as vices and vices as virtues in their eyes. However, with the passage of time and by exercising forbearance, the truths gradually become manifest.

Furthermore, it should be noted that the expression "much good" (khayran kathira), which is promised in the verse to spouses who exercise forbearance, has a broad meaning, one of the clear instances of which is righteous, capable, and valuable children.

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 257.

Ayahs

وَإِنْ أَرَدتُّمُ ٱسْتِبْدَالَ زَوْجٍ مَّكَانَ زَوْجٍ وَءَاتَيْتُمْ إِحْدَىٰهُنَّ قِنطَارًا فَلَا تَأْخُذُوا۟ مِنْهُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ أَتَأْخُذُونَهُۥ بُهْتَـٰنًا وَإِثْمًا مُّبِينًا (٢٠)
If you desire to take a wife in place of another, and you have given one of them a quintal [of gold], do not take anything away from it. Would you take it by way of calumny and flagrant sin?! (20)
وَكَيْفَ تَأْخُذُونَهُۥ وَقَدْ أَفْضَىٰ بَعْضُكُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ وَأَخَذْنَ مِنكُم مِّيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا (٢١)
How could you take it back, when you have known each other, and they have taken from you a solemn covenant? (21)

Occasion of Revelation

Before Islam, it was a custom that if men wished to divorce their former wife and enter into a new marriage, they would accuse their wife of immoral acts to escape paying the dower (mahr). They would treat her with such severity that she would be forced to surrender her dower—which was usually received beforehand—to obtain a divorce. They would then use that same dower for the second wife.

The above verse strictly prevents this ugly practice and condemns it. [^1]

Commentary

وَ إِنْ أَرَدْتُمُ اسْتِبْدالَ زَوْجٍ‌ ...

This verse was also revealed to protect another part of women's rights. It commands all Muslims that when they decide to separate from a wife and choose a new spouse, they have no right to withhold any part of the first wife's dower, or to take it back if they have already paid it, no matter how large the dower may be—expressed here as a "Qintar" (قنطار). As we have previously mentioned, "Qintar" refers to a vast amount of wealth and property. Raghib states in the book Al-Mufradat that the root of "Qintar" is "Qantarah" (قنطره), meaning a bridge; since vast wealth is like a bridge that a person can use in life, it is referred to as a Qintar. [^2] This is because the assumption here is that the divorce is taking place for the husband's interests and not due to the wife's deviation from the path of chastity; therefore, there is no reason for their established rights to be trampled upon.

أَ تَأْخُذُونَهُ بُهْتاناً وَ إِثْماً مُبِيناً Then, referring to the practice of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) regarding this matter—where they would accuse their wives of immoral acts—He says: "Would you take it back by slander and manifest sin?" This means the act itself is an injustice and a sin, and resorting to such a cowardly and false means is another manifest sin.

وَ كَيْفَ تَأْخُذُونَهُ وَ قَدْ أَفْضى‌ [^1] بَعْضُكُمْ إِلى‌ بَعْضٍ‌ In this verse, again using a rhetorical question to stir the human emotions of men, He adds: You and your wives have been together in privacy and solitude for a long time, like one soul in two bodies, having complete intimacy and association; how can you, after all this closeness and connection, treat one another like strangers and enemies and trample upon their established rights?! This is exactly like the expression we have in Persian today: if two close friends start a quarrel, we say to them, "You have shared bread and salt for years, why do you fight?" In reality, committing injustice in such cases against a life partner is an injustice against oneself.

Then He says: "Furthermore, your wives have taken a solemn covenant from you at the time of the marriage contract; how can you ignore this sacred and firm covenant and proceed to commit an explicit breach of promise?"

وَ أَخَذْنَ مِنْكُمْ مِيثاقاً غَلِيظاً Additionally, it should be noted that although this verse was revealed regarding the divorcing of a former wife to choose a new one, it is not exclusive to that case. Rather, the intent is that in any case where divorce and separation occur at the husband's proposal and the wife does not desire the separation, the full dower must be paid, or if it has been paid, nothing should be taken back, whether they intend to remarry or not. Therefore, the phrase إِنْ أَرَدْتُمُ اسْتِبْدالَ زَوْجٍ‌ ("If you want to choose another wife") actually refers to the situation during the Age of Ignorance and does not restrict the essence of the ruling.

It is also necessary to mention that "Istibdal" (استبدال) means seeking to exchange; thus, the meaning of seeking and will is inherent within it. If we see it coupled with "Aradtum" (أردتم - you will/intend), it is because He wants to point out that when preparing and deciding to replace your wife, you must not begin with illegitimate and cowardly preliminaries.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the aforementioned verse. [^2]: For further explanation, please refer to Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 340. [^1]: Ifda’ (افضاء) is originally from the root fada’ (فضا) meaning expansion; therefore, ifda’ means to expand. When someone comes into complete contact with another, they have effectively transformed their limited existence into a broader existence, and thus ifda’ has come to mean making contact.

Ayah

وَلَا تَنكِحُوا۟ مَا نَكَحَ ءَابَآؤُكُم مِّنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ إِلَّا مَا قَدْ سَلَفَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فَـٰحِشَةً وَمَقْتًا وَسَآءَ سَبِيلًا (٢٢)
Do not marry any of the women whom your fathers had married, excluding what is already past. That is indeed an indecency, an outrage and an evil course. (22)

Occasion of Revelation

In the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), it was customary that whenever a man passed away and left behind a wife and children, if that wife was the stepmother of his children, the children would inherit the stepmother just like his property. In this way, they had the right to marry their stepmother themselves or give her in marriage to someone else. After the advent of Islam, an incident occurred for one of the Muslims as follows:

One of the Ansar named Abu Qays passed away. His son proposed marriage to his stepmother. The woman said: "I consider you my own son and do not see such an act as appropriate; however, I will seek a ruling from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله." She then presented the matter to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and sought guidance. The above verse was revealed and strictly forbade this practice.

Commentary

As mentioned in the occasion of revelation, the verse draws a line of cancellation through one of the indecent practices of the era of Ignorance and says: وَ لا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكَحَ آباؤُكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ:

"And do not marry those women whom your fathers married."

However, since no law usually applies retroactively to the past, He adds: إِلَّا ما قَدْ سَلَفَ: "except what has already passed."

Then, to emphasize the matter, He expresses three severe descriptions regarding this type of marriage: First, He says: this act is a very lewd deed (إِنَّهُ كانَ فاحِشَةً), and then He adds: it is an act that causes hatred in the minds of the people, meaning human nature does not approve of it (وَ مَقْتاً), and finally He states: it is an evil way (وَ ساءَ سَبِيلًا). We even read in history that the people of the Age of Ignorance also called this type of marriage "Maqt" (hateful) and the children who were the fruit of it "Maqit" (hated children).

It is clear that this ruling was established for various benefits and philosophies; for on one hand, marrying a stepmother is like marrying one's mother, as a stepmother is considered a second mother. On the other hand, it is a violation of the father's sanctity and a desecration of his respect.

Furthermore, this act sows the seeds of discord among a person's children, as conflict may arise between them over the possession of the stepmother. It even creates rivalry between father and son, because usually, rivalry and jealousy exist between a first wife and a second wife. If this act (marrying the stepmother) occurs during the father's lifetime (after the stepmother's divorce), the reason for jealousy is clear; and if it occurs after his death, the son might still develop a kind of jealousy toward his deceased father.

It is not unlikely that the three expressions used to condemn this act in the above verse refer respectively to the three philosophies mentioned above.

Ayah

حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أُمَّهَـٰتُكُمْ وَبَنَاتُكُمْ وَأَخَوَٰتُكُمْ وَعَمَّـٰتُكُمْ وَخَـٰلَـٰتُكُمْ وَبَنَاتُ ٱلْأَخِ وَبَنَاتُ ٱلْأُخْتِ وَأُمَّهَـٰتُكُمُ ٱلَّـٰتِىٓ أَرْضَعْنَكُمْ وَأَخَوَٰتُكُم مِّنَ ٱلرَّضَـٰعَةِ وَأُمَّهَـٰتُ نِسَآئِكُمْ وَرَبَـٰٓئِبُكُمُ ٱلَّـٰتِى فِى حُجُورِكُم مِّن نِّسَآئِكُمُ ٱلَّـٰتِى دَخَلْتُم بِهِنَّ فَإِن لَّمْ تَكُونُوا۟ دَخَلْتُم بِهِنَّ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَحَلَـٰٓئِلُ أَبْنَآئِكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِنْ أَصْلَـٰبِكُمْ وَأَن تَجْمَعُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱلْأُخْتَيْنِ إِلَّا مَا قَدْ سَلَفَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٢٣)
Forbidden to you are your mothers, your daughters and your sisters, your paternal aunts and your maternal aunts, your brother’s daughters and your sister’s daughters, your [foster-]mothers who have suckled you and your sisters through fosterage, your wives’ mothers, and your stepdaughters who are under your care [born] of the wives whom you have gone into—but if you have not gone into them there is no sin upon you—and the wives of your sons who are from your own loins, and that you should marry two sisters at one time—excluding what is already past; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful— (23)

Commentary

The Prohibition of Marriage with Mahrams

This verse refers to Mahrams, meaning women with whom marriage is forbidden. Based on this, Mahram status (prohibited degrees of relationship) can arise from three avenues:

  1. Birth, which is referred to as "consanguinity" (lineal relationship).
  2. Through marriage, which is called "affinity" (relationship by marriage).
  3. Through breastfeeding, which is known as "fosterage" (suckling relationship).

First, it mentions the consanguineous Mahrams, who consist of seven categories, stating:

حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أُمَّهاتُكُمْ وَ بَناتُكُمْ وَ أَخَواتُكُمْ وَ عَمَّاتُكُمْ وَ خالاتُكُمْ وَ بَناتُ الْأَخِ وَ بَناتُ الْأُخْتِ: "Prohibited to you [for marriage] are your mothers, your daughters, your sisters, your father's sisters, your mother's sisters, your brother's daughters and your sister's daughters."

It should be noted that "mother" does not only refer to the woman from whom a person is directly born; rather, it includes grandmothers, great-grandmothers, paternal grandmothers, and the like. Similarly, "daughter" does not only mean the immediate daughter, but also includes granddaughters (from sons or daughters) and their descendants. The same principle applies to the other five categories.

It goes without saying that all individuals naturally feel an aversion to such marriages. For this reason, almost all tribes and nations (except for a very few) consider marriage with Mahrams forbidden. Even the Zoroastrians, who in some of their original sources allowed such marriages, deny it today.

Although some attempt to attribute this to an ancient habit or custom, we know that the universality of a law among all human beings throughout long centuries and ages usually indicates its innate (fitari) nature, as mere customs and habits cannot become universal and permanent.

Furthermore, it has been scientifically proven today that marriage between blood relatives carries many risks; it manifests and intensifies latent hereditary diseases (rather than creating the disease itself). Some even discourage marriage with relatively distant relatives, such as cousins, believing it intensifies the risks of hereditary diseases [^1]. While this may not cause issues among distant relatives (as is often the case), it will certainly create problems among close relatives where "consanguinity" is more intense.

Additionally, sexual attraction usually does not exist among Mahrams because they often grow up together and are viewed by one another as ordinary and familiar beings. Rare and exceptional cases cannot serve as a measure for general and universal laws. We know that sexual attraction is a condition for the stability of the marital bond; therefore, if a marriage occurs among Mahrams, it will be unstable and weak.

Next, the verse refers to foster Mahrams, stating: وَ أُمَّهاتُكُمُ اللَّاتِي أَرْضَعْنَكُمْ وَ أَخَواتُكُمْ مِنَ الرَّضاعَةِ: "and your [foster] mothers who suckled you and your sisters through nursing."

Although the Quran mentions only two categories here—foster mothers and foster sisters—according to numerous available narrations, foster Mahrams are not limited to these. Rather, according to the famous Hadith narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"يحرم من الرضاع ما يحرم من النسب" "Whatever is prohibited through consanguinity is also prohibited through fosterage."

Of course, the amount of breastfeeding that results in Mahram status, as well as its conditions and specifics, involve many details found in books of jurisprudence (Fiqh).

[The Philosophy of Prohibiting Foster Mahrams]

The philosophy behind prohibiting foster Mahrams is that by the growth of their flesh and bones through the milk of a specific person, they develop a resemblance to her children. For example, when a woman breastfeeds an infant to the extent that the infant's body grows specifically from that milk, a type of resemblance is formed between that child and the woman's other children. In reality, each is considered a part of that mother's body and they are like two biological brothers.

In the final stage, the verse mentions the third category of Mahrams under several headings:

  1. وَ أُمَّهاتُ نِسائِكُمْ: "and the mothers of your wives." This means as soon as a woman is married to a man and the marriage contract is recited, her mother, grandmother, and so on, become eternally forbidden to him.

  2. وَ رَبائِبُكُمُ اللَّاتِي فِي حُجُورِكُمْ مِنْ نِسائِكُمُ اللَّاتِي دَخَلْتُمْ بِهِنَّ: "and your step-daughters under your guardianship [born] of your wives unto whom you have gone in." This means that by the mere legal contract with a woman, her daughters (from another husband) do not become forbidden to the husband; rather, it is conditional upon having had sexual intercourse with that woman in addition to the legal contract. The presence of this condition here confirms that the ruling regarding the mother-in-law mentioned in the previous phrase is not subject to such a condition, thereby strengthening the absolute nature (itlaq) of that ruling.

Although the literal meaning of the phrase "فِي حُجُورِكُمْ" (under your guardianship/in your laps) might suggest that if a wife's daughter from another husband is not raised by the man, she is not forbidden to him, according to narrations and the established nature of the ruling, this is not a "restrictive condition" (qayd ihtirazi). Rather, it points to the reason for the prohibition, as such daughters whose mothers remarry are usually young and are often raised in the new husband's home like his own daughters. The verse says these are effectively like your own daughters; would anyone marry his own daughter? The choice of the term "rabā'ib" (plural of rabībah, meaning "one who is raised/nurtured") is for this very reason.

Following this, to emphasize the point, it adds that if you have not had sexual intercourse with them (the mothers), their daughters are not forbidden to you (فَإِنْ لَمْ تَكُونُوا دَخَلْتُمْ بِهِنَّ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ).

  1. وَ حَلائِلُ [^2] أَبْنائِكُمُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ أَصْلابِكُمْ: "and the wives of your sons who are from your own loins." In fact, the expression "مِنْ أَصْلابِكُمْ" (from your own loins/lineage) is used to abolish a false custom of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah). At that time, it was common for people to adopt others as their sons; a person who was the biological son of someone else would be called one's own son, and the adopted son was subject to all the rulings of a biological son. For this reason, they would not marry the wives of their adopted sons. Adoption and its associated rulings are entirely baseless in Islam.

[Prohibition of Combining Two Sisters]

  1. وَ أَنْ تَجْمَعُوا بَيْنَ الْأُخْتَيْنِ: "and that you take [in marriage] two sisters at the same time." This means marrying two sisters simultaneously is not permitted. Therefore, if one marries two or more sisters at different times and after separating from the previous sister, there is no objection.

[^1]: Of course, in Islam, the marriage of cousins and the like is not prohibited because it is not the same as marriage with Mahrams, and the probability of such incidents occurring from these marriages is lower. We ourselves have witnessed many such marriages where the children resulting from them were healthy and intellectually gifted. [^2]: "Halā'il" is the plural of "halīlah," from the root "hall," meaning a woman who is lawful to a man, or from the root "hulūl," meaning a woman who lives and cohabits with a man in one place.

Since combining two sisters was common during the Age of Ignorance and some individuals had entered into such marriages, the Quran says after the above phrase: "إِلَّا ما قَدْ سَلَفَ" (except what has already passed). This means this ruling (like other rulings) is not retroactive. Those who entered into such marriages before the revelation of this law face no penalty or punishment, although they must now choose one of the two and release the other.

The reason Islam prevented such a marriage might be that two sisters, by virtue of their lineage and natural bond, have a strong affection for one another. However, when they become rivals, they naturally cannot maintain that previous affection. Thus, a type of emotional conflict arises within them that is harmful to their lives, as the motive of "love" and the motive of "rivalry" are constantly struggling and clashing within them.

Some commentators have suggested that the phrase "إِلَّا ما قَدْ سَلَفَ" refers back to all the Mahrams mentioned in the verse. This would mean that if, before the revelation of this verse, one had married any of the above Mahrams according to the laws prevalent at that time, the ruling of prohibition would not apply to those past marriages, and their children would be considered legitimate. However, after the revelation of this verse, it was mandatory to separate immediately.

The end of the verse, "إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً" (Indeed, Allah is Ever-Forgiving, Most Merciful), is also consistent with this meaning.

Ayah

۞ وَٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتُ مِنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ إِلَّا مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ ۖ كِتَـٰبَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَأُحِلَّ لَكُم مَّا وَرَآءَ ذَٰلِكُمْ أَن تَبْتَغُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِكُم مُّحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسَـٰفِحِينَ ۚ فَمَا ٱسْتَمْتَعْتُم بِهِۦ مِنْهُنَّ فَـَٔاتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً ۚ وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيمَا تَرَٰضَيْتُم بِهِۦ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلْفَرِيضَةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (٢٤)
and married women, excepting your slave-women. This is Allah’s ordinance for you. As to others than these, it is lawful for you to seek [temporary union with them] with your wealth, in wedlock, not in license. For the enjoyment you have had from them thereby, give them their dowries, by way of settlement, and there is no sin upon you in what you may agree upon after the settlement. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (24)

Commentary

وَ الْمُحْصَناتُ مِنَ النِّساءِ This verse continues the discussion from the previous verse regarding women with whom marriage is prohibited, adding: "Marriage and sexual intercourse with married women is also forbidden."

"Muhsanat" (محصنات) is the plural of "Muhsanah" (محصنة), derived from the root "Hisn" (حصن), meaning a castle or fortress. Accordingly, it is used to refer to married women, as well as chaste and virtuous women who protect themselves from sexual intercourse with others, or those who are under the protection and guardianship of men.

Sometimes it is also used to refer to free women as opposed to bondwomen, because their freedom is effectively like a sanctuary drawn around them, and no one has the right to penetrate their sanctuary without their permission. However, it is clear that in the above verse, it refers specifically to married women.

This ruling is not exclusive to Muslim women; rather, married women of any religion or nation fall under this ruling—meaning marriage with them is prohibited.

The only exception to this rule concerns non-Muslim women who are taken captive by Muslims in wars. Islam considers their captivity as a "divorce" from their former husbands and allows Muslims to marry them after their waiting period (Iddah) [1] is completed, or to treat them as bondwomen (إِلَّا ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ).

However, this exception is what is termed a "disconnected exception" (Istithna' Munqati'). This means that for such married women who fall into the captivity of Muslims, their relationship with their husbands is severed upon captivity, just as the relationship of a non-Muslim woman who embraces Islam is severed from her former husband (if he remains in disbelief), and she joins the ranks of unmarried women.

From this, it becomes clear that Islam in no way permits Muslims to marry married women, even those from other nations and religions. For this reason, a waiting period (Iddah) has been prescribed for them, and marital relations are prohibited during this period.

The philosophy behind this ruling is that such women must either be returned to the environment of "disbelief," or remain among Muslims without a "husband," or have their relationship with their former husbands severed so they can enter a new marriage. The first option is contrary to Islamic educational principles, and the second is oppressive; therefore, the only viable path is the third.

[1]: The duration of their Iddah is one menstrual cycle, or if they are pregnant, until they give birth.

It appears from some narrations reaching the famous companion Abu Sa'id al-Khudri that the above verse was revealed regarding the captives of the Battle of Autas [1]. After ensuring that the captive women were not pregnant, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله permitted them to marry Muslims or be taken as bondwomen—this Hadith also supports the aforementioned commentary.

كِتابَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ In this phrase, to emphasize the preceding rulings regarding prohibited degrees of marriage and the like, He says: "These are matters that Allah has prescribed and written for you." Therefore, they are in no way subject to change or deviation.

وَ أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ ما وَراءَ ذلِكُمْ أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا بِأَمْوالِكُمْ مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ Then He says: Aside from these groups mentioned in this verse and the preceding verses, you may marry other women, provided it is according to Islamic laws, accompanied by chastity and virtue, and far from indecency and impurity.

Therefore, "Muhsinin" (محصنين) in the above verse, which refers to the state of men, means "chaste ones," and "غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ" (not fornicators) serves as its emphasis. The root "Sifah" (سفاح) means adultery/fornication, originally derived from "Safh" (سفح), meaning the pouring of water or vain and reckless actions. Since the Quran always uses metaphorical language in such matters, it uses this as a metaphor for illicit intercourse.

The phrase أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا بِأَمْوالِكُمْ (that you seek them with your wealth) indicates that the marital relationship must either be in the form of marriage with the payment of a dowry (Mahr) or in the form of owning a bondwoman by paying her price [2].

[1]: Autas is the name of a place where one of the Islamic battles occurred. [2]: Regarding Islam's program for the liberation of slaves and the precise plan designed in this regard, we will discuss it in detail under the appropriate verses.

Furthermore, the expression "غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ" in the above verse may also point to the fact that your goal in marriage should not be merely lust and the satisfaction of sexual instinct; rather, this vital matter is for a higher purpose, which the instinct serves: the preservation of the human race and its protection from impurities.

Temporary Marriage in Islam

فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً This part of the verse refers to the issue of temporary marriage, known as "Mut'ah." It says: "Those women whom you marry temporarily, you must pay them their dowries as an obligation." From this, it is understood that the original legislation of temporary marriage was already established for Muslims before the revelation of this verse, as the verse advises regarding the payment of their dowries. Since this is one of the important exegetical, legal, and social discussions, it must be examined from several perspectives:

  1. The internal evidence within the verse that emphasizes its reference to temporary marriage.
  2. Temporary marriage existed during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and was not subsequently abrogated.
  3. The social necessity of this type of marriage.
  4. Responses to certain objections.

Regarding the first part, it should be noted that:

Firstly, the word "Mut'ah," from which "Istamta'tum" is derived, means temporary marriage in Islam and is considered a "technical religious term" (Haqiqah Shar'iyyah) in this regard. The evidence for this is that this word (Mut'ah) was repeatedly used with this meaning in the narrations of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the words of the companions [1]. Secondly, if the word did not carry this meaning, it would have to be interpreted according to its literal meaning, "to benefit from." Consequently, the verse would mean: "If you benefit from permanent wives, pay them their dowries."
