However, we know that the payment of the dowry is not conditional upon "benefiting" from the woman; rather, the entire dowry (according to the popular view [2]) or at least half of it becomes obligatory immediately upon the permanent marriage contract.

Thirdly, the great "Companions" and "Successors" (Tabi'un) [3], such as the famous scholar and commentator Ibn Abbas, Ubayy ibn Ka'b, Jabir ibn Abdullah, Imran ibn Husayn, Sa'id ibn Jubayr, Mujahid, Qatadah, Suddi, and a large group of Sunni commentators, as well as all the commentators of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, have all understood the ruling of temporary marriage from the above verse. This is to the extent that Fakhr al-Razi, despite his reputation for raising objections in matters related to the Shi'a, says after a detailed discussion: "We do not dispute that the ruling of the permissibility of Mut'ah is derived from the above verse; rather, we say that the said ruling was abrogated after a period."

Fourthly, the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, who were the most knowledgeable of all regarding the secrets of revelation, unanimously interpreted the verse in this sense. Numerous narrations have been transmitted in this regard, including:

It is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "The ruling of Mut'ah was revealed in the Quran and the Sunnah of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله was established according to it." [4]

[1]: Refer to the books Kanz al-Irfan, Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, al-Burhan, and al-Ghadir Vol. 6. [2]: The popular or most famous view is that with the permanent marriage contract, the entire dowry becomes obligatory for the man, although divorce before consummation causes half of it to return. [3]: Those who came after the "Companions" and did not live during the time of the Prophet. [4]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 467; Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 360.

And it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that in response to Abu Basir's question regarding Mut'ah, he said: "It was revealed in the Quran: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً..." [1]. It is also narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that in response to a person named Abdullah ibn Umayr al-Laythi regarding Mut'ah, he said: "Allah made it lawful in His Book and through the tongue of His Prophet, so it is lawful until the Day of Resurrection." [2]

Was This Ruling Abrogated?!

The consensus of all Muslim scholars, and indeed a necessity of the religion, is that temporary marriage was legitimate at the beginning of Islam (and the discussion regarding the verse's indication of the legitimacy of Mut'ah does not contradict the certainty of the ruling itself, as opponents believe its legitimacy was established by the Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله). Muslims even practiced it at the beginning of Islam. The famous statement narrated from Umar: "Two Mut'ahs were [practiced] during the time of the Messenger of Allah, and I forbid them both and will punish for them: the Mut'ah of women and the Mut'ah of Hajj" [3] (which is a specific type of Hajj) is clear evidence of the existence of this ruling during the Prophet's era. However, opponents of this ruling claim that it was later abrogated and prohibited.

Interestingly, the narrations they claim regarding the abrogation of this ruling are completely contradictory and confused. Some say the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself abrogated it, thus the abrogator is the Sunnah and Hadith of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Others say the abrogator is the verse of divorce: "إِذا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّساءَ فَطَلِّقُوهُنَّ لِعِدَّتِهِنَّ" (When you divorce women, divorce them for their waiting period), while this verse has no connection to the matter at hand because it discusses divorce, whereas temporary marriage does not involve divorce; its separation occurs at the end of its term.

What is certain is that the original legitimacy of this type of marriage during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is definitive, and there is no reliable evidence regarding its abrogation. Therefore, according to the established law in the science of principles (Usul), one must rule for the continuation of this law.

The famous statement narrated from Umar is also clear evidence that this ruling was never abrogated during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

It is obvious that no one except the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has the right to abrogate rulings, and only he can abrogate certain rulings by Allah's command. After the passing of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the door of abrogation is completely closed; otherwise, anyone could abrogate a part of the divine rulings based on their own Ijtihad, and nothing called an eternal and everlasting Sharia would remain. Fundamentally, Ijtihad in the face of the Prophet's words is "Ijtihad against the text" (Ijtihad fi muqabalat al-nass), which lacks any validity.

Interestingly, in Sahih al-Tirmidhi, which is among the famous Sunni Sahih books, as well as from "Daraqutni" [1], we read: A man from Syria asked Abdullah ibn Umar about the Mut'ah of Hajj. He replied explicitly that it is lawful and good.

[1]: Previous source. [2]: Tafsir al-Burhan under the verse (it should be noted that this Hadith and the two previous ones are in the book al-Kafi). [3]: Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 2, p. 158—the said Hadith is narrated from the commentaries of al-Qurtubi and al-Tabari with similar wording. It is also mentioned in al-Sunan al-Kubra by al-Bayhaqi in Vol. 7, Book of Marriage. [1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 2, p. 762, under verse 195 of al-Baqarah.

The Syrian man said: "Your father forbade this act." Abdullah ibn Umar became upset and said: "If my father forbids such an act and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله permitted it, should I abandon the sacred Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and follow my father's word? Get up and leave me!" [1] A similar narration regarding temporary marriage is narrated from Abdullah ibn Umar in Sahih al-Tirmidhi exactly as read above [2]. It is also narrated from the Muhadarat of Raghib that one of the Muslims used to practice temporary marriage. They asked him: "From whom did you take the lawfulness of this act?" He said: "From Umar!" They said in surprise: "How is such a thing possible when Umar forbade it and even threatened punishment?" He said: "Very well, that is exactly why I say so; for Umar used to say: 'The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله made it lawful and I make it forbidden.' I accept its legitimacy from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but I will not accept its prohibition from anyone!" [3]

Another point that must be mentioned here is that those who claim the abrogation of this ruling face significant problems:

First, it is explicitly stated in numerous narrations from Sunni sources that this ruling was never abrogated during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but was forbidden during the time of Umar. Therefore, proponents of abrogation must find an answer for all these narrations. These narrations amount to twenty-four, which Allamah Amini has detailed in al-Ghadir, Vol. 6. Two examples are mentioned below:

  1. In Sahih Muslim, it is narrated from Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari that he said: "We used to practice temporary marriage simply during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and this situation continued until Umar prevented it (entirely) in the case of Amr ibn Hurayth." [1] In another Hadith in Malik's Muwatta and al-Bayhaqi's al-Sunan al-Kubra, it is narrated from Urwah ibn al-Zubayr that: A woman named Khawlah bint Hakim came to Umar and informed him that one of the Muslims named Rabi'ah ibn Umayyah had practiced Mut'ah. He said: "If I had forbidden this earlier, I would have stoned him (but from now on I prevent it!)" [2] In the book Bidayat al-Mujtahid by Ibn Rushd al-Andalusi, we also read that Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari said: "Temporary marriage was common among us during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the caliphate of Abu Bakr, and half of the caliphate of Umar; then Umar forbade it." [3]

Another problem is that the narrations recounting the abrogation of this ruling during the Prophet's time are very confused and contradictory. Some say it was abrogated at the Battle of Khaybar, others on the day of the Conquest of Mecca, some at the Battle of Tabuk, some at the Battle of Autas, and so on. Therefore, it appears that the narrations of abrogation are all fabricated, which is why they contradict each other so much.

[1]: The meaning of Mut'ah of Hajj which Umar prohibited is to forgo Hajj al-Tamattu'. Hajj al-Tamattu' consists of entering Ihram, and after performing the Umrah rituals, exiting Ihram (where everything, even sexual intercourse, becomes permissible for them), and then entering Ihram again to perform the Hajj rituals from the 9th of Dhu al-Hijjah. In the Age of Ignorance, they did not consider this correct and were surprised that someone would enter Mecca during the Hajj days and perform Umrah and exit Ihram before performing Hajj. However, Islam explicitly permitted this, and it is stated in verse 186 of Surah al-Baqarah. [2]: Sharh al-Lum'ah, Vol. 2, Book of Marriage. [3]: Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 2, p. 159 (footnote). [1]: al-Ghadir, Vol. 6, p. 206. [2]: al-Ghadir, Vol. 6, p. 210. [3]: Bidayat al-Mujtahid, Book of Marriage.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that what the author of Tafsir al-Manar says—"We had previously stated in volumes 3 and 4 of al-Manar magazine that Umar forbade Mut'ah, but later we came across reports showing it was abrogated during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and not during Umar's time, so we correct our previous statement and seek forgiveness for it" [1]—is a biased statement. This is because, against the contradictory narrations claiming abrogation during the Prophet's time, we have narrations that are explicit about its continuation until Umar's time. Therefore, there is no place for apology or seeking forgiveness, and the evidence we mentioned above shows that his first statement was closer to the truth than the second! It goes without saying that neither Umar nor any other person, nor even the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام who are the true successors of the Prophet, can abrogate rulings that existed during the Prophet's era. Fundamentally, abrogation after the passing of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the closing of the door of revelation has no meaning. The fact that some have interpreted Umar's words as Ijtihad is surprising, as "Ijtihad" is not possible in the face of "Text" (Nass).

Even more strange is that a group of Sunni jurists have considered the verses related to marriage rulings (such as verse 6 of Surah al-Mu'minun) as abrogating the above verse regarding Mut'ah, as if they imagined that temporary marriage is not marriage at all, while it is certainly one of the types of marriage.

Temporary Marriage: A Social Necessity

It is a general and universal law that if human natural instincts are not responded to in a correct manner, they will turn toward deviant paths for satisfaction. This is because the fact that natural instincts cannot be eliminated is undeniable. Even if we could eliminate them, such an action would not be wise, as it is a form of struggle against the law of creation.

Therefore, the correct way is to satisfy them through a rational path and utilize them in the direction of construction.

It also cannot be denied that the sexual instinct is one of the most powerful human instincts, to the extent that some psychoanalysts consider it the only original human instinct and trace all other instincts back to it.

Now the question arises: in many conditions and environments, many individuals at certain ages are unable to enter permanent marriage, or married individuals on long journeys or missions face the problem of unsatisfied sexual instincts.

This issue has taken a more acute form especially in our era, where the age of marriage has risen due to the lengthening of the educational period and complex social issues, and fewer young people can marry at a young age—the peak period of the sexual instinct.

What should be done in this situation?

Should people be encouraged to suppress this instinct (like monks and nuns)?

Or should they be left free in the face of sexual promiscuity, and the current ugly and shameful scenes be permitted?

Or should we take a third path that neither brings the burdens of permanent marriage nor that sexual promiscuity?

In short, "permanent marriage" alone, neither in the past nor today, has been or is sufficient to meet the sexual needs of all classes of people. We stand at a crossroads: we must either permit "prostitution" (as the modern material world has practically endorsed and recognized) or accept the plan of temporary marriage. It is not clear what answer those who oppose both temporary marriage and prostitution have thought of for this question! The plan for temporary marriage neither has the heavy conditions of permanent marriage that might not suit financial inability or educational preoccupations, nor does it carry the harms of sexual tragedies and prostitution.

Objections Raised Against Temporary Marriage

However, certain objections are raised here that must be answered briefly:

  1. It is sometimes asked: what is the difference between "temporary marriage" and "prostitution"? Both are considered "selling oneself" in exchange for a sum of money, and in reality, this type of marriage is a mask over the face of prostitution and sexual impurities! The only difference between the two is the recitation of two simple sentences, i.e., the performance of the formula (Sighah).

Answer: Those who say this seem to have no knowledge of the concept of temporary marriage at all. Temporary marriage does not end with just saying two sentences; rather, it has regulations similar to permanent marriage. That is, such a woman, during the entire period of the temporary marriage, must be exclusively available to this man, and when the period ends, she must observe a waiting period (Iddah). This means she must refrain from any marriage with another person for at least forty-five days so that if she has become pregnant by the first man, her condition becomes clear. Even if she used contraceptive methods to prevent conception, observing this period is still obligatory. If she has a child by him, the child must be supported by him just like a child from a permanent marriage, and all the rulings of a child will apply to him. In prostitution, none of these conditions and constraints exist. Can these two ever be compared?

Of course, temporary marriage has differences from permanent marriage regarding the issue of inheritance (between husband and wife) [1] and maintenance (Nafaqah) and some other rulings, but these differences will never place it in the rank of prostitution. In any case, it is a form of marriage with the regulations of marriage.

  1. "Temporary marriage" causes some hedonistic individuals to abuse this law and perform all kinds of prostitution behind this curtain, to the extent that respectable people never submit to temporary marriage, and women of character avoid it.

Answer: Which law in the world has not been abused? Should a natural law and social necessity be blocked because of abuse? Or should we stop the abusers?

If, for instance, a group of people abused the pilgrimage to the House of Allah and engaged in selling drugs during this journey, should we stop people from participating in this great Islamic congress or stop the abusers?! And if we observe that today respectable people dislike this Islamic law, it is not the fault of the law, but the fault of those who practice the law, or more correctly, those who abuse it. If in today's society temporary marriage also takes a healthy form and the Islamic government implements this matter correctly under specific criteria and regulations, both abuses will be prevented and respectable people (in times of social necessity) will not dislike it.

  1. They say: temporary marriage causes unsupported individuals, like illegitimate children, to be handed over to society.

Answer: From what we have said, the answer to this objection is completely clear. Illegitimate children are legally tied to neither the father nor the mother, whereas children of temporary marriage do not have the slightest difference from children of permanent marriage, even in inheritance and other social rights. It seems a lack of attention to this fact is the source of the above objection.

"Russell" and Temporary Marriage

At the end of this discussion, it seems useful to mention what Bertrand Russell, the famous English scholar, wrote in his book Marriage and Morals under the title "Trial Marriage":

After mentioning the plan of a juvenile court judge named "Ben B. Lindsey" regarding "companionate marriage" or trial marriage, he writes: "According to Lindsey's plan, young people should be able to enter into a new type of marriage that differs from ordinary (permanent) marriages in three ways: first, that the parties do not intend to have children, therefore they should be taught the best methods of contraception; second, that their separation occurs easily; and third, that after divorce, the woman has no right to maintenance."

"Russell," after mentioning Lindsey's proposal, the summary of which is stated above, says: "I imagine that if such a matter were to receive legal approval, a large group of young people, including university students, would submit to temporary marriage and enter into a temporary shared life—a life that involves freedom and is free from many of the current chaotic disorders and sexual relations." [1] As you can see, the above plan regarding temporary marriage is similar to the Islamic plan in many respects, but the conditions and characteristics that Islam has brought for temporary marriage are clearer and more complete in many ways. In Islamic temporary marriage, preventing children is completely permissible, separation is easy, and maintenance is not obligatory.

وَ لا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيما تَراضَيْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ الْفَرِيضَةِ At the end of the verse, after mentioning the necessity of paying the dowry, it points out that: "There is no blame on you if you mutually agree on something after the obligation [of the dowry]." Therefore, the dowry is a type of debt that can be changed with mutual consent.

(In this matter, there is no difference between temporary and permanent contracts, although the verse, as we explained in detail, discusses temporary marriage).

There is another possibility in the interpretation of the above verse, which is: there is no obstacle if, after performing the temporary marriage, the parties agree to increase the duration of the marriage as well as the amount of the dowry. That is, temporary marriage is extendable even before the end of the term, in such a way that the man and woman agree to increase the duration by a certain amount in exchange for increasing the dowry by a specified sum (this interpretation is also mentioned in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام).

إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً The rulings mentioned in the verse are rulings that involve the goodness and happiness of human beings because: "Allah is Aware of the interests of His servants and Wise in His legislation."

[1]: From the book Marriage and Morals, pp. 189 and 190. [1]: Of course, children of temporary marriage have no difference whatsoever from children of a permanent contract.

Ayah

وَمَن لَّمْ يَسْتَطِعْ مِنكُمْ طَوْلًا أَن يَنكِحَ ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ فَمِن مَّا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُم مِّن فَتَيَـٰتِكُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِإِيمَـٰنِكُم ۚ بَعْضُكُم مِّنۢ بَعْضٍ ۚ فَٱنكِحُوهُنَّ بِإِذْنِ أَهْلِهِنَّ وَءَاتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ مُحْصَنَـٰتٍ غَيْرَ مُسَـٰفِحَـٰتٍ وَلَا مُتَّخِذَٰتِ أَخْدَانٍ ۚ فَإِذَآ أُحْصِنَّ فَإِنْ أَتَيْنَ بِفَـٰحِشَةٍ فَعَلَيْهِنَّ نِصْفُ مَا عَلَى ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَنْ خَشِىَ ٱلْعَنَتَ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَأَن تَصْبِرُوا۟ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٢٥)
As for those of you who cannot afford to marry faithful free women, then [let them marry] from what you own, from among your faithful slave-women. Your faith is best known [only] to Allah; you are all [on a] similar [footing]. So marry them with their masters’ permission, and give them their dowries in an honourable manner—[such of them] as are chaste women, not licentious ones or those who take paramours. But should they commit an indecent act on marrying, there shall be for them [only] half the punishment for free women. This is for those of you who fear falling into fornication; but it is better that you be continent, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (25)

Commentary

Marriage with Slave-Girls

وَ مَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ مِنْكُمْ طَوْلًا أَنْ يَنْكِحَ‌ ...

Following the discussions regarding marriage, this verse explains the conditions for marrying slave-girls. First, it states that those who do not have the financial means to marry free women may marry slave-girls, whose dower (mahr) and other expenses are usually lighter and easier to manage. [^1] Of course, "marrying slave-girls" here does not mean a master marrying his own slave-girl; rather, he can treat her as a spouse under specific conditions mentioned in books of jurisprudence. Therefore, this refers to the marriage of non-owners with slave-girls.

Furthermore, from the expression "believing" (مؤمنات), it is understood that the slave-girl must necessarily be a Muslim for marriage to be permissible; thus, marrying slave-girls from the People of the Book (Ahl al-Kitab) is not valid.

Interestingly, in this verse, the Quran refers to slave-girls as "fatiyat," which is the plural of "fatah." This term is usually imbued with a particular respect regarding women and is often used for young girls.

وَ اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِإِيمانِكُمْ‌ This phrase indicates that in discerning their faith, you are tasked with judging based on their outward declarations; as for their inner reality and hidden secrets, "Allah is more knowing of your faith and belief."

بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ‌ Since some felt a sense of aversion toward marrying slave-girls, the Quran states that you are all descended from one father and mother, "and you are of one another." Therefore, you should not feel aversion toward marrying slave-girls, who are no different from you in terms of humanity. In terms of spiritual value, their worth—like that of others—depends on their piety and God-wariness, as all are considered members of one body.

[^1]: The word "tawl" (rhyming with "naw'") is originally derived from the root "tul" (rhyming with "nur") and carries the meaning of ability, reach, financial means, and the like.

فَانْكِحُوهُنَّ بِإِذْنِ أَهْلِهِنَ‌ However, "this marriage must take place with the permission of the owner," and without his permission, it is void. Referring to the owner as "ahl" (family/folk) suggests that they should not treat their slave-girls like mere merchandise; rather, they should maintain a completely humane conduct toward them, like the guardian of a family toward his children and kin.

وَ آتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ‌ From this phrase, it is understood that a suitable and worthy dower must be designated for them and given to them directly. That is, the slave-girls themselves will be the "owners of the dower." Although a group of commentators believes there is an elided word and it was originally "Give their owners their dowers," this interpretation does not align with the literal appearance of the verse, even if some narrations support it. Additionally, from the literal sense of the verse, it is inferred that slaves can also own property they have acquired through legitimate means.

From the expression "bil-ma'ruf" (in a fair/fitting manner), it is understood that there should be no injustice or oppression in determining their dower; rather, their true right should be paid according to custom.

مُحْصَناتٍ غَيْرَ مُسافِحاتٍ وَ لا مُتَّخِذاتِ أَخْدانٍ‌ Another condition for this marriage is that slave-girls should be chosen who do not commit acts contrary to chastity, whether openly (ghayra musafihat) or by taking secret lovers (wa la muttakhidhati akhdan). [^1]

[^1]: "Akhdan" is the plural of "khidn," which originally means friend or companion. However, it is usually applied to individuals who establish illicit and secret relationships with the opposite sex. It should also be noted that "khidn" is applied to both "man" and "woman" in the Holy Quran.

Here, a question may arise: with the prohibition of adultery through the expression "ghayra musafihat," was there a need to prohibit taking secret lovers (akhdan)?

However, considering that some in the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) believed that only open adultery was disgraceful while taking a secret lover was unobjectionable, it becomes clear why the Holy Quran explicitly mentioned both parts.

فَإِذا أُحْصِنَّ فَإِنْ أَتَيْنَ بِفاحِشَةٍ فَعَلَيْهِنَّ نِصْفُ ما عَلَى الْمُحْصَناتِ مِنَ الْعَذابِ‌:

In this phrase, in line with the rulings mentioned regarding marriage with slave-girls and the protection of their rights, a discussion arises regarding their punishment when they deviate from the path of chastity. It states that if they commit an act contrary to chastity in this state, half the punishment of free women is applied to them—meaning they should be given only fifty lashes.

Another point to be noted here is that the Quran says:

"Idha uhsinna," meaning "if they are 'muhsanah,' such a punishment is applied to them." What is meant by being "muhsanah" here? Commentators have offered several possibilities: some take it to mean "married" (according to the well-known jurisprudential term and the previous verse), and some take it to mean "Muslim." However, considering that the word "muhsan" is mentioned twice in this sentence and both should carry the same meaning, and on the other hand, the punishment for married free women is stoning, not lashing, it becomes clear that the first interpretation (married) is not acceptable, just as there is no evidence for the second interpretation (Muslim).

The truth is that, given that the word "muhsanat" in the Holy Quran often means chaste and virtuous women, it appears that the above verse also refers to this meaning. That is, slave-girls who were forced into prostitution by their owners are exempt from punishment, but slave-girls who are not under such pressure and can live chastely, if they commit an act contrary to chastity, are punished like free women, but their punishment is half that of free women.

ذلِكَ لِمَنْ خَشِيَ الْعَنَتَ مِنْكُمْ‌ "Anat" originally means the re-breaking of a bone that was previously broken—that is, after healing and mending, it breaks again due to an accident. Clearly, this type of fracture is very painful and agonizing. For this reason, "anat" is used for unbearable hardships and agonizing tasks.

The Holy Quran says in the above phrase: "This marriage with slave-girls is for those who are under severe pressure from their sexual instinct and are unable to marry free women." Therefore, it is not permissible for others.

The philosophy of this ruling may be that slave-girls, especially at that time, were raised in undesirable educational conditions and naturally had deficiencies in terms of "morality," "psychology," and "emotions." Certainly, the children who were the fruit of marriage with them would more or less carry the moral color of the mother. For this reason, Islam devised a precise plan for the gradual liberation of slaves so they would not face this fate, while also allowing slaves to marry one another. Of course, this does not contradict the fact that some slave-girls had an exceptional status in terms of morality and upbringing, as what was mentioned above pertains to the majority. If we see that the mothers of some great figures of Islam were slave-girls, it is from this perspective. However, it must be noted that what is prohibited regarding slave-girls except in cases of necessity is marriage with them, not sexual intimacy through ownership.

وَ أَنْ تَصْبِرُوا خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ‌ "To refrain from marrying slave-girls as long as you have the ability and your honor is not tainted by sin is better for you."

وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌ And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful regarding what you have done in the past out of ignorance.

Ayahs

يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ سُنَنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَيَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٢٦)
Allah desires to explain [the laws] to you, and to guide you to the customs of those who were before you, and to turn toward you clemently, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (26)
وَٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَيُرِيدُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ ٱلشَّهَوَٰتِ أَن تَمِيلُوا۟ مَيْلًا عَظِيمًا (٢٧)
Allah desires to turn toward you clemently, but those who pursue their [base] appetites desire that you fall into gross waywardness. (27)
يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُخَفِّفَ عَنكُمْ ۚ وَخُلِقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ ضَعِيفًا (٢٨)
Allah desires to lighten your burden, for man was created weak. (28)

Commentary

What Are These Restrictions For?

يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ وَ يَهْدِيَكُمْ سُنَنَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ‌ Following the various rulings regarding marriage and the constraints and conditions mentioned in the previous verses, a question might arise in the minds of some: what is the purpose of all these legal restrictions and bonds? Would it not have been better if individuals were given freedom of action in these matters, and just as some world-worshippers utilize every means of pleasure, others could do the same?

The above verses actually provide the answer to these questions, stating: "Allah intends to clarify the truths for you through these regulations, and to guide you to the paths wherein lie your interests and benefits. Furthermore, you are not alone in this program; the pure nations of the past also had similar traditions. Additionally, Allah wants to forgive you and restore His blessings to you—which were cut off due to your deviations—once again." And this is contingent upon you returning from those deviant paths you followed during the age of ignorance (Jahiliyyah) and before Islam.

وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ‌—Allah is aware of the secrets of His rulings, and based on His wisdom, He has legislated them for you.

وَ اللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَنْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ يُرِيدُ الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الشَّهَواتِ أَنْ تَمِيلُوا مَيْلًا عَظِيماً.

It emphasizes once again that "Allah intends, through these rulings, that the blessings and bounties which were cut off from you due to indulgence in lusts be restored to you; however, the lust-worshippers who are drowned in the waves of sins want you to deviate completely from the path of prosperity" and, like them, become contaminated from head to toe with all kinds of sins. Now consider: is that restriction mixed with prosperity and honor better for you, or this freedom and licentiousness coupled with pollution, misery, and degradation?!

These verses, in fact, respond to individuals in our own age and time who criticize religious laws, especially regarding sexual matters, asserting that these unconditional freedoms are nothing more than a mirage. The result is a great deviation from the path of happiness and human perfection, and becoming trapped in wildernesses and precipices—many examples of which we witness with our own eyes in the form of the disintegration of families, various sexual crimes, illegitimate children prone to criminality, and various venereal diseases and psychological distresses.

يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ أَنْ يُخَفِّفَ عَنْكُمْ وَ خُلِقَ الْإِنْسانُ ضَعِيفاً The verse points to the fact that "the previous ruling regarding the freedom to marry slave girls under certain conditions is actually considered a type of alleviation and expansion, because man is fundamentally a weak creature." In the face of the storm of various instincts attacking him from every side, legitimate ways must be presented to him for the satisfaction of these instincts so that he can protect himself from deviation.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَأْكُلُوٓا۟ أَمْوَٰلَكُم بَيْنَكُم بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَ تِجَـٰرَةً عَن تَرَاضٍ مِّنكُمْ ۚ وَلَا تَقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيمًا (٢٩)
O you who have faith! Do not eat up your wealth among yourselves unrightfully, but it should be trade by mutual consent. And do not kill yourselves. Indeed Allah is most merciful to you. (29)
وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ عُدْوَٰنًا وَظُلْمًا فَسَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِ نَارًا ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا (٣٠)
And whoever does that in aggression and injustice, We will soon make him enter the Fire, and that is easy for Allah. (30)

Commentary

The Dependence of Social Health on Economic Health

يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوَالَكُمْ بَيْنَكُمْ بِالْبَاطِلِ This verse, in fact, forms the foundation of Islamic laws regarding "transactions and financial exchanges." For this reason, Islamic jurists cite it in all chapters concerning transactions. The verse addresses the believers, saying: "Do not consume one another's wealth through unjust, wrong, or false means." This means that any form of appropriation of another's property that is without right and lacks a logical or rational justification is considered forbidden, and all such acts are categorized under the term "falsehood" (batil), which has a broad conceptual scope.

We know that "falsehood" (batil) is the opposite of "truth" (haqq) and encompasses anything that is unjust, aimless, and baseless.

In other verses of the Quran, this subject is emphasized with similar phrasing. For instance, when rebuking the Jewish people and mentioning their evil deeds, He says:

وَأَكْلِهِمْ أَمْوَالَ النَّاسِ بِالْبَاطِلِ (An-Nisa: 161): "And their consuming of the people's wealth unjustly and without justification." And in verse 188 of Surah Al-Baqarah, the phrase "لَا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوَالَكُمْ بَيْنَكُمْ بِالْبَاطِلِ" is mentioned as a preamble to the prohibition of dragging people to courts through vain and baseless claims to consume their wealth.

Therefore, any form of transgression, fraud, deception, usurious transactions, deals where the boundaries and limits are completely vague, the buying and selling of goods that lack logical and rational benefit, and the buying and selling of means of corruption and sin, all fall under this general law. If in numerous narrations the word "falsehood" (batil) has been interpreted as gambling, usury, and the like, it is in fact to introduce the clearest examples of this word, not to limit it to them.

It perhaps needs no mention that the expression "consuming" (akl) is a metaphor for any kind of appropriation, whether it be in the form of literal eating, wearing, inhabiting, or otherwise. This expression, in addition to Arabic, is also quite common in modern Persian.

إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونَ تِجَارَةً عَنْ تَرَاضٍ This phrase is an exception to the previous general law, but it is what is termed a "disconnected exception" (istithna' munqati'). [^1] This means that what is mentioned in this phrase was not included in the previous law from the beginning and is mentioned only as an emphasis and reminder. This, in turn, is also a general law, for He says: unless your appropriation of others' wealth is through trade that stems from the mutual consent of both parties. According to this statement, all financial exchanges and types of commerce prevalent among people are permissible in Islam if they occur with mutual consent and have a rational and logical basis (except in cases where there is an explicit prohibition due to specific interests).

وَلَا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيمًا Then, at the end of the verse, He restrains people from killing themselves. Its apparent meaning, supported by the phrase "إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيمًا" ("Indeed, Allah is Merciful to you"), is a prohibition against suicide. That is, the Merciful God is not only dissatisfied with someone else killing you, but He also does not permit you to hand yourself over to destruction by your own will. In the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام, the above verse has also been interpreted with this meaning of "suicide." [^2]

[^1]: A disconnected exception often comes to emphasize the universality of the general rule. This meaning holds true for the above verse, and additionally, it announces the fact that by prohibiting false appropriations, the path of life is not closed to you, and you can fulfill your goals through legitimate trade.

[^2]: Refer to Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan under the verse and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 472.

Now the question arises: what is the connection between the issue of "killing oneself" and the "false and unjust appropriation of people's wealth"?! The answer to this question is clear. In fact, by mentioning these two rulings consecutively, the Quran points to an important social point: if the financial relations of people are not established on a correct basis, and the economy of society does not progress in a healthy manner, and they unjustly appropriate one another's wealth, the society will suffer a kind of suicide. In addition to the increase in individual suicides, social suicide is also one of its implicit consequences.

The events and revolutions that have occurred in various societies of the contemporary world are a vivid witness to this reality. Since Allah is Merciful toward His servants, He warns them and signals the danger so they may be careful lest incorrect financial exchanges and an unhealthy economy lead their society toward destruction and collapse.

وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذَلِكَ عُدْوَانًا وَظُلْمًا فَسَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِ نَارًا "And whoever commits that in enmity and injustice—polluting himself with consuming others' wealth unjustly or committing suicide—he will not only burn in the fire of this world but will also burn in the fire of the Lord's wrath and anger." And this is easy for Allah (وَكَانَ ذَلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ يَسِيرًا). [^1]

Ayah

إِن تَجْتَنِبُوا۟ كَبَآئِرَ مَا تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَنُدْخِلْكُم مُّدْخَلًا كَرِيمًا (٣١)
If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you to a noble abode. (31)

Commentary

Major and Minor Sins

إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ‌ ...

This verse explicitly states: "If you avoid the major sins which you are forbidden, We will remove from you your lesser sins and admit you to a noble entrance."

From this expression, it is understood that sins are divided into two categories: one category which the Quran calls "Kabirah" (Major), and another category which it terms "Sayyi'ah" (Evil deed/Lesser sin). In verse 32 of Surah An-Najm, instead of "Sayyi'ah," the term "Lamam" [^1] is used. Furthermore, in verse 49 of Surah Al-Kahf, "Saghirah" (Minor) is mentioned in contrast to "Kabirah" (Major), where it says: لا يُغادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَ لا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصاها ("It leaves out nothing small or great, but has enumerated it").

From the aforementioned expressions, it is clearly established that sins are divided into two distinct groups, sometimes referred to as "Major" and "Minor," sometimes "Major" and "Sayyi'ah," and sometimes "Major" and "Lamam."

Now, we must see what the criterion and standard are for determining a minor or major sin.

Some say: These two are relative matters; that is, when comparing two sins, the one with greater gravity is "Major" and the one with less is "Minor." Consequently, every sin is minor compared to a greater sin and major compared to a smaller one. [^2]

[^1]: Lamam (rhyming with qasam) means small and insignificant actions.

[^2]: The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan attributed this view to Shi'a scholars, whereas this is not the case, and many of our scholars hold a different view which we will explain.

However, it is clear that this meaning does not align with the above verse at all, because the verse separates these two categories from each other, places them in opposition, and considers the avoidance of one as the cause for the forgiveness of the other (reflect on this).

But if we return to the literal meaning of "Kabirah," a major sin is any sin that is great and significant from the perspective of Islam. A sign of its significance can be that in the Holy Quran, Allah has not sufficed with a mere prohibition, but has followed it with a threat of the punishment of Hell—such as murder, adultery, usury, and the like. Therefore, we read in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام:

الكبائر اللتى اوجب اللَّه عز و جل عليها النار

"Major sins are those for which Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, has decreed the punishment of Fire." [^1] The essence of this hadith has been narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, and Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام.

Accordingly, identifying and recognizing major sins based on the above criterion is an easy task. If we observe that in some narrations the number of major sins is mentioned as seven, in others twenty, and in some seventy, this does not contradict what was stated above. In reality, some of these narrations refer to first-degree major sins, some to second-degree major sins, and some to all major sins.

Question: [Is the forgiveness of minor sins an encouragement to sin?]

It might be argued that this verse encourages people to commit minor sins by saying: "By avoiding major sins, there is no harm in committing small sins!"

Answer

The answer to this objection becomes clear from the expression used in the verse, as the Quran says: "نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ" ("We will cover/expiate your evil deeds"). This means that avoiding major sins—especially when the grounds for committing them are present—creates a kind of "spiritual piety" in a person that can wash away the effects of minor sins from his being. In reality, the above verse is similar to the verse: إِنَّ الْحَسَناتِ يُذْهِبْنَ السَّيِّئاتِ (Hud: 114): "Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds." It actually points to one of the real effects of righteous deeds. This is exactly like saying: if a person avoids dangerous toxic substances and maintains a healthy constitution, his healthy temperament can eliminate the undesirable effects of some unsuitable foods.

Or to put it another way: the forgiveness of minor sins is a type of "spiritual reward" for those who abandon major sins, and this itself has an encouraging effect on the abandonment of major sins.

When does a minor sin turn into a major one?

However, an important point to note here is that minor sins remain minor only if they are not repeated, and furthermore, are not committed out of indifference, arrogance, or rebellion. According to what is derived from the Quran and Islamic narrations, minor sins turn into major ones in several cases:

  1. When repeated: As narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام who said: لا صغيرة مع الاصرار "No sin is minor when accompanied by persistence (repetition)." [^1]
  2. When the sin is considered small and belittled: In Nahj al-Balagha, we read: اشد الذنوب ما استهان به صاحبه‌ "The most severe of sins is that which its doer considers insignificant." [^2]

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 473. [^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 288. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings.

  1. When committed out of rebellion, arrogance, and defiance against the command of the Lord: This subject can be inferred generally from various verses, including verse 37 of Surah An-Nazi'at: "Then as for him who rebelled and preferred the life of this world, indeed, Hellfire will be his refuge."
  2. When committed by individuals who hold a special position in society, such that their lapses are not considered equal to others. As the Quran says regarding the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in Surah Al-Ahzab, verse 30: "If you commit a clear immorality, the punishment for you will be doubled." And it is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: من سن سنة سيئة فعليه وزرها و وزر من عمل بها لا ينقص من اوزارهم شيئا "Whoever establishes an evil sunnah (practice), its burden is upon him, as well as the burden of all who act upon it, without anything being diminished from their burdens!" [^1]
  3. When one is happy and joyful about committing the sin and takes pride in it: As narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: من اذنب ذنبا و هو ضاحك دخل النار و هو باك‌ "Whoever commits a sin while laughing shall enter the Fire while weeping."
  4. When one considers Allah's lack of immediate punishment for his sin as a sign of God's pleasure, and deems himself immune from punishment or beloved by God. As the Quran narrates in verse 8 of Surah Al-Mujadila from the tongues of some arrogant sinners that they say to themselves: "Why does Allah not punish us for what we say?" Then the Quran adds that the Fire of Hell is sufficient for them.

[^1]: Al-Mahajjat al-Bayda, Vol. 7, p. 61.

Ayah

وَلَا تَتَمَنَّوْا۟ مَا فَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ لِّلرِّجَالِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا ٱكْتَسَبُوا۟ ۖ وَلِلنِّسَآءِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا ٱكْتَسَبْنَ ۚ وَسْـَٔلُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مِن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمًا (٣٢)
Do not covet the advantage, which Allah has given some of you over others. To men belongs a share of what they have earned, and to women a share of what they have earned. And ask Allah for His bounty. Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things. (32)

Occasion of Revelation

The renowned commentator, al-Tabarsi, narrates in Majma' al-Bayan that Umm Salama (one of the Prophet's wives) said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Why is it that men go to Jihad while women do not? And why is only half of their inheritance prescribed for us? If only we were men so that we could go to Jihad like them and possess their social standing." Consequently, the above verse was revealed, providing an answer to these and similar questions.

In the commentary Al-Manar, we read: When the verse of inheritance was revealed and mentioned the share of men as double that of women, a group of Muslim men said: "If only our spiritual reward and recompense were also like this (double) compared to them." Conversely, a group of women said: "If only our punishments and penalties were half of those for men, just as our share of inheritance is half of theirs." The above verse was revealed to answer them.

This same occasion of revelation is mentioned in the commentaries Fi Zilal and Ruh al-Ma'ani with slight variations.

Commentary

وَ لا تَتَمَنَّوْا ما فَضَّلَ اللَّهُ بِهِ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ‌ ...

As mentioned in the occasion of revelation, the difference in the inheritance shares of men and women had become a question for a group of Muslims. They seemingly did not realize that this difference exists because the cost of living is generally borne by men, while women are exempt from it; furthermore, the women's own expenses are also the responsibility of men. As previously noted, the actual share of women effectively becomes double that of men. Therefore, the above verse states:

"Do not covet the things in which Allah has made some of you excel others," because each of these differences contains secrets hidden from you—whether they are differences in creation, gender, and physical or psychological traits that form the foundation of your social order, or legal differences established due to various positions, such as inheritance. All these differences are according to Divine justice and law. Had any other arrangement been in your best interest, He would have granted it to you. Therefore, wishing to change them is a form of opposition to the Will of the Lord, which is the essence of truth and justice.

Of course, one must not mistake this; the verse refers to real and natural differences, not artificial differences created by "colonialism" and class "exploitation." Such artificial disparities are neither the Will of God nor is it wrong to wish for their change; rather, they are oppressive and irrational differences that one must strive to eliminate. For instance, women cannot wish they were men, and men should not wish they were women, as these two genders are the foundation of the human social system. Yet, at the same time, this gender difference must not become a pretext for one gender to trample upon the rights of the other. Those who have used this verse as an excuse to perpetuate unjust social discrimination are gravely mistaken.

لِلرِّجالِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا اكْتَسَبُوا وَ لِلنِّساءِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا اكْتَسَبْنَ‌ Therefore, He immediately says: "For men is a share of what they have earned, and for women is a share of what they have earned," whether it be a natural position (like the difference between the male and female genders) or a difference resulting from voluntary efforts and endeavors.

It is noteworthy that the word "iktisab" (acquisition), which means to attain or gain, has a broad meaning; it encompasses both voluntary efforts and what a human being can obtain through their natural constitution.

وَ سْئَلُوا اللَّهَ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ‌ Then He says: "Instead of wishing for such differences, ask Allah for His grace, kindness, and generosity, that He may bestow upon you various blessings, successes, and good rewards." Consequently, you may be happy and prosperous individuals, whether you are male or female, and whether you belong to one race or another. In any case, ask for what is truly good and conducive to your happiness, not what you merely imagine (and the expression "of His grace" perhaps points to this meaning).

It is clear, of course, that requesting the grace and favor of the Lord does not mean that a person should not pursue the means and factors of achieving things; rather, His grace and mercy must be sought within the framework of the means He has ordained.

إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيماً "For Allah is Ever All-Knowing of everything." He knows what differences are necessary for the social order, whether natural or legal. Therefore, in His work, there is no unjust discrimination or unfairness. He is also aware of the inner secrets of people and knows which individuals harbor improper desires in their hearts and which individuals think of what is positive and constructive.

What are these differences for?

Many ask themselves: Why do some individuals have more talent and others less? Why are some beautiful while others have less beauty? Why are some physically exceptionally strong while others are average? Are these "natural differences" compatible with the principle of the Lord's justice?

In response, several points must be considered:

  1. A portion of the physical and psychological differences among people is the result of class distinctions, social injustices, or individual negligence, which have no connection to the system of creation. For example, many children of the wealthy are physically stronger, more beautiful, and more talented than the children of the poor because they benefit from adequate nutrition and healthcare while the latter suffer from deprivation. Or there are individuals who lose their physical and mental powers due to laziness and negligence. Such differences should be considered "artificial and groundless differences" that will disappear with the elimination of the class system and the generalization of social justice. Islam and the Quran have never endorsed such differences.

  2. Another portion of these differences is natural and a necessity of human creation. That is, even if a society enjoys perfect social justice, all its members will not be identical like products from a factory; they will naturally have differences. However, it must be understood that Divine gifts and physical and mental talents are usually distributed such that everyone possesses a portion of them. It is rare to find someone who possesses all these gifts at once. One may have sufficient physical strength, another a great talent for mathematics, one a poetic flair, another a passion for trade, some a profound intelligence for agriculture, and others possess different specialized talents. The important thing is for society or the individuals themselves to discover these talents and nurture them in a healthy environment so that every human can manifest their point of strength and benefit from it.

  3. It must also be reminded that a society, like a human body, requires various tissues, muscles, and cells. Just as a body made entirely of delicate cells like those of the eye and brain would not survive, or if all its cells were hard and inflexible like bone cells, it would lack the capacity for various functions—rather, it must be composed of diverse cells where one is responsible for thinking, another for seeing, another for hearing, and another for speaking—similarly, for a "perfect society" to emerge, there is a need for different talents, tastes, and physical and intellectual structures. However, this does not mean that some members of the social body should live in deprivation, or that their services should be considered minor or belittled. Just as the cells of the body, despite all their differences, all benefit from food, air, and other necessities in the required amount.

In other words, the difference in psychological and physical structure in those parts that are natural (not oppressive or imposed) is a requirement of the "Wisdom" of the Lord, and justice can never be separated from wisdom. For instance, if all the cells of the human body were created identical, it would be far from wisdom, and justice—in the sense of placing everything in its proper place—would not exist within it. Likewise, if one day all people in society thought exactly alike and had identical talents, the state of society would completely collapse that very day.

Therefore, what is mentioned in the above verse regarding the difference in the structure of man and woman is, in fact, an allusion to this very subject. For it is obvious that if all human beings were men or all were women, the human race would soon become extinct, in addition to the fact that a significant portion of legitimate human pleasures would vanish. Now, if a group objects as to why some are created female and some male and how this aligns with the Lord's justice, it is certain that this objection is not logical, for they have not reflected upon its wisdom.

Ayah

وَلِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنَا مَوَٰلِىَ مِمَّا تَرَكَ ٱلْوَٰلِدَانِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبُونَ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ عَقَدَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ فَـَٔاتُوهُمْ نَصِيبَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدًا (٣٣)
For everyone We have appointed heirs to what the parents and near relatives leave, as well as those with whom you have made a compact; so give them their share [of the heritage]. Indeed Allah is witness to all things. (33)

Commentary

وَ لِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنا مَوالِيَ [^1] مِمَّا تَرَكَ الْوالِدانِ وَ الْأَقْرَبُونَ Once again, the Quran returns to the issues of inheritance, stating: "For everyone, whether male or female, We have appointed heirs who inherit from them, and whatever parents and near relatives leave behind is distributed among them according to a specific program."

This sentence is, in fact, a summary of the rulings on inheritance that were explained in the preceding verses regarding kinsfolk and relatives, and it serves as an introduction to the ruling that follows it.

[^1]: - Mawali is the plural of Mawla, which originally stems from the root Wilayah, meaning connection and attachment. It is applied to all individuals who have some form of connection to one another; however, in some instances, it refers to the connection between a "guardian" and "followers," and in the above verse, it means "heirs."

وَ الَّذِينَ عَقَدَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ فَآتُوهُمْ نَصِيبَهُمْ Then it adds: "And to those with whom you have made a pledge, give them their share [of inheritance]." The reason the verse refers to a pledge as ‘aqadat aymanukum (binding with your right hands) is that a person usually uses the right hand more for any task, and a pledge is similar to a kind of binding or knotting.

Now let us see who these "allies" are, whose share of inheritance must be paid.

Some commentators have suggested that this refers to "husband and wife," as they have entered into a marriage contract with one another. However, this possibility seems unlikely because referring to marriage as ‘aqd al-yamin or similar terms is very rare in the Quran; furthermore, it would be considered a repetition of previous points.

What is closer to the meaning of the verse is the pledge of Daman al-Jarirah (Guarantee of Offense), which existed before Islam. Islam reformed it and, because it had a constructive aspect, endorsed it. It was such that: "Two people would agree to help each other like brothers in their affairs, assist one another in the face of difficulties, and when one of them passed away, the survivor would inherit from him." Islam recognized this pledge of friendship and brotherhood but emphasized that the inheritance of such an ally is exclusively in the absence of the classes of blood relatives. That is, if no relatives remain, the person who has established Wala' Daman al-Jarirah and concluded such a treaty inherits from him. (A further explanation of this subject is found in the books of jurisprudence under the chapter of Inheritance).[^1]

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 3, p. 368

[^1]: - The form of the Daman al-Jarirah pledge is as follows: "‘Aqadtuka ‘ala an tansurani wa ansuraka wa ta‘qila ‘anni wa a‘qila ‘anka wa tarithani wa arithaka," to which the other person says, "Qabiltu." (I have pledged to you that you help me and I help you, and you pay the blood money/fines for me and I pay for you, and you inherit from me and I inherit from you. Then the other person says: "I accepted.")

إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ شَهِيداً Whether you fall short in giving the shares to the rightful heirs or fulfill their rights completely, in any case, Allah is aware, "for He is a Witness and Observer over every deed and everything."

Ayah

ٱلرِّجَالُ قَوَّٰمُونَ عَلَى ٱلنِّسَآءِ بِمَا فَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ بَعْضَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ وَبِمَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ مِنْ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ ۚ فَٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتُ قَـٰنِتَـٰتٌ حَـٰفِظَـٰتٌ لِّلْغَيْبِ بِمَا حَفِظَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَٱلَّـٰتِى تَخَافُونَ نُشُوزَهُنَّ فَعِظُوهُنَّ وَٱهْجُرُوهُنَّ فِى ٱلْمَضَاجِعِ وَٱضْرِبُوهُنَّ ۖ فَإِنْ أَطَعْنَكُمْ فَلَا تَبْغُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ سَبِيلًا ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيًّا كَبِيرًا (٣٤)
Men are the managers of women, because of the advantage Allah has granted some of them over others, and by virtue of their spending out of their wealth. Righteous women are obedient and watchful in the absence [of their husbands] in guarding what Allah has enjoined [them] to guard. As for those [wives] whose misconduct you fear, [first] advise them, and [if ineffective] keep away from them in the bed, and [as the last resort] beat them. Then if they obey you, do not seek any course [of action] against them. Indeed Allah is all-exalted, all-great. (34)

Commentary

Guardianship in the Family System

الرِّجالُ قَوَّامُونَ عَلَى النِّساءِ:

"Men are the guardians and servants of women"—To explain this sentence, it must be noted that the family is a small social unit, and like a large society, it must have a single leader and guardian. This is because collective leadership and guardianship, undertaken jointly by the man and the woman, is meaningless. Consequently, either the man or the woman must be the "head" of the family and the other the "assistant" under his or her supervision. Here, the Quran explicitly states that the position of guardianship must be given to the man (let there be no misunderstanding; this expression does not imply tyranny, injustice, or transgression, but rather a systematic unified leadership considering the necessary responsibilities and consultations).

This issue is clearer today than ever before: if a committee (even a two-person committee) is commissioned to perform a task, one of the two must certainly be the "head" and the other the "assistant or member"; otherwise, chaos will arise in their work. The guardianship of the man in the family is of this same nature.

This position is due to the existence of certain characteristics in men, such as the predominance of the power of reasoning over the force of emotion and feelings (contrary to women, who possess a greater abundance of emotional strength), and also having greater physical build and strength. With the former, he can think and devise plans, and with the latter, he can defend the sanctuary of his family.

Furthermore, his commitment to the woman and children regarding the payment of living expenses, the payment of the dower (mahr), and providing a dignified life for the spouse and children grants him the right to hold the duty of guardianship.

Of course, there may be women who excel over their husbands in the aforementioned aspects, but as we have repeatedly stated, laws do not look at every single individual and person; rather, they consider the type and the general rule. There is no doubt that, from a general perspective, men are more prepared for this task than women, even though women can also undertake duties whose importance is beyond doubt.

The phrase بِما فَضَّلَ اللَّهُ بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ وَ بِما أَنْفَقُوا مِنْ أَمْوالِهِمْ also refers to this same reality. In the first part, It says: "This guardianship is due to the differences that Allah, in His wisdom for the benefit of the human race, has placed between them in terms of creation." In the latter part, It says: "And also, this guardianship is due to the commitments that men undertake regarding spending and financial payments for women and the family."

However, it goes without saying that entrusting this duty to men is neither a proof of their higher human personality nor a cause for their distinction in the Hereafter, for that depends solely on piety and God-wariness. Just as the human personality of an assistant may be greater than that of a head in various aspects, yet the head is more suited for the guardianship of the task assigned to him than the assistant.

فالصالحات قانعات حافظات للغيب: Here, It adds that women are of two groups regarding the duties they hold in the family:

The first group: "The righteous and the doers of good; they are those who are humble and committed to the family system and maintain 'guardianship of the unseen' (hifz al-ghayb), not only in the presence of the husband but also in his absence." This means they do not commit betrayal regarding wealth, honor, or the preservation of the husband's personality and family secrets in his absence. They perform their duties and responsibilities well in return for the rights that Allah has granted them, which is referred to by the phrase بِما حَفِظَ اللَّهُ.

It is evident that men are duty-bound to show the utmost respect and gratitude toward such women.

Wayward Women

The second group are women who defy their duties and in whom signs of incompatibility are seen. Men have duties and responsibilities toward such women that must be carried out step by step, and in any case, they must be careful not to exceed the bounds of justice. These duties are stated in the verse in the following order:

وَ اللَّاتِي تَخافُونَ نُشُوزَهُنَ [^1] فَعِظُوهُنَ: The first stage concerns women in whom signs of rebellion, enmity, and hostility become apparent, which the Quran describes in the above phrase: "Admonish and give counsel to those women from whose rebellion and defiance you fear." Thus, those who step beyond the bounds of the family system must first be brought back to the right path and made aware of their responsibility through friendly advice and by explaining the ill consequences of such actions.

وَ اهْجُرُوهُنَّ فِي الْمَضاجِعِ:

"If your advice proves of no use, forsake them in the bed." By this reaction, indifference, and so-called "shunning," manifest your dissatisfaction with their behavior; perhaps this "mild reaction" will be effective in their spirit.

وَ اضْرِبُوهُنَ: If the rebellion and the flouting of duties and responsibilities exceed the limit, and they continue to walk the path of law-breaking with stubbornness and persistence—where neither advice has an effect nor separation in bed and indifference yields any benefit, and no way remains but "firmness of action"—then to compel them to fulfill their commitments and responsibilities, the only remedy becomes firmness and severity. Here, it is permitted to compel them to perform their duties through "physical punishment."

Question: [How has Islam permitted the punishment of women?]

Some might object: how has Islam allowed men to resort to physical punishment in the case of women?!

Answer

The answer to this objection is not very complicated, considering the meaning of the verse, the narrations that have been transmitted in its explanation, the clarifications provided in books of jurisprudence, and also the explanations given by modern psychologists. This is because:

Firstly, the verse permits the issue of physical punishment only for irresponsible individuals for whom no other means has proven effective. Incidentally, this is not a new subject unique to Islam; in all the laws of the world, when peaceful methods to compel individuals to perform their duty are not effective, they resort to force—not only through striking but sometimes, in specific cases, they even prescribe punishments more severe than that, reaching the level of execution.

Secondly, "physical punishment" here—as stated in books of jurisprudence—must be mild and slight, such that it causes neither breakage nor wounding, nor results in any bruising of the body.

Thirdly, modern psychoanalysts believe that some women possess a condition called "masochism" (pleasure in suffering), and sometimes when this condition intensifies in them, the only way to calm them is a brief physical punishment. Therefore, it is possible that this refers to such individuals for whom mild physical punishment serves a calming purpose and is a type of psychological treatment.

It is certain that if any of these stages prove effective and the woman proceeds to perform her duty, the man has no right to make excuses or seek to harass the woman. Therefore, following this sentence, It says:

فَإِنْ أَطَعْنَكُمْ فَلا تَبْغُوا عَلَيْهِنَّ سَبِيلًا:

"If they obey you, do not seek a way against them." And if it is asked whether such rebellion, defiance, and transgression are also possible in men, and whether men will also be subject to such punishments?

In response, we say: Yes, men are also punished exactly like women in case of violation of duties, even with physical punishment. However, since this task is often beyond the capability of women, the Sharia judge is responsible for making wayward men aware of their duties through various means, and even through ta'zir (physical punishment).

The story of the man who had treated his wife unjustly and was not willing to submit to the truth at any price, and how Ali (عليه السلام) compelled him to submit with firmness and even by threatening with the sword, is well-known.

إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ عَلِيًّا كَبِيراً: And at the end, It warns men again not to abuse their position of guardianship in the family and to think of the power of God, which is above all powers, "for Allah is Ever High, Ever Great."

[^1]: "Nushuz" is from "nashz" (rhyming with nadhr), meaning elevated and high ground, and here it is an allusion to rebellion and defiance.

Ayah

وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ شِقَاقَ بَيْنِهِمَا فَٱبْعَثُوا۟ حَكَمًا مِّنْ أَهْلِهِۦ وَحَكَمًا مِّنْ أَهْلِهَآ إِن يُرِيدَآ إِصْلَـٰحًا يُوَفِّقِ ٱللَّهُ بَيْنَهُمَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا خَبِيرًا (٣٥)
If you fear a split between the two of them, then appoint an arbiter from his relatives and an arbiter from her relatives. If they desire reconcilement, Allah shall reconcile them. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-aware. (35)

Commentary

The Family Arbitration Court

وَ إِنْ خِفْتُمْ شِقاقَ بَيْنِهِما ...

This verse refers to the issue of disputes and conflicts arising between spouses, stating:

"If signs of a breach and separation appear between the two spouses, in order to investigate the causes and aspects of the discord and to prepare the grounds for peace and reconciliation, appoint one arbiter (hakam) from the man's family and one arbiter from the woman's family." Then it continues: "إِنْ يُرِيدا إِصْلاحاً يُوَفِّقِ اللَّهُ بَيْنَهُما": "If these two arbiters enter the matter with good intentions and sincerity, and their goal is to reconcile the spouses, Allah will help and, through them, create affection between the husband and wife."

And to warn the "two arbiters" to exercise good faith, He says at the end of the verse: "Allah is aware and cognizant of their intentions" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ عَلِيماً خَبِيراً).

The family arbitration court mentioned in the above verse is one of the masterpieces of Islam. This court possesses advantages that other courts lack, including:

1- The family environment is a center of feelings and emotions; naturally, the criteria used in this environment must differ from those used in other settings. That is, just as one cannot operate based on love and emotion in "criminal courts," one cannot proceed solely with the cold criteria of law and soulless regulations in a family environment. Here, disputes should be resolved through emotional means as much as possible. Therefore, He commands that the arbiters of this court be those who have kinship ties with the spouses and can stimulate their emotions toward reconciliation. Obviously, this advantage exists only in this court, and other courts lack it.

2- In ordinary judicial courts, the parties to the lawsuit are forced to reveal any secrets they have to defend themselves. It is certain that if a man and woman reveal their marital secrets before strangers and outsiders, they will wound each other's feelings to such an extent that even if they return home by court order, there will be no trace of the former intimacy and love. They would become like two strangers who must perform duties by force of law. In principle, experience has shown that a husband and wife who head to such courts are no longer the same husband and wife as before.

However, in the family arbitration court, such matters are either not raised due to a sense of modesty (sharm-e huzur), or if they are, because it is before acquaintances and confidants (mahram), it will not have that adverse effect.

3- Arbiters in ordinary courts are often indifferent to the course of disputes; it makes no difference to them how the case ends—whether the spouses return home or separate forever.

In the family arbitration court, however, the matter is quite the opposite. Since the arbiters of this court are close relatives of the man and woman, the separation or reconciliation of the two affects the lives of these relatives both emotionally and in terms of the resulting responsibilities. Therefore, they exert their utmost effort to establish peace and sincerity between the two and, so to speak, bring the "diverted water back to its stream."

4- Beyond all this, such a court has none of the problems, staggering costs, and frustrations of ordinary courts, and without any formalities, the parties can achieve their goal in the shortest possible time.

It goes without saying that the two arbiters must be chosen from among the mature, tactful, and wise individuals of the two families.

With these advantages we have enumerated, it becomes clear that the chance of success for this court in reconciling the spouses is far greater than that of other courts.

The issue of the two arbiters, their conditions, and the scope of their authority and judgment regarding the spouses has been detailed in Islamic jurisprudence (Fiqh). Among these conditions: the two arbiters must be mature (baligh), sane (aqil), just (adil), and insightful regarding their task.

As for the binding nature of their judgment regarding the spouses, some jurists consider their ruling to be mandatory regardless of what it is. The literal meaning of the term "hakam" (arbiter/judge) in the verse also conveys this meaning, as the concept of arbitration and judgment implies the binding nature of the ruling. However, most jurists consider the opinion of the two arbiters to be binding only regarding reconciliation and resolving the dispute. They even believe that if the arbiters set conditions for the husband or wife, those are binding, but regarding separation (divorce), their ruling alone is not binding. The end of the verse, which refers to the issue of reconciliation (islah), is more consistent with this view. Read more details on this matter in books of jurisprudence.

Ayah

۞ وَٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا وَبِذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱلْجَارِ ذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْجَارِ ٱلْجُنُبِ وَٱلصَّاحِبِ بِٱلْجَنۢبِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَمَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ مَن كَانَ مُخْتَالًا فَخُورًا (٣٦)
Worship Allah and do not ascribe any partners to Him, and be good to parents, the relatives, the orphans, the needy, the near neighbour and the distant neighbour, the companion at your side, the traveller, and your slaves. Indeed Allah does not like those who are arrogant and boastful. (36)

Commentary

The above verse outlines a series of Islamic rights, encompassing both the rights of Allah and the rights of His servants, as well as the etiquette of social interaction. Altogether, ten instructions can be derived from it:

1-

وَ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ وَ لا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئاً

First, it invites people to the worship and servitude of the Lord and to abandon shirk (polytheism) and idol-worship, which is the primary root of all Islamic programs. The call to Tawhid (monotheism) and the worship of the One God purifies the soul, makes the intention sincere, strengthens the will, and firms one's resolve to carry out every beneficial program. Since the verse explains a series of Islamic rights, it mentions Allah's right over the people before anything else, saying: "Worship Allah and associate nothing with Him."

2-

وَ بِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً

Then, it refers to the rights of parents and recommends that you show kindness to them. The rights of parents are among the issues heavily emphasized in the Holy Quran; few subjects have received such stress. In four places in the Quran, this command is placed immediately after Tawhid [^1]. From these repeated expressions, it is understood that there is a connection and bond between the two. In reality, it is so, because the greatest blessing is the blessing of existence and life, which stems primarily from Allah, and in subsequent stages, is related to the parents, as the child is a part of the parents' existence. Therefore, neglecting the rights of parents is akin to shirk (associating partners with Allah).

We have detailed discussions regarding the rights of parents which, God willing, will come under the appropriate verses in Surah Al-Isra and Surah Luqman.

3-

وَ بِذِي الْقُرْبى‌

Next, it commands kindness toward all relatives. This subject is also among those heavily emphasized in the Quran, sometimes under the title of "Silat al-Rahim" (maintaining family ties) and sometimes as "Ihsan" (kindness) toward them. In fact, through this, Islam intends to create stronger bonds within smaller, organized units called "kinship" and "family," in addition to the broad bond it establishes among all human beings, so that they may assist one another in the face of problems and incidents and defend each other's rights.

4-

وَ الْيَتامى‌

Then, it points to the rights of "orphans" and advises the faithful to be kind to them. In every society, due to various incidents, there are always orphaned children whose neglect not only endangers their own situation but also endangers the state of society. If orphaned children are left without a guardian

[^1]: Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 83; Surah Al-An'am, verse 151; Surah Al-Isra, verse 23 (in addition to the above verse).

or are not sufficiently saturated with affection, they grow up to be wayward, dangerous, and criminal individuals. Therefore, kindness toward orphans is both kindness to the individual and kindness to society!

5-

وَ الْمَساكِينِ‌

After that, it reminds of the rights of the needy (al-masakin). Even in a healthy society where justice is established, there will be disabled, incapacitated individuals and the like, whose neglect is contrary to all human principles. Furthermore, if poverty and deprivation afflict healthy individuals due to a deviation from the principles of social justice, it must be combated.

6-

وَ الْجارِ ذِي الْقُرْبى‌

Then, it recommends kindness toward "the neighbor who is a relative." Commentators have offered various possibilities regarding what is meant by "near neighbor." Some have interpreted it as neighbors who are also kin. However, this interpretation seems unlikely given that the previous phrases of this verse already mentioned the rights of relatives. Rather, it refers to geographical proximity, as closer neighbors have greater rights and respect; or it may refer to neighbors who are close to one in terms of religion and faith.

7-

وَ الْجارِ الْجُنُبِ‌

Then, it counsels regarding "the distant neighbor." As we mentioned, this refers to geographical distance—for according to some narrations, up to forty houses in all four directions are considered neighbors [^1], which in small towns encompasses almost the entire town.

(Because if we imagine every person's house as the center of a circle with a radius of forty houses in every direction, a simple calculation of the area of such a circle reveals that the total number of surrounding houses is approximately five thousand, and certainly, small towns do not have more houses than that).

It is noteworthy that in the above verse, in addition to mentioning "near neighbors," the Quran explicitly states the right of "distant neighbors." This is because the word "neighbor" usually has a limited concept and only includes near neighbors; thus, to draw attention to the breadth of its concept in the view of Islam, there was no way but to explicitly mention distant neighbors as well.

It is also possible that "distant neighbors" refers to non-Muslim neighbors, as the "right of neighborliness" in Islam is not exclusive to Muslim neighbors and includes non-Muslims as well (except those who are at war with Muslims).

The "right of neighborliness" is so important in Islam that we read in the famous testament of Amir al-Mu'minin عليه السلام:

"The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله continued to enjoin us regarding them until we thought he might order that neighbors should inherit from one another." (This hadith is also mentioned in famous Sunni sources; in Tafsir al-Manar and Tafsir al-Qurtubi, the same meaning is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله via Bukhari).

In another hadith narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, he said three times one day:

"By Allah, such a person does not have faith..." Someone asked, "Who?!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"The one whose neighbor is not safe from his harm!" [^1] And again in another hadith, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"Whoever believes in Allah and the Last Day should be kind to his neighbor." [^2]
