[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 480. [^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 3, p. 1754. [^2]: Al-Manar, Vol. 5, p. 92, Beirut edition.

And it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said:

"Good neighborliness prospers the lands and increases lifespans." [^1] In the mechanized world where neighbors have the slightest knowledge of one another, and sometimes two neighbors do not even know each other's names after twenty years, this great Islamic instruction has a special brilliance. Islam places extraordinary importance on emotional issues and human cooperation, while in mechanized life, emotions are day by day eroding and giving way to hard-heartedness.

8-

وَ الصَّاحِبِ بِالْجَنْبِ‌

Then, it advises regarding those who have friendship and companionship with a person. However, it should be noted that "al-sahib bi-al-janb" has a broader meaning than just a friend or companion; in fact, it encompasses anyone who sits or associates with a person in any way, whether it be a permanent friend or a temporary one (such as someone who sits with a person during a journey). If we see in some narrations that "al-sahib bi-al-janb" is interpreted as a travel companion (rafiquka fi al-safar) or someone who comes to a person hoping for a benefit (al-munqati' ilayka yarju naf'aka), the intention is not to limit it to them, but rather to demonstrate the breadth of this expression's concept, which includes all these cases. Thus, the verse is a comprehensive and general instruction for good social conduct toward everyone with whom a person has a connection, including true friends, colleagues, fellow travelers, petitioners, students, consultants, and servants.

In some narrations, "al-sahib bi-al-janb" has been interpreted as "spouse," as the authors of Al-Manar, Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, and Qurtubi have narrated this meaning from Ali عليه السلام under this verse. However, it is not unlikely that this is also an explanation of one of the instances of the verse.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Safi, p. 120.

9-

وَ ابْنِ السَّبِيلِ‌

Another group recommended here are those who find themselves in need while traveling or in a foreign land. Although they might be wealthy individuals in their own city, they become stranded during travel for some reason. The interesting expression "ibn al-sabil" (son of the road) is used because we have no acquaintance with them to attribute them to a tribe, family, or person; solely by virtue of being needy travelers, they must be supported.

10-

وَ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ‌

In the final stage, it recommends being kind toward slaves. In truth, the verse begins with the right of Allah and ends with the rights of slaves, for these rights are not separate from one another. It is not only this verse that recommends kindness toward slaves; various other verses have also discussed this matter. Furthermore, Islam has organized a precise program for the gradual liberation of slaves leading to their "absolute freedom," which, God willing, we will discuss in detail under the appropriate verses.

إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُحِبُّ مَنْ كانَ مُخْتالًا فَخُوراً At the end of the verse, it warns with this sentence: "Indeed, Allah does not love those who are arrogant and boastful." This serves as a warning that whoever defies Allah's command and, out of arrogance, refuses to observe the rights of relatives, parents, orphans, the needy, the wayfarer, and friends, is not beloved by Allah and is not under His grace. And he who is not included in His grace is deprived of every good and happiness.

Evidence for this meaning is a narration regarding this verse: one of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله says: "I recited this verse in his presence. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله enumerated the ugliness of arrogance and its evil consequences to such an extent that I wept. He asked: 'Why do you weep?' I said: 'I like my clothes to be attractive and beautiful, and I fear that by this very act I may be among the arrogant.' He said: 'No, you are of the people of Paradise, and these are not signs of arrogance. Arrogance is when a person is not humble before the truth and considers himself superior to the people and belittles them (and refuses to fulfill their rights).'"

In summary, it appears from the final part of the verse that the primary source of shirk and the trampling of people's rights is often selfishness and arrogance. Fulfilling the aforementioned rights, especially regarding slaves, orphans, the destitute, and the like, requires a spirit of modesty and humility. [^1]

[^1]: It should be noted that "mukhtal" is from the root "khayal," meaning one who perceives himself as great through a series of "imaginations" (takhayyulat). If a horse is called "khayl," it is because it walks in a manner similar to the arrogant. "Fakhur" is from the root "fakhr," meaning one who boasts. Therefore, the difference between these two words here is that one refers to arrogant mental imaginations and the other to external arrogant actions.

Ayahs

ٱلَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ وَيَأْمُرُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ بِٱلْبُخْلِ وَيَكْتُمُونَ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا (٣٧)
—Those who are [themselves] stingy and bid [other] people to be stingy, and conceal whatever Allah has given them out of His bounty; and We have prepared for the faithless a humiliating punishment. (37)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ رِئَآءَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَلَا بِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۗ وَمَن يَكُنِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لَهُۥ قَرِينًا فَسَآءَ قَرِينًا (٣٨)
And those who spend their wealth to be seen by people, and believe neither in Allah nor in the Last Day. As for him who has Satan for his companion—an evil companion is he! (38)
وَمَاذَا عَلَيْهِمْ لَوْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَأَنفَقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِمْ عَلِيمًا (٣٩)
What harm would it have done them had they believed in Allah and the Last Day, and spent out of what Allah has provided them? Allah knows them well. (39)

Commentary

Ostentatious and Divine Spendings

This verse is, in fact, a continuation of the preceding verses and refers to arrogant and selfish individuals.

"They are those who not only withhold from doing good to people out of stinginess, but also urge others to be stingy (الَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ وَ يَأْمُرُونَ النَّاسَ بِالْبُخْلِ).

Furthermore, they strive to conceal what Allah has bestowed upon them of His bounty, lest the members of society develop expectations of them (وَ يَكْتُمُونَ ما آتاهُمُ اللَّهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ).

Then, it describes their ultimate end and consequence: We have prepared for the disbelievers a humiliating punishment (وَ أَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ عَذاباً مُهِيناً). Perhaps the secret behind this expression is that "stinginess" often stems from disbelief; for stingy individuals, in reality, lack complete faith in the infinite blessings of the Lord and His promises to the doers of good; they think that helping others will lead them to ruin.

And the statement that their punishment is "humiliating" is so that they may experience the recompense for their "arrogance" and "superiority complex" through this path.

Additionally, it should be noted that stinginess is not limited to financial matters; rather, it encompasses withholding any type of Divine blessing. There are many who are not stingy in financial matters but are stingy regarding knowledge, science, and other similar issues! In the second verse, it points to another quality of the selfish arrogant ones, stating:

وَ الَّذِينَ يُنْفِقُونَ أَمْوالَهُمْ رِئاءَ النَّاسِ وَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَ لا بِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ ...:

"They are those who, if they spend, do so for the sake of showing off to people and gaining fame and status; their goal is not to serve the creation or seek the pleasure of the Creator. For this reason, in instances of spending, they are not bound by the merit of the recipient; rather, they are constantly thinking about how to spend so they can benefit more for themselves and solidify their own position. This is because they do not believe in Allah or the Last Day, and therefore, there is no spiritual motive in their spending; rather, their motive is that very name, fame, and the acquisition of a false persona through this means, which is also among the effects of their arrogance and selfishness.

وَ مَنْ يَكُنِ الشَّيْطانُ لَهُ قَرِيناً فَساءَ قَرِيناً: They have chosen Satan as their friend and companion, and whoever is such has chosen a very evil companion for himself and will have no better fate. This is because their logic and program are the same as the logic and program of their companion, Satan; it is he who tells them that sincere spending leads to poverty: (الشَّيْطانُ يَعِدُكُمُ الْفَقْرَ—Al-Baqarah, 268). Consequently, they either do not spend and practice stinginess (as mentioned in the previous verse) or, if they do spend, it is in cases where they will personally benefit (as mentioned in this verse).

From this verse, it is also understood how much an evil companion can influence a person's destiny, to the point of dragging him to the lowest degree of fall.

It is also understood that the relationship of the "arrogant" with "Satan and satanic deeds" is a continuous relationship, not temporary or occasional, because they have chosen Satan as a "Qarin" (close companion) and associate.

وَ ما ذا عَلَيْهِمْ لَوْ آمَنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَ أَنْفَقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَهُمُ اللَّهُ ...

Here, as an expression of regret for the state of this group, it says: "What [harm] would it have done them if they had turned back from these wrong paths and believed in Allah and the Last Day, and spent from the blessings Allah has placed at their disposal with sincere intention and pure thought for the servants of Allah, thereby acquiring happiness and prosperity in this world and the Hereafter for themselves?" Since this path is smoother, clearer, and more beneficial, and the path they have chosen results in nothing but loss and misery, why do they not reconsider their actions?!

وَ كانَ اللَّهُ بِهِمْ عَلِيماً: And in any case, Allah is well-aware of their intentions and actions and will reward or punish them accordingly.

It is noteworthy that in the previous verse, which spoke of ostentatious spending, the spending was attributed to "wealth" (amwal), while in this verse, it is attributed to "that which Allah has provided them" (mimma razaqahumullah). This difference in expression may point to three points:

First, in ostentatious spending, no attention is paid to whether the wealth is lawful (halal) or unlawful (haram), whereas in Divine spending, lawfulness and being an instance of "that which Allah has provided" is considered.

Second, in ostentatious spending, because the spenders consider the wealth to belong to themselves, they do not refrain from arrogance and boasting. In contrast, in Divine spending, because they realize that Allah has given them the wealth and that if they spend a portion of it in His way, there is no room for boasting, they avoid any kind of arrogance or reminders of favor.

On the other hand, ostentatious spending is often limited to wealth, because such individuals are devoid of spiritual capital to spend from. However, Divine spending has a vast scope and encompasses all material and spiritual blessings, including wealth, knowledge, social status, and the like.

Ayah

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَظْلِمُ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ ۖ وَإِن تَكُ حَسَنَةً يُضَـٰعِفْهَا وَيُؤْتِ مِن لَّدُنْهُ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (٤٠)
Indeed Allah does not wrong [anyone] [even to the extent of] an atom’s weight, and if it be a good deed He doubles it[s reward], and gives from Himself a great reward. (40)

Commentary

What is a "Zarrah"?

"Zarrah" (ذره) originally refers to very tiny ants that are barely visible. Some have said it originally refers to the extremely small particles of dust suspended in the air, which become visible when sunlight shines through a small opening into a dark area. It has also been said that if a person places their hand on soil or something similar and then blows on their hand, each of the particles that scatter into the air is called a "Zarrah."

However, gradually, the term "Zarrah" came to be applied to any small thing. Today, the "atom," which is the smallest component of matter, is also called "Zarrah." This is because if it was previously applied to dust particles, it was because they were imagined to be the smallest components of matter. But today, since it has been proven that the smallest components of a "compound substance" are molecules, and the smallest components of a "simple substance" are atoms—which are significantly smaller than molecules—this name has been chosen in scientific terminology for the "atom." The atom is not only invisible to the eye but cannot even be observed with the most powerful electronic microscopes; its existence has been proven only through scientific formulas and specific types of photography performed with extraordinary equipment. Since "Mithqal" (مثقال) means "weight," the expression "مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ" means the weight of an extraordinarily small object.

The above verse states: Allah does not do injustice even to the extent of the weight of a "Zarrah." Not only does He not do injustice, but if a good deed is performed, He multiplies it and grants a great reward from Himself in return for it.

In reality, this verse says to the faithless and stingy individuals—whose condition was mentioned in the preceding verses: "These punishments that overtake you are the product of your own actions, and no injustice will come from Allah's side. Conversely, if instead of stinginess and disbelief, you had followed and chosen the path of Allah, you would have enjoyed His multiplied and great rewards."

Furthermore, it should be noted that "D'if" (ضعف) and "Muda'af" (مضاعف) in the Arabic language mean something to which its equivalent or several times its equivalent is added. Therefore, the above verse has no contradiction with the verses that state the reward for spending (Infaq) sometimes reaches ten times, and sometimes seven hundred times or more. In any case, it narrates the grace of Allah toward His servants, as He does not punish their sins more than the amount they have committed, yet He rewards their good deeds many times more than what they have performed.

As for the reason why Allah does not commit injustice, it is clear; for injustice and oppression usually stem from either ignorance, need, or psychological deficiencies. One who is All-Knowing regarding everything and everyone, is Independent of all, and has no deficiency in His Holy Essence—it is impossible for Him to commit injustice. This does not mean He is unable to do injustice, nor that injustice is inconceivable regarding Him (as the Ash'arite sect has imagined); rather, while possessing the power, He refrains from injustice because He is Wise and All-Knowing. He places everything in its proper position within this vast universe of existence and treats everyone according to their merit and actions.

Ayahs

فَكَيْفَ إِذَا جِئْنَا مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍۭ بِشَهِيدٍ وَجِئْنَا بِكَ عَلَىٰ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ شَهِيدًا (٤١)
So how shall it be, when We bring a witness from every nation and We bring you as a witness to them? (41)
يَوْمَئِذٍ يَوَدُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَعَصَوُا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ لَوْ تُسَوَّىٰ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَلَا يَكْتُمُونَ ٱللَّهَ حَدِيثًا (٤٢)
On that day those who were faithless and [who] disobeyed the Apostle will wish the earth were levelled with them, and they will not conceal any matter from Allah. (42)

Commentary

[The Witnesses of the Resurrection]

فَكَيْفَ إِذا جِئْنا مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ بِشَهِيدٍ Following the previous verses regarding the punishments and rewards of the evildoers and the righteous, this verse points to the issue of the witnesses of the Resurrection, stating: "How will it be for these individuals on that Day when We bring a witness from every nation regarding their deeds, and We bring you as a witness over these?" In this manner, in addition to the testimony of the parts of man's own body, the testimony of the earth upon which he lived, and the testimony of Allah's angels over his deeds, every Prophet is also a witness to the deeds of his own nation. The Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), who is the final and greatest Divine Prophet, is likewise a witness over his own nation. With all these witnesses present, how can the evildoers deny any reality or distance themselves from the retribution of their actions?

Similar themes are found in several other verses of the Quran, including Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 143; Surah Al-Nahl, verse 89; and Surah Al-Hajj, verse 78.

Now, the question arises: how will the Prophets' testimony regarding the deeds of their nations be carried out?

If the word "these" (ha'ula') refers to the Muslims, as stated in Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, the answer to this question is clear. This is because every Prophet, as long as he is among his nation, is a witness and observer of their deeds, and after them, their infallible successors (Awsiya) and deputies will be the witnesses and observers of the nation's deeds. Therefore, regarding Prophet Jesus (عليه السلام), we read that on the Day of Judgment, in response to the Lord's question, he says:

ما قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا ما أَمَرْتَنِي بِهِ أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ رَبِّي وَ رَبَّكُمْ وَ كُنْتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً ما دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنْتَ أَنْتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ أَنْتَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ شَهِيدٌ (Al-Ma'idah: 117):

"O Lord, I said nothing to them except what You commanded me: 'Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord.' And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them; but when You took me up, You were Yourself the Watcher over them, and You are Witness over all things."

However, a group of commentators have suggested the possibility that the word "these" (ha'ula') refers to the witnesses of previous nations. That is: "O Prophet of Islam, We shall appoint you as a witness over all the witnesses and Prophets of the past." Some narrations also point to this interpretation [1]. Accordingly, the meaning of the verse would be that every Prophet, during his life and after death, observes the states of his entire nation through inward and spiritual vision, and the pure soul of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) likewise observes all previous nations and his own nation through this same means. In this way, they can testify regarding their deeds. Even the righteous of the nation and exemplary pious individuals may possess such awareness. This would imply that the soul of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) has existed since the beginning of Adam's creation, because the meaning of "witnessing" (shuhud) is awareness coupled with presence. However, this interpretation is not very consistent with the verse quoted regarding Prophet Jesus (عليه السلام), as that verse says: Jesus was not a witness over his entire nation [at all times], but rather a witness over them as long as he lived (reflect on this).

But if we take "testimony" to mean "practical testimony"—that is, the deeds of an exemplary individual serving as a standard for measuring the deeds of others—then the above interpretation would be free of objection. For every Prophet, with the distinguished qualities he possessed, was considered a standard of measurement for his nation; the good and the bad of the nation can be recognized by their similarity or lack thereof to him. Since the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) is the greatest of the Divine Prophets, his qualities and deeds become the standard of measurement for the character of all Prophets.

The only remaining question is whether "testimony" has been used in this sense or not. However, considering that the deeds, behavior, and thoughts of exemplary human beings practically testify that a human is capable of traversing spiritual stations to such an extent, such a meaning does not seem far-fetched (reflect on this).

يَوْمَئِذٍ يَوَدُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ عَصَوُا الرَّسُولَ...

"At this time, when the disbelievers and those who rose in opposition to the Messenger of the Lord see the court of Allah's justice and observe undeniable witnesses in this court, they become so regretful of their actions that they wish they were dust and were leveled with the earth," as the Quran says: لَوْ تُسَوَّى بِهِمُ الْأَرْضُ. This expression is similar to the expression we read in the final verse of Surah Al-Naba: وَ يَقُولُ الْكافِرُ يا لَيْتَنِي كُنْتُ تُراباً ("At this time the disbeliever says: 'O, I wish I were dust!'"). However, the expression "leveled" (tusawwa) points to another matter as well: that the disbelievers, in addition to wishing to become dust, desire that their dust and graves be lost in the earth and leveled with the surrounding ground, so they might be entirely forgotten!

At this moment, they cannot conceal any reality (وَ لا يَكْتُمُونَ اللَّهَ حَدِيثاً), because with all those witnesses and testimonies, there is no way to deny.

Of course, this statement does not contradict other verses which say that some disbelievers will conceal facts and tell lies even on the Day of Resurrection [1], because their lying occurs before the presentation of witnesses and testimonies. But after there remains no room for denial, they are forced to confess to all realities.

In one of the sermons of the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام), it is narrated that he said: "On the Day of Resurrection, Allah places the seal of silence upon the mouths of individuals so they cannot speak; at this time, hands begin to speak, feet bear witness, and the skins of the body recount their deeds. At this moment, no one can conceal any reality" [2]. Some commentators have suggested the possibility that the meaning of لا يَكْتُمُونَ اللَّهَ حَدِيثاً is that they wish: "O, if only while we were in the world, we had not concealed the realities, especially regarding the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله)." Thus, the mentioned phrase would be coordinated with the phrase لَوْ تُسَوَّى بِهِمُ الْأَرْضُ. However, this interpretation is not consistent with the literal form of لا يَكْتُمُونَ, which is a present/future tense verb; if this meaning were intended, it should have been said "and they had not concealed" (wa lam yaktumu).

[^1]: Refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn and Tafsir al-Burhan under the above verse. [^1]: Such as verses 22 and 23 of Surah Al-An'am and verse 18 of Surah Al-Mujadilah. [^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 482, quoting Tafsir al-Ayyashi.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأَنتُمْ سُكَـٰرَىٰ حَتَّىٰ تَعْلَمُوا۟ مَا تَقُولُونَ وَلَا جُنُبًا إِلَّا عَابِرِى سَبِيلٍ حَتَّىٰ تَغْتَسِلُوا۟ ۚ وَإِن كُنتُم مَّرْضَىٰٓ أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ أَوْ جَآءَ أَحَدٌ مِّنكُم مِّنَ ٱلْغَآئِطِ أَوْ لَـٰمَسْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا۟ مَآءً فَتَيَمَّمُوا۟ صَعِيدًا طَيِّبًا فَٱمْسَحُوا۟ بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَفُوًّا غَفُورًا (٤٣)
O you who have faith! Do not approach prayer when you are intoxicated, [not] until you know what you are saying, nor [enter mosques] in the state of ritual impurity until you have washed yourselves, except while passing through. But if you are sick or on a journey, or any of you has come from the toilet, or you have touched women, and you cannot find water, then make your ablution on clean ground and wipe a part of your faces and your hands. Indeed Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving. (43)

Commentary

Several Juridical Rulings

Several Islamic rulings are derived from the above verse:

1- The prohibition of prayer while intoxicated; meaning, intoxicated individuals cannot engage in performing the obligatory prayer, and their prayer in this state is void. The philosophy behind this is clear, for prayer is a conversation between the servant and his Lord, a private supplication with God, and it must be performed in the utmost state of consciousness. Intoxicated individuals are far removed and estranged from this stage (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَقْرَبُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَأَنْتُمْ سُكَارَىٰ حَتَّىٰ تَعْلَمُوا مَا تَقُولُونَ).

Some might ask here: Does the above verse not imply that drinking alcoholic beverages is only forbidden if the intoxication persists until the time of prayer, thereby providing an implicit proof for its permissibility in other states?! The answer to this question—God willing—will come in detail in the commentary of verse 90 of Surah Al-Ma'idah. However, in brief, it is that Islam utilized a "gradual" method to implement many of its rulings. For instance, it implemented the prohibition of alcoholic beverages in several stages: first, identifying it as an undesirable drink and the opposite of "good provision" (An-Nahl: 67); then, preventing it if the intoxication occurs during prayer (the verse above); after that, comparing its benefits and harms and stating the prevalence of its harms (Al-Baqarah: 219); and in the final stage, issuing a decisive and explicit prohibition against it (Al-Ma'idah: 90).

Fundamentally, to uproot a social and moral vice with which an environment is deeply contaminated, there is no better way than this method of gradually preparing individuals and then announcing the final ruling. Furthermore, it should be noted that the above verse in no way indicates permission to drink wine; rather, it only speaks about intoxication during prayer and remains silent regarding non-prayer times until the final stage of the ruling arrives.

Of course, considering that the five prayer times—especially at that time when they were usually performed at five distinct times—are not far apart, performing prayer in a state of sobriety necessitates completely refraining from drinking intoxicating liquids during the intervals between these times, because their intoxication usually lasts until the next prayer time and sobriety is not achieved. Therefore, the ruling of the above verse is akin to a permanent and continuous prohibition.

It is also necessary to mention that in numerous narrations found in both Shi'a and Sunni books, the above verse has been interpreted as "the intoxication of sleep," meaning: do not start the prayer while you have not yet fully woken up, so that you may know what you are saying. [1] However, it seems that this interpretation is derived from the concept of "حَتَّىٰ تَعْلَمُوا مَا تَقُولُونَ" (until you know what you say), even if it does not fall under the literal meaning of "sukara" (intoxicated).

In other words, from the phrase "until you know what you say," it is understood that praying in any state where a person does not enjoy full consciousness is prohibited, whether it be the state of intoxication or the lingering state of sleep.

Additionally, from this phrase, it can be inferred that it is better for a person not to pray while in a state of lethargy or lack of focus, as the aforementioned state exists within him in a weak form. Perhaps for this reason, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that he said:

"Do not engage in prayer while you are lethargic, drowsy, or heavy, for Allah has forbidden the believers from praying while intoxicated." [2]

2- The invalidity of prayer in the state of janabah (ritual impurity)—which is referred to by the phrase "وَلَا جُنُبًا"—then He mentions an exception to this ruling, saying: "إِلَّا عَابِرِي سَبِيلٍ" (except those passing through). (And if you are a traveler and face a lack of water, in which case praying is permissible on the condition of tayammum, which will be mentioned at the end of the verse).

However, in narrations and reports, another interpretation for the verse has also been mentioned, [3] which is that "Salah" in the verse refers to the place of prayer, i.e., the mosque. Meaning: do not enter mosques while in a state of janabah. Then He exempts those who are just passing through the mosque, saying: "إِلَّا عَابِرِي سَبِيلٍ," meaning you may pass through the mosque while in a state of janabah.

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 483; and Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 3, p. 1771. [2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 483; a similar meaning is also found in Sahih al-Bukhari. [3]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 1, p. 486.

It is understood from some narrations that a group of Muslims and companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had built houses around the Prophet's mosque whose doors opened into the mosque, and they were permitted to pass through the mosque in a state of janabah without staying in it.

However, it should be noted that the consequence of the above interpretation would be that the word "Salah" in the verse is used in two meanings: one meaning the "prayer" itself, and the other meaning the "place of prayer." This is because two different rulings are stated in the above verse: one is refraining from prayer while intoxicated, and the other is refraining from entering mosques while in a state of janabah. (Of course, using one word for two or more meanings, as we have stated in Usul al-Fiqh, is not prohibited, but it is contrary to the apparent meaning and is not permissible without a context/clue; however, the aforementioned narrations can serve as that context).

3- The permissibility of praying or passing through the mosque after performing ghusl (ritual bath), which is expressed by the phrase "حَتَّىٰ تَغْتَسِلُوا" (until you have washed).

4- Tayammum for those with excuses—He then refers to the ruling of tayammum for those with excuses and says: "وَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ مَرْضَىٰ أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ..." (And if you are ill or on a journey...). In this phrase, in reality, all cases for the legislation of tayammum are gathered: first, the case where water is harmful to the body, and other cases where a person does not have access to water (or the use of water). With the phrase "أَوْ جَاءَ أَحَدٌ مِنْكُمْ مِنَ الْغَائِطِ أَوْ لَامَسْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ," He refers to the causes for needing tayammum, saying: "when one of you comes from the place of relieving oneself or you have had sexual intercourse with women," "فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا مَاءً" (and you find no water), "فَتَيَمَّمُوا صَعِيدًا طَيِّبًا" (then perform tayammum with clean earth). Then He explains the method of tayammum, saying: "فَامْسَحُوا بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُمْ" (then wipe your faces and your hands).

At the end of the verse, He refers to the fact that the aforementioned instruction is a type of facilitation and mitigation for you, because "Allah is Ever-Pardoning and All-Forgiving."

Here, attention to several points is necessary

1- The phrase "فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا مَاءً" (and you find no water), which begins with the "fa" of consequence, relates to the phrase "أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ" (or on a journey); meaning, when you are on a journey, it is possible that you may not find water and thus need tayammum—because when a person is in the city, this rarely happens. From here, it becomes clear that the statement made by some commentators, such as the author of Al-Manar, that "traveling alone is sufficient for performing tayammum instead of wudu, even if water is available," is a baseless statement. This is because the "fa" of consequence in "فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا" invalidates this claim, as its meaning is that travel sometimes leads to a lack of access to water, and in that case, one must perform tayammum, not that travel alone is a justification for tayammum. It is strange that the aforementioned author attacked the jurists of Islam in this regard, while the said attack has no merit.

2- The word "aw" (or) in "أَوْ جَاءَ أَحَدٌ مِنْكُمْ مِنَ الْغَائِطِ" is in the sense of "and," because simply being ill or being a traveler does not cause tayammum; rather, in such a state, if the causes for wudu or ghusl occur, then tayammum becomes obligatory.

3- The eloquence and modesty of the Quranic expression in this verse, like many other verses, is fully evident. When it wants to speak of relieving oneself, it chooses an expression that both conveys the meaning and avoids using an explicit or inappropriate term, saying: "أَوْ جَاءَ أَحَدٌ مِنْكُمْ مِنَ الْغَائِطِ." To explain, "ghait," contrary to the meaning understood from it today, [1] originally means a low-lying piece of ground that hides a person from view. Desert dwellers and travelers at that time would go there to "relieve themselves" to be away from people's sight. Therefore, the meaning of the phrase becomes:

"If one of you has come from a low-lying place..." which altogether is a metaphor for relieving oneself. Interestingly, instead of "you," "one of you" is used to make the expression even more modest. (Observe carefully). Similarly, where it speaks of sexual intercourse, it conveys the matter with the expression "أَوْ لَامَسْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ" (or you have touched women), and the word "touch" is a beautiful metaphor for sexual intercourse.

4- Regarding other specifics of tayammum from the phrase "صَعِيدًا طَيِّبًا," we will discuss it in detail, God willing, under verse 6 of Surah Al-Ma'idah.

The Philosophy of Tayammum

Many ask: what benefit can there be in striking the earth and wiping the forehead and the back of the hands? Especially since we know that much soil is contaminated and a carrier of microbes.

In response to such objections, two points should be noted:

A- The Moral Benefit: Tayammum is one of the acts of worship, and the spirit of worship in the true sense of the word is reflected in it. For a person touches his forehead, which is the most noble part of his body, with a hand that has struck the earth, to manifest his humility and lowliness before Him; meaning, my forehead and also my hands are humble and submissive before You to the utmost degree. Following this act, he turns his attention to prayer or other acts of worship that are conditioned upon wudu and ghusl. In this way, it leaves an effect on nurturing the spirit of humility, servitude, and gratitude in the servants.

B- The Hygienic Benefit: Today it is proven that soil, due to having numerous bacteria, can eliminate contaminations. These bacteria, whose job is to decompose organic matter and eliminate various types of infections, are usually abundant on the surface of the earth and at shallow depths where they can better utilize air and sunlight. For this reason,

[1]: In today's usage, "ghait" is mostly used to refer to human excrement.

when animal carcasses or human bodies are buried under the soil after death, as well as various contaminated materials on the ground, they are decomposed in a relatively short time, and the center of infection is dismantled by the attack of bacteria. It is certain that if this property were not in the soil, the Earth would have turned into a center of infection in a short time. Fundamentally, soil has a property similar to "antibiotic" substances, and its effect in killing microbes is extraordinarily high.

Therefore, clean soil is not only not contaminated but is also a destroyer of contaminations and can, in this respect, partially substitute for water, with the difference that water is a solvent—meaning it dissolves microbes and carries them away—but soil is a germ-killer.

However, it must be noted that the soil for tayammum must be completely pure, just as the Quran says in its beautiful expression: "tayyiban" (pure/clean).

It is noteworthy that the expression "sa'id," which is derived from the root "su'ud" (ascending), indicates that it is better to choose the surface soil of the earth for this purpose—the very soil that is exposed to sunlight and full of air and germ-killing bacteria. If such soil is also "tayyib" and pure, tayammum with it has the aforementioned effects without having the slightest harm. (We will also speak about this under verse 6 of Surah Al-Ma'idah).

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ يَشْتَرُونَ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةَ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَن تَضِلُّوا۟ ٱلسَّبِيلَ (٤٤)
Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book, who purchase error and desire that you [too] should lose the way? (44)
وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِأَعْدَآئِكُمْ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ نَصِيرًا (٤٥)
But Allah knows your enemies better, and Allah suffices as guardian, and Allah suffices as helper. (45)

Commentary

أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا نَصِيباً مِنَ الْكِتابِ يَشْتَرُونَ الضَّلالَةَ وَ يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ تَضِلُّوا السَّبِيلَ:

In this verse, Allah addresses His Prophet with an expression conveying wonder, stating: "It is astonishing, the state of a group who possessed a portion of the Heavenly Book, yet instead of purchasing guidance and prosperity for themselves and others with it, they bought misguidance for themselves and desire that you too should go astray."

In this manner, that which was meant to be a means of guidance for themselves and others was transformed—due to their evil intentions—into a means of becoming lost and leading others astray; for they were never seekers of the truth, but rather looked at everything through the dark lens of hypocrisy, envy, and materialism.

Then He says: Although these people present themselves in the guise of friends, they are your true enemies, and Allah is fully aware of them (وَ اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِأَعْدائِكُمْ‌).

What enmity could be greater than their opposition to your prosperity and guidance? Sometimes through the tongue of well-wishing, sometimes through slander, and at every moment in some form, they seek to realize their sinister goals.

However, you must never fear their enmity; you are not alone. It is sufficient that Allah is your Guardian and Protector, and your Helper and Supporter (وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ نَصِيراً).

For they can achieve nothing, and if you disregard their claims, there will be no cause for fear or concern.

Furthermore, from the phrase أُوتُوا نَصِيباً مِنَ الْكِتابِ ("a portion of the Book was given to them"), it is understood that what they possessed was not the entirety of the Heavenly Book, the Torah, but only a part of it. This is completely consistent with established historical facts, which show that parts of the original Torah were distorted or lost over time.

Ayah

مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ يُحَرِّفُونَ ٱلْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِۦ وَيَقُولُونَ سَمِعْنَا وَعَصَيْنَا وَٱسْمَعْ غَيْرَ مُسْمَعٍ وَرَٰعِنَا لَيًّۢا بِأَلْسِنَتِهِمْ وَطَعْنًا فِى ٱلدِّينِ ۚ وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا وَٱسْمَعْ وَٱنظُرْنَا لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ وَأَقْوَمَ وَلَـٰكِن لَّعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٤٦)
Among the Jews are those who pervert words from their meanings and say, ‘We hear and disobey’ and ‘Hear without listening!’ and ‘Ra’ina,’ twisting their tongues and reviling the faith. But had they said, ‘We hear and obey’ and ‘Listen’ and ‘Unzurna,’ it would have been better for them and more upright. But Allah has cursed them for their faithlessness, so they will not believe except a few. (46)

Commentary

Another Aspect of the Jews' Actions

Following the previous verses, this verse describes the characteristics of a group of the enemies of Islam and points to a portion of their actions.

First, it states that one of their deeds was the distortion of truths and changing the appearance of Allah’s commands: مِنَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا يُحَرِّفُونَ الْكَلِمَ عَنْ مَواضِعِهِ "Among those who are Jews, there are some who displace words from their places." This distortion might be literal (verbal) or conceptual and practical. However, the subsequent phrases indicate that the distortion intended here is literal distortion and the changing of phrasing, because following this sentence, He says:

وَ يَقُولُونَ سَمِعْنا وَ عَصَيْنا: "And they say: 'We hear and we disobey!'" That is, instead of saying Sami’na wa Ata’na ("We hear and we obey"), they say "We hear and we are defiant." This is exactly like the speech of those who sometimes say out of mockery and derision: "You keep talking, and we'll keep not listening!" Other phrases in the verse also testify to this interpretation.

Then, it points to another part of their hostile speech, mixed with audacity and rudeness, saying they say: وَ اسْمَعْ غَيْرَ مُسْمَعٍ: "And hear, may you never hear." In this way, to keep a group of uninformed people in the dark—in addition to distorting truths and betraying the delivery of the Heavenly Books, which constituted the primary capital for the salvation of their people and nation from the clutches of oppressors like Pharaoh—they resorted to the cowardly weapon of mockery and ridicule, which is the weapon of the selfish, arrogant, and stubborn.

Sometimes, in addition to all this, they would misuse phrases that the pure-hearted Muslims used in the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, using those phrases with different meanings to complete their mockery. For example, the phrase Ra’ina, which meant "Observe us and give us time." The sincere Muslims, at the beginning of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mission, would say this phrase to him so they could hear his words better and take them to heart. However, this group of Jews turned this phrase into a pretext and repeated it before him, intending its Hebrew meaning, which was "Hear, may you never hear," or they intended its other Arabic meaning, which was "Make us fools!" [1] This was an insinuation that the work of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله—Allah forbid—was to fool and deceive the people.

All of this was for the purpose of twisting truths from their original axis with their tongues and to malign the true religion (لَيًّا بِأَلْسِنَتِهِمْ وَ طَعْناً فِي الدِّينِ). (Layy, rhyming with Hayy, means twisting a rope and the like, and it has also come to mean changing and distorting).

وَ لَوْ أَنَّهُمْ قالُوا سَمِعْنا وَ أَطَعْنا وَ اسْمَعْ وَ انْظُرْنا لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ وَ أَقْوَمَ: "But if they had said: 'We hear and we obey,' and 'Hear,' and 'Look at us (give us time),' it would have been better for them and more proper." If instead of all this stubbornness, hostility toward the truth, audacity, and rudeness, they had taken the right path and said: "We heard the word of Allah and entered through the door of obedience; hear our words and observe us and give us time so that we may fully comprehend the truths," it would have been to their benefit and would have perfectly aligned with justice, logic, and etiquette.

وَ لكِنْ لَعَنَهُمُ اللَّهُ بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا: "But Allah has cursed them for their disbelief, so they do not believe, except for a few." Due to their disbelief, rebellion, and transgression, they have fallen far from Allah’s mercy, and their hearts are so dead that they will not soon become alive and awake to the truth. Only a small group of them are pure-hearted individuals who have the readiness to accept truths, hear the words of truth, and believe.

Some have considered this phrase to be among the reports of the unseen (ghayb) in the Quran, because as the Quran reported in this phrase, throughout the history of Islam, only a small number of Jews believed and joined Islam, while the rest of them, from that day until now, have been and remain at war with Islam.

[^1]: If Ra’ina is derived from the root r-’y, it means "observe us" and "give us time," and if it is derived from the root r-’-n (ru’unah), it means "make us fools." It should be noted that in the first case, the nun of Ra’ina is without a tashdid (doubling), and in the second case, it would be with a tashdid. It is understood from some narrations that the Jews apparently specifically added the tashdid to the nun and elongated its end.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِمَا نَزَّلْنَا مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن نَّطْمِسَ وُجُوهًا فَنَرُدَّهَا عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَارِهَآ أَوْ نَلْعَنَهُمْ كَمَا لَعَنَّآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلسَّبْتِ ۚ وَكَانَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ مَفْعُولًا (٤٧)
O you who were given the Book! Believe in what We have sent down confirming what is with you, before We blot out the faces and turn them backwards, or curse them as We cursed the People of the Sabbath, and Allah’s command is bound to be fulfilled. (47)

Commentary

The Fate of Obstinate Individuals

يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ آمِنُوا بِمَا نَزَّلْنَا مُصَدِّقًا لِمَا مَعَكُمْ Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the People of the Book, the address here is directed toward them, stating: "O you who have been given the Heavenly Book! Believe in the verses of the Majestic Quran, which are consistent with the signs mentioned in your own books regarding it." Certainly, possessing all these signs, you are more deserving than others to embrace this pure faith.

He then threatens them, urging them to submit to the Truth before they are afflicted by one of two punishments. The first is that He will completely erase your faces, destroying all the organs through which you see, hear, and perceive realities, and then turn your faces backward (مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَطْمِسَ وُجُوهًا فَنَرُدَّهَا عَلَىٰ أَدْبَارِهَا) [^1]. It perhaps needs no reminder that this phrase refers to the disabling of their intellect, intelligence, eyes, and ears in terms of failing to perceive the realities of life and deviating from the Straight Path. This is as narrated in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, who said: "It means erasing their faces from the path of guidance and returning them backward into the path of error and misguidance." [^2] To explain: when the People of the Book, especially the Jews, did not submit to the Truth despite all those clear signs and rose in conscious obstinacy and enmity—repeating this deliberate falsehood and wrongdoing in various scenes—it gradually became a second nature for them. It is as if their thoughts were completely transformed (metamorphosed) and their eyes and ears became blind and deaf. Such people, instead of moving forward in life, regress backward, and this is the recompense for those who knowingly deny the Truth. This is, in fact, similar to what was pointed out at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 6.

Therefore, the meaning of "erasing, blotting out, and returning to the back" in the above verse refers to intellectual and spiritual erasure and spiritual regression.

As for the second punishment with which they are threatened, it is that: "We shall distance them from Our mercy

[^1]: - Tams (طمس) originally means erasing the traces of something, such as when a house is demolished and its site is then completely leveled, removing all traces of the former building. However, it is also used metaphorically for things that have lost their effect and function.

[^2]: - Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 3, p. 55, under the aforementioned verse.

just as We distanced the People of the Sabbath [^1]" (أَوْ نَلْعَنَهُمْ كَمَا لَعَنَّا أَصْحَابَ السَّبْتِ).

Here a question arises: what is the difference between these two threats that they are joined by the word "or" (aw)?

Some commentators believe that the first threat has a spiritual aspect, while the second threat has an outward aspect and physical metamorphosis (maskh), based on the evidence that Allah says in this verse:

"Just as We distanced the People of the Sabbath from Our mercy, We shall also distance these people from Our mercy." And we know that the People of the Sabbath (as will come, God willing, in Surah Al-A'raf) were physically metamorphosed.

Others believe that this curse and distancing from Allah's mercy also had a spiritual aspect, with the difference being that the first threat refers to their deviation, misguidance, and regression, while the second threat means destruction and annihilation (one of the meanings of la'n is annihilation).

In short, by persisting and insisting on opposing the Truth, the People of the Book either regress and fall, or they are destroyed.

Another question arises here: was this threat carried out against them or not?

There is no doubt that the first threat was carried out regarding many of them, and the second threat regarding some of them. At the very least, a large group of them were crushed in the Islamic wars and their power vanished. World history shows that after that, they were also

[^1]: - The People of the Sabbath (Ashab al-Sabt), as the account of their story will come in Surah Al-A'raf under verses 163 to 166, were a group of Jews who, on the Sabbath day—when they were commanded to cease work—engaged in fishing contrary to the command of their Prophet, reaching the limit of rebellion and were afflicted with a painful punishment.

subjected to severe pressure in various countries and lost many people, and even now they live in very unfavorable and dangerous conditions.

At the end of the verse, to emphasize these threats, He says: "The command of Allah is always executed, and no power shall prevent it" (وَكَانَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ مَفْعُولًا).

Ayah

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِۦ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدِ ٱفْتَرَىٰٓ إِثْمًا عَظِيمًا (٤٨)
Indeed Allah does not forgive that partners should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes. Whoever ascribes partners to Allah has indeed fabricated [a lie] in great sinfulness. (48)

Commentary

The Most Hope-Inspiring Verses of the Quran

The above verse explicitly declares that all sins may be subject to pardon and forgiveness, but "shirk" (associating partners with Allah) is by no means forgiven, unless one abandons it, repents, and becomes a monotheist. In other words, no sin by itself destroys faith, just as no righteous deed saves a person if accompanied by shirk (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَ يَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ).

The connection between this verse and the preceding verses lies in the fact that the Jews and Christians were each, in some way, polytheists. Through this verse, the Quran warns them to abandon this belief, as it is an unforgivable sin. Then, at the end of the verse, it states the reason for this: "He who associates partners with Allah has indeed committed a grave sin" (وَ مَنْ يُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ فَقَدِ افْتَرى‌ إِثْماً عَظِيماً) [^1]. This verse is among those that encourage monotheists toward the grace and mercy of the Lord, because in it, Allah mentions the possibility of forgiving sins other than shirk. According to a narration that the late Tabarsi cited in Majma' al-Bayan from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, this verse is the most hope-inspiring verse in the Quran:

(ما فى القرآن آية ارجى عندى من هذه الاية) "There is no verse in the Quran more hope-inspiring to me than this verse."

And according to Ibn Abbas: "This verse is among the verses Allah revealed in Surah An-Nisa, and it is better for the believers than everything upon which the sun shines."

This is because there are many individuals who commit great sins and become forever despondent of Divine mercy and forgiveness, and that very despair causes them to continue the path of sin and error with the same intensity for the remainder of their lives. However, hope in Allah's forgiveness and pardon serves as an effective deterrent for them against sin and rebellion. Therefore, the verse actually pursues an educational and transformative objective.

When we see (according to some commentators and certain narrations cited under this verse) that a criminal individual like "Wahshi"—the killer of the brave officer of Islam, Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the uncle of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—embraces faith upon the revelation of this verse and ceases his crimes, this hope arises for other sinners as well: that they should not despair of the Lord's mercy and should not further pollute themselves beyond the sins they have already committed.

It might be argued that this verse simultaneously encourages people to sin, as it promises the forgiveness of "all sins except shirk."

[^1]: Iftira (slander/fabrication) is derived from the root fara (rhyming with fard), which originally means "to cut." Since cutting a part of something healthy causes it to become corrupt and ruined, it refers to any wrongful act, including shirk, lying, and false accusation.

However, there is no doubt that the intent of this promise of forgiveness is not an unconditional promise; rather, it includes individuals who demonstrate a degree of merit. As we have previously pointed out, the "Mashiyyah" (Will) and desire of Allah mentioned in this and similar verses refers to Divine "Wisdom," for Allah's Will is never separate from His Wisdom. Certainly, His Wisdom does not necessitate pardoning someone without merit. Therefore, the educational and constructive aspect of the verse far outweighs any potential misuse that might be made of it.

The Causes of Forgiveness of Sins

A noteworthy point is that the above verse is not related to the issue of repentance (tawbah), because repentance and returning from sin wash away all sins, even shirk. Rather, it refers to the possibility of Divine pardon encompassing those who did not find the success (tawfiq) to repent—meaning they died before regretting their actions, or after regretting them but before compensating for their evil deeds.

To explain: It is understood from numerous verses of the Holy Quran that the means of pardon and forgiveness of sins are multiple, which can be summarized into five categories:

  1. Repentance and returning to Allah, which must be accompanied by regret for past sins, a resolve to avoid sin in the future, and practical compensation for evil deeds through good deeds (verses indicating this are numerous), including the verse: وَ هُوَ الَّذِي يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبادِهِ وَ يَعْفُوا عَنِ السَّيِّئاتِ‌: "And He it is who accepts repentance from His servants and pardons the evil deeds." [^1]

  2. Extraordinary good deeds that cause the forgiveness of ugly actions,

[^1]: Surah Ash-Shura, Verse 25.

as He says: إِنَّ الْحَسَناتِ يُذْهِبْنَ السَّيِّئاتِ‌: "Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds" (Hud: 114).

  1. Intercession (Shafa'ah), the explanation of which was given in Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 160.

  2. Avoiding "Major" sins, which leads to the forgiveness of "minor" sins, as explained under verses 31 and 32 of this same Surah.

  3. Divine Pardon, which includes individuals who are worthy of it, as stated in the verse under discussion.

We remind again that Divine pardon is conditional upon His Will, and thus it is not a general and unconditional matter. His Will and Volition apply only to individuals who have practically proven their merit in some way. From here, it becomes clear why shirk is not forgivable: because the polytheist has completely severed his connection with Allah and has committed an act that goes against the entire foundation of religions and the laws of creation.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُزَكُّونَ أَنفُسَهُم ۚ بَلِ ٱللَّهُ يُزَكِّى مَن يَشَآءُ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا (٤٩)
Have you not regarded those who style themselves as pure? Indeed, it is Allah who purifies whomever He wishes, and they will not be wronged [so much as] a single date-thread. (49)
ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ ۖ وَكَفَىٰ بِهِۦٓ إِثْمًا مُّبِينًا (٥٠)
Look, how they fabricate lies against Allah! That suffices for a flagrant sin. (50)

Circumstances of Revelation

In many Islamic commentaries, it is narrated regarding this verse that the Jews and Christians claimed certain privileges for themselves. As mentioned in other verses of the Quran, they would sometimes say, "We are the children of Allah," and at other times, "Paradise is reserved for us, and no one else shall enter it" (Surah Al-Ma'idah, Verse 18, and Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 111). The above verses were revealed to respond to these vain imaginings.

Commentary

Self-Praise

أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ يُزَكُّونَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ [^1] In this verse, reference is made to one of the reprehensible traits that afflicts many individuals and nations: self-praise, portraying oneself as pure, and fabricating virtues for oneself. The verse says: "Have you not seen those who claim purity for themselves?"...

Then He says: "Nay, but Allah purifies whom He wills" (بَلِ اللَّهُ يُزَكِّي مَنْ يَشاءُ).

And He alone is the One who, out of His wisdom and supreme will, praises and commends individuals according to the merits they possess, without any addition or subtraction. And never shall anyone be wronged even by the amount of a hair on a date-seed

[^1]: Yuzakkuna is derived from the root tazkiyah, meaning to consider pure or to present as pure. Sometimes it also means to purify, to educate, and to cause to grow. In reality, it means purification; if it has a practical aspect, it is commendable, but if it is merely through speech and claims, it is reprehensible.

(وَ لا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا [^1]). In truth, virtue is that which Allah considers a virtue, not what the self-praisers claim for themselves out of selfishness, thereby wronging themselves and others.

Although the address is directed at the Jews and Christians who claimed baseless and incorrect privileges for themselves and presented themselves as the chosen people—sometimes saying: لَنْ تَمَسَّنَا النَّارُ إِلَّا أَيَّاماً مَعْدُودَةً "The Fire shall not touch us but for a few numbered days" (Al-Baqarah: 80), and sometimes saying: نَحْنُ أَبْناءُ اللَّهِ وَ أَحِبَّاؤُهُ "We are the children of Allah and His beloved ones" (Al-Ma'idah: 18)—its concept is not exclusive to any specific people or group. Rather, it encompasses all individuals and nations afflicted by this reprehensible trait.

In Surah An-Najm, Verse 32, the Quran explicitly addresses all Muslims, saying: فَلا تُزَكُّوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ اتَّقى "So do not claim purity for yourselves; He knows best who is God-fearing."

The source of this behavior is vanity, pride, and self-centeredness, which gradually manifests as self-praise and ultimately leads to arrogance and seeking superiority.

This wrong habit, which unfortunately exists among many nations, classes, and individuals, is the source of a significant portion of social disorders, wars, colonialisms, and quests for supremacy. Past history shows that some nations of the world, due to this false feeling, considered themselves superior to other nations and therefore felt entitled to make them their servants and slaves. The Arabs of the Age of Ignorance, despite all their backwardness and comprehensive poverty, considered themselves a superior race! And among their tribes, each considered itself the "superior tribe."

[^1]: Fatil literally refers to the very thin thread found in the cleft of a date-stone and is an allegory for extremely small things. Its origin is from the root fatl, meaning to twist.

In the recent era, the issue of the superiority of the German race or the Israeli race became the source of world wars or regional conflicts.

At the dawn of Islam, the Jews and Christians were also afflicted with such a delusion regarding others, and thus they were hardly willing to submit to the truths of Islam. For this reason, in the following verse, the Quran severely crushes such delusions and quests for superiority, introducing them as a form of fabrication and attributing lies to Allah, and a great and manifest sin, saying:

انْظُرْ كَيْفَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ وَ كَفى‌ بِهِ إِثْماً مُبِيناً: "See how they invent lies against Allah; and that is enough as a manifest sin. (Behold how this group, by fabricating false virtues and attributing them to Allah, lie against their Lord; if they had no sin other than this, it would be sufficient for their punishment)."

Imam Ali عليه السلام, in the famous "Hammam" sermon regarding the distinguished qualities of the God-fearing, says:

لا يرضون من اعمالهم القليل و لا يستكثرون الكثير فهم لانفسهم متهمون و من اعمالهم مشفقون اذا زكى احد منهم خاف مما يقال له فيقول انا اعلم بنفسى من غيرى و ربى اعلم بى من نفسى اللهم لا تؤاخذنى بما يقولون و اجعلنى افضل مما يظنون و اغفر لى ما لا يعلمون

"They are never satisfied with their few deeds and never consider their many deeds as great. They always hold themselves suspect in fulfilling their duties and are fearful of their own actions. When one of them is praised, he becomes terrified of what is said about him and says: 'I know myself better than others do, and my Lord knows me better than I know myself. O Allah! Do not take me to task for what they say, and make me better than what they think of me, and forgive me for those [faults] of mine which they do not know!'"

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْجِبْتِ وَٱلطَّـٰغُوتِ وَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ أَهْدَىٰ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ سَبِيلًا (٥١)
Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book, believing in idols and fake deities and saying of the pagans: ‘These are better guided on the way than the faithful’? (51)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَمَن يَلْعَنِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ نَصِيرًا (٥٢)
They are the ones whom Allah has cursed, and whomever Allah curses, you will never find any helper for him. (52)

Occasion of Revelation

Many commentators have stated regarding the occasion of revelation for the above verses that after the event of "Uhud," one of the Jewish leaders named "Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf," accompanied by seventy Jews, went to Makkah to form an alliance with the polytheists of Makkah against the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and to break the treaty they had with the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. Ka'b entered the house of Abu Sufyan, and Abu Sufyan honored him. The Jews were hosted as guests in various houses of the Quraysh. One of the people of Makkah said to Ka'b: "You are People of the Book, and Muhammad also possesses a Book; the truth is, we suspect this might be a plot devised to destroy us. If you want us to ally with you, the first condition is that you prostrate before these two idols (pointing to two large idols) and believe in them." They did so.

Then Ka'b suggested to the people of Makkah that thirty people from you and thirty from us should go to the side of the Kaaba, press our bellies against the wall of the Kaaba, and make a covenant with the Lord of the Kaaba that we shall not fall short in the battle against Muhammad. This plan was also carried out. After its completion, Abu Sufyan turned to Ka'b and said: "You are a learned man, while we are illiterate and uneducated! In your opinion, who is closer to the truth, us or Muhammad?" Ka'b said: "Describe your religion to me completely." Abu Sufyan said:

"We sacrifice large camels for the pilgrims, provide them with water, honor the guest, free the captives, maintain ties of kinship, keep the House of our Lord inhabited and prosperous, and perform Tawaf around it. We are the people of Allah's Sanctuary, the land of Makkah. But Muhammad has abandoned the religion of his ancestors, severed ties of kinship, and departed from Allah's Sanctuary and our ancient faith. Muhammad's religion is a new and fledgling one." Ka'b said: "By Allah, your religion is better than Muhammad's religion!" At this moment, the above verses were revealed and responded to them.

Commentary

The Compromisers

In light of the occasion of revelation mentioned above, this verse reflects another of the reprehensible traits of the Jews: that to advance their goals, they showed such compromise with any group that they would even prostrate before idols to win the favor of idolaters. They trampled upon what they had seen or read regarding the greatness of Islam and the attributes of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. To please the idolaters, they even preferred their superstitious and disgraceful creed over Islam, despite being People of the Book whose common ground with Islam was far greater than with the idolaters. Therefore, the above verse says in a tone of astonishment: "Have you not seen those who were given a portion of the Book, yet they prostrate before idols and express faith in transgressors (أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا نَصِيباً مِنَ الْكِتابِ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْجِبْتِ وَ الطَّاغُوتِ‌)."

They did not stop at this, but rather said to the disbelievers: "Your way is more rightly guided than that of the believers" (وَ يَقُولُونَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا هؤُلاءِ أَهْدى‌ مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا سَبِيلًا).

Jibt and Taghut

The word "Jibt" is used only in this verse of the Holy Quran. It is a triliteral noun with no derivatives. It is said to have originally been an Abyssinian word used to mean "sorcery," "sorcerer," or "Satan." Later, it entered the Arabic language and was used with these meanings or to mean an idol and any deity other than Allah. It is also said that it was originally "Jibs," and then the "s" was changed to "t."

The word "Taghut" is used in eight places in the Holy Quran. As we mentioned in the first volume of this Tafsir on page 207 under verse 256 of Surah Al-Baqarah, it is an intensive form [^1] derived from the root "Tughyan," meaning transgression and exceeding the limit. It is applied to anything that causes one to exceed the limit (including idols). For this reason, Satan, idols, tyrannical and arrogant rulers, any deity other than Allah, and any path that leads to other than the Truth are called Taghut. This is the general meaning of these two terms.

However, regarding what is meant by these two words in the verse under discussion, commentators have various interpretations. Some have said these were the names of two idols that the Jewish group prostrated to in the aforementioned story. Others have said: "Jibt" here means an idol, and "Taghut" means idol-worshippers or the supporters of idols who, acting as spokespersons for the idols, would quote things from them and falsely attribute them to the idols to deceive the people [^1]. This meaning is more consistent with what was said in the occasion of revelation and the commentary of the verse, as the Jews both prostrated before the idols and submitted to the idol-worshippers.

Then, in the next verse, it describes the fate of such compromisers, saying:

"Those are the ones whom Allah has cursed (removed from His mercy), and whomever Allah curses, you will never find for him any helper" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ اللَّهُ وَ مَنْ يَلْعَنِ اللَّهُ فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لَهُ نَصِيراً).

And just as this verse says, the Jews gained no result from their disgraceful compromises and ultimately met with failure and defeat, and the Quran's prediction regarding them came true.

Although the above verses were revealed concerning a specific group, they are certainly not exclusive to them. They encompass all compromising individuals who sacrifice their personality, dignity, and even their faith and beliefs to achieve lowly goals. Such compromisers are far from Allah's mercy in this world and the Hereafter and are often faced with failure.

It is noteworthy that the aforementioned reprehensible spirit still strongly persists in this people. We see that to reach their goals, they do not shy away from any kind of compromise under any circumstances, and for this reason, they have been plagued by failures throughout past history and today.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 3, p. 35. According to some, it is an infinitive used in an adjectival sense and as an intensive form. [^1]: Tafsir al-Tibyan and Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani.

Ayahs

أَمْ لَهُمْ نَصِيبٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُلْكِ فَإِذًا لَّا يُؤْتُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ نَقِيرًا (٥٣)
Do they have a share in sovereignty? If so, they will not give those people [so much as] a speck on a date-stone! (53)
أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ عَلَىٰ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۖ فَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَآ ءَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم مُّلْكًا عَظِيمًا (٥٤)
Do they envy those people for what Allah has given them out of His bounty? We have certainly given the progeny of Abraham the Book and wisdom, and We have given them a great sovereignty. (54)
فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ ءَامَنَ بِهِۦ وَمِنْهُم مَّن صَدَّ عَنْهُ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِجَهَنَّمَ سَعِيرًا (٥٥)
Of them are some who believe in him, and of them are some who deter [others] from him; and hell suffices for a blaze! (55)

Commentary

In the commentary of the previous two verses, it was mentioned that the Jews, in order to attract the attention of the idolaters of Mecca, testified that the polytheism of the Quraysh was better than the monotheism of the Muslims! They even prostrated before the idols themselves! In these verses, it is reminded that their judgment lacks value and credibility for two reasons:

1- From a social standing, they (the Jews) do not possess the status to judge or rule among people, and the people have never delegated the right of governance and judgment to them so that they could undertake such an action (أَمْ لَهُمْ نَصِيبٌ مِنَ الْمُلْكِ).

Furthermore, they never possess the merit for material or spiritual leadership over the people, because the spirit of monopolization has so dominated them that if they were to attain such a position, they would grant no right to anyone else, and would reserve all privileges exclusively for themselves! (فَإِذاً لا يُؤْتُونَ النَّاسَ نَقِيراً) [^1] Therefore, considering that the judgment of the Jews stems from such a mindset—where they always grant the right to themselves or to those who serve their interests—Muslims should never allow themselves to feel concerned by such statements.

2- These types of incorrect judgments stem from their envy toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his household, and for this reason, they are worthless. Due to their oppression, transgression, and ingratitude for blessings, they lost the station of prophethood and governance. Consequently, they do not wish for this divine position to be entrusted to anyone else. Thus, they harbor envy toward the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and his household, who have been encompassed by this divine favor, and through such baseless judgments, they seek to pour water on the flames of their own envy (أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ النَّاسَ عَلى‌ ما آتاهُمُ اللَّهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ‌).

Then He says: Why are you amazed and terrified by the granting of such a position to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the household of Bani Hashim, and why do you envy them, while Allah gave you and the progeny of the House of Abraham the Heavenly Book, wisdom, knowledge, and a vast kingdom (such as the rule of Moses, Solomon, and David)? But unfortunately, you unworthy descendants lost those valuable spiritual and material assets due to your wickedness and hard-heartedness (فَقَدْ آتَيْنا آلَ إِبْراهِيمَ الْكِتابَ وَ الْحِكْمَةَ وَ آتَيْناهُمْ مُلْكاً عَظِيماً).

[^1]: "Naqir" (نقير) is derived from the root "naqr" (نقر), meaning to strike something until it creates a pit or a hole. For this reason, a beak is called "minqar" (منقار). Some say "naqir" refers to the tiny speck or groove found on the back of a date stone, and it is usually an allusion to extremely insignificant matters.

From what we have stated, it becomes clear that the meaning of "the people" (الناس) in the verse أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ النَّاسَ refers to the Prophet of Islam and his household. This is because "nas" (ناس) means a group of people, and applying it to a single person (the Prophet alone) is not permissible unless there is a contextual clue [^1]. Additionally, the phrase "the House of Abraham" (آل إبراهيم) is another clue that "the people" refers to the Prophet of Islam and his household. From the context of comparison, it is understood that it is not surprising if We gave such a position to the household of Bani Hashim, for We also granted all those spiritual and material positions to the House of Abraham due to their merit.

In numerous narrations found in both Sunni and Shi'a sources, it is explicitly stated that "the people" refers to the household of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

It is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding this verse that he said: "Allah placed among the House of Abraham prophets, messengers, and leaders; (then addressing the Jews) how is it that you are willing to confess to that, yet you deny it regarding the House of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله?" [^2] In another narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, when asked about this verse, he said:

"نحن المحسودون" "We are the ones who are envied (by the enemies)." [^3] In the commentary Al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated from Ibn Mundhir and Tabarani from Ibn Abbas that he said regarding this verse: "The 'people' in this verse refers to us and no one else."

Then the Quran says in the following verse: "A group of people at that time believed in the Heavenly Book revealed to the House of Abraham, while others not only did not believe,

[^1]: The word "nas" (ناس) is a collective noun, and the plural pronoun referring back to it in the verse also confirms this meaning.

[^2] and [^3]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 376. A hadith with the same theme is also narrated in Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 5, p. 52.

but also created obstacles in the path of its progress, and the blazing flame of Hell is sufficient for them" (فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ آمَنَ بِهِ وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ صَدَّ عَنْهُ وَ كَفى‌ بِجَهَنَّمَ سَعِيراً).

Similarly, those who disbelieve in this Heavenly Book revealed to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله will meet the same fate.

Key Points Envy and Crimes

"Envy" (Hasad), which in Persian is expressed as "rashk," means the desire for the removal of a blessing from others, whether that blessing reaches the envious person or not. Therefore, the action of the envious person is focused on destruction and the wish for destruction, not necessarily that the asset or blessing must be transferred to him.

Envy is the source of many social disorders, including:

1- The envious person spends all or most of his physical and mental energies—which should be used for advancing social goals—on the path of destroying and ruining what exists. Thus, he destroys both his own existential assets and the assets of society.

2- Envy is the motive behind a portion of the world's crimes. If we examine the primary factors and causes of murders, thefts, transgressions, and the like, we will see that a significant portion of them stems from the factor of envy. Perhaps it is for this reason that it has been likened to a spark of fire that can endanger the existence of the envious person or the society in which he lives.

One scholar says: "Envy and ill-will are among the most dangerous traits, and they must be regarded as the most terrifying enemy of happiness, and one must strive to repel them."

Societies composed of envious and narrow-minded individuals are backward societies, because as we mentioned, the envious person always strives to pull others back, and this is exactly contrary to the spirit of evolution and progress.

3- Beyond all this, envy leaves very undesirable effects on the human body and health. Envious individuals are usually ailing people and are often distressed and ill in terms of their nerves and various bodily systems. Today, it has become an established fact that physical illnesses in many cases have psychological factors, and in modern medicine, detailed discussions are found under the title of "psychosomatic" illnesses dedicated to this category of diseases.

Interestingly, the leaders of Islam have emphasized this subject in their narrations:

In a narration from Ali عليه السلام, we read: "صحة الجسد من قلة الحسد" "The health of the body is from the lack of envy." Elsewhere he says: "العجب لغفلة الحساد عن سلامة الاجساد" "It is strange how the envious are completely heedless of the health of their own bodies." We even read in some hadiths that envy begins with the envious person before it harms the envied one, and gradually kills him!

4- From a spiritual perspective, envy is a sign of a lack of personality, ignorance, narrow-mindedness, and weakness or deficiency of faith. This is because the envious person actually sees himself as too weak to reach the status of the envied one or higher, and thus tries to pull the envied one back. Furthermore, he is practically objecting to the wisdom of Allah, who is the original Giver of these blessings, and has a grievance regarding Allah's granting of blessings to individuals. Therefore, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "الحسد اصله من عمى القلب و الجحود لفضل اللَّه تعالى و هما جناحان للكفر و بالحسد وقع ابن آدم فى حسرة الابد و هلك مهلكا لا ينجو منه ابدا" "Envy and ill-will stem from the darkness of the heart and blindness of the soul, and originate from the denial of Allah's favors to individuals. These two (blindness of heart and objecting to God's giving) are the two wings of disbelief. It was because of envy that the son of Adam fell into eternal regret and perished in a destruction from which he will never escape." [^1] The Holy Quran says: The first murder and killing that occurred on earth was caused by envy. [^2] In Nahj al-Balagha, it is narrated from Ali عليه السلام that he said: "ان الحسد ياكل الايمان كما تاكل النار الحطب" "Indeed, envy gradually consumes faith just as fire gradually consumes wood." [^3] This is because the envious person's suspicion toward Allah and His wisdom and justice gradually increases, and it is this very suspicion that pulls him out of the realm of faith.

The spiritual, material, individual, and social harms of envy are exceptionally vast, and what we have mentioned is, in fact, only a summary of them.

[^1]: Mustadrak al-Wasa'il, Vol. 2, p. 327. [^2]: Surah Al-Ma'idah, Verse 27. [^3]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 86.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا سَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِمْ نَارًا كُلَّمَا نَضِجَتْ جُلُودُهُم بَدَّلْنَـٰهُمْ جُلُودًا غَيْرَهَا لِيَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا (٥٦)
Indeed We shall soon make those who deny Our signs enter a Fire: as often as their skins become scorched, We shall replace them with other skins, so that they may taste the punishment. Indeed Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (56)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۖ لَّهُمْ فِيهَآ أَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ ۖ وَنُدْخِلُهُمْ ظِلًّا ظَلِيلًا (٥٧)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We shall admit them into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them forever. In it, there will be chaste mates for them, and We shall admit them into a deep shade. (57)

Commentary

Following the previous verses, these two verses describe the fate of the believers and the disbelievers. The first verse says: "We shall cast the disbelievers into the Fire, and every time their skins are burnt away, We shall replace them with other skins so that they may fully taste the punishment of the Lord."

(إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِنا سَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِمْ ناراً كُلَّما نَضِجَتْ جُلُودُهُمْ بَدَّلْناهُمْ جُلُوداً غَيْرَها لِيَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ‌) [^1]

[^1]: "نصليهم" is derived from the root "صلى" (salā), meaning to cast into fire, to burn with fire, or to be warmed by fire. "نضجت" is from the root "نضج" (nudj), meaning to be roasted or thoroughly cooked.

The reason for this replacement of skins is apparently because when the skin is completely burnt, the sensation of pain may decrease. However, so that their punishment is not lightened and they fully feel the pain and suffering, new skins grow on their bodies. This is the result of their persistence in trampling upon truth and justice and deviating from Allah's command.

At the end of the verse, He says: "Allah is both All-Mighty and Capable of carrying out such punishments, and He is All-Wise, punishing based on a precise account" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ عَزِيزاً حَكِيماً).
