In the next verse, He promises those who have faith and perform righteous deeds that "they will soon live in gardens of Paradise beneath whose trees rivers flow—an eternal and everlasting life. Furthermore, He grants them purified spouses who are a source of peace for their souls and bodies, and they shall live under the shade of trees which, unlike the fleeting shadows of this world, are perpetual and never reached by hot winds or the sting of cold" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً لَهُمْ فِيها أَزْواجٌ مُطَهَّرَةٌ وَ نُدْخِلُهُمْ ظِلًّا ظَلِيلًا) [^1]

Key Points

One of the noteworthy points derived from comparing these two verses is the vastness of Divine mercy and how His mercy precedes His wrath. This is because in the first verse, the promise of punishment for the disbelievers is mentioned with the word "سوف" (saufa), while the promise of reward for the believers in the second verse is expressed with the prefix "س" (sa) in "سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ‌". As stated in Arabic linguistics,

[^1]: "ظليل" (zalīl) is derived from the root "ظل" (zill), meaning shade, and is used here for emphasis. The phrase "ظل ظليل" literally means "shady shade," which is an allusion to a complete, perpetual, and comfortable shade.

"سوف" is typically used for the distant future, while "س" is used for the near future. Even though we know both verses pertain to the Day of Resurrection, and the punishment of the wicked and the reward of the righteous in that world are at an equal temporal distance from us, this difference in expression is intended to point toward the speed and vastness of Allah's mercy and the distance and limitation of the Lord's anger. This is similar to the expression we read in supplications: "O He whose mercy precedes His wrath" (يا من سبقت رحمته غضبه).

Question

Some might object that the above verses state that when the skin of the wicked burns, We place new skins in its stead so they may taste the Divine punishment, whereas the original skins were the ones that sinned. Is the punishment of new skins consistent with the principle of justice?

Answer

This exact question was asked by "Ibn Abi al-Awja," a famous materialist contemporary of Imam Sadiq عليه السلام. After reciting the above verse, he asked: "What is the sin of the other skins?" (ما ذنب الغير).

The Imam gave him a short yet profound answer, saying: "It is the same, and yet it is different!" (هى هى و هى غيرها).

Ibn Abi al-Awja, knowing that a secret was hidden in this short phrase, said: "Give me an example of this from the matters of this world." The Imam said: "Have you seen that if a man takes a brick and breaks it, then returns it to its mold (and reshapes it), it is the same, and yet it is different?" (أ رايت لو ان رجلا اخذ لبنة فكسرها، ثم ردها فى ملبنها، فهى هى، و هى غيرها).

(The original material is preserved, and only its form has changed) [^1]. From this narration, it is understood that the new skins are formed from the same materials as the previous skins.

Furthermore, it should be noted that reward and punishment are, in reality, connected to the soul and the human faculty of perception; the body is always a medium for transmitting reward and punishment to the human soul.

Ayah

۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَن تُؤَدُّوا۟ ٱلْأَمَـٰنَـٰتِ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهَا وَإِذَا حَكَمْتُم بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ أَن تَحْكُمُوا۟ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ نِعِمَّا يَعِظُكُم بِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ سَمِيعًۢا بَصِيرًا (٥٨)
Indeed Allah commands you to deliver the trusts to their [rightful] owners, and to judge with fairness when you judge between people. Excellent indeed is what Allah advises you. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing. (58)

Circumstances of Revelation

It is narrated in Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and some other Islamic commentaries that this verse was revealed when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله entered the city of Mecca in total victory. He summoned Uthman ibn Talhah, who was the key-bearer of the Kaaba, and took the key from him in order to cleanse the interior of the Kaaba from the presence of idols. After this purpose was achieved, Abbas, the uncle of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, requested that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله hand over the key of the House of Allah to him, so that the position of key-bearer of the House—which was a prominent and lofty station among the Arabs—would be entrusted to him (it seems Abbas wished to utilize the social and political influence of his nephew for his own personal benefit). However, contrary to this request, after purifying the Kaaba from the filth of idols, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله closed the door and returned the key to Uthman ibn Talhah, while reciting this verse: "Indeed, Allah commands you to render trusts to their owners..." (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَنْ تُؤَدُّوا الْأَماناتِ إِلى‌ أَهْلِها ...) [^1]

[^1]: Majalis of al-Shaykh and Ihtijaj of al-Tabarsi.

Commentary

Two Important Islamic Laws

Although this verse, like many others, was revealed regarding a specific incident, it is evident that a general and universal ruling is derived from it. It explicitly states:

"Indeed, Allah commands you to render trusts to their owners." It is clear that "trust" (amanah) has a broad meaning and encompasses every type of material and spiritual asset. According to the explicit text of this verse, every Muslim has the duty not to commit treachery regarding any trust toward anyone (without exception), whether the owner of the trust is a Muslim or a non-Muslim. This is, in fact, one of the articles of the "Declaration of Human Rights in Islam," before which all human beings are equal. It is noteworthy that in the aforementioned circumstances of revelation, the trust was not merely a material one, and the party involved was a polytheist.

In the second part of the verse, reference is made to another important command, which is the issue of "justice in governance."

The verse says that Allah has also commanded you that when you judge or rule among people, you must judge with justice:

[^1]: Some commentators believe that the verse was revealed before the Conquest of Mecca and do not consider the aforementioned circumstances of revelation to be correct; however, whether the circumstances of revelation are correct or not, it does not affect the important law derived from the verse.

(وَ إِذا حَكَمْتُمْ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ أَنْ تُحْكُمُوا بِالْعَدْلِ‌).

Then, to emphasize these two important commands, He says: "Excellent is that which Allah instructs you" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ نِعِمَّا يَعِظُكُمْ بِهِ‌). He emphasizes again, saying: "In any case, Allah is watchful over your deeds; He both hears your words and sees your actions" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ سَمِيعاً بَصِيراً). This law is also a general and universal law, encompassing every type of arbitration and governance, whether in great matters or small. So much so that we read in Islamic hadiths: One day, two young children had each written a line of calligraphy and came to Imam Hasan عليه السلام to judge between them and choose the best handwriting. Ali عليه السلام, who was observing this scene, immediately said to his son:

"O my son! Look carefully at how you judge, for this is indeed a form of judgment, and Allah will question you about it on the Day of Resurrection!" [^1] These two important Islamic laws (safeguarding trusts and justice in governance) are the foundation of a healthy human society, and no society—whether material or divine—can be organized without the implementation of these two principles.

The first principle states: properties, wealth, posts, responsibilities, human capital, cultures, and historical heritages are all divine trusts entrusted to various individuals in society. Everyone is duty-bound to strive in safeguarding these trusts and delivering them to their original owners, and under no circumstances should these trusts be betrayed.

On the other hand, there are always encounters, conflicts, and clashes of interests in societies, which must be resolved through just governance so that any form of discrimination, undue privilege, and oppression is eradicated from society.

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 3, p. 64.

As mentioned above, trust is not limited to the property that people entrust to one another; rather, scholars are also trustees in society who are duty-bound not to conceal truths. Even a person's children are divine trusts; if there is negligence in their education and upbringing, it constitutes a breach of trust. Higher than that, the very existence and being of man and all the powers Allah has given him are the trusts of the Lord, which man is duty-bound to strive to preserve. He must not neglect the preservation of physical health, spiritual health, the abundant energy of youth, and thought and intellect; therefore, he cannot commit suicide or cause harm to himself. It is even understood from some Islamic hadiths that the sciences, secrets, and deposits of the Imamate, which each Imam must hand over to the next Imam, are included in the above verse. [^1] It is noteworthy that in the above verse, the issue of "rendering trusts" is mentioned before "justice." This might be because the issue of justice in judgment is always required in the face of treachery, for the fundamental principle is that all people should be trustworthy; however, if an individual or individuals deviate from this principle, it becomes the turn of justice to acquaint them with their duty.

The Importance of Trust and Justice in Islam

This subject has been emphasized in Islamic sources to an extent rarely seen regarding other rulings. The following short hadiths illustrate this reality:

  1. It is narrated from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام that he said:

"Do not (merely) look at the length of a person's bowing (ruku') and prostration (sujud), for that might be something they have become accustomed to, such that if they were to abandon it, they would feel uneasy. Rather, look at the truthfulness of their speech and the rendering of their trusts." [^1]

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 496.

  1. In another hadith, it is narrated from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "If Ali عليه السلام attained such a high station with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it was because of truthfulness in speech and the rendering of trusts." [^2]

  2. It is also narrated from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام that he said to one of his friends:

"If the one who struck Ali with the sword and killed him were to entrust me with something, or seek my advice, or consult me, and I accepted that from him, I would certainly fulfill the right of the trust." [^3]

  1. In narrations recorded in both Shi'a and Sunni sources from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, this great saying shines:

"The signs of a hypocrite are three: when he speaks, he lies; when he promises, he breaks it; and when he is entrusted, he betrays." [^4]

  1. The Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to Ali عليه السلام: "When two parties of a dispute come before you, observe equality and justice even in your glances at them, and in the amount and manner of the words you speak to them." (Sawwi bayna al-khasmayn fi lahzika wa lafzika) [^5]

[^1], [^2], and [^3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 496.

[^4]: Sahih al-Tirmidhi and al-Nasa'i as quoted in al-Manar; the same meaning appears in Safinat al-Bihar.

[^5]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 3, p. 64.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ وَأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَمْرِ مِنكُمْ ۖ فَإِن تَنَـٰزَعْتُمْ فِى شَىْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَٱلرَّسُولِ إِن كُنتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا (٥٩)
O you who have faith! Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you. And if you dispute concerning anything, refer it to Allah and the Apostle, if you have faith in Allah and the Last Day. That is better and more favourable in outcome. (59)

Commentary

This verse and the following few verses discuss one of the most important Islamic issues, namely the matter of leadership, and identify the true authorities for Muslims in various religious and social matters.

First, it commands the people of faith to obey Allah. It is evident that for a person of faith, all forms of obedience must ultimately lead to obedience to the Lord, and any form of leadership must originate from His Pure Essence and be according to His command. This is because He is the Creator-Sovereign and Owner of the universe, and any sovereignty or ownership must be by His decree: (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ—O you who have believed, obey Allah).

In the next stage, it commands obedience to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), a Prophet who is infallible (Ma'sum) and never speaks out of desire or whim; a Prophet who is God's representative among the people, whose word is God's word, and to whom Allah has granted this station and position. Therefore, obedience to Allah is a requirement of His Essence as Creator and Sovereign, while obedience to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is born of the Lord's command. In other words, Allah is "Obligatory to Obey by Essence" (Wajib al-Ita'ah bidh-Dhat), and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is "Obligatory to Obey by Another" (Wajib al-Ita'ah bil-Ghayr). Perhaps the repetition of "obey" (ati'u) in the verse refers to this very point—the difference between the two types of obedience: (وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ—and obey the Messenger). The third stage commands obedience to the Ulul-Amr (those vested with authority) who arise from the heart of the Islamic society and are the guardians of the people's religion and worldly affairs.

Who are the Ulul-Amr?!

There is much discussion among Islamic commentators regarding the meaning of Ulul-Amr, which can be summarized in several points:

  1. A group of Sunni commentators believe that Ulul-Amr refers to the rulers, governors, and sources of authority in every time and environment, without any exceptions. The result of this view is that Muslims would be duty-bound to follow any government in any form, even if it were a Mongol government.

  2. Other commentators, such as the authors of Tafsir al-Manar and Tafsir Fi Zilal al-Quran, believe that Ulul-Amr refers to the representatives of all classes of society, rulers, governors, scholars, and officials in all affairs of people's lives. However, this is not absolute or unconditional; rather, their obedience is conditional upon it not being contrary to the rulings and regulations of Islam.

  3. According to others, Ulul-Amr refers to spiritual and intellectual leaders, namely the scholars ('Ulama) and thinkers—scholars who are just and possess complete knowledge of the contents of the Book and the Sunnah.

  4. Some Sunni commentators believe this term refers exclusively to the first four Caliphs and does not include anyone else. Consequently, in other eras, the Ulul-Amr would have no external existence.

  5. Other commentators consider Ulul-Amr to mean the Companions (Sahaba) of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله).

  6. Another possibility mentioned in the interpretation of Ulul-Amr is that it refers to the commanders of the Islamic army.

(Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 3, p. 436)

All Shia commentators are unanimous in the view that Ulul-Amr refers to the Infallible Imams (علیهم السلام), to whom the material and spiritual leadership of the Islamic society in all aspects of life has been entrusted by Allah and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). It does not include anyone else. Of course, those appointed by them to a position or who undertake a role in Islamic society must be obeyed under specific conditions, not because they are the Ulul-Amr, but because they are the representatives of the Ulul-Amr.

Now, let us briefly analyze the above interpretations:

There is no doubt that the first interpretation is in no way compatible with the concept of the verse or the spirit of Islamic teachings. It is impossible for the following of any government, unconditionally, to be placed alongside obedience to Allah and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). For this reason, in addition to Shia commentators, great Sunni commentators have also rejected it.

As for the second interpretation, it is also incompatible with the absolute nature of the noble verse, as the verse deems obedience to the Ulul-Amr necessary and obligatory without any stated condition.

The third interpretation—interpreting Ulul-Amr as just scholars who are knowledgeable of the Book and Sunnah—is also incompatible with the absolute nature of the verse. Following scholars has conditions, including that their words must not contradict the Book and Sunnah. Therefore, if they make a mistake (since they are not infallible and do make mistakes) or deviate from the truth for any other reason, obedience to them is not required. However, the verse mandates obedience to the Ulul-Amr absolutely, just like obedience to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). Furthermore, obedience to scholars is regarding rulings they have derived from the Book and Sunnah; thus, it would be nothing other than obedience to Allah and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and would not need separate mention.

The fourth interpretation (restricting it to the first four Caliphs) implies that today there is no instance of Ulul-Amr among Muslims. Moreover, there is no evidence for such a restriction.

The fifth and sixth interpretations—restricting it to the Companions or army commanders—suffer from the same flaw: there is no evidence for such a restriction.

A group of Sunni commentators, such as the famous Egyptian scholar Muhammad Abduh, following some remarks by the renowned commentator Fakhr al-Razi, have attempted to accept the second possibility (that Ulul-Amr are all representatives of various classes of Islamic society, including scholars, rulers, etc.) with several conditions. These include: they must be Muslims (as derived from the word minkum—"from among you"—in the verse), their ruling must not contradict the Book and Sunnah, they must rule by choice and not compulsion, they must rule in accordance with the interests of Muslims, they must speak on matters they have the right to intervene in (not like acts of worship which have fixed regulations in Islam), and there must be no specific text (nass) from the Sharia regarding the matter they are ruling on. Additionally, they must reach a consensus.

Since they believe that the entire Ummah or the totality of its representatives cannot fall into error—in other words, the Ummah as a whole is infallible—the result of these conditions is that obedience to such a ruling would be absolute and unconditional, just like obedience to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). (The result of this argument is the authority of "Consensus" or Ijma'). However, it must be noted that this interpretation also faces several problems:

Firstly, consensus on social issues occurs very rarely. Consequently, there would be a permanent state of indecision and disorder in most affairs of the Muslims. If they wish to accept the majority opinion, the problem arises that the majority is never infallible; therefore, absolute obedience to it is not required.

Secondly, it is established in the principles of jurisprudence (Usul al-Fiqh) that there is no evidence for the infallibility of the "entire Ummah" apart from the presence of an Infallible Imam.

Thirdly, one of the conditions mentioned by the proponents of this interpretation was that their ruling must not contradict the Book and Sunnah. We must see who identifies whether a ruling contradicts the Sunnah or not. It must be the Mujtahids and scholars knowledgeable of the Book and Sunnah. The result of this would be that obedience to the Ulul-Amr is not permissible without the permission of the Mujtahids and scholars; rather, obedience to them would be higher than obedience to the Ulul-Amr, which is inconsistent with the apparent meaning of the noble verse.

It is true that they included scholars among the Ulul-Amr, but in reality, according to this interpretation, scholars and Mujtahids are recognized as supervisors and a higher authority than the representatives of other classes, not an authority on the same level. This is because scholars must supervise the work of others for compliance with the Book and Sunnah, making them the supreme authority, which does not align with the aforementioned interpretation.

Therefore, the above interpretation faces difficulties from several aspects.

The only interpretation that remains free from the previous objections is the seventh one: interpreting Ulul-Amr as the Infallible Leaders and Imams (علیهم السلام). This interpretation is perfectly consistent with the absolute obligation of obedience derived from the verse. This is because the station of "Infallibility" ('Ismah) protects the Imam from any error, mistake, or sin. Thus, his command, like that of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), is obligatory to obey without any condition, and it is fitting that it be placed alongside obedience to Him, even being conjoined to "the Messenger" without repeating the word "obey" (ati'u).

It is noteworthy that some famous Sunni scholars, including the renowned commentator Fakhr al-Razi, admitted this truth at the beginning of his discussion under this verse, saying:

"One whose obedience Allah makes absolutely and unquestionably mandatory must be infallible. For if he were not protected from error, then at the moment he commits a mistake, Allah would have made his obedience mandatory, thereby requiring the following of an error. This would create a contradiction in Divine command, because on one hand, performing that act is forbidden, and on the other hand, following the Ulul-Amr is mandatory. This leads to the convergence of a 'command' and a 'prohibition' regarding the same act.

Therefore, we see on one hand that Allah has made obedience to the command of the Ulul-Amr mandatory without any condition, and on the other hand, if the Ulul-Amr were not protected from error, such a command would not be correct. From this premise, we conclude that the Ulul-Amr referred to in the above verse must necessarily be infallible." [^1] Fakhr al-Razi then continues that this infallible is either the entire Ummah or some members of the Islamic Ummah. The second possibility is not acceptable [to him] because we must know this "some" and have access to them, which is not the case. Since this possibility is eliminated, only the first remains: that the infallible is the entire Ummah. This itself is evidence that the consensus and agreement of the Ummah is a proof (hujjah) and acceptable, and is considered among the valid evidences." ^2

As we see, although Fakhr al-Razi is known for raising objections in various scholarly matters, he has accepted the verse's indication that the Ulul-Amr must be infallible individuals. However, because he was not acquainted with the school of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) and the Imams and leaders of this school, he ignored the possibility that the Ulul-Amr could be specific individuals from the Ummah. He was thus forced to interpret Ulul-Amr as the entire Ummah (or the representatives of all classes of Muslims), whereas this possibility is unacceptable. As we stated, the Ulul-Amr must be the leader of the Islamic society, and the Islamic government and the resolution of the Muslims' problems must be carried out by him. We know that collective governance by everyone, or even their representatives through unanimous agreement, is practically impossible. In the various social, political, cultural, moral, and economic issues facing Muslims, obtaining the unanimous agreement of the entire Ummah or its representatives is often impossible, and following the majority is not considered following the Ulul-Amr. Therefore, the consequence of Fakhr al-Razi's words and those contemporary scholars who followed his view is that, in practice, obedience to the Ulul-Amr would be suspended or become an extremely rare and exceptional matter.

From the sum of the above statements, we conclude that the noble verse only proves the leadership of the Infallible Leaders who constitute a group within the Ummah. (Reflect on this).

Answer to Several Questions

Here, some objections have been raised against the above interpretation which must be addressed for the sake of impartiality in the discussion:

  1. If Ulul-Amr refers to the Infallible Imams, it is inconsistent with the word Ulul which is plural, because there is only one Infallible Imam at any given time.

The answer to this question is that although there is only one Infallible Imam at any one time, across all times they constitute multiple individuals. We know the verse does not only determine the duty of the people of one specific time.

  1. According to this meaning, the Ulul-Amr did not exist during the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله); in that case, how could obedience to them be commanded?

The answer to this question is also clear from the above: the verse is not restricted to a specific time and clarifies the duty of Muslims in all ages and centuries. In other words, we can say that the Ulul-Amr during the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself. This is because the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) held two offices: one was the office of "Messengership" (Risalah), mentioned in the verse as (أَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ—obey the Messenger), and the other was the office of "Leadership" and governance of the Islamic Ummah, which the Quran refers to as Ulul-Amr. Therefore, the Infallible Leader during the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself, who held this office in addition to the office of Messengership and conveying Islamic rulings. Perhaps the non-repetition of "obey" (ati'u) between "the Messenger" and "those vested with authority" is not without indication of this meaning. In other words, the office of "Messengership" and the office of "Ulul-Amr-ship" are two different offices that were combined in the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) but are separated in the Imam, who holds only the second office.

  1. If Ulul-Amr refers to the Infallible Imams and leaders, then why does the end of the verse, which describes the issue of dispute and disagreement among Muslims, say: (فَإِنْ تَنَازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى اللَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ ذَلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا—And if you disagree over anything, refer it to Allah and the Messenger, if you should believe in Allah and the Last Day. That is the best [way] and best in result)? As we see, no mention is made of the Ulul-Amr here, and the reference for resolving disputes is introduced only as Allah (the Book of Allah, the Quran) and the Prophet (the Sunnah).

In response to this objection, it must be said: Firstly, this objection is not exclusive to the interpretation of Shia scholars; rather, with a little reflection, it applies to other interpretations as well. Secondly, there is no doubt that "dispute and disagreement" in the above sentence refers to disagreement regarding rulings (ahkam), not issues related to the details of governance and leadership of the Muslims. In those matters, one must certainly obey the Ulul-Amr (as explicitly stated in the first part of the verse). Therefore, it refers to disagreement in the general rulings and laws of Islam, the legislation of which belongs to Allah and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). We know that the Imam is only the executor of the rulings; he neither legislates nor abrogates laws, but always acts in the path of implementing Allah's rulings and the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) Sunnah. Hence, we read in the traditions of the Ahl al-Bayt (علیهم السلام): "If a statement is narrated from us that contradicts the Book of Allah and the word of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), never accept it; it is impossible for us to say anything contrary to the Book of Allah and the Sunnah of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)." Thus, the primary reference for resolving people's disputes regarding Islamic rulings and laws is Allah and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to whom revelation is sent. If the Infallible Imams explain a ruling, that too is not from themselves but from the Book of Allah or the knowledge that reached them from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). In this way, the reason for not mentioning the Ulul-Amr alongside the references for resolving disputes in rulings becomes clear. [^1]

Testimony of the Hadiths

In Islamic sources, traditions have also been recorded that confirm the interpretation of Ulul-Amr as the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt, including:

  1. The famous Islamic commentator Abu Hayyan al-Andalusi al-Maghribi (d. 756 AH) writes in the commentary al-Bahr al-Muhit that this verse was revealed concerning Ali (عليه السلام) and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام). [^1]

  2. The Sunni scholar Abu Bakr ibn Mu'min al-Shirazi, in the treatise al-I'tiqad (as narrated in Manaqib Kashi), quotes from Ibn Abbas that the above verse was revealed concerning Ali (عليه السلام) when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) left him in Medina (during the Battle of Tabuk). Ali (عليه السلام) said: "O Prophet! Do you leave me in the city like women and children?" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) replied:

"Are you not pleased to be to me as Aaron was to Moses, when he said: 'Take my place among my people and act righteously'? Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed: 'And those vested with authority from among you'." ^2

  1. Sheikh Sulayman al-Hanafi al-Qunduzi, a well-known Sunni scholar, narrates in the book Yanabi' al-Mawaddah from the book al-Manaqib from Sulaym ibn Qays al-Hilali that one day a man came to Ali (عليه السلام) and asked: "What is the minimum thing by which a person becomes one of the believers? And what is the minimum thing by which one becomes one of the disbelievers or the straying?" The Imam replied: "As for the minimum thing by which a person enters the ranks of the straying, it is that he does not recognize the Proof (Hujjah) of God, His representative, and His witness, whose obedience and guardianship (wilayah) are mandatory." The man said: "O Commander of the Faithful, identify them for me." Ali (عليه السلام) said: "They are those whom Allah has placed alongside Himself and His Prophet, saying: 'O you who have believed, obey Allah and obey the Messenger and those vested with authority from among you'."

The man said: "May I be sacrificed for you, please explain more clearly." Ali (عليه السلام) said: "They are those whom the Messenger of Allah mentioned on various occasions and in the sermon on the last day of his life, saying:

'I have left among you two things; you will never go astray after me if you hold fast to them: the Book of Allah and my progeny, the people of my house (Ahl al-Bayti).'" [^1]

  1. The same scholar also writes in Yanabi' al-Mawaddah that the author of al-Manaqib narrated from the commentary of Mujahid that this verse was revealed concerning Ali (عليه السلام). ^2

  2. Numerous narrations are recorded in Shia sources such as al-Kafi, Tafsir al-Ayyashi, the books of al-Saduq, and others, all of which testify that Ulul-Amr refers to the Infallible Imams, and in some of them, the names of the Imams are explicitly mentioned one by one. [^3]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 10, p. 144, Egypt edition, 1357.

[^1]: If we see in verse 83 of this same Surah that for resolving some problems, reference is made to the Ulul-Amr, it does not mean disagreement in the general rulings and laws of Sharia, but rather, as will come in the commentary of that verse, it concerns matters related to the method of implementing rulings. More details on this subject will be read, God willing, under that verse. [^1]: al-Bahr al-Muhit, Vol. 3, Egypt edition, p. 278. ^2: Ihqaq al-Haq, Vol. 3, p. 425. [^1]: Yanabi' al-Mawaddah, p. 117. ^2: Yanabi' al-Mawaddah, p. 114. [^3]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, pp. 381-387.

Ayah

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَزْعُمُونَ أَنَّهُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلِكَ يُرِيدُونَ أَن يَتَحَاكَمُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلطَّـٰغُوتِ وَقَدْ أُمِرُوٓا۟ أَن يَكْفُرُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَيُرِيدُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَن يُضِلَّهُمْ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا (٦٠)
Have you not regarded those who claim that they believe in what has been sent down to you and what was sent down before you? They desire to seek the judgment of fake deities, though they were commanded to reject them, and Satan desires to lead them astray into far error. (60)

Occasion of Revelation

A Jew from Medina had a dispute with a Muslim hypocrite. They decided to choose someone as an arbitrator between them. Because the Jewish man was confident in the justice and impartiality of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, he said: "I agree to the arbitration of your Prophet." However, the hypocrite chose one of the Jewish leaders named "Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf," because he knew he could sway his opinion in his favor with a gift. In this way, he opposed the arbitration of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. The noble verse was revealed and severely rebuked such individuals [^1]. Some commentators have also narrated other occasions of revelation for this verse, indicating that some new Muslims, following the customs of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), would take their disputes to Jewish scholars or soothsayers at the beginning of Islam. The above verse was revealed and strictly forbade them from doing so [^2].

Commentary

The Rule of Taghut

The above verse is, in fact, a complement to the previous verse. While the previous verse invited the believers to obey the commands of Allah, the Prophet, and the Ulul-Amr (those vested with authority), and to seek judgment from the Book and the Sunnah, this verse forbids obedience to, following of, and seeking judgment from the Taghut.

As we have previously mentioned, "Taghut" is derived from the root tughyan (transgression). This word, along with all its derivatives, means rebellion and breaking limits and constraints, or anything that serves as a means for tyranny or rebellion. Therefore, those who judge falsely are "Taghut," because they have broken the divine boundaries and the limits of truth and justice. In a hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, he said:

الطاغوت كل من يتحاكم اليه من يحكم بغير الحق‌

Meaning: "The Taghut is anyone who judges by other than the truth and whom people seek for arbitration."

The above verse reproaches those Muslims who went to [unjust] rulers for judgment, saying: "O Prophet! Do you not see those who claim to be Muslims and say they believe in all the heavenly books revealed to you and the previous prophets, yet they take their disputes to the Taghut for judgment, while they have been commanded never to obey the command of the Taghut?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ يَزْعُمُونَ أَنَّهُمْ آمَنُوا بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَ ما أُنْزِلَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يَتَحاكَمُوا إِلَى الطَّاغُوتِ وَ قَدْ أُمِرُوا أَنْ يَكْفُرُوا بِهِ).

Then the Quran adds: "Referring to the Taghut is a satanic trap that intends to cast humans away from the straight path into far-reaching deviations" (وَ يُرِيدُ الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ يُضِلَّهُمْ ضَلالًا بَعِيداً).

It goes without saying that this verse, like other verses of the Quran, expresses a general and eternal ruling for all Muslims throughout all ages and centuries. It warns them that referring to false rulers and seeking judgment from the Taghut is inconsistent with faith in Allah and the heavenly books. Furthermore, it flings man from the path of truth into bypasses whose distance from the truth is very great. The corruptions of such judgments in disrupting the social structure of humanity are hidden from no one, and it is considered one of the factors for the regression of societies.

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan—the above occasion of revelation has been narrated by most commentators. [^2]: Tafsir al-Manar, Volume 5, Page 222.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ وَإِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ رَأَيْتَ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنكَ صُدُودًا (٦١)
When they are told, ‘Come to what Allah has sent down and [come] to the Apostle,’ you see the hypocrites keep away from you aversely. (61)
فَكَيْفَ إِذَآ أَصَـٰبَتْهُم مُّصِيبَةٌۢ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ ثُمَّ جَآءُوكَ يَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَآ إِلَّآ إِحْسَـٰنًا وَتَوْفِيقًا (٦٢)
But how will it be when an affliction visits them because of what their hands have sent ahead? Then they will come to you, swearing by Allah, ‘We desired nothing but to do good and to bring about comity.’ (62)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُ ٱللَّهُ مَا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَعِظْهُمْ وَقُل لَّهُمْ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ قَوْلًۢا بَلِيغًا (٦٣)
They are the ones whom Allah knows as to what is in their hearts. So let them alone, and advise them, and speak to them concerning themselves far-reaching words. (63)

Commentary

The Consequence of the Judgment of Taghut

Following the severe prohibition against referring to Taghut (false deities or illegitimate authorities) and unjust judges mentioned in the previous verse, these three verses examine the consequences of such judgments and the pretexts to which the hypocrites clung to justify their actions.

In the first verse, He says: "These pseudo-Muslims not only seek out the Taghut for judgment, but when they are reminded to come toward the command of Allah and accept the judgment of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they exhibit resistance and turn away, refusing to accept your invitation, and they persist in this act with stubbornness."

In reality, the Quran states that their referral to the Taghut was not a fleeting mistake that could be corrected with a simple reminder; rather, their resistance and persistence in this act demonstrate their spirit of hypocrisy and weakness of faith. Otherwise, they would have been awakened by the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله invitation and confessed to their mistake: (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا إِلى‌ ما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ وَ إِلَى الرَّسُولِ رَأَيْتَ الْمُنافِقِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْكَ صُدُوداً).

In the next verse, it expresses the reality that these same hypocrites, when they are afflicted by a calamity as a result of their own actions and find themselves in a dead-end, come to you out of necessity: (فَكَيْفَ إِذا أَصابَتْهُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ ثُمَّ جاؤُكَ‌ ...).

At this point, they swear oaths that our intention and goal in taking the dispute to others was nothing but doing good and creating harmony between the two parties: (يَحْلِفُونَ بِاللَّهِ إِنْ أَرَدْنا إِلَّا إِحْساناً وَ تَوْفِيقاً).

Here, two points must be noted: First, what is meant by the "calamity" that afflicts them? It is not unlikely that it refers to the disorders, miseries, and misfortunes that befall them due to the judgment of the Taghut. For there is no doubt that if, through the judgment of unqualified and oppressive individuals, a momentary benefit is gained by one of the parties, it will not be long before the continuation of such judgments leads to the spread of injustice, corruption, chaos, and the disintegration of the social structure. Therefore, such individuals will soon see the results of their actions and regret what they have done.

Some commentators have suggested that "calamity" refers to the public disgrace of the hypocrites among the people, or the afflictions that strike them by Allah's command (such as unexpected disasters and defeats).

The second point is whether the hypocrites' use of the word "goodness" (ihsan) referred to doing good to the parties of the dispute, or toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله? It is possible they meant both. They had fabricated ridiculous excuses for referring the judgment to outsiders, including the claim that bringing a dispute before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was beneath his dignity! Because the parties of a dispute often engage in shouting and commotion, and this is not compatible with the status of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله!

Furthermore, judgment and arbitration always end to the detriment of one party and naturally create enemies; seemingly, with such excuses, they wanted to exonerate themselves by claiming their intention was to serve the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the disputing parties.

Or perhaps they claimed that their intention was not judgment at all, but rather their aim was reconciliation and creating harmony and agreement between the conflicting parties.

However, in the third verse, Allah pulls the mask off their faces and invalidates such false pretenses, saying: "These are the ones whose inner secrets of their hearts Allah knows" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُ اللَّهُ ما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ‌).

Yet, at the same time, He commands His Prophet to overlook their punishment (فاعرض عنهم). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله always dealt with the hypocrites with as much leniency as possible because of their outward profession of Islam, as he was commanded to judge by outward appearances and did not punish them except in exceptional cases. This was because they were ostensibly within the ranks of the Muslims, and their punishment might have been interpreted as a form of personal settlement of accounts.

Then He commands: admonish them, give them counsel, and speak to them with an eloquent speech that penetrates their hearts and souls, and warn them of the consequences of their actions: (وَ عِظْهُمْ وَ قُلْ لَهُمْ فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ قَوْلًا بَلِيغاً).

Ayah

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطَاعَ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ إِذ ظَّلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ جَآءُوكَ فَٱسْتَغْفَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱسْتَغْفَرَ لَهُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ لَوَجَدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ تَوَّابًا رَّحِيمًا (٦٤)
We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave. Had they, when they wronged themselves, come to you and pleaded to Allah for forgiveness, and the Apostle had pleaded for them [to Allah]for forgiveness, they would have surely found Allah all-clement, all-merciful. (64)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, the Quran severely condemned referring to unjust judges for arbitration. In this verse, as an emphasis on that point, it states: All the Prophets We sent were for the purpose of being obeyed by Allah’s command, and no opposition should be shown toward them (وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطَاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ). This is because they were both the Messengers of Allah and the heads of the divine government; therefore, the people were duty-bound to follow them both in terms of the explanation of Allah’s laws and the manner of their implementation, and they should not suffice with a mere claim of faith.

From this sentence, it is clearly understood that the purpose of sending Prophets was the obedience and submission of all people. Now, if some people misused their freedom and did not obey, the fault lies with them. Therefore, the above verse refutes the belief of the determinists (Jabriyun) who claim that some people are destined to obey from the beginning, while others are condemned to sin and opposition.

Furthermore, from the expression "by Allah’s leave" (بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ), it is understood that whatever the divine Prophets possess is from Allah’s side. In other words, the obligation to obey them is not intrinsic (bi-dhat), but rather it is by the command of the Lord and from His side.

Then, in the continuation of the verse, the way of return is opened for the sinners and those who referred to the Taghut (false deities/unjust rulers), or committed a sin in any way, stating:

"And if, when they had wronged themselves, they had but come to you and asked forgiveness of Allah, and the Messenger had asked forgiveness for them, they would have found Allah Relenting and Merciful" (وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ إِذْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ جَاءُوكَ فَاسْتَغْفَرَوا اللَّهَ وَاسْتَغْفَرَ لَهُمُ الرَّسُولُ لَوَجَدُوا اللَّهَ تَوَّابًا رَحِيمًا).

It is noteworthy that instead of saying "they disobeyed Allah and referred to unjust judges," the Quran says: "when they had wronged themselves" (إِذْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ). This is an indication that the benefit of obeying the command of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله returns to you yourselves, and opposing it is, in reality, a type of self-wrongdoing; for it disrupts your material life and causes your spiritual regression.

From this verse, the answer to those who consider seeking intercession (Tawassul) from the Prophet or the Imam as a form of polytheism (Shirk) becomes clear. This verse explicitly states that coming to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and making him an intercessor at the threshold of Allah, and his mediation and seeking of forgiveness for the sinners, is effective and leads to the acceptance of repentance and divine mercy.

If mediation, prayer, seeking forgiveness, and requesting intercession from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله were polytheism, how could the Quran give such an instruction to the sinners? However, the wrongdoers must first repent themselves and turn back from the path of error, then utilize the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله seeking of forgiveness for the acceptance of their repentance.

It is obvious that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is not the forgiver of sins; he can only seek forgiveness from Allah. This verse is a crushing response to those who deny this kind of mediation (reflect deeply). It is interesting that the Quran does not say: "you seek forgiveness for them," but rather says "the Messenger" (الرَّسُولُ) should seek forgiveness for them. This expression likely hints that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله should use his status and position to seek forgiveness for the repentant wrongdoers.

This meaning (the effectiveness of the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله seeking of forgiveness for the believers) has appeared in other verses of the Quran as well, such as verse 19 of Surah Muhammad, verse 5 of Surah Al-Munafiqun, and verse 114 of Surah At-Tawbah, which refers to Ibrahim’s seeking of forgiveness for his father (uncle). There are also other verses that forbid seeking forgiveness for the polytheists, the implication of which is that seeking forgiveness for the believers is permissible. Furthermore, it is understood from some verses that the angels seek forgiveness before Allah for a group of sinful believers (Surah Ghafir, verse 7 and Surah Ash-Shura, verse 5).

In summary, many verses of the Holy Quran narrate this meaning: that Prophets, angels, or pure-hearted believers can seek forgiveness for some wrongdoers, and their seeking of forgiveness is effective in the presence of Allah. This is itself one of the meanings of the intercession (Shafa'ah) of the Prophet, the angels, or the pure-hearted believers for the wrongdoers. However, as we have said, such intercession requires the existence of a foundation, merit, and readiness within the wrongdoers themselves.

It is astonishing that from the words of some commentators, it appears they have tried to interpret the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله seeking of forgiveness in the above verse as being related to the violation of the Prophet’s own personal rights. They claim that because they had wronged the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself, it was necessary to obtain his satisfaction so that Allah would overlook their error! But it is clear that referring a judgment to someone other than the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is not a wrong against the person of the Prophet, but rather opposition to his specific office, or in other words, opposition to Allah’s command. Even assuming it were a wrong against the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the Quran has not emphasized that; rather, the Quran’s emphasis is on the fact that they acted contrary to Allah’s command.

Additionally, if we wrong someone, their satisfaction is sufficient; what need is there for them to seek forgiveness for us in the presence of Allah? Above all, even if we were to accept such an interpretation for the above verse, what would we say about all those other verses that consider the seeking of forgiveness by Prophets, angels, and believers for wrongdoers to be effective? Was there a matter of personal rights involved in those cases as well?!

Ayah

فَلَا وَرَبِّكَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّىٰ يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيمَا شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ حَرَجًا مِّمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا۟ تَسْلِيمًا (٦٥)
But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge in their disputes, then do not find within their hearts any dissent to your verdict and submit in full submission. (65)

Occasion of Revelation

"Zubayr ibn al-Awwam," who was among the Muhajirun (Emigrants), had a dispute with one of the Ansar (the Muslims of Medina) regarding the irrigation of their palm groves, which were situated adjacent to one another. Both came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to resolve the dispute. Since Zubayr's orchard was located on the upper part of the stream and the Ansari's orchard was on the lower part, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله commanded Zubayr to irrigate his gardens first and then the Ansari Muslim (this was in accordance with the established custom for adjacent gardens). However, this Ansari man, who was a Muslim only in appearance, became upset by the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله just ruling and said: "Was this judgment made because Zubayr is your cousin?!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله became so deeply disturbed by this statement that the color of His face changed. At this moment, the above verse was revealed, serving as a warning to the Muslims.

In some Islamic commentaries, other occasions of revelation have been mentioned that bear more or less similarity to the one mentioned above (Tafsir al-Tibyan, Tabarsi, and al-Manar).

Commentary

Submission to the Truth

Although a specific occasion of revelation was cited above for this verse, as we have stated many times, specific occasions of revelation never contradict the generality of a verse's meaning. For this reason, this verse can also serve as a complement to the discussion in the preceding verses.

In this verse, Allah swears an oath that individuals will only possess true faith if they seek the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله judgment in their disputes and do not refer to strangers (فَلا وَ رَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيما شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ) [^1]. He then states: "Not only must they bring their judgment to You, but when You issue a ruling among them—whether it be in their favor or to their detriment—they must not only refrain from protesting but also feel no discomfort in their hearts and be completely submissive" (ثُمَّ لا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَرَجاً مِمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَ يُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيماً).

Although internal discomfort regarding judgments that may happen to be against one's interests is often involuntary, through moral training, cultivating a spirit of submission to truth and justice, and recognizing the true status of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, a state arises within a person where they will never feel distressed by the judgment of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, nor even by the scholars who are His successors. In any case, true Muslims are duty-bound to cultivate the spirit of submission to the truth within themselves.

In the above verse, the signs of true and deep-rooted faith are expressed in three stages:

  1. In all cases of dispute, whether large or small, they must refer to the judgment and arbitration of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, which stems from Divine law, and not to the Taghut (tyrants) and false judges.

  2. They must never feel any discomfort, even in their hearts, regarding the judgments and commands of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—which are the commands of Allah—and they must not be cynical toward His rulings and decrees.

  3. In the stage of action, they must implement it precisely and be completely submissive to the truth.

It is clear that accepting a school of thought and its commands in cases where they result in one's benefit is not evidence of faith in that school. Rather, it is when a ruling is apparently to one's detriment but is in reality according to truth and justice, and it is accepted, that it becomes a sign of faith.

In a hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام in the book al-Kafi regarding the interpretation of this verse, we read: "If a people worship Allah, establish prayer, pay the zakat, observe the fast of Ramadan, and perform the Hajj, yet they look with suspicion upon the actions performed by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or say: 'If only He had not done such-and-such a thing,' they are not in reality true believers." Then the Imam عليه السلام recited the above verse and said: "It is incumbent upon you to always be submissive before Allah and the truth."

From the above verse, two important points are also derived:

  1. The verse is one of the proofs of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله infallibility ('Ismah), because the command for absolute submission in word and deed to all the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله commands, and even internal submission of the heart to Him, is a clear sign that He neither errs in His decrees, commands, and judgments, nor does He intentionally say anything contrary to the truth. He is protected from error and protected from sin.

  2. The above verse negates any form of Ijtihad (independent reasoning) in the presence of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله text (Nass) and the expression of opinion in cases where an explicit ruling has arrived from Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Therefore, if we see in Islamic history that sometimes certain individuals practiced Ijtihad or expressed opinions against the command of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—for example, saying "the Prophet said this, but we say that"—we must accept that their action was in direct violation of the above verse.

[^1]: "Shajar" (شجر) is originally derived from the root "shajar" (on the weight of qamar), meaning "tree." Since in a quarrel or dispute, a type of confusion and entanglement appears similar to the entanglement of tree branches with one another, it has come to mean dispute and conflict, and it is used in this sense in the above verse.

Ayahs

وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ أَنِ ٱقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ أَوِ ٱخْرُجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِكُم مَّا فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِّنْهُمْ ۖ وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ فَعَلُوا۟ مَا يُوعَظُونَ بِهِۦ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ وَأَشَدَّ تَثْبِيتًا (٦٦)
Had We prescribed for them, [commanding]: ‘Slay [the guilty among] your folks or leave your habitations,’ they would not have done it except a few of them. And if they had done as they were advised, it would have been better for them and stronger in confirming [their faith]. (66)
وَإِذًا لَّـَٔاتَيْنَـٰهُم مِّن لَّدُنَّآ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (٦٧)
Then We would surely have given them from Us a great reward, (67)
وَلَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمْ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا (٦٨)
and We would have guided them to a straight path. (68)

Commentary

To complete the previous discussion regarding those who sometimes felt uneasy about the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله just rulings, here He refers to some of the grueling obligations of previous nations and says:

"We did not place a heavy and difficult burden upon their shoulders. If We had given them such heavy and difficult commands as We did to some previous nations (such as the Jews, who, after falling into idolatry and calf-worship, were commanded to kill one another as an atonement for that great sin, or to leave their beloved homeland), how could they have endured its performance? These people, who are not submissive regarding the irrigation of a palm grove and the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله judgment concerning it, how can they pass other trials?" Certainly, if We had given them such a command to kill one another or to leave their homeland, only a few of them would have carried it out.

(وَ لَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنا عَلَيْهِمْ أَنِ اقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ أَوِ اخْرُجُوا مِنْ دِيارِكُمْ ما فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِنْهُمْ‌) "And if We had decreed upon them, 'Kill yourselves' or 'Leave your homes,' they would not have done it, except for a few of them."

According to some commentators, the matter of "submitting to being killed" and "leaving one's homeland" are similar in several respects; for the body is the homeland of the human soul, just as the homeland is like the human body. Both leaving the homeland of the body is a difficult task, and so is renouncing the homeland that is a person's birthplace and residence.

Then He says: "If they would accept the counsels of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it would be both to their benefit and a cause for strengthening their faith," (وَ لَوْ أَنَّهُمْ فَعَلُوا ما يُوعَظُونَ بِهِ لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ وَ أَشَدَّ تَثْبِيتاً).

It is noteworthy that in this verse, Divine commands and laws are referred to as "exhortation" (mou'izah) and "counsel," indicating that the aforementioned laws are not something that benefits the Commander (Allah), but are in fact counsels that are for your own benefit. Therefore, He immediately states that obedience to these is both to your advantage and leads to the strengthening of your faith.

One should also note that the end of the verse says: "The more a person walks the path of obedience to Allah's command, the more his stability and steadfastness increase." In reality, obeying the command is a type of spiritual exercise for man, the continuation and repetition of which—like physical exercises—creates more strength, power, stability, and firmness day by day. Gradually, man reaches a point where no power can overcome the strength of his faith or deceive him.

In the following verse, the third benefit of submission and obedience to Allah is stated:

"At that time (in addition to what has been said), We shall also grant them a great reward" (وَ إِذاً لَآتَيْناهُمْ مِنْ لَدُنَّا أَجْراً عَظِيماً).

And in the final verse of the above passages, He points to the fourth result, saying:

"We shall guide them to a straight path" (وَ لَهَدَيْناهُمْ صِراطاً مُسْتَقِيماً).

It is clear that the meaning of this guidance is not guidance to the foundation of the religion and creed, but rather new graces granted by Allah in the form of secondary guidance and as a reward to such worthy individuals. This is similar to what is mentioned in verse 17 of Surah Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله: وَ الَّذِينَ اهْتَدَوْا زادَهُمْ هُدىً: "And those who are guided, He increases them in guidance."

It is narrated that when the verse "And if We had decreed upon them, 'Kill yourselves'..." was revealed, one of the faithful Muslims said: "By Allah, if such heavy commands were given to us by Allah, we would certainly have performed them; but thanks be to Allah that we have been exempted from such a command." This statement reached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and he said:

ان من امتى لرجالا الايمان اثبت فى قلوبهم من الجبال الرواسى: "Indeed, among my Ummah are men whose faith in their hearts is firmer than the anchored mountains!" [1]

[1]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 2, p. 428.

Ayahs

وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلرَّسُولَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ وَٱلصِّدِّيقِينَ وَٱلشُّهَدَآءِ وَٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ ۚ وَحَسُنَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ رَفِيقًا (٦٩)
Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle—they are with those whom Allah has blessed, including the prophets and the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous, and excellent companions are they! (69)
ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَضْلُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ عَلِيمًا (٧٠)
That is the grace of Allah, and Allah suffices as knower [of His creatures]. (70)

Occasion of Revelation

One of the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) named "Thawban," who held an intense love and affection for him, came to his presence one day in a state of great distress. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) asked him the reason for his sadness. He replied: "Whenever I am away from you and do not see you, I become grieved. Today, I was immersed in the thought that on the Day of Resurrection, if I am among the people of Paradise, I will certainly not be in your rank and position; therefore, I will never see you. And if I am not among the people of Paradise, then my fate is clear. Thus, in either case, I will be deprived of the honor of your presence. With this thought, how can I not be depressed?!" The above verses were revealed, giving glad tidings to such individuals that those who obey the Lord will be companions of the prophets and the chosen ones of God in Paradise. Then the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "By Allah, the faith of a Muslim is not complete unless he loves me more than himself, his father, his mother, and all his relatives, and is submissive to my word."

Commentary

Heavenly Companions

In this verse, another of the honors for those who obey the command of Allah and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is mentioned. In fact, it completes the privileges mentioned in the previous verses, which is companionship with those upon whom Allah has perfected His blessing: (وَ مَنْ يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَ الرَّسُولَ فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ‌ ...) "And whoever obeys Allah and the Messenger—those will be with the ones upon whom Allah has bestowed favor..." And as stated in Surah Al-Fatihah, those who are included in this blessing always walk upon the Straight Path and do not have the slightest deviation or error.

Then, in explaining this sentence and identifying those upon whom Allah has completed His blessing, He refers to four groups who are, in fact, the four pillars of this subject:

1- "The Prophets" (النَّبِيِّينَ) and the special messengers of the Lord who take the first step for the guidance and leadership of the people and the invitation to the Straight Path. 2- "The Truthful" (الصِّدِّيقِينَ), those who speak the truth and prove the veracity of their words through their actions and deeds, showing that they do not merely claim faith but truly believe in the Divine commands.

From this expression, it becomes clear that after the station of Prophethood, there is no station higher than the station of truthfulness and sincerity—not only truthfulness in speech but truthfulness in action and conduct, which also includes trustworthiness and sincerity. For "trustworthiness" is the same as honesty in action, just as "truthfulness" is trustworthiness in speech. Conversely, there is no ugly trait worse than lying, hypocrisy, and betrayal in word and deed after disbelief. (It should be noted that Siddiq is an intensive form and refers to someone who is truth and integrity from head to toe).

In some narrations, "Siddiq" has been interpreted as Ali (عليه السلام) and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام). As we have said many times, such interpretations are expressions of the most prominent and supreme examples of the verses and do not imply exclusivity.

3- "The Martyrs" (الشُّهَداءِ) and those killed in the way of the pure Divine goal and creed, or prominent individuals who are witnesses to the deeds of humanity on the Day of Resurrection. [^1] 4- "The Righteous" (الصَّالِحِينَ) and worthy, prominent individuals who have attained high stations by performing positive, constructive, and useful deeds and by following the commands of the prophets.

For this reason, in our narrations, "the righteous" has been interpreted as the elite companions of the Imams, and this, like what was mentioned regarding "the truthful," is of the nature of identifying the most distinguished individuals.

A point that must be reminded here is that the mention of these four stages may be an allusion to the fact that to build a healthy and worthy human society, first the rightful leaders and prophets must enter the field. Following them, the truthful callers—whose words and actions are in harmony—must spread their goals everywhere. Following this period of intellectual construction, a group must stand against the corrupt elements and those who are obstacles in the path of truth, making sacrifices and becoming martyrs. The product of these efforts and struggles is the emergence of "the righteous" and a "pure and worthy society." It is clear that the righteous will also perform these three duties—leading, preaching, and sacrificing—to keep the torch of truth lit for future generations.

Furthermore, from the above verses, this reality is clearly derived: that the subject of good associates and valuable companions is so important that even in the Hereafter, to complete the blessings of Paradise, this great blessing is bestowed upon the "obedient." In addition to all other honors, they will have companions such as the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous.

It perhaps needs no mention that the companionship of the obedient with these four groups does not mean they are equal in rank and degree in every respect. Rather, while associating with one another, each has a specific share (according to their station) of the bounties and favors of Allah. Just as, for example, the trees, flowers, and plants of a garden, while being together and benefiting from the sunlight and rain, do not have identical benefits or values.

Then, in the next verse, to state the importance of this great privilege (companionship with the chosen ones), He says: "This is a bounty from Allah, and He is sufficient as Knower of the state of His servants, their intentions, and their worthiness" (ذلِكَ الْفَضْلُ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ عَلِيماً). Of course, "dhalika" (that), which is a demonstrative pronoun for the distant, is used in such cases to indicate importance and the loftiness of the station.

[^1]: "Shahid" originally means witness; however, sometimes a person bears witness to the truth through speech, and sometimes through action and being killed in the path of pure goals.

And we too, inspired by the above verse, say: Praise be to the Lord that despite all the obstacles that exist in the way of performing positive works, by Divine grace, the third volume of Tafsir Nemouneh has concluded here in a worthy manner. ذلِكَ الْفَضْلُ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ عَلِيماً! O Allah! Make our intentions pure and our deeds fruitful and sincere for Your Holy Essence.

End of Volume Three of Tafsir Nemouneh

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ خُذُوا۟ حِذْرَكُمْ فَٱنفِرُوا۟ ثُبَاتٍ أَوِ ٱنفِرُوا۟ جَمِيعًا (٧١)
O you who have faith! Take your precautions, then go forth in companies, or go forth en masse. (71)

Commentary

Constant Readiness

"Hadhar" (حذر), rhyming with "khadhar," means alertness, readiness, and vigilance against danger. Sometimes it is also used to refer to the means by which danger is combated.

"Thubat" (ثبات) is the plural of "thubah" (ثبة), rhyming with "gunah," meaning scattered groups. Originally, it is derived from the root "thaba" (ثبى), meaning "to gather." In the above verse, the Quran addresses the general body of Muslims and gives them two important instructions for preserving the existence of their society.

First, It says: "O you who have believed, take your precautions with utmost care, lest you be caught off guard and danger reaches you from the side of the enemy."

(يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا خُذُوا حِذْرَكُمْ) Then, He commands that for confronting the enemy, various methods and tactics should be utilized, and "move forward to repel the enemy in separate detachments or as a collective body."

(فَانْفِرُوا ثُباتٍ أَوِ انْفِرُوا جَمِيعاً).

Where it is necessary to move in different and scattered groups, enter through that path; and where it is required for everyone to rush to the enemy's field as a single, cohesive army, do not forget the collective assembly.

Some commentators have interpreted "hadhar" in the above verse solely as "weapons," whereas "hadhar" has a broad meaning and is not limited to "weapons." Furthermore, in verse 102 of this same Surah, there is clear evidence that "hadhar" is different from weapons, where He says: ...

أَنْ تَضَعُوا أَسْلِحَتَكُمْ وَ خُذُوا حِذْرَكُمْ.

"There is no blame upon you if you are under necessity, during prayer on the battlefield, to lay down your weapons, but do not lose your 'hadhar'—that is, your vigilance and readiness!" This verse provides a comprehensive and all-encompassing instruction to all Muslims across all centuries and eras to be constantly vigilant for the preservation of their security and the defense of their borders, and that a form of permanent material and spiritual readiness should govern their society.

Interestingly, the meaning of "hadhar" is so broad that it encompasses every kind of material and spiritual means. This includes that Muslims must at all times be informed of the enemy's position, their type of weaponry, their combat methods, their level of readiness, and the quantity and effectiveness of their arms; for all these matters are effective in preventing the danger of the enemy and in fulfilling the concept of "hadhar."

On the other hand, for self-defense, they must prepare every kind of readiness in terms of psychological and spiritual morale, the mobilization of cultural, economic, and human resources, as well as the utilization of the most advanced weaponry of the time and the knowledge of how to operate them.

Certainly, if Muslims had implemented this single verse in their lives, they would never have suffered defeat or failure throughout their eventful history.

And as the above verse indicates, one should not be rigid in utilizing different methods of struggle; rather, one must act according to the requirements of time and place and the nature of the enemy's position. Where the enemy's situation is such that they must be approached in different detachments, they should use that method, with each group having its own specific plan for the confrontation. And where it is required for everyone to start the offensive with a single plan, they should stand in one rank.

From this, it becomes clear that the insistence of some individuals that Muslims must choose a single method in all their social struggles and have no variation in tactics is not only inconsistent with logic and experience but also disagrees with the spirit of Islamic teachings.

Perhaps the above verse also contains an allusion to the fact that the important issue is the advancement of the actual goals, whether the situation requires everyone to use one method or various methods.

Additionally, from the word "jami'an" (جميعاً), it is understood that for confronting the enemy, all Muslims without exception must participate, and this ruling is not restricted to a specific group.

Ayahs

وَإِنَّ مِنكُمْ لَمَن لَّيُبَطِّئَنَّ فَإِنْ أَصَـٰبَتْكُم مُّصِيبَةٌ قَالَ قَدْ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىَّ إِذْ لَمْ أَكُن مَّعَهُمْ شَهِيدًا (٧٢)
Among you is indeed he who drags his feet, and should an affliction visit you, he says, ‘It was certainly Allah’s blessing that I did not accompany them!’ (72)
وَلَئِنْ أَصَـٰبَكُمْ فَضْلٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ لَيَقُولَنَّ كَأَن لَّمْ تَكُنۢ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُۥ مَوَدَّةٌ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى كُنتُ مَعَهُمْ فَأَفُوزَ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا (٧٣)
But should a bounty from Allah come to you, he will say—as if there were no [tie of friendship and] affection between you and him—‘I wish I were with them so that I had achieved a great success!’ (73)

Commentary

Following the general command for Jihad and preparedness against the enemy mentioned in the previous verse, this verse refers to the state of a group of hypocrites, stating:

"These double-faced individuals who are among you strive with persistence to refrain from participating in the ranks of the warriors in the way of Allah." (وَ إِنَّ مِنْكُمْ [^1] لَمَنْ لَيُبَطِّئَنَّ) [^2]

[^1]: It should be noted that although the above verse addresses the believers, it speaks of the hypocrites; yet, by using the expression "from among you" (منكم), it counts them as part of the believers. This is because the hypocrites have always been interspersed among the true believers and were outwardly considered part of their community.

[^2]: "ليبطئن" is derived from the root "بطؤ" (on the weight of 'qutb'), meaning slowness in movement. As a group of linguists and commentators have mentioned here, it carries both an intransitive and a transitive meaning; that is, they are slow in movement themselves, and they also encourage others to be so. Perhaps, considering that the word is in the 'Taf'eel' form, it only carries the transitive meaning, except that sometimes one forces oneself to be slow and sometimes others!

"But when the warriors return from the battlefield, or when news of the battlefield reaches them, if a defeat or martyrdom has befallen them, these [hypocrites] say with joy: 'What a great blessing Allah bestowed upon me that I was not present with them to witness such harrowing scenes.'"

(فَإِنْ أَصابَتْكُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ قالَ قَدْ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيَّ إِذْ لَمْ أَكُنْ مَعَهُمْ شَهِيداً) "But if they hear that the true believers have been victorious, and naturally have attained spoils of war, they—like strangers as if no bond of affection ever existed between them and the believers—say out of regret and longing: 'I wish we had been with the warriors so that we could have attained a great success!'" (وَ لَئِنْ أَصابَكُمْ فَضْلٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ لَيَقُولَنَّ كَأَنْ لَمْ تَكُنْ بَيْنَكُمْ وَ بَيْنَهُ مَوَدَّةٌ يا لَيْتَنِي كُنْتُ مَعَهُمْ فَأَفُوزَ فَوْزاً عَظِيماً).

Although the above verse does not explicitly mention "spoils," it is clear that for someone who considers martyrdom in the way of Allah a type of calamity and regards missing out on martyrdom as a Divine blessing, victory and "great success" (fawz azim) in his view would be nothing other than material victory and war booty.

These double-faced individuals, who unfortunately have existed and continue to exist in every society, immediately change their appearance in the face of the victories and defeats of the true believers. They never share in their sorrows, nor do they cooperate in hardships and difficulties; yet, they expect to have a large share in their victories and to gain privileges just like the true believers and warriors.

Ayah

۞ فَلْيُقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَشْرُونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَمَن يُقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَيُقْتَلْ أَوْ يَغْلِبْ فَسَوْفَ نُؤْتِيهِ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (٧٤)
Let those who sell the life of this world for the Hereafter fight in the way of Allah; and whoever fights in the way of Allah, and then is slain, or he subdues [the enemy], We shall soon give him a great reward. (74)

Commentary

Preparing the Believers for Jihad

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the hypocrites' refusal to join the ranks of the mujahideen, this verse and several subsequent verses invite the believers to Jihad in the way of Allah with an effective and stirring logic. Considering that these verses were revealed at a time when various enemies from within and without were threatening Islam, the importance of these verses in nurturing the spirit of Jihad among Muslims becomes clearer.

At the beginning of the verse, it states: "Those who are ready to exchange the lowly life of the material world for the eternal and everlasting life of the hereafter must fight in the way of Allah."

(فَلْيُقاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ الَّذِينَ يَشْرُونَ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا بِالْآخِرَةِ) This means that only those can be among the true mujahideen who are prepared for such a transaction, and have truly realized that the life of the material world—as implied by the word Dunya (meaning lower or more base)—holds no significance compared to an honorable death on the path to eternal life. However, those who consider material life to be fundamental, precious, and superior to sacred Divine and human goals will never be good mujahideen.

Then, at the end of the verse, it states: "The fate of such mujahideen is perfectly clear, for it is one of two outcomes: either they are martyred or they crush the enemy and emerge victorious over them; in either case, We shall grant them a great reward."

(وَ مَنْ يُقاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ فَيُقْتَلْ أَوْ يَغْلِبْ فَسَوْفَ نُؤْتِيهِ أَجْراً عَظِيماً) Certainly, for such soldiers, 'defeat' does not exist in their lexicon, and in both scenarios, they see themselves as victorious. Such a spirit alone is sufficient to provide the means for their victory over the enemy. History also bears witness that one of the factors for the rapid victory of Muslims over enemies who were far superior to them in terms of numbers, equipment, and combat readiness was this very spirit of invincibility.

Even foreign scholars who have discussed Islam and the rapid victories of the Muslims during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and thereafter have considered this logic as one of the effective factors in their progress.

One of the famous Western historians says in his book: "By the blessing of the new religion and the gifts promised to them in the hereafter, the Muslims did not fear death at all and did not attribute any permanence or originality to this life (apart from the other world) [1], and therefore they would renounce it for the sake of their goal and creed."

It is noteworthy that in the above verse, as in many other verses of the Quran, Jihad is considered sacred only when it is "فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ" (in the way of Allah), for the salvation of Allah's servants, and for the revival of the principles of truth, justice, purity, and piety—not wars fought for the sake of expansionism, prejudice, barbarism, colonialism, or exploitation.

[1]: The Civilization of Arabs, Gustave Le Bon, page 155.

Ayah

وَمَا لَكُمْ لَا تُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ ٱلرِّجَالِ وَٱلنِّسَآءِ وَٱلْوِلْدَٰنِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ أَخْرِجْنَا مِنْ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلظَّالِمِ أَهْلُهَا وَٱجْعَل لَّنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ وَلِيًّا وَٱجْعَل لَّنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ نَصِيرًا (٧٥)
Why should you not fight in the way of Allah and the oppressed men, women, and children, who say, ‘Our Lord, bring us out of this town whose people are oppressors, and appoint for us a guardian from Yourself, and appoint for us a helper from Yourself’? (75)

Commentary

Appealing to Human Emotions

In the previous verse, the believers were invited to Jihad, but the emphasis was placed on faith in Allah and the Resurrection, as well as the reasoning of profit and loss. However, this verse invites them toward Jihad by stirring human emotions, stating: "Why do you not fight in the way of Allah and in the way of the oppressed and defenseless men, women, and children who are caught in the clutches of oppressors? Do your human emotions allow you to remain silent and watch these pathetic scenes?"

(وَ ما لَكُمْ لا تُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَ الْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ‌ [^1] مِنَ الرِّجالِ وَ النِّساءِ وَ الْوِلْدانِ):

[^1]: There is a clear difference between Mustad'af (oppressed/weakened) and Dha'if (weak). A Dha'if person is one who is naturally weak, but a Mustad'af is someone who has been weakened due to the injustices and oppressions of others, whether this weakening is intellectual and cultural, moral, economic, or political and social. Thus, it is a comprehensive term that encompasses all types of colonialism and exploitation.

Then, to further ignite the human emotions of the believers, it says: "These oppressed ones are those who are trapped in suffocating environments and have lost hope in everything; therefore, they have raised their hands in prayer, asking their Lord to let them escape that environment of injustice and oppression."

(الَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا أَخْرِجْنا مِنْ هذِهِ الْقَرْيَةِ الظَّالِمِ أَهْلُها).
