And they also request from their Lord to send a guardian and protector to support them.

(وَ اجْعَلْ لَنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا).

And to raise a helper and assistant for their rescue.

(وَ اجْعَلْ لَنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ نَصِيراً).

In reality, the above verse indicates that Allah has answered their prayer and has placed this great human mission upon your shoulders. You are the "guardian" (Wali) and "helper" (Nasir) who have been designated by Allah to support and rescue them. Therefore, you must not easily lose this great opportunity and sublime position.

Furthermore, several other points can be derived from this verse:

1- Islamic Jihad, as previously mentioned, is not for acquiring wealth, status, or the natural resources and raw materials of other countries. It is not for securing consumer markets or imposing an ideology or policy. Rather, it is solely for spreading the principles of virtue and faith, and for defending the oppressed, the broken-winged men and women, and the deprived and victimized children. Thus, Jihad has two comprehensive goals mentioned in the above verse: one is the "Divine goal" and the other is the "human goal," and these two are not actually separate from one another but return to a single reality.

2- From the perspective of Islam, a livable environment is one where a person can freely act upon their correct beliefs. However, an environment engulfed in suffocation, where a person is not even free to say "I am a Muslim," is not livable, and people of faith long to leave such an environment, for such a place is the center of the oppressors' activities.

It is noteworthy that "Mecca" was both a very holy city and the original homeland of the Migrants (Muhajirun); nevertheless, its suffocating conditions caused them to ask their Lord to let them leave it.

3- At the end of the above verse, we read that the Muslims who were caught in the clutches of the enemy first requested a "guardian" (Wali) from Allah for their salvation, and then a "helper" (Nasir). To be rescued from the clutches of oppressors, the existence of a capable and compassionate "leader" and guardian is necessary before anything else, followed by helpers, assistants, and sufficient personnel. Therefore, the existence of helpers and assistants, no matter how numerous, is fruitless without the utilization of correct leadership.

4- People of faith ask for everything from Allah and do not extend their hand of need toward anyone other than Him. Even when they request a guardian or a helper, they ask it of Him.

Ayah

ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتِ فَقَـٰتِلُوٓا۟ أَوْلِيَآءَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۖ إِنَّ كَيْدَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ كَانَ ضَعِيفًا (٧٦)
Those who have faith fight in the way of Allah, and those who are faithless fight in the way of fake gods. So fight the friends of Satan; indeed the stratagems of Satan are always flimsy. (76)

Commentary

Then, in this verse, to encourage the mujahideen and incite them to struggle against the enemy, and to distinguish the ranks and objectives of the warriors, He says: "Those who believe fight in the way of Allah and that which is for the benefit of Allah's servants, but those who disbelieve fight in the way of Taghut (the transgressor), meaning destructive powers."

(الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ الطَّاغُوتِ‌)

This means that, in any case, life is not devoid of struggle; however, one group fights in the path of Truth and another in the path of Falsehood and Satan. Following this, He says: "Fight against the allies of Satan and do not fear them."

(فَقاتِلُوا أَوْلِياءَ الشَّيْطانِ‌)

Taghut and the rebellious, oppressive powers, even if they appear large and strong on the surface, are internally abject and weak. Do not be afraid of their equipped and decorated appearance, for they are hollow inside, and their plans—just like their powers—are frail and weak, because they do not rely on the eternal power of Allah, but rather rely on satanic forces.

(إِنَّ كَيْدَ الشَّيْطانِ كانَ ضَعِيفاً) "Indeed, the strategy of Satan is weak." The reason for this weakness and inability is clear: on one hand, the believers walk in the path of objectives and truths that are in harmony and resonance with the laws of creation and possess an eternal and everlasting nature. They fight in the way of liberating humanity and eradicating the manifestations of tyranny and oppression. Meanwhile, the supporters of Taghut strive in the path of colonization, the exploitation of mankind, and fleeting desires whose effect is the destruction of society and which run contrary to the laws of creation. On the other hand, the believers, by relying on spiritual forces, possess a tranquility that guarantees their victory and grants them strength, whereas the disbelievers have no firm support.

It is noteworthy that in this verse, the complete connection between "Taghut" and "Satan" is explained, showing how Taghut seeks aid from various demonic forces, to the extent that it says the allies of Taghut are the same as the allies of Satan. In verse 27 of Surah Al-A'raf, the same theme is mentioned:

(إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا الشَّياطِينَ أَوْلِياءَ لِلَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ‌):

"Indeed, We have made the devils allies to those who do not believe."

Ayah

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ كُفُّوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقِتَالُ إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمْ يَخْشَوْنَ ٱلنَّاسَ كَخَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْ أَشَدَّ خَشْيَةً ۚ وَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا لِمَ كَتَبْتَ عَلَيْنَا ٱلْقِتَالَ لَوْلَآ أَخَّرْتَنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ ۗ قُلْ مَتَـٰعُ ٱلدُّنْيَا قَلِيلٌ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِّمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ وَلَا تُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا (٧٧)
Have you not regarded those who were told, ‘Keep your hands off [from warfare], and maintain the prayer and give the zakat’? But when fighting was prescribed for them, behold, a part of them feared the people as if fearing Allah, or were even more afraid, and they said, ‘Our Lord! Why did You prescribe fighting for us? Why did You not respite us for a short time?!’ Say, ‘The enjoyments of this world are trifle and the Hereafter is better for the Godwary, and you will not be wronged so much as a single date-thread. (77)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators, such as the great commentator Sheikh Tusi, the author of Al-Tibyan, and the authors of the commentaries of Qurtubi and Al-Manar, have narrated from Ibn Abbas that:

A group of Muslims, while they were in Mecca and under severe pressure and persecution from the polytheists, came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and said: "We were honorable and respected before Islam, but after embracing Islam, our situation changed; we lost that honor and respect and are constantly being harassed by the enemies. If you permit us, we will fight the enemy to regain our honor." At that time, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "I am currently not commanded to fight." However, when the Muslims came to Medina and the ground was prepared for armed struggle and the command for Jihad was revealed, some of those same fiery and passionate individuals neglected to participate in the field of Jihad, and there was no sign of that previous fervor and heat. The above verse was revealed to encourage the Muslims and to reproach the negligent individuals, explaining certain realities.

Commentary

Those Who Are Men of Words

Here, the Quran says: "Truly amazing is the state of a group who, in an inappropriate situation, requested with extraordinary heat and passion to be given permission for Jihad, and they were told to refrain for now and engage in self-building, performing prayer, strengthening their ranks, and paying Zakat. But when the ground was prepared in every respect and the command for Jihad was revealed, fear and terror suddenly overwhelmed them, and they began to protest against this command" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ كُفُّوا أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَ أَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ آتُوا الزَّكاةَ فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقِتالُ إِذا فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ يَخْشَوْنَ النَّاسَ كَخَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ أَوْ أَشَدَّ خَشْيَةً).

In their protest, they "explicitly said: O Allah! Why have You revealed the command for Jihad so soon? How good it would have been if this command had been delayed for a while! Or if this mission had been left to future generations!" [^1] (وَ قالُوا رَبَّنا لِمَ كَتَبْتَ عَلَيْنَا الْقِتالَ لَوْ لا أَخَّرْتَنا إِلى‌ أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ‌).

The Quran gives two answers to such individuals: The first is the answer that passed within the phrase: "they fear people as they should fear Allah or even more." That is, instead of fearing the All-Powerful and Subduing God, they are terrified of weak and helpless humans; in fact, their terror of such humans is greater than their fear of Allah! Secondly, it must be said to such individuals that even assuming you live peacefully for a few days by abandoning Jihad, ultimately "this life is fleeting and worthless, but the eternal world is more valuable for the God-fearing, especially since they will receive their reward in full and not the slightest injustice will be done to them."

(قُلْ مَتاعُ الدُّنْيا قَلِيلٌ وَ الْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِمَنِ اتَّقى‌ وَ لا تُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا) [^2] In the commentary of this verse, several points must be noted:

1- The first question that arises is why, among all Islamic commands, only the issues of prayer and Zakat are mentioned, while Islamic commands are not limited to these.

The answer to the question is that "Prayer" is the symbol of the bond with Allah, and "Zakat" is the symbol of the bond with Allah's creation. Therefore, the intention is that Muslims were commanded to prepare their bodies, souls, and society for Jihad by establishing a firm bond with Allah and a firm bond with Allah's servants—in other words, to engage in self-building. Certainly, any kind of Jihad without the spiritual and physical readiness of individuals and without firm social bonds is doomed to failure. In the light of prayer and supplication to Allah, a Muslim strengthens his faith and nurtures his spirit, becoming ready for any kind of sacrifice and selflessness. Through Zakat, social gaps are filled, and the situation improves regarding the provision of trained personnel and tools of war, for which Zakat serves as an economic support. Thus, when the command for Jihad is issued, they will have sufficient readiness to fight the enemy.

2- We know that the law of Zakat was revealed in Medina, and Zakat had not been made obligatory for Muslims in Mecca. In this case, how can the above verse refer to the situation of Muslims in Mecca?

The late Sheikh Tusi, in the commentary of Al-Tibyan, says in response to this question that the meaning of Zakat in the above verse was "recommended Zakat" (charity) which existed in Mecca. That is, the Quran encouraged Muslims (even in Mecca) to provide financial assistance to the needy and to organize the affairs of the new converts.

3- The above verse also points to an important reality: that the Muslims had one program in Mecca and another in Medina. The thirteen-year period in Mecca was a period of human character-building for the Muslims. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) strove through his continuous day-and-night teachings to build such human beings out of those same idol-worshipping and superstitious elements of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) that they would not hesitate to show any resistance or sacrifice in the face of the greatest events of life. If the Meccan period had not existed, those striking and successive victories in Medina would never have been achieved by the Muslims.

The Meccan period was a period of university, training, and preparation for the Muslims. For this reason, about ninety out of the one hundred and fourteen surahs of the Quran were revealed in Mecca, which mostly had a doctrinal and ideological nature. However, the Medinan period was the period of forming a government and laying the foundations of a healthy society. For this reason, neither Jihad was obligatory in Mecca nor was Zakat, because Jihad is among the duties of the Islamic government, just as the establishment of the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) is also among the affairs of the government.

[^1]: It is understood from some hadiths that they had heard things about the uprising of the Mahdi; some of them expected the issue of Jihad to be postponed until the time of the uprising of the Mahdi (عليه السلام)! — Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 518.

[^2]: Fatil: is the thin thread located in the slit in the middle of a date stone, as its explanation passed in Vol. 3, p. 414.

Ayahs

أَيْنَمَا تَكُونُوا۟ يُدْرِككُّمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ وَلَوْ كُنتُمْ فِى بُرُوجٍ مُّشَيَّدَةٍ ۗ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ حَسَنَةٌ يَقُولُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِۦ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَقُولُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِۦ مِنْ عِندِكَ ۚ قُلْ كُلٌّ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَمَالِ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلْقَوْمِ لَا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ حَدِيثًا (٧٨)
Wherever you may be death will overtake you, even if you were in fortified towers.’ If any good befalls them, they say, ‘This is from Allah;’ and when an ill befalls them, they say, ‘This is from you.’ Say, ‘All is from Allah.’ What is the matter with these people that they would not understand any matter? (78)
مَّآ أَصَابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَآ أَصَابَكَ مِن سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِن نَّفْسِكَ ۚ وَأَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ لِلنَّاسِ رَسُولًا ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًا (٧٩)
Whatever good befalls you is from Allah; and whatever ill befalls you is from yourself. We sent you as an apostle to mankind, and Allah suffices as witness. (79)

Commentary

Based on the preceding and succeeding verses, it is understood that these two verses also pertain to a group of hypocrites who had taken their place within the ranks of the Muslims. As we read in the previous verses, they were terrified of participating in the field of Jihad, and when the command for Jihad was issued, they became distressed. The Quran provides two responses to them regarding this way of thinking:

The first response was what passed at the end of the previous verse:

قُلْ مَتاعُ الدُّنْيا قَلِيلٌ وَ الْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِمَنِ اتَّقى‌:

"Say, 'The enjoyment of this world is little, and the Hereafter is better for him who fears Allah.'"

The second response is what we read in the verse under discussion, which is: what benefit can fleeing from death have for you, "while wherever you may be, death will overtake you, and eventually one day it will swallow you, even if you are within lofty towers." Therefore, it is better that this certain and inevitable death occurs in a constructive and correct path like Jihad, rather than being futile and ineffective.

(أَيْنَما تَكُونُوا يُدْرِكْكُمُ الْمَوْتُ وَ لَوْ كُنْتُمْ فِي بُرُوجٍ مُشَيَّدَةٍ) It is noteworthy that in several verses of the Holy Quran, such as verse 99 of Surah Al-Hijr and verse 48 of Al-Muddaththir, death has been referred to as "Certainty" (Yaqin). This points to the fact that every nation and group, whatever belief they may hold and whatever they may be able to deny, cannot deny this reality—that life eventually has an end. Since human beings, due to their love for life or because they mistakenly equate death with absolute annihilation and non-existence, are always fleeing from its name and its manifestations, these verses give them a warning. In the verse under discussion, with the expression (يُدْرِكْكُمُ - overtakes you), it reminds them that fleeing from this definitive reality of the universe is futile, because the meaning of the root of "yudrikkum" is when someone flees from something and that thing runs after them.

In verse 8 of Surah Al-Jumu'ah, this truth is expressed even more clearly:

قُلْ إِنَّ الْمَوْتَ الَّذِي تَفرُّونَ مِنْهُ فَإِنَّهُ مُلاقِيكُمْ‌:

"Say, 'Indeed, the death from which you flee—indeed, it will meet you.'"

In light of this reality, is it rational for a person to withdraw themselves from the scene of Jihad and the attainment of the honor of martyrdom, only to die in bed at home? Even assuming that by not participating in Jihad, one lives a few days longer to repeat the same routines and remains deprived of the rewards of the warriors in the way of Allah—is this close to reason and logic?! Fundamentally, death is a great reality, and one must be prepared to welcome a death accompanied by honor.

Another point to be noted is that the above verse says:

"Nothing, not even lofty towers (بُرُوجٍ مُشَيَّدَةٍ) [1], can prevent death." The secret behind this is also clear, because death—contrary to what people imagine—does not penetrate from outside the human being; rather, it usually originates from within the human being. This is because the capacities of the various systems of the body are inevitably limited and will one day come to an end. Of course, unnatural deaths come to a person from the outside, but natural death comes from within; hence, lofty towers and firm fortresses can have no effect on it.

It is true that firm fortresses sometimes prevent unnatural deaths, but ultimately, what is the benefit? They cannot eliminate death entirely; a few days later, natural death will come for the person.

[1]: "Mushayyadah" is originally from the root "Shayd" (rhyming with shir) meaning gypsum and other strong materials used to strengthen a building. Since in that era, gypsum was usually the strongest material for reinforcing a building, it was mostly applied to this substance. Therefore, "buruj mushayyadah" means strong fortresses. If we see that mushayyadah has also come to mean "lofty" and "tall," it is because without using gypsum and the like, they could never build lofty and tall structures.

The Source of Victories and Defeats

At the end of this same verse, the Quran points to another of the baseless claims and vain thoughts of the hypocrites, saying: "Whenever they achieve a victory and obtain good things and blessings, they say, 'This is from Allah,'" meaning we were worthy of it, so Allah gave us such bounties.

(وَ إِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ حَسَنَةٌ يَقُولُوا هذِهِ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ‌) But when a defeat overtakes them or they suffer an injury on the battlefield, they say: "These were due to the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) poor management and the inadequacy of his military plans," for example, they considered the defeat in the Battle of Uhud to be caused by this.

(وَ إِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَقُولُوا هذِهِ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ‌) Some commentators have suggested that the above verse is about the Jews, and by "hasanah" (good) and "sayyi'ah" (evil), all good and bad events are meant; for the Jews, at the time of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) appearance, attributed the good events of their lives to Allah and considered the bad events to be due to the [ill-omen of the] Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) presence. However, the connection of the verse with the preceding and succeeding verses, which are about the hypocrites, shows that this verse is mostly related to them.

In any case, the Quran answers them that from the perspective of a sharp-sighted monotheist and worshipper of God, "all these events, victories, and defeats are from Allah," which are given to people according to their merits and existential values (قُلْ كُلٌّ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ‌).

At the end of the verse, as a protest against their lack of thought and deep reflection on various subjects of life, it says: "So why are these people not ready to understand the truths?"

(فَما لِهؤُلاءِ الْقَوْمِ لا يَكادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ حَدِيثاً).

Then, in the next verse, it states: "Whatever good, victory, and blessing reaches you is from Allah, and whatever evil, distress, and defeat overtakes you is from yourself!" (ما أَصابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ اللَّهِ وَ ما أَصابَكَ مِنْ سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِنْ نَفْسِكَ‌).

At the end of the verse, it responds to those who attributed their defeats and failures to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and, so to speak, considered them the effect of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) presence, saying that We "sent you as a Messenger to the people, and Allah is sufficient as a witness to this matter." Is it possible for the Messenger of Allah to be the cause of defeat, failure, and evil for the people?

(وَ أَرْسَلْناكَ لِلنَّاسِ رَسُولًا وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيداً).

Answer to an Important Question

Studying these two verses, which are placed back-to-back in the Quran, draws a question in the mind: why in the first verse are all goods and evils (hasanat and sayyi'at) attributed to Allah, while the second verse attributes only the goods to Allah and the evils and sayyi'at to the people?! Certainly, there is a point hidden here; otherwise, how could two consecutive verses have such a clear difference?

By studying and reflecting on the content of the two verses, we encounter several points, each of which can be a separate answer to this question:

1- If we analyze sayyi'at (evils) and bad things, they have two aspects: one is a positive aspect, and the other is a negative aspect. It is this negative aspect that gives it the appearance of a sayyi'ah and turns it into the form of a "relative loss."

For example: A person who kills an innocent person with a firearm or a cold weapon has certainly committed a sayyi'ah. Now we examine the factors for the existence of this bad deed: among these factors are human power, human thought, the power of a cold or hot weapon, accurate aiming, using the right opportunity, and the impact and power of the bullet—all of which are positive aspects of the case, because they can all be useful and beneficial, and if used in their proper place, they solve great problems. The only negative aspect of the case is that all these powers and forces were used in a place other than their own; for example, instead of being used to repel the danger of a predatory animal or a dangerous criminal killer, they were used against an innocent human being. It is this last negative aspect that turns it into a "sayyi'ah"; otherwise, neither the human's power of aiming is a bad thing, nor the power of gunpowder and the penetration of the bullet. All these are sources of power and are highly usable in their proper place.

Therefore, if we observe that in the first verse all hasanat and sayyi'at are attributed to Allah, it is because all sources of power—even the powers that are misused—are from Allah, and He is the source of the constructive and positive parts. And if in the second verse "sayyi'at" are attributed to the people, it refers to those same "negative aspects" of the case and the misuse of God-given blessings and powers. This is exactly like a father who gives capital to his son to build a good house, but he uses it for drugs, corruption, and crime, or for establishing a center of vice. There is no doubt that regarding the original capital, he is indebted to his father, but regarding the misuse, it is attributed to himself.

2- It is possible that the noble verse refers to the issue of "Al-Amr bayn al-Amrayn" (the matter between the two matters), which is mentioned in the discussion of predestination and free will. The summary of it is that all events in the world, even our actions and deeds, whether they be hasanah or sayyi'ah, good or bad, are related to Allah from one perspective, because it is He who gave us power and granted us choice and freedom of will. Therefore, what we choose and select with freedom of will is not contrary to Allah's will. Yet, at the same time, our actions are attributed to us and originate from our existence, because the determining factor of the action is our will and choice. For this reason, we are responsible for our actions, and the attribution of our actions to Allah, as mentioned, does not strip us of responsibility and does not lead to the belief in predestination (jabr).

Therefore, where He says: "hasanat" and "sayyi'at" are from Allah, He refers to that same agency (fa'iliyyah) of Allah toward everything. And where He says: sayyi'at are from you, He refers to our agency and the issue of our will and choice. In fact, the combination of the two verses proves the issue of "Amr bayn al-Amrayn" (reflect carefully).

3- Another interpretation that exists for these two verses, which is also mentioned in the reports of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), is that: what is meant by sayyi'at are the retributions for deeds and the punishments and penalties for sins. There is no doubt that these retributions are from Allah, but since they are the result of the deeds and actions of the servants, they are sometimes attributed to the servants and sometimes to Allah, and both are correct. For example, it is correct to say the judge cuts off the hand of the thief, and it is also correct to say it is the thief himself who cuts off his own hand!

Ayahs

مَّن يُطِعِ ٱلرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطَاعَ ٱللَّهَ ۖ وَمَن تَوَلَّىٰ فَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظًا (٨٠)
Whoever obeys the Apostle certainly obeys Allah; and as for those who turn their backs [on you]; We have not sent you to keep watch over them. (80)
وَيَقُولُونَ طَاعَةٌ فَإِذَا بَرَزُوا۟ مِنْ عِندِكَ بَيَّتَ طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُمْ غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى تَقُولُ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَكْتُبُ مَا يُبَيِّتُونَ ۖ فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا (٨١)
They profess obedience [to you], but when they go out from your presence, a group of them conspire overnight [to do] something other than what you say. But Allah records what they conspire overnight. So disregard them and put your trust in Allah, for Allah suffices as trustee. (81)

Commentary

The Sunnah of the Prophet is Like Divine Revelation

In this verse, the position of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in relation to the people and their "good deeds" and "evil deeds" is explained. First, it states: "He who obeys the Messenger has obeyed Allah."

(مَنْ يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطاعَ اللَّهَ‌).

Therefore, obedience to Allah cannot be separated from obedience to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, because the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله takes no step contrary to the will of Allah; his words, actions, and behavior are all in accordance with Allah's command.

Then it says: "If some turn away and rise in opposition to your commands, you have no responsibility for their actions, and you are not duty-bound to prevent them from every wrongdoing by means of compulsion. Your duty is to convey the message, enjoin the good, forbid the evil, and guide the lost and the ignorant."

(وَ مَنْ تَوَلَّى فَما أَرْسَلْناكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظاً) It should be noted that "Hafiz" (حفيظ), being a Sifah Mushabbahah (assimilated adjective), conveys a sense of stability and permanence, differing from "Hafiz" (حافظ) which is an active participle. Thus, "Hafiz" means someone who continuously guards or watches over something. Consequently, the meaning of the verse is that the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله responsibility is that of leadership, guidance, inviting to the Truth, and struggling against corruption. However, if individuals insist on following the path of transgression, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is neither responsible for their deviations—such that he must be present and watchful everywhere—nor is he required to prevent every sin and disobedience through force and compulsion; nor would he normally have the power to do such a thing through ordinary means.

Therefore, in events such as the Battle of Uhud—to which the verse may also be referring—the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had the duty to exercise maximum precision and care in terms of military tactics, planning the battle, and protecting the Muslims from the enemy's evil. Certainly, obeying the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in these instructions was obedience to Allah. But if some trampled upon the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله command and consequently suffered defeat, the responsibility lies with them, not the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

It must be noted that this verse is one of the clearest verses of the Quran proving the authority (hujjiyyah) of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله Sunnah and the acceptance of his Hadiths. Therefore, no one can say, "I accept the Quran but I do not accept the Hadith and Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله," because the above verse explicitly states: "Obedience to the Hadith and Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is obedience to the command of Allah."

And when we see that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, according to the Hadith al-Thaqalayn—which has appeared in well-known Islamic sources of both the Sunnis and the Shi'a—has explicitly considered the Hadiths of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام as a proof and authority, we conclude that obedience to the command of the Ahl al-Bayt is also inseparable from obedience to Allah's command. No one can say, "I accept the Quran but I do not accept the Hadiths of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام," for such a statement is contrary to the above verse and similar verses.

Hence, in several narrations mentioned in Tafsir al-Burhan regarding this verse, we read:

Allah, according to the above verse, gave the right of commanding and forbidding to His Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله likewise gave this right to Ali عليه السلام and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام. Therefore, the people are duty-bound not to turn away from their commands and prohibitions, for their commanding and forbidding is always from Allah and not from themselves. [1]

Then, in the second verse, it refers to the condition of a group of hypocrites or those weak in faith, saying: "When they stand in the ranks of the Muslims alongside the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they harmonize with others to protect their interests or ward off harm from themselves, expressing obedience to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله command, saying: 'We are ready to follow him with heart and soul.'" (وَ يَقُولُونَ طاعَةٌ).

But when the people leave the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, that group of hypocrites and those weak in faith consign their words and pledges to oblivion and, in nocturnal meetings, make decisions contrary to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله words.

(فَإِذا بَرَزُوا مِنْ عِنْدِكَ بَيَّتَ طائِفَةٌ مِنْهُمْ غَيْرَ الَّذِي تَقُولُ‌).

From this phrase, it is understood that the hypocrites during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not remain idle; rather, through their nightly gatherings and consultations with one another, they plotted schemes to sabotage the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله programs.

[1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 396.

However, Allah commands His Prophet to "turn away from them, not to fear their plots, and never to rely on them in his programs; rely only on Allah, for Allah is the best Helper, Supporter, and Defender."

(فأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَ تَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ وَكِيلًا).

Ayah

أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ ۚ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا۟ فِيهِ ٱخْتِلَـٰفًا كَثِيرًا (٨٢)
Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would have surely found much discrepancy in it. (82)

Commentary

A Living Document of the Quran's Miraculous Nature

Following the rebukes directed at the hypocrites in the preceding verses, the text here addresses them and all those who harbor doubts and uncertainties regarding the truth of the Majestic Quran, stating: "Do they not reflect upon the specific state of this Quran and examine its results? Had this Quran been revealed from other than Allah, they would surely have found within it many contradictions and discrepancies. Now that there is no discrepancy or contradiction in it, they must realize it has been revealed by Allah."

(أَ فَلا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَ لَوْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلافاً كَثِيراً).

The word Tadabbur (reflection/deliberation) is originally derived from the root dubur (meaning the back or the end of something). Therefore, Tadabbur means examining the results, consequences, and the "front and back" of a matter. Its difference from Tafakkur (thinking) is that Tafakkur relates to examining the causes and characteristics of an entity, whereas Tadabbur relates to examining its consequences and outcomes. Several points can be derived from the above verse:

1- People are duty-bound to study and investigate the principles of religion and matters such as the truthfulness of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله claim and the authenticity of the Quran, and they must avoid blind imitation and judgment.

2- Contrary to what some imagine, the Quran is understandable and comprehensible for everyone; for if it were not understandable, the command to "reflect" (Tadabbur) upon it would not have been given.

3- One of the proofs of the Quran's authenticity and its divine origin is that there is no contradiction or discrepancy throughout it. To clarify this reality, consider the following explanation:

The psyche of every human being is in a state of constant change. The law of evolution—under normal conditions and provided no exceptional circumstances arise—encompasses man, his psyche, and his thoughts. With the passage of days, months, and years, it constantly transforms the language, thought, and speech of human beings. If we look closely, the writings of a single author are never identical; in fact, even the beginning and the end of a single book often differ. This is especially true if someone is caught in the whirlwind of great events—events that lay the foundation for a comprehensive intellectual, social, and ideological revolution. No matter how much such a person tries to deliver his words in a uniform, consistent manner that references the past, he is unable to do so. This is particularly true if he is unschooled and raised in a completely backward environment.

However, the Quran, which was revealed over a period of 23 years according to the educational needs and requirements of the people under completely diverse conditions and circumstances, is a book that speaks on entirely varied subjects. It is not like ordinary books that pursue only a single social, political, philosophical, legal, or historical discussion. Rather, it sometimes speaks of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the secrets of creation; at other times, of rulings, laws, customs, and traditions; at another time, of past nations and their soul-stirring histories; and at yet another time, of exhortations, advice, acts of worship, and the relationship between servants and God. As Dr. Gustave Le Bon stated, the Quran, the heavenly book of Muslims, is not limited to religious teachings and instructions alone, but also includes political and social instructions for Muslims.

Such a book with these characteristics would normally be impossible to remain free of contradictions, discrepancies, inconsistent statements, and significant fluctuations. Yet, when we see that despite all these factors, all its verses are harmonious and free from any contradiction, discrepancy, or discordance, we can easily deduce that this book is not the product of human thoughts but is from Allah, just as the Quran itself has expressed this reality in the above verse. [^1]

Ayah

وَإِذَا جَآءَهُمْ أَمْرٌ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْنِ أَوِ ٱلْخَوْفِ أَذَاعُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۖ وَلَوْ رَدُّوهُ إِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ وَإِلَىٰٓ أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَمْرِ مِنْهُمْ لَعَلِمَهُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَنۢبِطُونَهُۥ مِنْهُمْ ۗ وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ لَٱتَّبَعْتُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٨٣)
When a report of safety or alarm comes to them, they immediately broadcast it; but had they referred it to the Apostle or to those vested with authority among them, those of them who investigate would have ascertained it. And were it not for Allah’s grace upon you and His mercy, you would have surely followed Satan, [all] except a few. (83)

Commentary

The Spreading of Rumors

In this verse, reference is made to another improper action of the hypocrites or those with weak faith, stating: "They are those who, when news reaches them concerning the victory or defeat of the Muslims, spread it everywhere without investigation." It often happens that such news is baseless, having been fabricated by enemies for specific purposes, and its dissemination ends up being to the detriment of the Muslims.

(وَ إِذا جاءَهُمْ أَمْرٌ مِنَ الْأَمْنِ أَوِ الْخَوْفِ أَذاعُوا بِهِ‌) "While their duty is to share such news with their leaders and guides before anyone else, and to benefit from their vast information and deep insight," so that they do not needlessly cause the Muslims to suffer the consequences of vanity arising from imaginary victories, nor weaken their morale due to false rumors regarding defeat.

(وَ لَوْ رَدُّوهُ إِلَى الرَّسُولِ وَ إِلى‌ أُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْهُمْ لَعَلِمَهُ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَنْبِطُونَهُ مِنْهُمْ‌).

"Yastanbitunahu" (يستنبطونه) is originally derived from the root "Nabat" (نبط) and refers to the first water drawn from a well that is extracted from the depths of the earth. For this reason, deriving any truth from various evidences and signs and extracting it from available documents is called "Istinbat" (استنباط), whether it be in matters of jurisprudence (Fiqh), philosophy, politics, or science.

The meaning of Uli al-Amr (أُولِي الْأَمْرِ - those in authority) here refers to those who possess the power of discernment and sufficient mastery over various issues, and who can clarify "truths" from "baseless rumors" and authentic matters from the false for the people. In the first degree, these are the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, his successors, and in the next degree, the scholars who are experts in such matters.

As narrated in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding this verse, he said: "They are the Imams," meaning the intended meaning of this verse is the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt.

Other narrations with this same theme have also been reported.

An objection might be raised against such narrations, stating that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله existed at the time of the verse's revelation, but the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt—especially with the office of Imamate—did not. The answer to this objection is clear, for this verse is not exclusive to the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; rather, it expresses a general law for all centuries and ages against rumors spread among Muslims by enemies or ignorant Muslims.

The Harms of Rumor-Mongering and the Dissemination of Rumors

One of the great calamities that afflicts various societies and kills the social spirit, understanding, and cooperation among them is the issue of rumor-mongering and the spreading of rumors. It often happens that a single hypocrite fabricates a false statement and tells it to a few people, and individuals—without investigation—strive to spread it, perhaps even adding their own embellishments to it. Consequently, they occupy a significant amount of the people's energy, thought, and time, creating anxiety and worry among the public. Frequently, rumors shake public confidence and make members of society weak and hesitant in performing necessary tasks.

Although societies under pressure and suffocation sometimes pursue rumor-mongering and the spreading of rumors as a form of struggle or vengeance, for healthy societies, the dissemination of rumors brings about many harms. If these rumors concern capable, positive, and useful individuals, they discourage them in their services and sometimes blow away their years of reputation, depriving the people of the benefits of their existence.

For this reason, Islam has explicitly fought against "rumor-mongering," considering fabrication, lying, and slander forbidden, as well as the spreading of rumors, and the above verse is an example of this.

Then, at the end of the verse, it points to this reality: "If the grace and mercy of Allah had not encompassed you, and if you had not been saved from the clutches of such rumors and their dire consequences through the guidance of the Lord, many of you would have stepped into the paths of Satan, and only a few would have been able to keep themselves from following Satan."

(وَ لَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ رَحْمَتُهُ لَاتَّبَعْتُمُ الشَّيْطانَ‌) This means that only the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the experts and meticulous, keen-sighted scholars can keep themselves away from the whispers of rumors and rumor-mongers. As for the majority of society, if they remain deprived of correct leadership, they will fall victim to the painful consequences of rumor-mongering and the spreading of rumors. [1]

[1]: From what we have said, it becomes clear that "except for a few" (إِلَّا قَلِيلًا) is an exception from the pronoun in "you would have followed" (اتبعتم), and there is no displacement or transposition in the verse (reflect on this).

Ayah

فَقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ لَا تُكَلَّفُ إِلَّا نَفْسَكَ ۚ وَحَرِّضِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۖ عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَكُفَّ بَأْسَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ أَشَدُّ بَأْسًا وَأَشَدُّ تَنكِيلًا (٨٤)
So fight in the way of Allah: you are responsible only for yourself, but urge on the faithful [to fight]. Maybe Allah will curb the might of the faithless, for Allah is greatest in might and severest in punishment. (84)

Occasion of Revelation

In the commentaries of Majma' al-Bayan, Qurtubi, and Ruh al-Ma'ani, the occasion of revelation for this verse is stated as follows: When Abu Sufyan and the army of the Quraysh returned victoriously from the battlefield of Uhud, Abu Sufyan made an agreement with the Prophet ﷺ to meet again at the season of Badr al-Sughra (a market held in the month of Dhu al-Qi'dah in the land of Badr). When the appointed time arrived, the Prophet ﷺ invited the Muslims to move toward the aforementioned location. However, a group of Muslims, who had not forgotten the bitter memory of the defeat at Uhud, strongly refused to move. The above verse was revealed, and the Prophet ﷺ once again invited the Muslims to march. At this time, only seventy people accompanied the Prophet ﷺ to the specified location. However, Abu Sufyan (due to the terror he felt at the prospect of facing the army of Islam) refrained from appearing there, and the Prophet ﷺ returned to Medina safely with his companions.

Commentary

Everyone is Responsible for Their Own Duty

Following the verses related to Jihad, an extraordinary command is given to the Prophet ﷺ in this verse: he is duty-bound to stand alone against the enemy, even if no one else steps into the battlefield with him! This is because he is solely responsible for his own duty, and regarding other people, he has no obligation other than encouraging and inviting them to Jihad.

(فَقاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ لا تُكَلَّفُ إِلَّا نَفْسَكَ وَ حَرِّضِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌) "So fight in the cause of Allah; you are not held responsible except for yourself, and encourage the believers." In reality, the verse contains an important social instruction, especially for leaders: they must be so determined, steadfast, and decisive in their work that even if no one responds to their call, they do not abandon the pursuit of their sacred goal. While inviting others to fulfill their duty, they should not make their own programs contingent upon the response of others. No leader is capable of leading or advancing their goals unless they possess such readiness—especially divine leaders whose primary reliance is on Allah, the Source of all power and strength.

Therefore, following this command, He says: "It is hoped that Allah, through your efforts and struggles—even if you are alone—will break the might and power of the disbelievers, for His power is above all powers and His punishment is above all punishments."

(عَسَى اللَّهُ أَنْ يَكُفَّ بَأْسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ اللَّهُ أَشَدُّ بَأْساً وَ أَشَدُّ تَنْكِيلًا) "Perhaps Allah will restrain the might of those who disbelieve. And Allah is greater in might and stronger in exemplary punishment." [^1]

[^1]: Ba's (بأس) in linguistics means strength, firmness, and courage. Tankil (تنكيل) is derived from the root nukul (نکول), meaning to refrain out of fear. Originally, it comes from nakl (نکل - on the weight of akl), meaning a horse's bridle. Therefore, tankil, which is the infinitive of the taf'il form, means performing an act that causes the opposing party to turn away from wrongdoing upon witnessing it. This is the very punishment that serves as a lesson both for the oppressor and for others.

The Meaning of "Asa" (عسى) and "La'alla" (لعل) in the Word of God

The word asa (عسى) in the Arabic language means "perhaps" and is mixed with a sense of uncertainty. La'alla (لعل) means hope and expectation regarding matters whose future existence is not certain but is possible.

Now the question arises: the use of such words in human speech is completely natural, as humans are not aware of all matters; furthermore, their power is limited and they are not capable of doing everything they wish. But for Allah, who is fully aware of the past, future, and present, and has the power to do whatever He wills, how can the use of such words—which imply "ignorance" or "lack of power"—be conceived?

For this reason, many scholars believe that when such words are used in the speech of Allah, they lose their original meaning and acquire new meanings. For example, asa means "promise" and la'alla means "request."

However, the truth is that these words in the speech of Allah also retain their original meaning, and this does not necessitate ignorance or lack of power. Rather, these words are usually used in contexts where reaching a goal requires several prerequisites. When one or more of these prerequisites are met, one can never issue a definitive judgment on the existence of that goal; instead, it must be expressed as a probabilistic judgment. For example, the Holy Quran says:

وَ إِذا قُرِئَ الْقُرْآنُ فَاسْتَمِعُوا لَهُ وَ أَنْصِتُوا لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ‌: "And when the Quran is recited, listen to it and be silent that you may receive mercy." (Al-A'raf: 204).

It is clear that merely listening to the verses of the Quran does not make a person subject to Allah's mercy; rather, this is one of the prerequisites. Other prerequisites include understanding and comprehending the verses, and then applying the instructions contained within them. Therefore, in such cases, one cannot issue a definitive judgment on the attainment of the result based solely on the existence of one prerequisite; it must be expressed as a probabilistic judgment. In other words, such expressions in the Divine Speech are a type of "wake-up call" and a way to draw the listener's attention to the fact that, besides this prerequisite, other conditions and prerequisites are also necessary to reach the destination. For instance, to receive Allah's mercy, besides listening to the Quran, acting upon it is also necessary.

Regarding the verse under discussion, this point is also fully applicable, because the dismantling of the disbelievers' power does not happen solely through inviting the believers and encouraging them to Jihad; rather, following that, the implementation of other programs of Jihad is necessary to realize the final goal.

Therefore, there is no necessity to divert these words from their literal meaning when they are used in the Word of God. [^1]

[^1]: Raghib, in the book Al-Mufradat, has mentioned another possibility in the interpretation of such words (asa and the like), which is that these expressions are meant to give hope to the addressee and the listener, not to express the hope of the Speaker. To put it more clearly, when Allah says asa and la'alla, the meaning is not "I hope," but rather "you should hope."

Ayah

مَّن يَشْفَعْ شَفَـٰعَةً حَسَنَةً يَكُن لَّهُۥ نَصِيبٌ مِّنْهَا ۖ وَمَن يَشْفَعْ شَفَـٰعَةً سَيِّئَةً يَكُن لَّهُۥ كِفْلٌ مِّنْهَا ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّقِيتًا (٨٥)
Whoever intercedes for a good cause shall receive a share of it, and whoever intercedes for an evil cause shall share its burden, and Allah is prepotent over all things. (85)

Commentary

The Result of Encouraging Good or Evil

As mentioned in the commentary of the previous verse, the Quran states that every individual is primarily responsible for their own actions, not the actions of others. However, to prevent any misuse of this concept, this verse declares: "It is true that everyone is responsible for their own deeds, but any human being who prompts another toward a good deed shall have a share in it, and whoever invites another to an evil deed shall have a portion of its burden."

(مَنْ يَشْفَعْ شَفاعَةً حَسَنَةً يَكُنْ لَهُ نَصِيبٌ مِنْها وَ مَنْ يَشْفَعْ شَفاعَةً سَيِّئَةً يَكُنْ لَهُ كِفْلٌ مِنْها).

Therefore, an individual's responsibility for their own actions does not mean they should abandon inviting others toward the truth and struggling against corruption, nor should they transform the social spirit of Islam into individualism and alienation from society, retreating into their own shell.

The word shafa'at (intercession) is originally derived from the root shaf' (rhyming with naf'), meaning "even" or "pair." Thus, the joining of one thing to another is called shafa'at. Sometimes this joining occurs in the context of guidance, counseling, and direction (as in the above verse), in which case it carries the meaning of "enjoining what is good and forbidding what is evil" (and shafa'at sayyi'ah means enjoining what is evil and forbidding what is good).

However, if it pertains to saving sinners from the consequences of their actions, it means assisting sinful individuals who possess the merit and worthiness for intercession.

In other words, intercession is sometimes prior to the action, meaning guidance, and sometimes after the action, meaning salvation from the consequences of the deed; both are instances of joining one thing to another.

Furthermore, it should be noted that although the verse encompasses a general concept and includes every kind of invitation to good or evil, since it was revealed in the context of the verses of Jihad, "shafa'at hasanah" refers to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله encouragement toward Jihad, and "shafa'at sayyi'ah" refers to the hypocrites' encouragement to avoid Jihad, each of whom will bear a share of the outcome of these actions.

Additionally, the use of the word shafa'at in this context—where the discussion is about leadership (leading toward good or evil)—might be an allusion to the point that the words of a leader (whether leaders of virtue or vice) will only influence others if they do not claim superiority over them, but rather place themselves alongside them as a peer and a "pair." This is a factor that is extraordinarily effective in advancing social goals.

And if we see in several Quranic verses in Surahs Ash-Shu'ara, Al-A'raf, Hud, An-Naml, and Al-Ankabut that when referring to the Prophets and Divine Messengers sent to guide and lead nations, the term "akhuhum" or "akhahum" (their brother) is used, it also points to this very fact.

Another point is that regarding the encouragement of good deeds (shafa'at hasanah), the Quran says a "nasib" (share) reaches the encourager, while regarding "shafa'at sayyi'ah," it says a "kifl" reaches them. This difference in terminology is because nasib means an abundant share of useful and beneficial matters, whereas kifl means a portion of things that are base and evil. [^1] The above verse clarifies one of the fundamental logics of Islam regarding social issues and explicitly states that people are partners in the fate of each other's actions through intercession, encouragement, and guidance. Therefore, whenever a person's words, actions, or even silence cause a group to be encouraged toward a good or evil deed, the encourager will have a significant share in the results of that deed without anything being diminished from the share of the original doer.

In a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated:

“Whoever enjoins what is good or forbids what is evil, or directs toward good, or points to it, he is a partner [in it]; and whoever enjoins evil or directs toward it, or points to it, he is a partner [in it].”

This hadith mentions three stages for inviting people to good or evil: the stage of commanding (amr), the stage of directing (dalalat), and the stage of pointing (isharah), which are respectively the strong, medium, and weak stages. Accordingly, any form of involvement in prompting another toward a good or evil deed causes one to be a partner in its harvest and outcome in the same proportion.

According to this Islamic logic, it is not only the perpetrators of sin who are sinners; rather, all those who—by using various means of propaganda, or preparing the grounds, or even saying a small encouraging word—incite the perpetrators of sin to their work, are partners in it. Likewise, those who use such programs in the path of charities and virtues have a share in them.

From some narrations regarding the commentary of this verse, it appears that one of the meanings of shafa'at hasanah or sayyi'ah is to pray for good or evil for someone, which is considered a type of intercession before Allah. It is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said:

“Whoever prays for his Muslim brother in his absence, it is answered for him, and the angel says to him: ‘For you is twice as much,’ and that is the ‘nasib’ mentioned in the verse.” [^1] This interpretation does not contradict the previous one; rather, it is an expansion of the meaning of intercession. That is, any Muslim who helps another in any way—whether through invitation and encouragement to good, or through prayer before Allah, or by any other means—will be a partner in its result.

This Islamic program keeps the spirit of sociality and the refusal to remain in the stage of individuality alive among Muslims. It proves the reality that by being mindful of others and stepping in the path of their interests, a person never falls behind and their individual interests will not be endangered; rather, they will share in the results.

[^1]: Kifl (rhyming with tifl) originally means the rear part of an animal's back, which is uncomfortable to ride upon; for this reason, any sin or evil portion is called kifl. Also, a task that involves weight and hardship is called kafalat.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the noble verse.

At the end of the verse, He says: "Allah is Powerful and He preserves and accounts for your deeds, and He will give the appropriate reward for good and evil deeds."

(وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ مُقِيتاً).

It should be noted that muqit is originally from the root qut, meaning the food that preserves a person's life. Therefore, muqit, which is the active participle from the if'al form, means one who provides the sustenance (qut) of another. Since such a person is the preserver of their life, the word muqit is also used to mean "Preserver" (hafiz). Furthermore, a person who provides sustenance must certainly have the power to do so; for this reason, the word has also come to mean "Powerful" (muqtadir). Such a person surely keeps the account of those under them; hence, it has come to mean "Reckoner" (hasib). In the above verse, all these meanings can be intended by the word muqit.

Ayah

وَإِذَا حُيِّيتُم بِتَحِيَّةٍ فَحَيُّوا۟ بِأَحْسَنَ مِنْهَآ أَوْ رُدُّوهَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَسِيبًا (٨٦)
When you are greeted with a salute, greet with a better one than it, or return it; indeed Allah takes account of all things. (86)

Commentary

Respond to Every Kind of Affection

Although some commentators believe that the connection between this verse and the preceding ones lies in the fact that the previous verses discussed Jihad, and this verse commands that if enemies approach from a door of friendship and peace, you should also provide an appropriate response; however, it is clear that this connection does not prevent it from being a general and universal rule regarding all greetings and expressions of affection shown by various individuals.

At the beginning, the verse states: "When you are greeted with a greeting, respond with a better one, or at least return it equally."

(وَ إِذا حُيِّيتُمْ بِتَحِيَّةٍ فَحَيُّوا بِأَحْسَنَ مِنْها أَوْ رُدُّوها) The word "Tahiyyah" (تحيت) literally stems from the root "Hayat" (حيات) and means to pray for the life of another, whether this prayer takes the form of "Salamun Alayk" (May Allah keep you in safety), "Hayyak Allah" (May Allah keep you alive), or the like. However, it usually encompasses every type of expression of affection that individuals exchange through speech, the clearest instance of which is the act of "Salam" (greeting).

Yet, from certain narrations and commentaries, it is understood that practical expressions of affection are also included in the concept of "Tahiyyah." In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that:

المراد بالتحية فى الايه السلام و غيره من البر

"The meaning of 'Tahiyyah' in the verse is Salam and every kind of goodness." Furthermore, we read in a narration in the book Al-Manaqib: "A slave girl gifted a branch of flowers to Imam al-Hasan عليه السلام. In return, the Imam set her free. When asked about the reason for this act, he said: 'Allah has taught us this etiquette where He says:

وَ إِذا حُيِّيتُمْ بِتَحِيَّةٍ فَحَيُّوا بِأَحْسَنَ مِنْها

And then he added: The better greeting was indeed setting her free!'" Thus, the verse is a general rule regarding responding to every type of expression of affection, whether verbal or practical.

At the end of the verse, so that people may know that the nature of "greetings" and "responses," and their superiority or equality at every level and stage, is not hidden from Allah, He says: Allah keeps account of all things.

(إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ حَسِيباً) "Salam" is the great Islamic greeting. As far as we know, all nations of the world have some form of greeting when they meet to express affection for one another, which sometimes takes a verbal form and sometimes a practical one as a symbol of greeting. In Islam, "Salam" is one of the clearest greetings, and as mentioned, although the above verse has a broad meaning, a clear instance of it is giving Salam. Therefore, according to this verse, all Muslims are duty-bound to answer a Salam in a superior manner or at least equally.

It is also understood from the verses of the Quran that Salam is a type of Tahiyyah. In Surah An-Nur, verse 61, we read:

فَإِذا دَخَلْتُمْ بُيُوتاً فَسَلِّمُوا عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِكُمْ تَحِيَّةً مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ مُبارَكَةً طَيِّبَةً:

"When you enter houses, greet one another with a greeting from Allah, blessed and good."

In this verse, Salam is introduced as a divine greeting that is both blessed and pure. Additionally, it can be inferred that the meaning of "Salamun Alaykum" is originally "Salamullah Alaykum," meaning "May the peace of the Lord be upon you," or "May Allah keep you in safety," and "May you be in peace and security." For this reason, giving Salam is considered a declaration of friendship, peace, and the cessation of hostility and war.

It is also understood from some verses of the Quran that the greeting of the people of Paradise is also Salam:

أُوْلئِكَ يُجْزَوْنَ الْغُرْفَةَ بِما صَبَرُوا وَ يُلَقَّوْنَ فِيها تَحِيَّةً وَ سَلاماً.

"Those will be rewarded with the chamber for what they patiently endured, and they will be received therein with greetings and peace" (Al-Furqan: 75). And in verse 23 of Surah Ibrahim and verse 10 of Surah Yunus regarding the inhabitants of Paradise, we read: تَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيها سَلامٌ: "Their greeting therein will be, 'Peace!'"

Furthermore, it is understood from the Quranic verses that Tahiyyah in the sense of Salam (or something equivalent) existed among previous nations. As in Surah Adh-Dhariyat, verse 25, in the story of Ibrahim, it says that when the angels commissioned to punish the people of Lut entered upon him incognito, they greeted him with Salam, and he responded to their greeting with Salam:

إِذْ دَخَلُوا عَلَيْهِ فَقالُوا سَلاماً قالَ سَلامٌ قَوْمٌ مُنْكَرُونَ‌.

It is also evident from the poetry of the pre-Islamic Arabs that greeting via Salam existed in those days:

And if Layla al-Akhyaliyyah were to greet me, While stones and slabs lie between us, I would return her greeting with cheerfulness, or a cry From the side of the grave would echo back to her. [^1]

If we impartially compare this Islamic greeting—which contains attention to God, a prayer for the safety of the other party, and a declaration of peace and security—with other greetings common among various nations, its value becomes clearer to us.

In Islamic narrations, great emphasis has been placed on Salam, to the extent that it is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

من بدء بالكلام قبل السلام فلا تجيبوه‌

"Whoever begins speaking before Salam, do not answer him." [^2] It is also narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that Allah says:

[^1]: These verses are by a pre-Islamic poet named "Tawbah." [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Greeting (At-Taslim).

البخيل من يبخل بالسلام‌

"The miser is he who is miserly even with the Salam." [^1] And in another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read:

ان اللَّه عز و جل يحب افشاء السلام‌

"Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, loves the spreading of Salam." [^2] The meaning of "spreading Salam" is to give Salam to various people.

In the Hadiths, many etiquettes regarding Salam have been mentioned, including that Salam is not exclusive to those with whom one has a specific acquaintance. As we have in a Hadith where the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was asked: "Which action is best?" He replied:

تطعم الطعام و تقرء السلام على من عرفت و من لم تعرف‌

"Feed the hungry and give Salam to those you know and those you do not know." [^3] It is also mentioned in Hadiths that the one riding should greet the one walking, and those with more expensive mounts should greet those with cheaper ones. It seems this instruction is a form of combating the arrogance arising from wealth and specific material statuses. This is exactly the opposite of what is seen today, where greeting and Salam are considered the duty of lower-ranking individuals, giving it a form of colonialism, enslavement, and idolatry. Therefore, we read in the accounts of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he would greet everyone, even children.

Of course, this does not contradict the instruction found in some narrations that younger individuals should greet older ones, as this is a form of human etiquette and humility and has no connection to the issue of class differences or disparities in wealth and material status.

In some narrations, it is commanded not to greet usurers, the wicked (fasiq), the deviant, and the like; this itself is a form of combating corruption, unless greeting them serves as a means for acquaintance and an invitation to abandon evil.

Additionally, it should be noted that the meaning of responding with a "better" greeting is to follow the Salam with other phrases such as "wa Rahmatullah" (and the mercy of Allah) and "wa Rahmatullahi wa Barakatuh" (and the mercy of Allah and His blessings).

In the commentary Ad-Durr al-Manthur, we read that a person said to the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "As-Salamu Alayk." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied: "As-Salamu Alayka wa Rahmatullah." Another person said: "As-Salamu Alayka wa Rahmatullah." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied: "Wa Alaykas-Salamu wa Rahmatullahi wa Barakatuh." Another person said: "As-Salamu Alayka wa Rahmatullahi wa Barakatuh." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied: "Wa Alayk." When the person asked why the response was shortened, he replied: "The Quran says: Respond to a greeting in a better way, but you left nothing extra!" In reality, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله responded to the first and second persons in a "better" manner, but to the third person equally, because the phrase "Wa Alayk" means "May all that you said be upon you as well." [^1]

[^1] and [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Greeting (At-Taslim). [^3]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, under the verse.

Ayah

ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۗ وَمَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ حَدِيثًا (٨٧)
Allah—there is no god except Him—will surely gather you on the Day of Resurrection, in which there is no doubt; and who is more truthful in speech than Allah? (87)

Commentary

The above verse serves as a completion of the previous verses and an introduction to the subsequent ones. In the preceding verse, after the command regarding "returning a greeting," it was stated: Allah keeps account of all your deeds. In this verse, reference is made to the issue of the Resurrection and the general court of the servants on the Day of Judgment, blending it with the principle of Monotheism and the Oneness of God, which is another pillar of faith. It states: "There is no deity except Him, and He will surely gather you all together on the Day of Resurrection, the Day about which there is no doubt."

(اللَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ‌).

The expression "He will surely gather you" (لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ) indicates that the Resurrection of all human beings will occur on a single day, just as in the end of Surah Maryam, verses 93 to 95, this reality is pointed out: that all servants of Allah, including the inhabitants of the Earth and the inhabitants of other spheres, will all be resurrected on one day.

The expression "there is no doubt in it" (لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ) regarding the Day of Resurrection in this verse and several other instances in the verses of the Quran is, in fact, an allusion to the definitive and certain proofs that foretell the existence of such a day—such as the "Law of Perfection," the "Wisdom and Philosophy of Creation," and the "Law of Divine Justice," which have been discussed in detail in the discourse on the Hereafter.

And at the end, to emphasize the matter, He says: "And who is more truthful than Allah in speech?" (وَ مَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ اللَّهِ حَدِيثاً).

Therefore, any promise He gives regarding the Day of Resurrection or otherwise should not be a place for doubt; for falsehood stems either from ignorance or from weakness and need. However, Allah, who is the most knowledgeable of all and the most self-sufficient of all, is more truthful than anyone, and fundamentally, falsehood has no meaning for Him.

Ayah

۞ فَمَا لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ فِئَتَيْنِ وَٱللَّهُ أَرْكَسَهُم بِمَا كَسَبُوٓا۟ ۚ أَتُرِيدُونَ أَن تَهْدُوا۟ مَنْ أَضَلَّ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ سَبِيلًا (٨٨)
Why should you be two groups concerning the hypocrites, while Allah has made them relapse [into unfaith] because of their deeds? Do you desire to guide someone Allah has led astray? Whomever Allah leads astray, you will never find any way for him. (88)

Occasion of Revelation

According to a report by a group of commentators from Ibn Abbas, a number of people in Mecca had ostensibly become Muslims, but in reality, they were among the ranks of the hypocrites. For this reason, they were unwilling to migrate to Medina and practically remained supporters and backers of the idolaters. However, they were eventually forced to leave Mecca (and come near Medina; perhaps they did this for the purpose of espionage due to their special position). They were pleased that the Muslims considered them as one of their own, and naturally, entering Medina would not pose a problem for them.

The Muslims became aware of the situation, but soon a disagreement arose among them regarding how to deal with this group. Some believed that this group should be rejected because they were actually supporters of the enemies of Islam. However, some superficial and simple-hearted individuals opposed this plan and said: "How strange! How can we fight against those who have testified to the Oneness of God and the Prophethood of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله? And how can we consider their blood permissible just because they did not migrate?" The above verse was revealed, rebuking the second group for this mistake and then providing guidance ^1.

Commentary

Given the above occasion of revelation, the connection between this verse and the subsequent verses with the verses previously mentioned regarding the hypocrites is perfectly clear.

At the beginning of the verse, He says: "What is the matter with you that you have become two groups regarding the hypocrites, each judging in a different way?" (فَما لَكُمْ فِي الْمُنافِقِينَ فِئَتَيْنِ‌) [^2]. This means that regarding these individuals—who have revealed their hypocrisy by abandoning migration, practically cooperating with the polytheists, and failing to join the ranks of the mujahideen of Islam—no one should doubt their fate. These are certainly first-rate hypocrites, and their actions are living testimony to their lack of faith. So why are some deceived by their profession of Monotheism and faith? And why do they stand up to intercede for them, even though it was mentioned in previous verses that "whoever intercedes for an evil cause shall have a share of its burden," and in this way, they make themselves partners in their grim fate?

Then He says: "Because of the ugly and disgraceful deeds they have committed, Allah has withdrawn His success and support from them and has completely overturned their thoughts, like someone who stands on his head instead of standing on his feet" (وَ اللَّهُ أَرْكَسَهُمْ بِما كَسَبُوا) ^1. Furthermore, from the phrase "because of what they earned" (بما كسبوا), it is understood that deviations from the path of guidance, prosperity, and salvation are the result of man's own actions. If this action is attributed to Allah, it is because Allah is Wise and He punishes everyone according to their deeds and rewards them according to their merit and worthiness.

At the end of the verse, addressing the simple-hearted individuals who were supporting this group of hypocrites, He says: "Do you wish to guide those whom Allah has deprived of guidance due to their evil deeds, while such individuals have no way toward guidance?"

(أَ تُرِيدُونَ أَنْ تَهْدُوا مَنْ أَضَلَّ اللَّهُ وَ مَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللَّهُ فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لَهُ سَبِيلًا). This is because it is an undying Divine Sunnah (law) that the effect of no one's actions is ever separated from them. How can you expect individuals whose thoughts are polluted, whose hearts are filled with hypocrisy, and whose actions support the enemies of Allah to be included in guidance? This is a groundless and misplaced expectation [^2].

^1: Regarding this verse and the subsequent verses, other occasions of revelation have been mentioned, including that some considered it related to the event of the Battle of Uhud. However, the subsequent verses that refer to the issue of migration are not consistent with that and are more suitable for the occasion of revelation we mentioned above.

[^2]: The above sentence actually has an omitted part that becomes clear in light of the rest of the sentence; the original sentence was actually: "فما لكم تفرقتم فى المنافقين فئتين" (What is the matter with you that you have divided into two groups regarding the hypocrites).

[^2]: In the first volume of this Tafsir, on page 101, a detailed discussion regarding guidance and misguidance was presented.

Ayah

وَدُّوا۟ لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ كَمَا كَفَرُوا۟ فَتَكُونُونَ سَوَآءً ۖ فَلَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ حَتَّىٰ يُهَاجِرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (٨٩)
They are eager that you should disbelieve like they have disbelieved, so that you all become alike. So do not make friends [with anyone] from among them, until they migrate in the way of Allah. But if they turn their backs, seize them and kill them wherever you find them, and do not take from among them friends or helpers, (89)

Commentary

Following the previous verse regarding the hypocrites whom some simple-hearted Muslims rose to support and intercede for—while the Quran declared their alienation from Islam—this verse states: The darkness within them is to such an extent that not only are they disbelievers themselves, but "they wish that you would disbelieve as they disbelieved so that you may be alike."

(وَدُّوا لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ كَما كَفَرُوا فَتَكُونُونَ سَواءً) Therefore, they are even worse than ordinary disbelievers; for ordinary disbelievers are not thieves and plunderers of the beliefs of others, but these individuals are, and they engage in persistent activities to destroy the faith of others.

Now that they are such, you Muslims must never take friends from among them (فَلا تَتَّخِذُوا مِنْهُمْ أَوْلِياءَ).

Unless they reconsider their conduct and cease their hypocrisy and destruction, the sign of which is that they migrate from the center of disbelief and hypocrisy to the center of Islam (from Mecca to Medina).

(حَتَّى يُهاجِرُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ‌).

"But if they refuse to migrate, know that they have not abandoned their disbelief and hypocrisy, and their profession of Islam is only for the purpose of espionage and subversion. In this case, you may seize them wherever you find them, or if necessary, put them to death."

(فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَخُذُوهُمْ وَ اقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ‌) And at the end of the verse, it emphasizes once more that you should never take a friend, protector, or helper from among them.

(وَ لا تَتَّخِذُوا مِنْهُمْ وَلِيًّا وَ لا نَصِيراً) This severity of action shown in the above verse toward this group of hypocrites is because there is no other way to save a living society, which is moving along the path of a reformative revolution, from the clutches of enemies disguised as friends and dangerous spies.

It is noteworthy that while Islam has taken non-Muslim individuals such as Jews and Christians under its protection under certain conditions and does not allow any harassment toward them, it has exercised such severity regarding this group of hypocrites. Despite their outward profession of Islam, He has issued the command for their captivity and even execution if necessary. This is for no reason other than that such individuals, under the cover of Islam, can strike blows that no enemy is capable of!

Question

It might be asked: The Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the hypocrites was such that He never issued a command for their execution, lest His enemies accuse Him of killing His companions, or lest some abuse this matter to settle personal scores with individuals by labeling them as hypocrites and killing them.

Answer

It must be noted that the Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was only regarding the hypocrites of Medina and their like, who adhered to the outward practices of Islam and did not have an explicit conflict with Islam and Muslims. However, those who, like the hypocrites of Mecca, had clear cooperation with the enemies of Islam, were not included in that ruling.

Ayah

إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍۭ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُم مِّيثَـٰقٌ أَوْ جَآءُوكُمْ حَصِرَتْ صُدُورُهُمْ أَن يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ أَوْ يُقَـٰتِلُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَسَلَّطَهُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ فَلَقَـٰتَلُوكُمْ ۚ فَإِنِ ٱعْتَزَلُوكُمْ فَلَمْ يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ وَأَلْقَوْا۟ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَمَ فَمَا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ سَبِيلًا (٩٠)
excepting those who join a people between whom and you there is a treaty, or such as come to you with hearts reluctant to fight you, or to fight their own people. Had Allah wished, He would have imposed them upon you, and then they would have surely fought you. So if they keep out of your way and do not fight you, and offer you peace, then Allah does not allow you any course [of action] against them. (90)

Occasion of Revelation

From various narrations regarding the occasion of the revelation of this verse, as cited by commentators in different exegeses, it is understood that there were two tribes among the Arab tribes named "Bani Damrah" and "Ashja'." The first tribe had entered into a non-aggression pact with the Muslims, and the Ashja' tribe was also allied with the Bani Damrah.

Some Muslims were apprehensive about the power of the Bani Damrah tribe and feared they might break the covenant; therefore, they suggested to the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that the Muslims should attack them before they could launch an assault. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

كلا فانهم ابر العرب بالوالدين و اوصلهم للرحم و اوفاهم بالعهد

"No, never do such a thing; for among all the Arab tribes, they are the most dutiful to their parents, the most kind toward their kindred and relatives, and the most faithful to their covenants and treaties!" After some time, the Muslims received news that the Ashja' tribe, led by "Mas'ud ibn Rujaylah" and numbering seven hundred people, had approached the vicinity of Medina. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent representatives to them to learn the purpose of their journey. They stated, "We have come to conclude a non-aggression treaty with Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله." When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw this, He ordered a large quantity of dates to be brought to them as a gift, and then He met with them. They stated, "On one hand, we do not have the strength to fight your enemies because our numbers are few, and on the other hand, we have neither the power nor the desire to fight you, as our location is near yours. Therefore, we have come to establish a non-aggression pact with you." At this moment, the above verses were revealed and provided the necessary instructions to the Muslims in this regard.

It is also understood from some narrations that a portion of the verse was revealed concerning the "Bani Mudlij" tribe, who came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and stated that they would neither join his voice nor take steps against him, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله concluded a non-aggression pact with them.

Commentary

Welcoming the Proposal of Peace

Following the command for severity against the hypocrites who cooperated closely with the enemies of Islam, this verse instructs that two groups are exempt from this rule:

1- Those who are connected to a people with whom you have a treaty. (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ إِلى‌ قَوْمٍ بَيْنَكُمْ وَ بَيْنَهُمْ مِيثاقٌ‌)

2- Those who, due to their specific situation, find themselves in a condition where they neither feel the strength to fight you, nor the ability to cooperate with you and fight their own tribe. (أَوْ جاؤُكُمْ حَصِرَتْ صُدُورُهُمْ أَنْ يُقاتِلُوكُمْ أَوْ يُقاتِلُوا قَوْمَهُمْ‌).

It is clear that the first group must be exempt from this rule out of respect for the treaty, and as for the second group, although they are not excused and should join the truth after recognizing it, since they have declared neutrality, attacking them would be contrary to the principles of justice and chivalry.

Then, so that the Muslims do not become arrogant in the face of these striking victories and do not attribute them solely to their own military power and initiative, and also to stimulate their human feelings toward this group of neutrals, He says: "If Allah willed, He could have given them (the group of the weak) power over you, and they would have fought you." (وَ لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ لَسَلَّطَهُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ فَلَقاتَلُوكُمْ‌)

Therefore, always remember Allah during victories and never become arrogant by your own strength, nor consider showing leniency toward the weak as a loss for yourselves.
