At the end of the verse, He emphasizes the latter group once more and explains with more detail: "If they withdraw from you and do not fight you and offer you peace, then Allah has allowed you no way against them, and you are duty-bound to shake the hand that is extended toward you for the purpose of peace." (فَإِنِ اعْتَزَلُوكُمْ فَلَمْ يُقاتِلُوكُمْ وَ أَلْقَوْا إِلَيْكُمُ السَّلَمَ فَما جَعَلَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ سَبِيلًا).

A noteworthy point is that the Quran, in this verse and several others, mentions the proposal of peace with the expression "casting peace" (إلقاء السلم), which may refer to the fact that the parties in a conflict usually distance themselves from one another before making peace and even propose peace with caution, as if they are standing far apart and "casting" or "throwing" this proposal toward each other.

Ayah

سَتَجِدُونَ ءَاخَرِينَ يُرِيدُونَ أَن يَأْمَنُوكُمْ وَيَأْمَنُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ كُلَّ مَا رُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلْفِتْنَةِ أُرْكِسُوا۟ فِيهَا ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ يَعْتَزِلُوكُمْ وَيُلْقُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَمَ وَيَكُفُّوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَهُمْ فَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ ثَقِفْتُمُوهُمْ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكُمْ جَعَلْنَا لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنًا مُّبِينًا (٩١)
You will find others desiring to be secure from you, and secure from their own people; yet whenever they are called back to polytheism, they relapse into it. So if they do not keep out of your way, nor offer you peace, nor keep their hands off [from fighting], then seize them and kill them wherever you confront them, and it is such against whom We have given you a clear sanction. (91)

Occasion of Revelation

Various occasions of revelation have been narrated for the above verse, one of the most famous being the following:

A group of people from Mecca would come to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and outwardly profess Islam out of deception and trickery. However, as soon as they were in the presence of the Quraysh and their idols, they would engage in the worship and adoration of those idols. In this way, they sought to remain secure from both the side of Islam and the side of the Quraysh, profiting from both and suffering harm from neither—playing a "double game" between these two groups. The above verse was revealed and commanded the Muslims to exercise severity toward this group.

Commentary

The Punishment for Those Who Play a Double Game!

Here, we encounter another group that stands in direct contrast to the group mentioned in the previous verse, regarding whom the command for peace was given. These are individuals who, in order to preserve their own interests, wish to have freedom of action among both the Muslims and the polytheists. To achieve this aim, they adopt a path of treachery and deception, professing cooperation and shared ideology with both sides.

(سَتَجِدُونَ آخَرِينَ يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يَأْمَنُوكُمْ وَ يَأْمَنُوا قَوْمَهُمْ‌) "You will find others who wish to be secure from you and secure from their own people."

For this very reason, "whenever the field for mischief-making and idolatry arises, all their plans are overturned and they plunge headlong into it!" (كُلَّما رُدُّوا إِلَى الْفِتْنَةِ أُرْكِسُوا فِيها).

These people are the exact opposite of the previous group; for while those sought to avoid conflict with the Muslims, these individuals harbor the intent to engage with them.

The former proposed peace with the Muslims, whereas these have the intent of war.

The former refrained from harassing and harming the Muslims, but these do not refrain.

These three differences—which are alluded to in the phrase "فَإِنْ لَمْ يَعْتَزِلُوكُمْ وَ يُلْقُوا إِلَيْكُمُ السَّلَمَ وَ يَكُفُّوا أَيْدِيَهُمْ‌" (So if they do not withdraw from you, nor offer you peace, nor restrain their hands)—have caused the ruling regarding them to be completely separate from the previous group. Muslims are commanded that "wherever you find them, capture them, and in case of resistance, kill them."

(فَخُذُوهُمْ وَ اقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ ثَقِفْتُمُوهُمْ‌) "So seize them and kill them wherever you find them."

Therefore, since the argument has been sufficiently established against them, He says at the end of the verse: "These are the ones against whom We have given you a clear authority."

(وَ أُولئِكُمْ جَعَلْنا لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطاناً مُبِيناً)

This "authority" (سلطان) could be from a logical perspective, as the logic of the Muslims was completely victorious over the polytheists, or from an outward and physical perspective, because these verses were revealed at a time when the Muslims had become sufficiently powerful.

The expression "ثَقِفْتُمُوهُمْ‌" in the above verse may point to a subtle nuance, as this word is derived from the root thaqafah, meaning "to come upon something with precision and skill." It differs from wajadtumuhum, which is from the root wijdan and means "to find in a general sense." It is as if this group of "double-dealing hypocrites"—who are the most dangerous of the hypocritical factions—cannot be easily identified or trapped. Thus, He says: "If you come upon them with skill and precision, execute Allah's command regarding them," indicating that apprehending them requires sufficient care and vigilance.

Ayah

وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ أَن يَقْتُلَ مُؤْمِنًا إِلَّا خَطَـًٔا ۚ وَمَن قَتَلَ مُؤْمِنًا خَطَـًٔا فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ وَدِيَةٌ مُّسَلَّمَةٌ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن يَصَّدَّقُوا۟ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ مِن قَوْمٍ عَدُوٍّ لَّكُمْ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ ۖ وَإِن كَانَ مِن قَوْمٍۭ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُم مِّيثَـٰقٌ فَدِيَةٌ مُّسَلَّمَةٌ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ وَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ ۖ فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ تَوْبَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (٩٢)
A believer may not kill another believer, unless it is by mistake. Anyone who kills a believer by mistake should set free a believing slave, and pay blood-money to his family, unless they remit it in charity. If he belongs to a people that are hostile to you but is a believer, then a believing slave is to be set free. And if he belongs to a people with whom you have a treaty, the blood-money is to be paid to his family and a believing slave is to be set free. He who cannot afford [to pay the blood-money], must fast two successive months as a penance from Allah, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (92)

Circumstances of Revelation

One of the idolaters of Mecca named "Harith bin Yazid," with the assistance of "Abu Jahl," for a long time tortured a Muslim named "Ayyash bin Abi Rabi'ah" for the crime of leaning toward Islam. After the migration of the Muslims to Medina, Ayyash also migrated to Medina and joined the ranks of the Muslims.

By chance, one day in one of the neighborhoods around Medina, he encountered his former torturer, Harith bin Yazid. Taking advantage of the opportunity, he killed him, believing he had struck down an enemy, while unaware that Harith had repented, become a Muslim, and was on his way to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله. When the incident was reported to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, the verse was revealed, explaining the ruling for a killing that occurs by mistake and error.

Commentary

Rulings on Accidental Homicide

Since the previous verses granted Muslims the freedom of action to crush hypocrites and dangerous internal enemies, this verse and the following one explain the rulings for accidental and intentional homicide. This is to prevent anyone from abusing this law to settle personal scores with those they dislike under the guise of them being hypocrites, or from shedding innocent blood through recklessness. In the matter of shedding blood, which is an exceptionally important and responsibility-laden subject in Islam, all necessary aspects must be observed.

At the beginning of this verse, where the ruling for accidental homicide is stated, It says: "It is not for a believer to kill a believer except by mistake."

(وَ ما كانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ أَنْ يَقْتُلَ مُؤْمِناً إِلَّا خَطَأً) This expression actually points to the fact that, fundamentally, a believer never allows himself to stain his hands with the blood of an innocent person. This is because, within the sanctuary of faith, all individuals are like members of a single body. Is it ever possible for one limb of a human body to destroy or harm another limb except by mistake? Therefore, those who intend such an act do not possess true faith and are unaware of the reality of faith.

The phrase إِلَّا خَطَأً (except by mistake) does not mean they are permitted to perform this act by mistake, for a mistake is not predictable, and a person is not aware of their error at the moment it happens. Rather, the meaning is that believers will never be tainted by such a great sin except in the case of an error.

Then, It explains the penalty and atonement (kaffarah) for accidental homicide in three stages:

The first case is when "an innocent person killed by mistake belongs to a Muslim family. In this case, the killer must do two things: first, free a Muslim slave, and second, pay the blood money (diyah) of the deceased to the owners of the blood (the heirs)."

(وَ مَنْ قَتَلَ مُؤْمِناً خَطَأً فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُؤْمِنَةٍ وَ دِيَةٌ مُسَلَّمَةٌ إِلى‌ أَهْلِهِ‌).

Unless the family of the deceased waives the blood money out of their own satisfaction (إِلَّا أَنْ يَصَّدَّقُوا). The second case is when the deceased belongs to a family that has hostility and enmity toward the Muslims. "In this case, the atonement for accidental homicide is only the freeing of a slave," and paying blood money to a group whose financial strengthening would be considered a danger to Muslims is not necessary. Furthermore, Islam has severed the connection of this individual with his family, who are all enemies of Islam; therefore, there is no place for compensation for loss.

(فَإِنْ كانَ مِنْ قَوْمٍ عَدُوٍّ لَكُمْ وَ هُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُؤْمِنَةٍ).

The third case is when "the family of the deceased are from the disbelievers who have a treaty with the Muslims. In this case, out of respect for the treaty, in addition to freeing a Muslim slave, the blood money must be paid to his survivors."

(وَ إِنْ كانَ مِنْ قَوْمٍ بَيْنَكُمْ وَ بَيْنَهُمْ مِيثاقٌ فَدِيَةٌ مُسَلَّمَةٌ إِلى‌ أَهْلِهِ وَ تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُؤْمِنَةٍ).

There is discussion among commentators as to whether the deceased in this case, like the previous two cases, is a believer or includes both a believer and a protected non-Muslim (Dhimmi). However, the apparent meaning of the verse and the narrations regarding its interpretation suggest that it also refers to a "believing victim." But can the blood money of such a Muslim victim be given to non-Muslim heirs, given that a non-Muslim does not inherit from a Muslim?

From the apparent meaning of the verse, it is understood that the said blood money must be given to his heirs even if they are disbelievers, and this is because of the treaty and covenant they have with the Muslims. However, since a disbeliever never inherits from a Muslim, a group of commentators believe that the meaning of the above phrase is that his blood money should only be given to his Muslim heirs, not the non-Muslim heirs. Some narrations also point to this subject. Yet, the apparent meaning of the phrase مِنْ قَوْمٍ بَيْنَكُمْ وَ بَيْنَهُمْ مِيثاقٌ (from a people with whom you have a treaty) is that the heirs of the deceased are not among the Muslims, because Muslims do not have a "special treaty" with one another (reflect on this).

At the end of the verse, regarding those who do not have access to freeing a slave (meaning they lack the financial power or cannot find a slave to free), It says:

"Such persons must fast for two consecutive months."

(فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتابِعَيْنِ‌).

And finally, It says: "This substitution of freeing a slave with two months of fasting is a kind of concession and Divine repentance, or all that was mentioned in the verse as atonement for accidental homicide is for the fulfillment of a Divine repentance, and Allah is always aware of everything and all His commands are according to wisdom."

(تَوْبَةً مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً).

In the above verse, there are several points to be noted:

1- Here, three matters are stated to compensate for accidental homicide, each of which is to compensate for a type of loss resulting from this act. First is the freeing of a slave, which is actually a kind of compensation for the social loss of the killing of a believing individual.

The second is the payment of blood money (diyah), which is actually a kind of compensation for the economic loss inflicted upon the family by the killing of a person. As we have said before, "diyah" is never the actual price of a human's blood, for the blood of an innocent human is beyond any price; rather, it is a type of compensation for the family's economic loss.

The third is the matter of two consecutive months of fasting, which is to compensate for the moral and spiritual loss that affects the accidental killer.

Of course, it should be noted that fasting for two consecutive months is the duty of those who do not have access to freeing a believing slave. That is, in the first instance, only freeing a slave is sufficient, and in the next stage, if they cannot, they must fast. However, it should be noted that freeing a slave is also considered a type of worship and will therefore have the spiritual effect of worship on the soul of the one who frees the slave.

2- In the case where the survivors of the deceased are Muslims, the phrase إِلَّا أَنْ يَصَّدَّقُوا (unless they waive it as charity) is mentioned, but in the case of those who are not Muslims, this phrase is not mentioned. The reason for this is clear, because in the first case, the grounds for such an act exist, but in the second case, such grounds do not exist. Furthermore, Muslims should not, as far as possible, be under obligation to non-Muslims in these matters.

3- It is noteworthy that in the first case, where the survivors are Muslims, "freeing a slave" is mentioned first and then "blood money." However, in the third case, where they are not Muslims, "blood money" comes first. Perhaps this difference in expression indicates that in the case of Muslims, a delay in blood money often does not have an undesirable reaction, whereas in the case of non-Muslims, blood money should be paid before anything else to extinguish the fire of conflict, so that enemies do not interpret it as a breach of treaty.

4- In the noble verse, no mention is made of the amount of blood money, and its explanation is left to the Sunnah. According to it, the full blood money is one thousand mithqals of gold, or one hundred camels, or two hundred cows, or the price of these animals if agreed upon. (Of course, the determination of gold or certain animals as blood money follows an Islamic tradition that chooses its scales from natural matters rather than artificial or conventional ones, so they do not change with the passage of time).

5- Some might object that "error" does not deserve punishment; why has Islam given it so much importance when the perpetrator has committed no sin? The answer to the objection is clear, because the matter of blood is not a simple matter. With this severe ruling, Islam intended for people to exercise the utmost care and caution so that no killing occurs, even by mistake, as many errors are preventable. Furthermore, people should know that they can never exonerate themselves by merely claiming an error in killing. The final phrase of the verse (تَوْبَةً مِنَ اللَّهِ ...) may refer to this very point: that mistakes usually stem from a lack of precision, and therefore, in important matters like homicide, they must be compensated in some way so that Divine repentance may include the perpetrators.

Ayah

وَمَن يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِنًا مُّتَعَمِّدًا فَجَزَآؤُهُۥ جَهَنَّمُ خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا وَغَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَلَعَنَهُۥ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُۥ عَذَابًا عَظِيمًا (٩٣)
Should anyone kill a believer intentionally, his requital shall be hell, to remain in it [forever]; Allah shall be wrathful at him and curse him and He shall prepare for him a great punishment. (93)

Occasion of Revelation

"Miqyas ibn Subabah al-Kinani," who was one of the Muslims, found the slain body of his brother "Hisham" in the neighborhood of "Bani al-Najjar." He reported the incident to the Prophet ﷺ. The Prophet ﷺ sent him along with "Qays ibn Hilal al-Fihri" to the elders of "Bani al-Najjar" and instructed that if they knew the killer of Hisham, they should hand him over to his brother Miqyas; and if they did not know him, they should pay the blood money (diyah). Since they did not know the killer, they paid the blood money to the legal heir, who accepted it and set out toward Medina accompanied by Qays ibn Hilal. On the way, remnants of Jahiliyyah (pre-Islamic ignorance) thoughts incited Miqyas, and he said to himself: "Accepting blood money is a cause of humiliation and abasement." Consequently, he killed his travel companion—who was from the tribe of Bani al-Najjar—in revenge for his brother's blood, fled toward Mecca, and apostatized from Islam.

In response to this treachery, the Prophet ﷺ declared his blood permissible (to be shed), and the above verse was revealed on this occasion, in which the punishment for intentional murder is stated.

Commentary

The Punishment for Intentional Murder

After explaining the ruling on accidental homicide, this verse points to the punishment of one who kills a person of faith intentionally.

Since homicide is one of the greatest crimes and dangerous sins, and if it is not combated, security—which is one of the most important conditions for a healthy society—will be completely destroyed, the Quran has mentioned it with extraordinary importance in various verses. It goes so far as to introduce the unjustified killing of one human being as equivalent to killing all the people on earth:

(مَنْ قَتَلَ نَفْساً بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ أَوْ فَسادٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَكَأَنَّما قَتَلَ النَّاسَ جَمِيعاً) "Whoever kills a soul unless for a soul or for corruption [done] in the land—it is as if he had slain mankind entirely" [^1]. For this reason, in the verse under discussion, four severe punishments and penalties in the Hereafter (in addition to the issue of Qisas [retaliation], which is a worldly punishment) are mentioned for those who intentionally kill a person of faith:

1- Khulud, meaning remaining eternally in the fire of Hell. (وَ مَنْ يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِناً مُتَعَمِّداً فَجَزاؤُهُ جَهَنَّمُ خالِداً فِيها).

2- Divine Wrath and Anger (وَ غَضِبَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ‌).

3- Exclusion from Allah's mercy (و لعنه).

4- Preparing a great punishment for him (وأَعَدَّ لَهُ عَذاباً عَظِيماً).

[^1]: Surah Al-Ma'idah, Verse 32.

In this way, regarding the punishment in the Hereafter, the maximum intensification has been applied to intentional murder, such that in no other instance in the Quran has such a severe punishment been stated. As for the worldly penalty for intentional murder, it is Qisas, the details of which were discussed under Verse 179 of Surah Al-Baqarah in Volume 1, page 442.

Does Homicide Result in Eternal Punishment?

A question arises here: "Khulud" or eternal punishment is reserved for those who leave this world without faith, whereas an intentional murderer might possess faith and even become regretful, sincerely repenting for the great sin he committed and compensating for the past as much as he is able.

In response to this question, it can be said:

1- The meaning of killing a believer in the verse is to kill a person because of their faith, or to consider killing them permissible (mubah). It is clear that such a killing is a sign of the killer's disbelief (kufr), and its consequence is eternity in punishment. A hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام has also been narrated to this effect. [^1]

2- There is also the possibility that killing innocent people of faith causes a person to leave this world without faith, and the success (tawfiq) of repentance is not granted to him, and because of this, he becomes trapped in eternal punishment.

3- It is also possible that the meaning of khulud in this verse is a very long duration of punishment, not necessarily an everlasting one.

[^1]: In the book al-Kafi and Tafsir al-Ayyashi, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (ع) under this verse that he said: "Whoever kills a believer for his religion, that is the intentional one about whom Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said in His Book: 'and He has prepared for him a great punishment.'"

Another question raised here is whether intentional murder is fundamentally capable of being repented for. A group of commentators explicitly give a negative answer to this question and say:

According to the above verse, homicide is not capable of repentance at all, and in some narrations mentioned under the verse, this meaning is also pointed out: "There is no repentance for him" (la tawbata lahu).

However, what is derived from the spirit of Islamic teachings, the narrations of the great leaders of the religion, and the philosophy of repentance—which is the foundation of education and protection from sin in the future of life—is that there is no sin that is not capable of being repented for, even if the repentance for some sins is very difficult and has heavy conditions. The Holy Quran says:

إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَ يَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ (An-Nisa: 48): "Indeed, Allah does not forgive association with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills." We previously mentioned under this very verse that it speaks about the forgiveness of sins through intercession and the like; otherwise, even the sin of Shirk (polytheism) is forgivable through repentance and returning to Monotheism and Islam, just as most Muslims of the early Islamic era were initially polytheists and then repented, and Allah forgave their sins. Therefore, Shirk is the only sin that is not forgiven without repentance, but with repentance, all sins, even Shirk, are forgivable, as we read in Surah Az-Zumar, Verses 53 and 54:

... إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ وَ أَنِيبُوا إِلى‌ رَبِّكُمْ وَ أَسْلِمُوا لَهُ‌: "...Indeed, Allah forgives all sins. Indeed, it is He who is the Forgiving, the Merciful. And return [in repentance] to your Lord and submit to Him."

And the fact that some commentators have said that the verses related to the forgiveness of all sins in the light of repentance are so-called "General" ('Amm) and are subject to "Specification" (Takhsis) is not correct. This is because the language of these verses, which are in the position of bestowing favor upon sinners and are accompanied by various emphases, is not subject to specification (in technical terms, they "refuse specification").

Furthermore, if indeed someone who committed intentional murder were to completely despair of Allah's forgiveness and remain under curse and eternal punishment forever (even after emphatic repentance and compensating for his foul deed with numerous good deeds), there would be no reason for him to spend the remainder of his life in great obedience to God and to cease from transgressive acts or even repeated murders. How would this be consistent with the spirit of the teachings of the Prophets, who came for the education of humanity at every stage? In Islamic history, we also see that the Holy Prophet ﷺ forgave dangerous sinners such as "Wahshi," the killer of Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib, and accepted his repentance. It cannot be said that homicide in a state of Shirk and faith differs so much that it is forgiven in one state and not forgivable in the other by any means.

Fundamentally, as we said, we have no sin greater than Shirk, and we know that even this sin is forgiven with repentance and the acceptance of Islam. How can it be believed that the sin of murder is not forgivable even with sincere repentance?

But let there be no mistake; what we said above does not mean that intentional murder is a small or insignificant matter, or that one can repent for it so easily. On the contrary, sincere repentance from this major sin is very difficult and requires compensation for this act, and compensating for it is no easy task. [^1] Therefore, our point is only that the path of repentance is not closed to such individuals entirely.

[^1]: In narrations, expressions regarding the importance of killing innocent and faithful individuals have been mentioned that make one's back tremble. In a hadith from the Holy Prophet ﷺ, it is narrated that he said: "The passing away of the world is easier for Allah than the killing of a Muslim man." He also said: "If a man were killed in the East and another was pleased with it in the West, he would be a partner in his blood." (Al-Manar, Vol. 5, p. 361).

Types of Homicide

The jurists (Fuqaha), in the books of Qisas and Diyat (blood money) within the books of jurisprudence, inspired by Islamic verses and narrations, have divided homicide into three types: "Intentional Murder" (Qatl al-Amd), "Quasi-Intentional Homicide" (Qatl Shibh al-Amd), and "Accidental Homicide" (Qatl al-Khata).

Intentional Murder is a killing that occurs with prior intent and by using means of killing (such as a person using a weapon, wood, stone, or hand with the intention of killing another).

Quasi-Intentional Homicide is when there is no intent to kill, but there is an intent to perform an action upon the victim that leads to death without prior consideration—such as someone intentionally beating another without the intent to kill him, but the blow happens to lead to death.

Accidental Homicide is when there is no intent to kill nor an intent to perform any action upon the victim—such as when someone wants to hunt an animal, but the arrow goes astray and hits a human, killing him.

Each of these three types has detailed rulings mentioned in the books of jurisprudence.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَتَبَيَّنُوا۟ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ لِمَنْ أَلْقَىٰٓ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمَ لَسْتَ مُؤْمِنًا تَبْتَغُونَ عَرَضَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا فَعِندَ ٱللَّهِ مَغَانِمُ كَثِيرَةٌ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كُنتُم مِّن قَبْلُ فَمَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَتَبَيَّنُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا (٩٤)
O you who have faith! When you issue forth in the way of Allah, try to ascertain: do not say to someone who offers you peace, ‘You are not a believer,’ seeking the transitory wares of the life of this world. Yet with Allah are plenteous gains. You too were such earlier, but Allah did you a favour. Therefore, do ascertain. Allah is indeed well aware of what you do. (94)

Occasion of Revelation

In Islamic traditions and commentaries, several accounts regarding the occasion of revelation for the above verse have been mentioned, which are more or less similar. Among them is that after returning from the Battle of Khaybar, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent Usama ibn Zayd with a group of Muslims toward a tribe of Jews living in one of the villages of "Fadak," to invite them to Islam or to accept the conditions of dhimma (protection).

One of the Jews, named "Mirdas," who had learned of the arrival of the Muslim army, placed his property and children under the shelter of a mountain and rushed to meet the Muslims while testifying to the Oneness of God and the Prophethood of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Usama ibn Zayd, assuming that the Jewish man was professing Islam out of fear for his life and to preserve his wealth, and was not a Muslim at heart, attacked and killed him, taking his sheep as spoils of war. When the news reached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He became deeply upset by this incident and said: "You killed a Muslim!" Usama became distressed and said: "This man professed Islam out of fear for his life and to protect his wealth." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied: "You did not know his heart; how do you know? Perhaps he had truly become a Muslim." At this moment, the above verse was revealed, warning the Muslims that for the sake of war spoils and the like, they should never deny the words of those who profess Islam; rather, whoever professes Islam, his word must be accepted.

Commentary

After the necessary emphasis in the preceding verses regarding the preservation of the lives of innocent people, this verse provides a precautionary instruction to protect the lives of innocent individuals who might be subjected to accusation, stating: "O you who have believed, when you go forth [to fight] in the cause of Allah, investigate; and do not say to one who gives you [a greeting of] peace 'You are not a believer'."

(يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ فَتَبَيَّنُوا وَلَا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ أَلْقَىٰ إِلَيْكُمُ السَّلَامَ لَسْتَ مُؤْمِنًا).

In this way, He commands that those who profess faith should be accepted with open arms, and any ill-suspicion or mistrust regarding their profession of faith should be set aside.

Then He adds that you must not, for the sake of the fleeting blessings of this world, accuse individuals who profess Islam and kill them as enemies to seize their property as spoils (تبتغون عرض الحياة الدنيا) [^1], while eternal and valuable spoils are with Allah (فَعِنْدَ اللَّهِ مَغانِمُ كَثِيرَةٌ).

Although you were like this in the past, and during the era of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) your wars were motivated by plunder (كَذلِكَ كُنْتُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ) [^2], now, in the light of Islam and the favor that Allah has bestowed upon you, you have been saved from that state. Therefore, in gratitude for this great blessing, it is necessary to investigate matters (فَمَنَّ اللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَتَبَيَّنُوا).

And know that Allah is well-aware of your actions and intentions.

(إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا).

Islamic Jihad Has No Material Aspect

The above verse clearly elucidates the fact that no Muslim should step into the field of Jihad for a material goal. For this reason, one must accept the first profession of faith from the enemy and respond to his call for peace, even if it means being deprived of abundant material spoils. This is because the goal of Islamic Jihad is not expansionism or the collection of spoils; rather, the goal is the liberation of human beings from the bonds of servitude to other servants and the lords of power and wealth. Whenever a window of hope opens toward this truth, one must hasten toward it. The above verse says: "You once had such debased thoughts and shed blood for the sake of material assets, but today that program has completely changed. Furthermore, when you yourselves entered Islam, what did you have other than a profession of faith? Why do you withhold from others the very law from which you yourselves benefited?"

Question

Considering the content of the verse, an objection might be raised that by accepting people's outward claims of joining this religion, Islam prepares the ground for nurturing "hypocrites" (munafiq) in the Islamic environment. With such a policy, many might exploit it and, under the cover of the name of Islam, engage in espionage and anti-Islamic activities.

Answer

Perhaps there is no law in the world that does not have a way to be exploited. The important thing is that the law possesses significant benefits. If the profession of Islam were not accepted under the pretext of not knowing the person's inner heart, many evils would arise whose harm would be far greater, and it would destroy the principles of human compassion. This is because anyone who has a grudge or a score to settle with another could accuse him of his Islam being merely outward and not in harmony with his heart, and in this way, much innocent blood would be shed.

Furthermore, at the beginning of an inclination toward any faith, there are individuals whose inclinations are simple, ceremonial, and outward. However, with the passage of time and continuous contact with that faith, they become firm and rooted. This group cannot be rejected either.

[^1]: "Arad" (rhyming with marad) refers to something that lacks stability and permanence. Therefore, "Arad al-Hayat al-Dunya" means the assets of worldly life, all of which, without exception, are fleeting.

[^2]: In the commentary of this phrase, another possibility has also been suggested: that you yourselves were in such a state at the beginning of your conversion to Islam—meaning you testified to the truth of Islam with your tongues and it was accepted from you, while the secrets of your hearts were not clear to anyone.

Ayahs

لَّا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْقَـٰعِدُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ غَيْرُ أُو۟لِى ٱلضَّرَرِ وَٱلْمُجَـٰهِدُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ ۚ فَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُجَـٰهِدِينَ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ عَلَى ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ دَرَجَةً ۚ وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَفَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُجَـٰهِدِينَ عَلَى ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (٩٥)
Not equal are those of the faithful who sit back—excepting those who suffer from some disability—and those who wage jihad in the way of Allah with their possession and their persons. Allah has graced those who wage jihad with their possessions and their persons by a degree over those who sit back; yet to each Allah has promised the best reward, and Allah has graced those who wage jihad over those who sit back with a great reward: (95)
دَرَجَـٰتٍ مِّنْهُ وَمَغْفِرَةً وَرَحْمَةً ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٩٦)
ranks from Him, forgiveness, and mercy, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (96)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, the discussion was about Jihad. These two verses provide a comparison between the Mujahideen (those who strive) and the non-Mujahideen, stating: "Those believers who refrain from participating in the field of Jihad, and do not possess a specific illness that prevents them from joining this field, are never equal to the Mujahideen who strive in the way of Allah and for the exaltation of the word of Truth with their wealth and their lives."

(لا يَسْتَوِي الْقاعِدُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ غَيْرُ أُولِي الضَّرَرِ وَ الْمُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ‌).

It is clear that the term "the sitters" (al-qa'idun) here refers to individuals who, despite having faith in the principles of Islam, did not participate in Jihad due to a lack of sufficient resolve, in cases where Jihad was not an individual obligation (wajib 'ayni) upon them. For if it were an individual obligation, the Quran would not have spoken about them in such a mild tone and promised them a reward at the end of the verse. Therefore, even when Jihad is not an individual obligation, the clear superiority of the "Mujahideen" over the "sitters" is undeniable.

In any case, the verse does not include those who refrained from participating in Jihad out of hypocrisy or enmity. Furthermore, it should be noted that the expression "those who possess no hurt" (ghayru uli al-darar) has a broad meaning that excludes all those who are unable to participate in Jihad due to physical disability, illness, extreme weakness, or the like.

Then, the superiority of the Mujahideen is stated once more in a more explicit and manifest manner, saying: "Allah has granted the Mujahideen who fight in His way with their wealth and their lives a great excellence over those who refrain from participating in the field of Jihad."

(فَضَّلَ اللَّهُ الْمُجاهِدِينَ بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ عَلَى الْقاعِدِينَ دَرَجَةً [1])

[1]: The word darajah (degree/rank) is mentioned in the indefinite form (nakirah), and as stated in books of literature, the indefinite form in such cases is used to express greatness and importance. It is as if their rank is so high that it cannot be fully known; this is similar to when one says of something of extraordinary value: "No one knows its price!"

However, at the same time—as we have mentioned—since the counterparts to this group of Mujahideen are individuals for whom Jihad was not an individual obligation, or those who were unable to participate due to illness, disability, or other reasons, Allah promises them a good reward as well, so that the reward for their righteous intention, faith, and other good deeds is not overlooked. He says: "To each (the Mujahideen and the non-Mujahideen) Allah has promised good" (وَ كُلًّا وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الْحُسْنى‌).

But it is obvious that there is a vast distance between the "good promise" given to these two groups. In reality, the Quran shows through this statement that the share of every good deed is preserved in its proper place and shall not be forgotten. This is especially true regarding the "sitters" who were interested in participating in Jihad and considered it a lofty and sacred goal, but the fact that it was not an individual obligation prevented the realization of this high aim; they too will have a reward in proportion to their interest in this work. Similarly, the "possessors of hurt" (those who did not participate due to illness or disability), while they desired and loved it with all their being, will also have a significant share of the reward of the Mujahideen. As narrated in a Hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, he said to the soldiers of Islam:

"You have left behind in Medina groups of people who did not travel any distance nor cross any valley except that they were with you; they are those whose intentions were pure, whose sincerity was complete, and whose hearts yearned for Jihad, but obstacles such as illness, harm, or other things held them back from the journey." [1]

However, since the importance of Jihad in the logic of Islam is even greater than this, the verse returns to the Mujahideen once more and emphasizes: "And Allah has preferred the Mujahideen over the sitters with a great reward."

(وَ فَضَّلَ اللَّهُ الْمُجاهِدِينَ عَلَى الْقاعِدِينَ أَجْراً عَظِيماً).

This "great reward" is interpreted in the following verse as: "Ranks from Him, and forgiveness and mercy" (دَرَجاتٍ مِنْهُ وَ مَغْفِرَةً وَ رَحْمَةً).

And if, in the process, some individuals committed lapses while performing their duty and are regretful of their actions, Allah has promised them forgiveness as well, stating at the end of the verse: "And Allah is Ever-Forgiving, Most Merciful" (وَ كانَ اللَّهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً).

Key Points

  1. In the above verse, the name "Mujahideen" is mentioned three times. The first time, the Mujahideen are mentioned along with the "goal" and "means" of Jihad (الْمُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ بِأَمْوالِهِمْ). The second time, the name of the Mujahideen is mentioned only with the means of Jihad, but nothing is said of the goal (الْمُجاهِدِينَ بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ). In the final stage, only the name of the Mujahideen is mentioned (المجاهدين...). This is one of the prominent points of eloquence in speech: as the listener becomes more familiar with the subject step by step, the qualifiers and specifications are reduced until familiarity reaches a point where everything is understood with just a single reference.

  2. In the first verse, the superiority of the Mujahideen over the sitters is mentioned in the singular form (darajah - a rank), while in the second verse, it appears in the plural form (darajat - ranks).

[1]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the aforementioned verse.

It is clear that there is no contradiction between these two expressions, because in the first expression, the intent is to state the principle of the Mujahideen's superiority over others. However, in the second expression, this superiority is detailed, and thus it is coupled with the mention of "forgiveness" and "mercy." In other words, the difference between the two is the difference between "brevity" (ijmal) and "detail" (tafsil).

Additionally, from the use of the term "ranks" (darajat), one can infer that the Mujahideen are not all on one level or footing; their spiritual stations vary according to the difference in their degree of sincerity, sacrifice, and endurance of hardships. For it is certain that all Mujahideen who stand in one rank against the enemy do not strive to the same extent and do not possess the same degree of sincerity. Therefore, each receives a reward in proportion to his work and intention.

The Extraordinary Importance of Jihad

Jihad is a general law in the world of creation, and all living beings in the world, including plants and animals, remove obstacles from their path through "striving" (Jihad) so they can reach their desired perfections.

As an example, we see the root of a tree constantly active and moving to obtain food and energy. If one day it were to abandon this activity and effort, the continuation of its life would be impossible. For this reason, when it encounters obstacles in its movement deep within the earth, it pierces through them if it can. It is amazing that delicate roots sometimes battle obstacles like steel drills, and if by chance they lack this ability, they bend their path and bypass the obstacle.

Within our own existence, throughout the day and night—even during the hours we sleep—there is a wondrous battle between our white blood cells and invading enemies. If this Jihad were to be silenced for one hour and the defenders of the body's territory were to cease fighting, various harmful microbes would penetrate different systems and jeopardize our health.

The exact same thing exists among human societies and the nations and peoples of the world. Those who always live in a state of "Jihad" and "vigilance" are always alive and victorious, while others who think only of pleasure-seeking and individual life will sooner or later perish, and a living, striving nation will take their place. This is why the Noble Messenger of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله says:

"Whoever abandons Jihad, Allah clothes him in the garment of humiliation and poverty in his livelihood and erasure in his religion. Indeed, Allah grants honor to my Ummah through the hooves of its horses and the points of its spears." [1]

On another occasion, he says: "Engage in Jihad so that you may bequeath glory and greatness to your children!" [2] And the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says at the beginning of the Sermon of Jihad:

"...Now then, surely Jihad is one of the doors of Paradise, which Allah has opened for His special friends. It is the garment of 'Taqwa' (piety), the impenetrable armor of Allah, and His trustworthy shield. Whoever abandons it out of aversion, Allah clothes him in the robe of humiliation, covers him with calamity, and he is kicked away with lowliness and insignificance..." [3]

[1] & [2]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Book of Jihad, Chapters on Jihad against the Enemy, Chapter 1, Hadiths 2 and 16. [3]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 27.

Furthermore, it should be noted that Jihad does not only mean war and armed combat; rather, it encompasses every type of effort and striving performed to advance sacred divine goals. In this way, in addition to defensive and sometimes offensive battles, it also includes scientific, logical, economic, cultural, and political struggles.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَوَفَّىٰهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ ظَالِمِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ قَالُوا۟ فِيمَ كُنتُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ كُنَّا مُسْتَضْعَفِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ أَلَمْ تَكُنْ أَرْضُ ٱللَّهِ وَٰسِعَةً فَتُهَاجِرُوا۟ فِيهَا ۚ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَسَآءَتْ مَصِيرًا (٩٧)
Indeed, those whom the angels take away while they are wronging themselves, they ask, ‘What state were you in?’ They reply, ‘We were oppressed in the land.’ They say, ‘Was not Allah’s earth vast enough so that you might migrate in it?’ The refuge of such shall be hell, and it is an evil destination. (97)
إِلَّا ٱلْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ ٱلرِّجَالِ وَٱلنِّسَآءِ وَٱلْوِلْدَٰنِ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ حِيلَةً وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ سَبِيلًا (٩٨)
Except the oppressed among men, women and children, who have neither access to any means nor are guided to any way. (98)
فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَعْفُوَ عَنْهُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَفُوًّا غَفُورًا (٩٩)
Maybe Allah will excuse them, for Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving. (99)

Circumstances of Revelation

Before the start of the Battle of Badr, the leaders of the Quraish issued a warning that all residents of Makkah capable of participating in the battlefield must march out to fight the Muslims, and anyone who opposed this would have their house destroyed and their property confiscated. Following this threat, a group of individuals who had outwardly embraced Islam but were unwilling to migrate due to their intense attachment to their homes, lives, and wealth, also marched toward the battlefield with the idolaters. They stood in the ranks of the polytheists on the field, fell into doubt and hesitation due to the small number of Muslims, and were ultimately killed in this battle. The above verse was revealed and described their grim fate.

Commentary

[The State of the Oppressed]

Following the discussions related to Jihad, these verses refer to the grim fate of those who claimed to be Muslims but did not implement the important Islamic program of "Hijrah" (migration). As a result, they were drawn into dangerous paths and lost their lives in the ranks of the polytheists. The Quran says: "Indeed, those whom the angels take in death while they were wronging themselves—[the angels] will say, 'In what [condition] were you?'" meaning, if you were Muslims, why were you in the ranks of the disbelievers and fighting against the Muslims? (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ قالُوا فِيمَ كُنْتُمْ‌).

In response, as an excuse, they say: "We were oppressed in the land," and for this reason, we did not have the ability to carry out Allah's command. (قالُوا كُنَّا مُسْتَضْعَفِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

But this excuse is not accepted from them, and they will soon hear the response from Allah's angels: "Was not the earth of Allah spacious [enough] for you to emigrate therein?" and free yourselves from that polluted and suffocating environment? (قالُوا أَ لَمْ تَكُنْ أارضُ اللَّهِ واسِعَةً فَتُهاجِرُوا فِيها).

And at the end, referring to their fate, He says: "Those persons who shirked the burden of migration with vain excuses and personal considerations of expediency, preferring life in a polluted and suffocating environment, their abode is Hell—and evil it is as a destination." (فَأُولئِكَ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَ ساءَتْ مَصِيراً).

In the next verse, the truly oppressed and weak (not the falsely oppressed) are excepted, and He says: "Except for the oppressed among men, women, and children who cannot devise a plan nor are they directed to a way" to migrate and escape that polluted environment; these are excepted from this ruling because they are truly excused, and it is impossible for Allah to impose a duty beyond one's capacity. (إِلَّا الْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ الرِّجالِ وَ النِّساءِ وَ الْوِلْدانِ لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ حِيلَةً وَ لا يَهْتَدُونَ سَبِيلًا).

And in the final verse, He says: "For those it is expected that Allah will pardon them, and Allah is ever Pardoning and Forgiving." (فَأُولئِكَ عَسَى اللَّهُ أَنْ يَعْفُوَ عَنْهُمْ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَفُوًّا غَفُوراً).

The question may arise: if these individuals are truly excused, why does He not say Allah will definitely forgive them, but instead says "Asa" (it is expected/perhaps)?

The answer to this question is the same as what was explained under verse 84 of this same Surah: such expressions are intended to show that the ruling mentioned in this verse has conditions that must be noted. That is, such persons are subject to Divine pardon when they have not shown the slightest negligence in performing migration when the opportunity arose—meaning they are not "guilty in the preliminaries"—and are currently ready to migrate at the first possible opportunity.

Several points should be noted here

1- The Independence of the Soul. The use of the term Tawaffi in the noble verse instead of death is actually an indication of the point that death does not mean annihilation and non-existence, but rather a kind of "reception of the human soul by the angels." That is, they take his soul, which is the most fundamental part of his existence, and carry it with them to another world. This type of expression, which appears repeatedly in the Quran, is one of the clearest indications in the Quran regarding the existence of the soul and its survival after death, the details of which will come under appropriate verses. It is a response to those who say the Quran makes no reference to the issue of the soul. [^1]

2- The Angel of Death or Angels? From an examination of numerous instances in the Holy Quran (12 instances) where Tawaffi and death are discussed, it is understood that the taking of souls is not by one specific angel alone; rather, there are angels who hold this responsibility and are commissioned to transfer the souls of humans from this world to the next. The above verse, where "angels" appears in the plural form (الْمَلائِكَةُ), is also one of the evidences for this subject.

In Surah Al-An'am, verse 61, we read: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا: "Until, when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him."

And if we see that in some verses this matter is attributed to the Malak al-Mawt (Angel of Death), [^1] it is because he is the chief of the angels commissioned to take souls, and he is the same one referred to in the Hadiths by the name "Azrael."

Therefore, the question some ask—how one angel can be present everywhere at once and take the souls of humans—is clarified by the explanation given. Furthermore, even assuming there were no [other] angels and only one angel, it would still not create a problem, because his incorporeal existence (immateriality) necessitates that the sphere of his influence and action be extraordinarily vast. An existence free from matter can have a wide encompassment over the world of matter, just as it is narrated in a Hadith regarding the Angel of Death (Malak al-Mawt) from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked about his encompassment of the world, he replied: ما الدنيا كلها عندى فيما سخرها اللَّه لى و مكننى عليها الا كالدرهم فى كف الرجل يقلبه كيف يشاء "This world and all that is in it, with the authority and encompassment Allah has granted me, is to me like a coin in the palm of a man, which he turns however he wishes." [^2]

[^1]: For an explanation of the linguistic meaning of "Tawaffi," please refer to Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 430. [^1]: Surah As-Sajdah, verse 11. [^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 391, under the first verse of Surah al-Isra.

Additionally, if we see that in some verses of the Quran the taking of the soul is attributed to Allah, such as: اللَّهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها: "Allah takes the souls at the time of their death" (Surah Az-Zumar, 42), it does not contradict the previous verses. In cases where a task is performed through intermediaries, sometimes the task is attributed to the intermediaries and sometimes to the One who prompted the means and intermediaries; both attributions are correct.

Interestingly, in the Quran, many events of the world are attributed to the angels who are Allah's agents in the universe. As we know, "angel" (malak) has a broad meaning that ranges from "rational incorporeal beings" to "natural forces and powers."

3- Who is the "Mustad'af" (Oppressed)? From an examination of Quranic verses and narrations, it is understood that individuals who are so weak intellectually, physically, or economically that they are unable to distinguish truth from falsehood, or who, despite recognizing the correct belief, are unable to fully perform their duties or migrate due to physical inability, financial weakness, or the restrictions imposed on them by their environment, are called Mustad'af.

It is narrated from Ali عليه السلام that he said: و لا يقع اسم الاستضعاف على من بلغته الحجة فسمعتها اذنه و وعاها قلبه‌ "The name of 'oppression' (istid'af) does not apply to one whom the proof has reached, whose ear has heard it, and whose heart has grasped it." [^1] And Imam Musa ibn Jafar عليه السلام was asked who the Mustad'afun are.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 536.

The Imam wrote in response to this question: الضعيف من لم ترفع له حجة و لم يعرف الاختلاف فاذا عرف الاختلاف فليس بضعيف‌ "The weak (Mustad'af) is one to whom the proof and evidence has not reached and who has not realized the existence of difference (in religions and beliefs which motivates research); but once he realizes this matter, he is no longer a Mustad'af." [^1] It is clear that the Mustad'af in the two aforementioned narrations refers to the intellectual and ideological Mustad'af, but in the verse under discussion and verse 75 of this same Surah which passed, the meaning of Mustad'af is the practical Mustad'af—that is, one who has recognized the truth but the suffocating environment does not allow him to act.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 536.

Ayah

۞ وَمَن يُهَاجِرْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ يَجِدْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُرَٰغَمًا كَثِيرًا وَسَعَةً ۚ وَمَن يَخْرُجْ مِنۢ بَيْتِهِۦ مُهَاجِرًا إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ثُمَّ يُدْرِكْهُ ٱلْمَوْتُ فَقَدْ وَقَعَ أَجْرُهُۥ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١٠٠)
Whoever migrates in the way of Allah will find many havens and plenitude in the earth. And whoever leaves his home migrating toward Allah and His Apostle, and is then overtaken by death, his reward shall certainly fall on Allah, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (100)

Commentary

Migration: A Constructive Islamic Mandate

Following the discussion regarding individuals who submit to various forms of humiliation and misery due to negligence in performing the obligation of migration (Hijrah), this verse discusses the importance of migration with total decisiveness in two parts:

First, it refers to the effects and blessings of migration in the life of this world, stating: "Those who migrate in the way of Allah and for the sake of Allah will find many and vast places of refuge in this expansive world of God where they can implement the truth and rub the noses of their opponents in the dust."

(وَ مَنْ يُهاجِرْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ يَجِدْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُراغَماً كَثِيراً وَ سَعَةً)

It should be noted that "muragham" is derived from the root ragham (on the weight of kalam), meaning "dust," and irgham means to rub someone's nose in the dust and to humiliate them. "Muragham" is both a passive participle and a noun of place; in the above verse, it is used as a noun of place, meaning a location where they can implement the truth, and if someone opposes the truth out of enmity, they can condemn him and rub his nose in the dust! Then, it points to the spiritual and otherworldly aspect of migration, stating: "If anyone leaves his home and homeland with the intention of migrating toward Allah and His Messenger (peace be upon him and his family), and death overtakes him before reaching the place of migration, his reward is incumbent upon Allah, and Allah forgives their sins."

(وَ مَنْ يَخْرُجْ مِنْ بَيْتِهِ مُهاجِراً إِلَى اللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ ثُمَّ يُدْرِكْهُ الْمَوْتُ فَقَدْ وَقَعَ أَجْرُهُ عَلَى اللَّهِ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً)

Therefore, migrants achieve a great victory in any case—whether they manage to reach their destination and benefit from freedom and liberty in performing their duties, or whether they fail and lose their lives in this path. Although all rewards for the righteous are from Allah, it is specifically stated here: فَقَدْ وَقَعَ أَجْرُهُ عَلَى اللَّهِ ("his reward has become incumbent upon Allah"), which clarifies the ultimate greatness and importance of the migrants' reward.

Islam and Migration

According to this verse and many other verses of the Quran, Islam explicitly commands that if you are unable to perform your duties in an environment due to certain factors, you must "migrate" to another safe environment or region; for given the vastness of the universe, "one cannot die in humiliation just because I was born here!"

The reason for this command is clear, as Islam is not regional and is not tied or limited to a specific place or environment. Thus, excessive attachment to one's place of birth and homeland, or other various attachments, cannot prevent a Muslim from migrating from the perspective of Islam.

Hence, we see that in the early days of Islam, all these attachments were severed for the sake of the preservation and advancement of Islam. As one Western historian noted: "The tribe and family are the only tree that grows in the desert, and no individual can live except under its protection; yet Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family), through his migration, cut the tree that had been nourished by the flesh and blood of his family for the sake of his Lord." (And he severed his ties with the Quraysh) [1]

[1] Muhammad, the Seal of the Prophets, Vol. 1.

Beyond this, "migration" exists among all living beings when they see their existence in danger. Many humans of the past, after changes in the Earth's geographical conditions, moved from their birthplaces to other locations to continue their lives. Not only humans, but among other living creatures, many species are known as migrants, such as migratory birds that sometimes travel across almost the entire globe to sustain life—some traveling from the North Pole to the South Pole—thus flying about 18,000 kilometers a year to preserve their lives. This itself shows that migration is one of the eternal laws of life and existence.

Is it possible for a human to be less than a bird?! When spiritual life, dignity, and sacred goals—which are more valuable than man's material life—are endangered, can one submit to all kinds of belittlement, humiliation, deprivation, the stripping of freedoms, and the destruction of one's goals under the excuse that "this is my birthplace"?! Or should one, according to that same general law of life, migrate from such a spot and move to a place prepared for his material and spiritual growth and development?

It is interesting that migration—not just for self-preservation but for the preservation of the religion of Islam—is the starting point of the Muslim calendar and forms the foundation of all our political, missionary, and social events.

But why was the year of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) migration chosen as the starting point of Islamic history?

This is a noteworthy subject, for we know that every nation and people has its own historical starting point; for example, Christians have set the year of Christ's birth as their starting point. In Islam, despite many important events such as the birth of the Prophet of Islam, his appointment (Bi'thah), the conquest of Mecca, and the passing of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), none were chosen; only the time of the Messenger of Allah's (peace be upon him and his family) migration was selected as the starting point of history.

History records that during the time of the second Caliph, when Islam had naturally expanded, the Muslims thought of determining a historical starting point that would be general and universal. After much discussion, they accepted the opinion of Ali (peace be upon him) regarding the selection of the Hijrah as the starting point of history. [1] In reality, it had to be so, because migration was the most brilliant program implemented in Islam and became the beginning of a new chapter in Islamic history.

As long as the Muslims were in Mecca undergoing their period of training, they seemingly had no social or political power. However, immediately after the migration, the Islamic state was formed and progressed with great speed in all fields. If the Muslims had not undertaken such a migration by the command of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), not only would Islam not have moved beyond the environment of Mecca, but it might have been buried and forgotten right there.

It is clear that "migration" was not a ruling specific to the time of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family); rather, in every age, time, and place, if the same conditions arise, Muslims are duty-bound to migrate.

Fundamentally, the Quran considers migration to be the source of freedom and comfort, as explicitly stated in the verse under discussion. In Surah An-Nahl, verse 41, this truth is mentioned in another way:

[1] Tarikh al-Tabari, Vol. 2, p. 112.

وَ الَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا فِي اللَّهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما ظُلِمُوا لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةً

"And those who migrated for the sake of Allah after they had been wronged—We will surely settle them in a good place in this world."

It is also necessary to mention that from the perspective of Islam, migration is not only a physical and external migration of place; rather, before this migration, a migration must begin from within. That is the "migration" and "distancing" from things that contradict human integrity and honors, so that in its shadow, one becomes prepared for external and physical migration. This migration is necessary so that if one has no need for physical migration, he may enter the ranks of the migrants in the way of Allah through this internal migration.

In principle, the spirit of migration is the flight from "darkness" to "light," from disbelief to faith, and from sin and disobedience to obedience to Allah's command. Therefore, we read in the Hadiths: migrants whose bodies have migrated but who have had no migration within their souls are not in the ranks of the migrants; conversely, those who had no need for physical migration but undertook a migration within their own existence are among the migrants. The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), says:

و يقول الرجل هاجرت، و لم يهاجر، انما المهاجرون الذين يهجرون السيئات و لم ياتوا بها

"A man says, 'I have migrated,' while he has not truly migrated. The true migrants are those who migrate from sins and do not commit them." [1] The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said:

من فر بدينه من ارض الى ارض و ان كان شبرا من الارض استوجب الجنة و كان رفيق محمد و ابراهيم عليهما السلام

"Whoever flees with his religion from one land to another, even if it be the distance of a span of land, becomes entitled to Paradise and will be the companion of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) and Ibrahim (peace be upon him)" (for these two great Prophets were the leaders of the world's migrants). [2]

[1] Safinat al-Bihar (Root: H-J-R). [2] Ibid.

Ayah

وَإِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَقْصُرُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ إِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَن يَفْتِنَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ كَانُوا۟ لَكُمْ عَدُوًّا مُّبِينًا (١٠١)
When you journey in the land, there is no sin upon you in shortening the prayers, if you fear that the faithless may trouble you; indeed the faithless are your manifest enemies. (101)

Commentary

The Traveler’s Prayer

Following the previous verses which discussed "Jihad" and "Migration," this verse refers to the issue of the "Traveler’s Prayer" (Salat al-Musafir), stating: "When you travel through the land, there is no blame upon you for shortening the prayer if you fear that the disbelievers may harm you, for the disbelievers are ever to you a clear enemy."

(وَ إِذا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ أَنْ تَقْصُرُوا مِنَ الصَّلاةِ إِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَنْ يَفْتِنَكُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنَّ الْكافِرِينَ كانُوا لَكُمْ عَدُوًّا مُبِيناً).

In this verse, travel is expressed as darb fil-ard (striking through the earth), because a traveler strikes the ground with his feet during a journey [1]. Here, a question arises: the above verse makes the shortening of prayer conditional upon the fear of danger from the enemy, whereas in jurisprudential discussions, we read that shortening the prayer (Qasr) is a general rule, and there is no difference between dangerous journeys or those in peace and security. Numerous narrations from both Shi'a and Sunni sources regarding the traveler’s prayer also confirm this generality [2]. In response, it must be said: it is possible that restricting the rule of Qasr to the issue of "fear" is due to one of several reasons:

A- This condition refers to the situation of Muslims at the beginning of Islam and is what is termed a "predominant condition" (qayd al-ghalibi); meaning, their travels were predominantly accompanied by fear. As established in the principles of jurisprudence (Usul al-Fiqh), "predominant conditions" do not imply a restrictive concept (mafhum). We encounter the same issue in the verse: "and your step-daughters under your guardianship [born] of your wives" (وَرَبائِبُكُمُ اللَّاتِي فِي حُجُورِكُمْ) [3], because step-daughters are absolutely among the mahram (forbidden for marriage), whether they were raised in the man's lap (guardianship) or not. However, since divorced women who remarry are often young and have small children who are raised in the second husband's home, the condition "in your guardianship" (fi hujurikum) is mentioned in the said verse.

B- Some commentators believe that the issue of shortening the prayer was first legislated for times of fear (according to the above verse), and then this rule was expanded and became general for all cases.

C- It is possible that this condition is for emphasis; meaning, the shortened prayer is necessary for the traveler everywhere, but it is emphasized more when there is fear of the enemy.

In any case, there is no doubt that, considering the interpretation of the verse in numerous Islamic narrations, the traveler’s prayer is not exclusive to the state of fear. For this reason, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله also prayed the shortened prayer during his journeys and even during the Hajj rituals (in the land of Mina).

Another question that arises is that the above verse says: la junaha 'alaykum (there is no blame/sin upon you) and does not say you must pray the shortened prayer. So how can it be said that the shortened prayer is an individual obligation (wajib 'ayni) and not an optional one (wajib takhyiri)?

[Does the phrase "la junah" imply the obligation of the shortened prayer?] In response, it must be said

This exact question was asked of the leaders of Islam, and in response, they pointed to two points:

First: The expression la junah (there is no blame/sin...) is used in the Holy Quran itself in some instances to mean obligation, such as:

"Indeed, as-Safa and al-Marwah are among the symbols of Allah. So whoever makes Hajj to the House or performs 'Umrah—there is no blame upon him for walking between them." (Al-Baqarah: 158). While we know that the Sa'y between Safa and Marwah is obligatory in both Hajj and 'Umrah.

Therefore, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and all Muslims perform it—this same point is mentioned in a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام [1].

In other words: the expression la junah in the verse under discussion, as well as in the verse regarding Hajj, is to negate the illusion of prohibition. This is because, at the beginning of Islam, idols were placed atop Safa and Marwah, and because of them, some Muslims thought that the Sa'y between Safa and Marwah was a custom of idolaters. Since this was not the case, to negate this illusion, He says: "there is no blame for you to perform the Sa'y of Safa and Marwah." Similarly, regarding the traveler, there was room for the illusion that some might imagine shortening the prayer during travel to be a kind of sin; thus, the Quran removes this illusion with the expression la junah.

Another point is that some narrations also mention that shortening the prayer during travel is a type of divine concession (takhfif), and etiquette dictates that a person should not reject this concession or show indifference toward it. In Sunni narrations, it is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said regarding the shortened prayer:

"It is a charity that Allah has bestowed upon you, so accept His charity" [1].

A similar hadith has also been recorded in Shi'a sources. Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام narrates from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he used to say: "Breaking the fast during travel and the shortened prayer are among the divine gifts; whoever refrains from this has rejected the divine gift" [2].

Another point to be noted is that some have imagined that the above verse explains the rule for the "Fear Prayer" (Salat al-Khawf—prayer on the battlefield and the like) and have taken the expression "if you fear" (in khiftum) as evidence for this.

However, the phrase "when you travel through the land" (idha darabtum fil-ard) has a general meaning that includes any kind of travel, whether it be an ordinary journey or a journey for Jihad. Furthermore, the rule for the Fear Prayer is mentioned separately and independently in the following verse. As we said, the expression "if you fear" is a type of predominant condition that existed for Muslims in most travels of that time; therefore, it does not specifically denote the Fear Prayer. Additionally, on the battlefield, there is always fear of enemy attacks, and it is not a place to say "if you fear that the enemy might attack you." This itself is another proof that the verse refers to all journeys in which dangers might exist.

Meanwhile, it should be noted that the conditions for the traveler’s prayer, like the conditions and details of other Islamic laws, are not mentioned in the Quran but are referred to in the Sunnah.

Among these is that the shortened prayer is not for journeys less than eight farsakhs, because at that time, a traveler usually covered eight farsakhs in one day.

Also, individuals who are always traveling or whose travel has become part of their life routine are excluded from this rule, because traveling for them is a normal occurrence, not an extraordinary one.

Furthermore, those whose journey is a journey of sin are not included in this law, because this rule is a type of divine concession, and those who walk the path of sin cannot be eligible for it.

Additionally, a traveler should not pray the shortened prayer until they reach the "limit of concession" (hadd al-tarakkhus—the point where the city's call to prayer is no longer heard or the city walls are no longer visible), because they have not yet left the city's territory and have not yet taken on the title of "traveler." There are also other rules detailed in books of jurisprudence, and the related hadiths have been mentioned by traditionists in books of Hadith.

[^1]: Raghib's Mufradat, under the root "D-R-B". [^2]: For more information, refer to Volume 5 of the book Wasa'il al-Shi'a and the book Sunan al-Bayhaqi, Volume 3, page 134, and others. [^3]: An-Nisa: 23. [^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 1, page 542. [^1]: This hadith is narrated in Sunan al-Bayhaqi, Volume 3, page 134, from Sahih Muslim, and it also appears in commentaries and books of jurisprudence. [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Volume 5, page 540.

Ayah

وَإِذَا كُنتَ فِيهِمْ فَأَقَمْتَ لَهُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ فَلْتَقُمْ طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُم مَّعَكَ وَلْيَأْخُذُوٓا۟ أَسْلِحَتَهُمْ فَإِذَا سَجَدُوا۟ فَلْيَكُونُوا۟ مِن وَرَآئِكُمْ وَلْتَأْتِ طَآئِفَةٌ أُخْرَىٰ لَمْ يُصَلُّوا۟ فَلْيُصَلُّوا۟ مَعَكَ وَلْيَأْخُذُوا۟ حِذْرَهُمْ وَأَسْلِحَتَهُمْ ۗ وَدَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْ تَغْفُلُونَ عَنْ أَسْلِحَتِكُمْ وَأَمْتِعَتِكُمْ فَيَمِيلُونَ عَلَيْكُم مَّيْلَةً وَٰحِدَةً ۚ وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِن كَانَ بِكُمْ أَذًى مِّن مَّطَرٍ أَوْ كُنتُم مَّرْضَىٰٓ أَن تَضَعُوٓا۟ أَسْلِحَتَكُمْ ۖ وَخُذُوا۟ حِذْرَكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَعَدَّ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا (١٠٢)
When you are among them, leading them in prayers, let a group of them stand with you, carrying their weapons. And when they have done the prostrations, let them withdraw to the rear, then let the other group which has not prayed come and pray with you, taking their precautions and [bearing] their weapons. The faithless are eager that you should be oblivious of your weapons and your baggage, so that they could assault you all at once. But there is no sin upon you, if you are troubled by rain or are sick, to set aside your weapons; but take your precautions. Indeed Allah has prepared for the faithless a humiliating punishment. (102)

Occasion of Revelation

When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, accompanied by a group of Muslims, entered the land of "Hudaybiyyah" with the intention of reaching "Mecca," news reached the Quraysh. Khalid ibn al-Walid, leading a group of two hundred men, stationed himself in the mountains near Mecca to prevent the Muslims' advance. At noon, "Bilal" called the Adhan, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله performed the Dhuhr prayer in congregation with the Muslims. Observing this scene, Khalid fell into deep thought and told his men that during the Asr prayer—which is highly precious to the Muslims, even dearer to them than the light of their own eyes—they must seize the opportunity. He planned to launch a lightning-fast, surprise attack during the prayer to finish off the Muslims once and for all. At this moment, the above verse was revealed, instructing the Muslims on the "Prayer of Fear" (Salat al-Khawf), which prevents any surprise attack. This is one of the miraculous aspects of the Quran, as it frustrated the enemy's plans before they could act. Consequently, it is said that Khalid ibn al-Walid embraced Islam and became a Muslim after witnessing this event [1].

Commentary

Following the verses related to Jihad, this verse teaches the Muslims the method of the Prayer of Fear, which is to be performed during war. Addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the verse says: "When you are among them and lead them in congregational prayer, the Muslims must be divided into two groups. First, one group should stand in prayer with you while carrying their weapons."

(وَ إِذا كُنْتَ فِيهِمْ فَأَقَمْتَ لَهُمُ الصَّلاةَ فَلْتَقُمْ طائِفَةٌ مِنْهُمْ مَعَكَ وَ لْيَأْخُذُوا أَسْلِحَتَهُمْ‌).

"Then, when this group has prostrated (and completed their first unit of prayer, you remain in your position), and they quickly finish their second unit

[^1]: Tafsir al-Tibyan, Vol. 3, p. 311, and other commentaries.

on their own and return to the battlefield to face the enemy), and the second group, who has not yet prayed, takes the place of the first group and prays with you."

(فَإِذا سَجَدُوا فَلْيَكُونُوا مِنْ وَرائِكُمْ وَ لْتَأْتِ طائِفَةٌ أُخْرى‌ لَمْ يُصَلُّوا فَلْيَصَلُّوا مَعَكَ‌).

"The second group must also carry their defensive gear and weapons and not lay them down."

(وَ لْيَأْخُذُوا حِذْرَهُمْ وَ أَسْلِحَتَهُمْ‌).

This manner of praying is so that the enemy does not catch you off guard, for the enemy is always lying in wait to seize an opportunity "and wishes that you would neglect your weapons and your belongings" so they may launch a single, sudden assault upon you.

(وَدَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ تَغْفُلُونَ عَنْ أَسْلِحَتِكُمْ وَ أَمْتِعَتِكُمْ فَيَمِيلُونَ عَلَيْكُمْ مَيْلَةً واحِدَةً).

However, since necessities may arise where carrying both weapons and defensive gear during prayer becomes difficult, or due to weakness, illness, or injuries sustained on the battlefield, carrying weapons might cause hardship, the end of the verse provides the following instruction:

"And there is no sin upon you if you are troubled by rain or are ill, that you lay down your weapons."

(وَ لا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِنْ كانَ بِكُمْ أَذىً مِنْ مَطَرٍ أَوْ كُنْتُمْ مَرْضى‌ أَنْ تَضَعُوا أَسْلِحَتَكُمْ‌).

"But in any case, do not neglect to carry your protective and safety gear (such as armor, helmets, and the like), and even when excused, you must have them with you so that if the enemy happens to attack, you can protect yourselves until help arrives" (وَ خُذُوا حِذْرَهُمْ).

Follow these instructions and be certain that victory is yours, "for Allah has prepared for the disbelievers a humiliating punishment."

(إِنَّ اللَّهَ أَعَدَّ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَذاباً مُهِيناً).

Here, several points should be noted:

1- It is clear that the mention of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله being among the Muslims to establish the Prayer of Fear does not mean that this prayer is conditional upon his physical presence. Rather, it refers to the presence of an Imam or leader to lead the congregation among the soldiers and mujahideen. Thus, Ali عليه السلام and Imam Hussain عليه السلام also performed the Prayer of Fear, and even several commanders of the Islamic armies, such as Hudhayfah, implemented this Islamic protocol when necessary [1].

2- In the verse, the first group is commanded to carry their weapons during the Prayer of Fear, but the second group is told to lay down neither their defensive gear (like armor) nor their weapons. This difference might be because when the first group is praying, the enemy is not yet fully aware of the procedure, so the likelihood of an attack is lower. However, for the second group, as the enemy realizes the prayer ritual is taking place, the probability of an assault increases.

3- The meaning of "protecting belongings" (amtia'tikum) is that in addition to protecting yourselves, you must also be vigilant in guarding other military equipment, travel supplies, food, and the animals you have brought for sustenance.

4- We know that congregational prayer is not obligatory in Islam, but it is an extremely emphasized Sunnah (Mustahabb). The above verse is a living testament to the emphasis on this Islamic practice, showing that even on the battlefield, the method of the Prayer of Fear is used to perform it.

[^1]: Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 1, p. 191.

This subject demonstrates both the importance of "prayer itself" and the importance of "congregation." It certainly has a specific psychological impact on the mujahideen in terms of unity of purpose, and on the enemies by observing the Muslims' dedication to their duties even on the battlefield.

The Method of the Prayer of Fear

In the above verse, there is not much detail regarding the specific method of the Prayer of Fear; this is the Quran's style—to state general principles and leave the details to the "Sunnah."

The method of the Prayer of Fear, as derived from the Sunnah, is that four-unit (rak'ah) prayers are reduced to two units. The first group performs one unit with the Imam, and after the Imam completes one unit, he remains stationary. That group then completes the second unit individually and returns to the battlefront. Then, the second group takes their place, performing their first unit with the Imam and their second unit individually. (There are other opinions regarding the method of the Prayer of Fear, but what we have mentioned above is the most well-known view).

Ayah

فَإِذَا قَضَيْتُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قِيَـٰمًا وَقُعُودًا وَعَلَىٰ جُنُوبِكُمْ ۚ فَإِذَا ٱطْمَأْنَنتُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ كَانَتْ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ كِتَـٰبًا مَّوْقُوتًا (١٠٣)
When you have finished the prayers, remember Allah, standing, sitting, and lying down, and when you feel secure, perform the [complete] prayers, for the prayer is indeed a timed prescription for the faithful. (103)

Commentary

The Importance of the Obligatory Prayer

Following the instruction for the Prayer of Fear (Salat al-Khawf) in the previous verse and the necessity of establishing prayer even during war, this verse states: "After you have finished the prayer, do not forget the remembrance of Allah, and remember Allah and seek His help while standing, sitting, and lying on your sides."

(فَإِذا قَضَيْتُمُ الصَّلاةَ فَاذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ قِياماً وَ قُعُوداً [^1] وَ عَلى‌ جُنُوبِكُمْ‌) The meaning of remembering Allah while standing, sitting, and lying on one's side may refer to the states of rest during the intervals that occur on the battlefield.
