It may also refer to the various combat positions where soldiers use different weapons, including archery equipment, sometimes while standing, sometimes sitting, and sometimes lying on their sides.

The above verse, in reality, points to an important Islamic instruction: that performing prayer at specific times does not mean that in other states a person should remain heedless of God. Rather, prayer is a disciplinary instruction that revives the spirit of mindfulness toward the Lord within a person, enabling them to keep Allah in mind during the intervals between prayers, whether on the battlefield or elsewhere.

In numerous narrations, the above verse has been interpreted regarding the manner in which the sick perform prayer; that if they are able, they should pray standing, if they cannot, then sitting, and if they still cannot, then lying on their side. This interpretation is, in fact, a type of generalization and expansion of the verse's meaning, even though the verse is not exclusive to this case. [^1] Then the Quran says: The instruction for the Prayer of Fear is an exceptional rule, and "as soon as the state of fear is removed, you must perform the prayer in the same normal manner."

(فَإِذَا اطْمَأْنَنْتُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ).

And at the end, it explains the secret behind all these recommendations and precision regarding prayer as follows:

"For prayer is a fixed and unchanging duty for the believers."

(إِنَّ الصَّلاةَ كانَتْ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ كِتاباً مَوْقُوتاً).

The word "Mawqut" is derived from the root "Waqt" (time). Therefore, the meaning of the verse is that if you observe that even on the battlefield Muslims must perform this Islamic duty, it is because prayer has specific times that cannot be violated. [^2] However, in several narrations mentioned regarding this verse, "Mawqutan" has been interpreted as "fixed" and "obligatory," which is certainly compatible with the concept of the verse, and its result is nearly the same as the first meaning.

Question: [What is the necessity of performing prayer at specific times?]

Some say: We do not deny the philosophy and importance of prayer and its educational effects, but what is the necessity for it to be performed at specific times? Would it not be better if people were left free so that everyone could perform this duty whenever they had the opportunity and spiritual readiness?

Answer

Experience has shown that if educational matters are not placed under discipline and specific conditions, some people will consign them to oblivion, and their foundation will be completely shaken. Such matters must certainly be placed at specific times and under strict discipline so that no one has an excuse or pretext to abandon them. Especially since performing these acts of worship at a specific time, particularly in congregation, possesses a special glory, impact, and greatness that cannot be denied; in reality, it forms a great class for human development.

[^1]: "Qiyam" has both an infinitive meaning (i.e., standing) and is the plural of "Qa'im" (i.e., those standing), and "Qu'ud" is the same, meaning both "sitting" and "those sitting." In the above verse, both meanings are possible.

[^1]: To learn about the aforementioned Hadiths, refer to Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 1, Page 545.

[^2]: In Kanz al-Irfan, Volume 1, Page 59, this meaning is confirmed, and in Tafsir al-Tibyan and Majma' al-Bayan, it is mentioned as one opinion.

Ayah

وَلَا تَهِنُوا۟ فِى ٱبْتِغَآءِ ٱلْقَوْمِ ۖ إِن تَكُونُوا۟ تَأْلَمُونَ فَإِنَّهُمْ يَأْلَمُونَ كَمَا تَأْلَمُونَ ۖ وَتَرْجُونَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَرْجُونَ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١٠٤)
Do not slacken in the pursuit of these people. If you are suffering, they are also suffering like you, but you expect from Allah what they do not expect, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (104)

Occasion of Revelation: A Similar Weapon for Every Weapon

It has been narrated from Ibn Abbas and some other commentators that after the painful events of the Battle of Uhud, the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله ascended Mount Uhud, and Abu Sufyan also stood upon the mountain. In a triumphant tone, Abu Sufyan shouted: "O Muhammad! One day we are victorious, and another day you!" (meaning this victory of ours is in exchange for the defeat we suffered at Badr). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله told the Muslims: "Respond to him immediately" (as if He wanted to prove to Abu Sufyan that those raised in His school are all fully aware). The Muslims said:

"Our situation is never equal to yours; our martyrs are in Paradise, and your slain are in Hellfire." Abu Sufyan shouted and uttered this sentence as a boastful slogan:

Lana al-Uzza wa la Uzza lakum.

("We possess the great idol 'Uzza,' and you do not.") The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "In response to their slogan, you say:

Allahu Mawlana wa la Mawla lakum.

('Allah is our Guardian and Protector, and you have no guardian or protector.')" Abu Sufyan, seeing himself powerless against this vibrant Islamic slogan, abandoned the idol "Uzza" and clung to the idol "Hubal," shouting: U'lu Hubal! ("Exalted be Hubal!").

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله commanded that this ignorant slogan also be crushed with a stronger and firmer slogan, saying:

Allahu A'la wa Ajall!

("Allah is Higher and more Sublime!") Abu Sufyan, having gained nothing from his various slogans, shouted: "Our meeting place shall be the land of Badr al-Sughra."

The Muslims returned from the battlefield with many wounds and injuries, while deeply distressed by the painful events of Uhud. At this moment, the above verse was revealed, warning them not to be lax in pursuing the polytheists and not to be disheartened by these painful events. Despite their condition, the Muslims rose to pursue the enemy, and when the news reached the polytheists, they quickly distanced themselves from Medina and returned to Mecca. [^1]

This occasion of revelation teaches us that Muslims must not overlook any of the enemy's tactics. Against every means of struggle—whether "physical" or "psychological"—they must prepare a firmer and more crushing means. Against the logic of the enemies, they must provide more powerful logic; against their weapons, superior weapons; and even against their slogans, more crushing slogans. Otherwise, events will shift in favor of the enemy.

Therefore, in an era like ours, instead of merely grieving over the painful events and horrific corruptions surrounding Muslims from every side, they must take active measures. Against unhealthy books and publications, they must provide healthy books and publications. Against the equipped propaganda tools of the enemies, they must utilize the most equipped modern propaganda tools. Against unhealthy centers, they must provide healthy recreation for their youth. And against the plans, theses, and doctrines presented by various political, economic, and social schools, they must provide comprehensive Islamic plans in a modern form to everyone. It is only by using this method that they can preserve their existence and emerge as a leading group in the world.

Commentary

Following the verses related to Jihad and Hijrah, the above verse says the following to revive the spirit of sacrifice in Muslims: "And do not weaken in pursuit of the enemy."

(وَ لا تَهِنُوا فِي ابْتِغاءِ الْقَوْمِ)

This is an indication that you should never adopt a purely defensive posture against stubborn enemies; rather, always maintain an offensive spirit against such individuals. Psychologically, this has a tremendous effect on crushing the enemy's morale. Just as in the event of Uhud, after that severe defeat, using this method caused the enemies of Islam—who had left the field without a complete victory—to abandon the thought of returning to the battlefield that had occurred to them midway, and to quickly distance themselves from Medina.

Then, it presents a vivid and clear argument for this command, saying:

"Why should you show weakness, while if you suffer pain and hardship in Jihad, your enemies also have a share of these discomforts? With the difference that you have hope in the help and vast mercy of the Lord of the worlds, while they lack such hope."

(إِنْ تَكُونُوا تَأْلَمُونَ فَإِنَّهُمْ يَأْلَمُونَ كَما تَأْلَمُونَ وَ تَرْجُونَ مِنَ اللَّهِ ما لا يَرْجُونَ)

And at the end, for further emphasis, He says: "Do not forget that all these discomforts, sufferings, efforts, and occasionally your weaknesses and negligence are not hidden from the view of Allah's knowledge" (وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً), and therefore, you will see the result of all of them.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Tibyan, Vol. 3, p. 314; Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 3, p. 105.

Ayahs

إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِمَآ أَرَىٰكَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَلَا تَكُن لِّلْخَآئِنِينَ خَصِيمًا (١٠٥)
Indeed We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, so that you may judge between the people by what Allah has shown you; do not be an advocate for the traitors, (105)
وَٱسْتَغْفِرِ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١٠٦)
and plead to Allah for forgiveness; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (106)

Circumstances of Revelation

Regarding the circumstances of the revelation of the above verses, a detailed account has been narrated, the summary of which is as follows:

The tribe of Bani Ubayriq was a relatively well-known tribe. There were three brothers from this tribe named "Bishr," "Bashir," and "Mubashir." "Bashir" broke into the house of a Muslim named "Rifa'ah" and stole a sword, armor, and some food supplies. Rifa'ah's nephew, named "Qatadah," who was among the mujahideen of Badr, reported the incident to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. However, the three brothers accused a faithful Muslim named "Labid," who lived in that house with them, of this crime.

"Labid" was deeply enraged by this unjust accusation; he drew his sword, approached them, and shouted: "Do you accuse me of theft? While you are more deserving of this act! You are the same hypocrites who used to lampoon the Messenger of Allah and attribute your satirical poems to the Quraysh. You must either prove this accusation you have leveled against me, or I shall strike you with my sword."

When the thieving brothers saw this, they tried to pacify him. However, when they learned that the matter had reached the ears of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله through Qatadah, they sought out one of the eloquent speakers of their tribe to go with a group to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and, with a self-righteous appearance, exonerate the thieves and accuse Qatadah of making a false allegation.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, according to the "duty to act based on outward evidence," accepted the testimony of this group and rebuked Qatadah. Qatadah, who was innocent, was greatly distressed by this turn of events and returned to his uncle, expressing profound regret. His uncle comforted him and said: "Do not worry; Allah is our supporter."

The above verses were revealed, exonerating this innocent man and severely rebuking the actual traitors.

Another circumstance of revelation has been narrated for the verse: that a suit of armor belonging to one of the Ansar was stolen during one of the battles. They suspected a person from the tribe of Bani Ubayriq. When the thief realized the danger, he threw the armor into the house of a Jewish man and asked his tribe to testify to his innocence before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and to take the presence of the armor in the Jew's house as proof of his exoneration. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, seeing this, exonerated him based on the outward evidence, and the Jewish man was condemned. Then the above verses were revealed and clarified the truth.

Commentary

Do Not Support the Traitors

In these verses, Allah first advises the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that the purpose of sending this Heavenly Book is for the principles of truth and justice to be implemented among the people: "Indeed, We have sent down to you the Book in truth so that you may judge between the people by what Allah has shown you."

(إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِما أَراكَ اللَّهُ‌).

Then He warns the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله never to support the traitors (وَ لا تَكُنْ لِلْخائِنِينَ خَصِيماً).

Although the address in this verse is to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, there is no doubt that this ruling is a general one regarding all judges and arbiters. For this reason, such an address does not imply that such an act could possibly stem from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, as the aforementioned ruling applies to all individuals.

In the next verse, He commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to seek forgiveness from the presence of Allah (وَ اسْتَغْفِرِ اللَّهَ‌), for Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.

(إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً).

Regarding what the "seeking of forgiveness" (istighfar) is for here, several possibilities exist:

First, that the "istighfar" is for that "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla) which occurred due to the haste in judgment regarding the circumstances of the revelation of the verses. That is, although that amount of confession and testimony from the parties was sufficient for your judgment, it would have been better to conduct even more investigation in this case.

Second, that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله judged in this instance according to the judicial laws of Islam, and since the evidence of the traitors was stronger in outward appearance, the right was given to them. After the reality became clear and the right reached its rightful owner, He commands him to seek forgiveness from Allah—not because a sin was committed, but because due to the orchestrations of some, the right of a Muslim was on the verge of being destroyed (meaning, so to speak, the istighfar is for the actual reality, not the outward judgment).

The possibility has also been raised that the istighfar here was for the parties of the dispute who engaged in falsehoods while presenting and pursuing the case.

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated that he said:

"I am only a human being, and you bring your disputes to me. Perhaps some of you are more eloquent in presenting their arguments than others, and I judge according to what I hear. So, if I judge in favor of someone regarding something that belongs to his brother, let him not take it, for I am only giving him a piece of the Fire!"

From this hadith, it is clearly evident that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is duty-bound to judge according to the outward evidence and the arguments of the parties to the dispute. Of course, in such an arbitration, the right usually reaches the rightful owner, but sometimes the outward evidence and the testimony of witnesses might not coincide with reality. It is here that one must realize that the judge's ruling in no way changes the reality; truth does not become falsehood, and falsehood does not become truth.

[^1]: Al-Manar, Vol. 5, p. 394, citing Sahih Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.

Ayahs

وَلَا تُجَـٰدِلْ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْتَانُونَ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ مَن كَانَ خَوَّانًا أَثِيمًا (١٠٧)
And do not plead for those who betray themselves; indeed Allah does not like someone who is treacherous and sinful. (107)
يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَا يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ وَهُوَ مَعَهُمْ إِذْ يُبَيِّتُونَ مَا لَا يَرْضَىٰ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطًا (١٠٨)
They try to hide [their real character] from people, but they do not try to hide from Allah, though He is with them when they conspire overnight with a discourse that He does not approve of. And Allah comprehends whatever they do. (108)
هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ جَـٰدَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا فَمَن يُجَـٰدِلُ ٱللَّهَ عَنْهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ أَم مَّن يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكِيلًا (١٠٩)
Aha! There you are, pleading for them in the life of this world! But who will plead for them with Allah on the Day of Resurrection, or will be their defender? (109)

Commentary

Following the previous instructions regarding the lack of support for traitors, these verses continue by stating: "Never support the traitors and those who have betrayed themselves."

(وَ لا تُجادِلْ عَنِ الَّذِينَ يَخْتانُونَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ‌)

"For Allah does not love the sinful traitors."

(إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُحِبُّ مَنْ كانَ خَوَّاناً أَثِيماً)

It is noteworthy that in this verse He says: "those who betrayed themselves," while we know that according to the circumstances of the verse's revelation, the treachery was committed against others. This refers to that subtle point which the Quran mentions repeatedly: that whatever action a human performs, its good or bad effects—whether spiritual or material—reach himself before anyone else. As He has stated elsewhere:

إِنْ أَحْسَنْتُمْ أَحْسَنْتُمْ لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ وَ إِنْ أَسَأْتُمْ فَلَها:

"If you do good, you do good for yourselves; and if you do evil, [you do it] to yourselves." [^1] Alternatively, it may refer to another point that the Quran also affirms: that all human beings are like members of one body, and if someone harms another, it is as if he has harmed himself—just like someone who slaps his own face with his own hand.

Another point is that the verse is not about those who, for instance, committed a betrayal once and then regretted it; for such people, one should not exercise severity but rather leniency. The verse concerns those for whom betrayal has become a part of their life's program, as evidenced by "yakh-tanun" (يختانون), which is a present continuous verb indicating persistence, and by "khawwan" (خوان), which is an intensive form meaning "one who betrays much," and "athim" (اثيم), which means "sinful" and is mentioned as an emphasis for "khawwan." In the preceding verse, they were also referred to as "kha'in" (خائن), which is an active participle and carries a descriptive meaning, indicating the repetition of the act.

Then, He rebukes such traitors, saying: "They are ashamed to have the inner reality of their actions revealed to the people, but they are not ashamed before Allah!" (يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ النَّاسِ وَ لا يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ اللَّهِ‌)

[^1]: Surah Al-Isra, 7.

"Allah, who is with them everywhere, and was with them at that time when they were plotting schemes of betrayal in the heart of the night and saying words that Allah was not pleased with, encompasses all their actions."

(وَ هُوَ مَعَهُمْ إِذْ يُبَيِّتُونَ ما لا يَرْضى‌ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطاً)

Then, addressing the tribe of the thief who defended him, He says: "Suppose you defend them in the life of this world, but who is it that can defend them on the Day of Resurrection against Allah, or who will be their advocate to manage their affairs and resolve their troubles?" (ها أَنْتُمْ هؤُلاءِ جادَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا فَمَنْ يُجادِلُ اللَّهَ عَنْهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ أَمْ مَنْ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكِيلًا)

Therefore, your defense of them is of very little effect, for in the eternal life, and that too before Allah, there is no defender for them.

In reality, in the three verses above, first the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and all righteous judges are advised to be fully vigilant so that individuals do not trample the rights of others through staged scenes and false witnesses.

Then, the treacherous individuals, and subsequently their defenders, are warned to be mindful of the evil consequences of their actions in this world and the next.

This is one of the secrets of the Quran's eloquence: that in an incident—even if it appears small on the surface and revolves around a piece of armor and some food items, or involves a Jew and an enemy of Islam—it examines all aspects of the matter and provides the necessary reminders and warnings in every case. From the great Prophet of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, whose mantle is pure from any stain of sin by virtue of his infallibility (Ismah), to the sinful treacherous individuals and those who defend such people out of tribal prejudice—each is addressed according to their respective position.

Ayahs

وَمَن يَعْمَلْ سُوٓءًا أَوْ يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُۥ ثُمَّ يَسْتَغْفِرِ ٱللَّهَ يَجِدِ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١١٠)
Whoever commits evil or wrongs himself and then pleads to Allah for forgiveness, will find Allah all-forgiving, all-merciful. (110)
وَمَن يَكْسِبْ إِثْمًا فَإِنَّمَا يَكْسِبُهُۥ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١١١)
Whoever commits a sin, commits it only against himself; and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (111)
وَمَن يَكْسِبْ خَطِيٓـَٔةً أَوْ إِثْمًا ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِۦ بَرِيٓـًٔا فَقَدِ ٱحْتَمَلَ بُهْتَـٰنًا وَإِثْمًا مُّبِينًا (١١٢)
But someone who commits an iniquity or sin and then accuses an innocent person of it, is indeed guilty of calumny and a flagrant sin. (112)

Commentary

In these three verses, following the discussions regarding treachery and false accusations mentioned in the previous verses, three general rulings are stated:

1- First, reference is made to the fact that the path of repentance is open to evildoers in any case: "Whoever does evil or wrongs himself and then truly regrets it and seeks forgiveness from Allah and sets out to make amends, shall find Allah Forgiving and Merciful."

(وَ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً أَوْ يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُ ثُمَّ يَسْتَغْفِرِ اللَّهَ يَجِدِ اللَّهَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً.)

It should be noted that two things are mentioned in the verse: one is su’ (evil) and the other is dhulm al-nafs (wronging oneself). Considering the context of contrast and the linguistic root of su’, which means harming another, it is inferred that every type of sin—whether a person harms another or himself—is forgivable upon sincere repentance and compensation.

Furthermore, from the expression "shall find Allah Forgiving and Merciful" (يَجِدِ اللَّهَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً), it is understood that sincere repentance has such an effect that a person finds its result within his own soul. On one hand, the distressing effect of the sin vanishes due to Allah being Forgiving (Ghafur), and on the other hand, he feels his distance from Allah’s mercy and favors—which was the result of disobedience—transformed into closeness by virtue of His Mercifulness (Rahimiyyah).

2- The second verse explains the same reality that was briefly mentioned in the preceding verses, which is: "Every sin a person commits, he ultimately harms himself and takes a step to his own detriment."

(وَ مَنْ يَكْسِبْ إِثْماً فَإِنَّما يَكْسِبُهُ عَلى‌ نَفْسِهِ‌).

At the end of the verse, He says: "Allah is All-Knowing and aware of the deeds of His servants, and He is All-Wise, punishing everyone according to what they deserve."

(وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً).

In this way, although sins may appear different on the surface—sometimes the harm reaches another and sometimes it reaches oneself—after final analysis, they all return to the person himself. The evil effects of sin appear first and foremost in the spirit and soul of the individual. The first flame catches the hem of the "striker of the flint"; This is the retribution for one who kisses the threshold of tyranny. [^1]

3- In the final verse, referring to the gravity of the sin of falsely accusing innocent people, He says: "Whoever commits a fault or a sin and then throws the blame upon an innocent person, has indeed burdened himself with a calumny and a manifest sin."

(وَ مَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ بَرِيئاً فَقَدِ احْتَمَلَ بُهْتاناً وَ إِثْماً مُبِيناً).

In this verse, the sins a person commits and blames on another are divided into two categories: khati’ah (fault) and ithm (sin). Regarding the difference between these two, commentators and linguists have said much, but what seems closer to the truth is that khati’ah (from khata’) originally refers to slips and sins committed without intent, which sometimes carry an expiation or fine. However, the meaning of khati’ah gradually expanded to include every sin, whether intentional or unintentional, because no sin (intentional or otherwise) is compatible with the sound human spirit; if it occurs, it is in reality a type of slip and error unworthy of his station. Consequently, khati’ah has a broad meaning encompassing both intentional and unintentional sins. However, ithm is usually applied to intentional and voluntary sins. Originally, ithm means something that holds a person back from a task, and since sins hold man back from good deeds, they are called ithm.

Additionally, it should be noted that a subtle expression is used in the verse regarding false accusation: the sin is likened to an "arrow" and attributing it to another is likened to "shooting at a target" (يَرْمِ بِهِ). This indicates that just as shooting an arrow at someone might destroy them, throwing the arrow of sin at someone who did not commit it may destroy their reputation, which is equivalent to their blood. Naturally, the burden and consequence of this act will remain on the shoulders of the accuser forever. The expression ihtamala (he carries/burdens himself) also points to the weight and permanence of this responsibility.

The Crime of False Accusation

Falsely accusing an innocent person is among the foulest deeds, which Islam has severely condemned. The above verse and numerous Islamic narrations regarding this subject clarify Islam's view on this matter. Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام narrates from a wise man:

"Accusing an innocent person is heavier than the firm-rooted mountains!" [^1]

Accusing innocent people is incompatible with the spirit of faith, as narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"When a believer accuses his brother, faith dissolves in his heart just as salt dissolves in water!" [^2]

In reality, calumny (buhtan) and false accusation are the worst types of lying, for they possess the great evils of lying, the harms of backbiting, and are the worst type of oppression. Therefore, it is narrated from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

"Whoever accuses a believing man or woman, or says something about them that is not in them, Allah the Almighty will place him on a mound of fire on the Day of Resurrection until he comes out of [the responsibility of] what he said." [^1]

In truth, the prevalence of this cowardly act in an environment leads to the collapse of the social order and justice, the tainting of truth with falsehood, the entrapment of the innocent, the exoneration of the guilty, and the destruction of public trust.

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, under the root b-h-t. [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 269, Chapter on Accusation and Ill-Thinking.

Ayah

وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ لَهَمَّت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُمْ أَن يُضِلُّوكَ وَمَا يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۖ وَمَا يَضُرُّونَكَ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ وَأَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَكَ مَا لَمْ تَكُن تَعْلَمُ ۚ وَكَانَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ عَظِيمًا (١١٣)
Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy on you, a group of them were bent on leading you astray; but they do not mislead anyone except themselves, and they cannot do you any harm. Allah has sent down to you the Book and wisdom, and He has taught you what you did not know, and great is Allah’s grace upon you. (113)

Commentary

This verse refers to another aspect of the incident involving the Banu Ubayriq, which was mentioned in the context of the revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) a few verses prior. The verse states: "If it were not for the grace and mercy of your Lord upon you, a group of hypocrites or those like them had resolved to divert you from the path of truth and justice, but the Divine grace encompassed you and protected you."

(وَ لَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ وَ رَهْمَتُهُ لَهَمَّتْ طائِفَةٌ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُضِلُّوكَ‌).

They intended to strike a blow to the social and spiritual personality of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله by accusing an innocent person and then drawing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله into this affair, while simultaneously fulfilling their malicious intentions against an innocent Muslim. However, Allah, who is the Protector of His Prophet, rendered their schemes null and void.

Some have mentioned another occasion of revelation for this verse, stating that a delegation from the "Banu Thaqif" tribe came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "We will pledge allegiance to you on two conditions: first, that we do not break our idols with our own hands, and second, that you grant us a respite for one more year to worship the idol 'Uzza'!" Allah commanded His Prophet not to show any flexibility toward their proposals. The above verse was revealed, announcing to the Prophet that the grace of God protects him against such temptations.

Then the Quran says: "They only mislead themselves and they cannot harm you in the least."

(وَ ما يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَ ما يُضرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ). Finally, it explains the reason for the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله immunity from deviation, error, and sin as follows: "Allah has sent down to you the Book and wisdom and taught you what you did not know."

(وَ أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ وَ الْحِكْمَةَ وَ عَلَّمَكَ ما لَمْ تَكُنْ تَعْلَمُ‌).

And at the end of the verse, He says: "And the grace of Allah upon you has been ever great." (وَ كانَ فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ عَظِيماً).

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 111.

The Source of the Infallibility of the Prophets

The above verse is among those that point to the issue of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله immunity from error, mistake, and sin. It states that if Divine assistance had not been with you, they would have misled you; however, with this assistance, they will not be capable of doing so and will cause you no harm in this regard.

In this way, so that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله can be a role model for the Ummah in everything and a pattern for goodness and virtues, and to remain far from the painful consequences of slips that might affect a great leader, and so that the Ummah remains safe from confusion regarding the issue of obeying the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and does not get caught in a contradiction between obedience and non-obedience, Allah insures His Prophet against error and sin. This is to attract the complete trust of the people, which is one of the primary conditions of Divine leadership.

In the latter part of the verse, one of the fundamental reasons for the issue of Infallibility (Ismah) is mentioned in brief: Allah has taught the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sciences and knowledge in the light of which he becomes insured against sin and error. This is because knowledge and awareness (at the ultimate level) result in infallibility. For example, a physician who sees contaminated water filled with various microbes—cholera, malaria, and dozens of other dangerous diseases—and has studied it under a microscope in a laboratory and clearly perceived its lethal effect, it is impossible for him to drink that water. That is, this knowledge gives him immunity against committing this act, whereas ignorance of it might lead to its commission.

Similarly, the source of many mistakes is ignorance of the premises or the requirements and consequences of an action. Therefore, one who, through Divine revelation and the teaching of the Lord, possesses complete awareness of various matters, will neither fall into a slip, nor deviation, nor sin.

However, let there be no misunderstanding: although such knowledge for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is from Allah's side, it does not take on a compulsory (forced) nature. That is, the Prophet is never forced to act upon his knowledge; rather, he acts upon it by his own choice. Just as the aforementioned physician, despite having knowledge of the condition of that contaminated water, is not forced to refrain from drinking it, but rather refrains from drinking it by his own will.

And if it is asked why the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was the recipient of such Divine grace and not others, it must be said that this is because of the heavy responsibility of leadership placed upon his shoulders, which is not placed upon others. For Allah grants power and strength in proportion to the responsibility He gives. (Reflect on this).

Ayah

۞ لَّا خَيْرَ فِى كَثِيرٍ مِّن نَّجْوَىٰهُمْ إِلَّا مَنْ أَمَرَ بِصَدَقَةٍ أَوْ مَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ إِصْلَـٰحٍۭ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ فَسَوْفَ نُؤْتِيهِ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (١١٤)
There is no good in much of their secret talks, excepting him who enjoins charity or what is right or reconciliation between people, and whoever does that, seeking Allah’s pleasure, soon We shall give him a great reward. (114)

Commentary

Secret Conversations

In the preceding verses, reference was made to the secret nighttime meetings and the mischievous plots of some hypocrites or those like them. In this verse, the matter is discussed in more detail under the title of "Najwa" (secret talk).

"Najwa" does not only mean whispering in the ear; rather, it encompasses any kind of secret and private meetings. This is because it is originally derived from the root "Najwah" (on the weight of daf'ah), meaning "elevated land." Since elevated lands are separated from their surroundings, and because secret meetings and whispered conversations are separated from those around them, they are called "Najwa." Some believe that all these terms are derived from the root najat, meaning "salvation" or "rescue," because an elevated point is safe from the rush of a flood, and a secret meeting or whispered talk is kept away from the knowledge of others.

In any case, the verse says: "In most of their confidential and secret meetings, which are built upon mischievous plans, there is no good or benefit."

(لا خَيْرَ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنْ نَجْواهُمْ‌) Then, so that it is not imagined that every kind of Najwa, whispered talk, or secret meeting is blameworthy and forbidden, several cases are mentioned as an introduction to a general law, stated as exceptions at the end of the verse, saying:

"Except for one who, in his secret talk, recommends charity and helping others, or performing a good deed, or reconciliation between people."

(إِلَّا مَنْ أَمَرَ بِصَدَقَةٍ أَوْ مَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ إِصْلاحٍ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ‌) And "these types of secret talks, if they are not for the sake of showing off and hypocrisy, but rather the intention behind them is to seek the pleasure of the Lord, Allah will ordain a great reward for them."

(وَ مَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ ابْتِغاءَ مَرْضاتِ اللَّهِ فَسَوْفَ نُؤْتِيهِ أَجْراً عَظِيماً) Fundamentally, Najwa, whispered conversations, and the formation of secret meetings are introduced in the Quran as a satanic act.

"إِنَّمَا النَّجْوى‌ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ‌":

"Secret talk is only from Satan" [^1] because this act is often carried out for incorrect deeds; for performing a good, useful, and positive deed is usually not a confidential or hidden matter that people would want to carry out through whispered conversations.

However, since extraordinary circumstances sometimes arise where a person is forced to use the method of Najwa for good deeds, this exception has appeared repeatedly in the Quran:

يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا تَناجَيْتُمْ فَلا تَتَناجَوْا بِالْإِثْمِ وَ الْعُدْوانِ وَ مَعْصِيَةِ الرَّسُولِ وَ تَناجَوْا بِالْبِرِّ وَ تَقْوى‌:

"O you who have believed, when you converse privately, do not converse about sin and aggression and disobedience to the Messenger, but converse about righteousness and piety." [^2] Essentially, if Najwa is performed in the presence of a group, it arouses the suspicion of individuals and sometimes even creates pessimism among friends. For this reason, it is better that this method not be used except in cases of necessity, and the philosophy of the aforementioned ruling in the Quran is precisely this.

Of course, sometimes preserving a person's dignity requires the use of Najwa, including financial aid, which is mentioned in the above verse as "charity" (Sadaqah).

Or "enjoining what is good" (Amr bil-Ma'ruf), which sometimes, if said openly, causes the person to feel ashamed before the crowd, and perhaps for this reason, they might refuse to accept it and show resistance; this is expressed in the above verse as "Ma'ruf" (good deed).

Or in cases of "reconciliation between people," where sometimes speaking about issues openly prevents reconciliation, and it becomes necessary to speak with each of the parties to the dispute separately and in the form of Najwa so that the plan for reconciliation can be implemented.

In these three cases and those similar to them, necessity dictates that the positive work be placed under the umbrella of Najwa.

It is noteworthy that the three aforementioned cases are all included under the title of "Sadaqah" (charity), because he who enjoins what is good is paying the Zakat of his knowledge, and he who reconciles between people is paying the Zakat of his influence and status among the people. As it has been narrated from Ali عليه السلام that he said:

ان اللَّه فرض عليكم زكاة جاهكم كما فرض عليكم زكاة ما ملكت ايديكم‌

"Allah has made it obligatory upon you to pay the Zakat of your influence and social status, just as He has made it obligatory upon you to give the Zakat of your wealth." [^1] And it is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said to Abu Ayyub:

الا ادلك على صدقة يحبها اللَّه و رسوله تصلح بين الناس اذا تفاسدوا و تقرب بينهم اذا تباعدوا

"Shall I not guide you to a charity that Allah and His Messenger love? Reconcile between people when they become corrupted [in their relations] and bring them close together when they have become distant." [^1]

[^1]: Surah Al-Mujadila, Verse 10. [^2]: Surah Al-Mujadila, Verse 9. [^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 1, Page 550, and other commentaries.

Ayah

وَمَن يُشَاقِقِ ٱلرَّسُولَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَيَتَّبِعْ غَيْرَ سَبِيلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ نُوَلِّهِۦ مَا تَوَلَّىٰ وَنُصْلِهِۦ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ وَسَآءَتْ مَصِيرًا (١١٥)
Whoever defies the Apostle, after the guidance has become manifest to him, and follows a way other than that of the faithful, We shall abandon him to his devices and We shall make him enter hell, and it is an evil destination. (115)

Circumstances of Revelation

In the circumstances of revelation for the previous verses, we mentioned that Bashir ibn Ubayriq, after stealing from a Muslim, accused an innocent person and, through staging a scene in the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, attempted to exonerate himself. However, with the revelation of the preceding verses, he was disgraced. Following this disgrace, instead of repenting and returning to the right path, he chose the path of disbelief and officially left the ranks of the Muslims. The above verse was revealed, and while referring to this matter, it expressed a general and universal Islamic ruling.

Commentary

When a human being commits a wrong, two paths lie before them after becoming aware of it: the path of return and repentance—the effect of which in washing away sin was explained in the previous few verses—and the other path, which is the path of obstinacy and enmity. The grim consequence of this latter path is mentioned in this verse, where He says:

"And whoever opposes the Messenger after guidance has become clear to him and follows other than the way of the believers - We will let him pursue what he has turned to and observe him in Hell, and evil it is as a destination."

(وَ مَنْ يُشاقِقِ الرَّسُولَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ الْهُدى‌ وَ يَتَّبِعْ غَيْرَ سَبِيلِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ نُوَلِّهِ ما تَوَلَّى وَ نُصْلِهِ جَهَنَّمَ وَ ساءَتْ مَصِيراً).

It should be noted that "yushaqiq" (يُشاقِقِ) is derived from the root "shiqaq" (شقاق), meaning conscious opposition accompanied by hostility and enmity. The phrase "after guidance has become clear to him" (مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ الْهُدى‌) also emphasizes this meaning. In reality, such individuals cannot have a fate better than this—a fate that has both a grim end in this world and a painful end in the hereafter.

As for this world, as the Quran states, they become more entrenched in their wrong path day by day, and the angle of their deviation from the road of truth increases as they advance further into the wilderness. This is a destiny they have chosen for themselves, and it is a structure whose foundation was laid by their own hands. Therefore, no injustice has been done to them. When He says: "We will let him pursue what he has turned to" (نُوَلِّهِ ما تَوَلَّى‌), it refers to this deprivation of spiritual success (tawfiq), the inability to distinguish the truth, and advancing further into error (we have explained this subject in the commentary on guidance and misguidance in Volume 1, page 50).

And where He says: "and observe him in Hell" (نُصْلِهِ جَهَنَّمَ‌), it refers to their fate on the Day of Resurrection.

Regarding the phrase "We will let him pursue what he has turned to" (نُوَلِّهِ ما تَوَلَّى‌), there is another interpretation: "We will place such individuals under the guardianship of the fabricated deities they have chosen for themselves."

The Authority of Consensus (Ijma')

One of the four proofs of jurisprudence is "consensus" (Ijma'), meaning the agreement of Islamic scholars on a jurisprudential issue. In the principles of jurisprudence (Usul al-Fiqh), various proofs have been mentioned to establish the authority of consensus, including the above verse, which a group considers a proof for the authority of consensus. This is because it says:

"Whoever chooses a path other than the path of the believers has a grim fate in this world and the hereafter." Therefore, when the believers choose a path regarding an issue, everyone must follow it.

However, the truth is that the above verse has no connection to the issue of the authority of consensus. (Of course, we consider consensus to be a proof, provided that it reveals the statement of the Infallible (Ma'sum), or that the Infallible is personally present among the parties to the consensus, even if anonymously. But the proof for the authority of such a consensus is the authority of the Sunnah and the word of the Infallible, not the above verse). This is because:

Firstly, the punishments specified in the verse are for those who consciously oppose the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and choose a path other than that of the believers; that is, these two must go hand in hand to produce such a grim result, and furthermore, it must be done out of knowledge and awareness. This matter has no connection to the issue of the authority of consensus and does not make consensus a proof on its own.

Secondly, what is meant by "the way of the believers" (سَبِيلِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ) is the path of monotheism, the worship of God, and the essence of Islam, not jurisprudential edicts (fatawa) and secondary rulings, as the apparent meaning of the verse, in addition to its circumstances of revelation, testifies to this fact.

In reality, following a path other than that of the believers is nothing but opposition to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and both return to the same point.

Therefore, we read in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام:

When the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, was in Kufa, a group came to him and requested that he appoint an Imam for them (so they could pray the recommended prayers known as Tarawih in congregation, which they used to pray in congregation during the time of Umar). The Imam refused this and forbade such a congregation (because congregation in recommended prayers is not prescribed). Despite hearing this definitive ruling from their Imam and leader, this group persisted in their obstinacy and raised a clamor, saying: "Come and weep in this month of Ramadan!" A group of Ali's عليه السلام friends came to him and said: "Some people are not submitting to your command." He said: "Leave them be; let them choose whoever they want to perform this (unlawful) congregation," and then he recited the above verse [^1]. This hadith also confirms what we said regarding the interpretation of the verse.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 1, page 551.

Ayah

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِۦ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا (١١٦)
Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes. And whoever ascribes partners to Allah has certainly strayed into far error. (116)

Commentary

Polytheism, the Unforgivable Sin

Here, following the discussions regarding the hypocrites and apostates—those who return to disbelief after accepting Islam—the verse once again points to the significance of the sin of polytheism (shirk). It is a sin that is unforgivable, and no sin greater than it can be imagined.

The content of this verse appeared previously in this same Surah with a slight variation (Verse 48).

Of course, such repetition in educational and formative matters is a requirement of eloquence (balaghah), because fundamental and important issues must be repeated at various intervals so that they become firmly rooted in souls and thoughts.

In reality, sins are like various diseases; as long as the disease has not attacked the primary centers of the body and rendered them dysfunctional, the body's defensive power carries the hope of recovery. However, if, for example, the disease attacks the primary center of the body—the brain—and paralyzes it, the doors of hope will be closed, and certain death will follow. "Polytheism" is something that disables the sensitive center of the human soul and scatters darkness and gloom within one's being. In its presence, there is no hope of salvation. But if the reality of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the worship of the One—which is the source of every virtue, movement, and progression—remains alive, there is hope for forgiveness regarding other sins.

(إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَ يَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ) "Indeed, Allah does not forgive joining partners with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills." As we mentioned, this verse has been repeated twice in this same Surah so that the effects of polytheism and idol-worship, which had nested in the depths of the people's souls for long years, might be washed away forever, and the spiritual and material fruits of Monotheism might appear on the branches of their existence. However, the endings of the two verses differ slightly. Here He says:

"And whoever associates partners with Allah has certainly strayed into a remote error."

(وَ مَنْ يُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلالًا بَعِيداً). But previously He said: "And he who associates partners with Allah has certainly fabricated a tremendous sin."

(وَ مَنْ يُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ فَقَدِ افْتَرى‌ إِثْماً عَظِيماً).

In fact, there (Verse 48), the reference was to the great corruption of polytheism from the Divine perspective and the recognition of God, while here, its irreparable harms for the people themselves are expressed. There, the verse examines the theoretical aspect of the issue, and here, the practical aspect and its external results. It is clear that these two are, so to speak, inseparable (we have provided further explanations regarding this verse in Volume 3 of this Tafsir, page 409).

Ayahs

إِن يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ إِنَـٰثًا وَإِن يَدْعُونَ إِلَّا شَيْطَـٰنًا مَّرِيدًا (١١٧)
They invoke none but females besides Him, and invoke none but a froward Satan, (117)
لَّعَنَهُ ٱللَّهُ ۘ وَقَالَ لَأَتَّخِذَنَّ مِنْ عِبَادِكَ نَصِيبًا مَّفْرُوضًا (١١٨)
whom Allah has cursed, and who said, ‘I will surely take of Your servants a settled share, (118)
وَلَأُضِلَّنَّهُمْ وَلَأُمَنِّيَنَّهُمْ وَلَـَٔامُرَنَّهُمْ فَلَيُبَتِّكُنَّ ءَاذَانَ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ وَلَـَٔامُرَنَّهُمْ فَلَيُغَيِّرُنَّ خَلْقَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يَتَّخِذِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ وَلِيًّا مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ خَسِرَ خُسْرَانًا مُّبِينًا (١١٩)
and I will lead them astray and give them [false] hopes, and prompt them to slit the ears of cattle, and I will prompt them to alter Allah’s creation.’ Whoever takes Satan as a guardian instead of Allah has certainly incurred a manifest loss. (119)
يَعِدُهُمْ وَيُمَنِّيهِمْ ۖ وَمَا يَعِدُهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ إِلَّا غُرُورًا (١٢٠)
He makes them promises and gives them [false] hopes, yet Satan does not promise them anything but delusion. (120)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَلَا يَجِدُونَ عَنْهَا مَحِيصًا (١٢١)
The refuge of such shall be hell, and they will not find any escape from it. (121)

Commentary

The Schemes of Satan

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 4; p. 135

The first [verse] is an explanation of the state of the polytheists, whose grim fate was mentioned in the previous verse; in reality, it explains the cause of their severe deviation, stating: "They are so narrow-minded that they have abandoned the Creator of the vast universe and bow down before beings that possess not the slightest positive effect, but are sometimes, like Satan, destructive and misleading."

(إِنْ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلَّا إِناثاً وَ إِنْ يَدْعُونَ إِلَّا شَيْطاناً مَرِيداً) It is noteworthy that the deities of the polytheists in this verse are identified as being limited to two things: "Inath" (females) and "Shaytan Marid" (a rebellious Satan).

Inath is the plural of Untha, derived from the root Anath (on the weight of Adab), meaning a soft and flexible being. Therefore, when iron becomes soft in the fire, the Arabs say Anatha al-Hadid. If the female gender is called Inath or Mu'annath, it is because it is a more delicate and flexible gender.

However, some commentators believe that here the Quran refers to the famous idols of the Arab tribes, each of whom chose an idol for themselves and gave it a feminine name: Al-Lat meaning "goddess," the feminine of Allah; Uzza, the feminine of A'azz; as well as Manat, Isaf, Na'ilah, and the like. Yet, other great commentators believe that Inath here does not refer to the common meaning of "female," but rather to its linguistic root. That is, they worshipped deities that were nothing more than weak creatures, easily shaped by human hands into any form; their entire existence was one of "impressionability," "flexibility," and submission to events. In clearer terms, they were beings that possessed no will or choice of their own and were not the source of any benefit or harm.

As for the word Marid, linguistically it is derived from the root Marad (on the weight of Zard), meaning the shedding of leaves and branches from a tree. For this reason, a youth who has not yet grown hair on his face is called Amrad.

Accordingly, Shaytan Marid means a Satan whose every virtuous quality has fallen from the branches of his existence, leaving nothing of strength within him. Alternatively, it may be from the root Murud (on the weight of Surud), meaning rebellion and defiance; thus, their deity is a rebellious and destructive Satan.

In reality, the Quran categorizes their deities such that one group is ineffective and useless, while the other is rebellious and destructive. Anyone who submits to such deities is in manifest error!

Then, in the subsequent verses, it refers to the attributes of Satan, his goals, and the specific enmity he holds toward the children of Adam, explaining various parts of his programs. Before anything else, it states: "Allah has cursed him" (لَعَنَهُ اللَّهُ), meaning He has removed him from His mercy. In truth, the root of all his misfortunes and destructiveness is this distance from Allah's mercy, which befell him due to the pride and arrogance that seized him. It is obvious that such a being, who is void of any good or virtue due to his distance from Allah, cannot have a beneficial effect on the lives of others. How can one who has not found existence from the Giver of Existence become a creator of existence? Not only will he not be beneficial, but he will also be harmful.

Then He says: Satan has sworn to implement several programs:

1- I will take a determined portion from Your servants: (وَ قالَ لَأَتَّخِذَنَّ مِنْ عِبادِكَ نَصِيباً مَفْرُوضاً). He knows he does not have the power to mislead all of Allah's servants; only those who are pleasure-seekers, weak in faith, and weak in will are the ones who submit to him.

2- I will surely mislead them (وَ لَأُضِلَّنَّهُمْ).

3- I will keep them occupied with long and colorful desires (وَ لَأُمَنِّيَنَّهُمْ) [1].

4- I will invite them to superstitious acts; among them, I will command them to slit or cut the ears of cattle. (وَ لَآمُرَنَّهُمْ فَلَيُبَتِّكُنَّ آذانَ الْأَنْعامِ). This refers to one of the ugly practices of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) prevalent among idolaters, where they would slit or completely cut off the ears of certain cattle, deeming it forbidden to ride them and making no use of them whatsoever.

5- I will command them to change the pure creation of Allah. (وَ لَآمُرَنَّهُمْ فَلَيُغَيِّرُنَّ خَلْقَ اللَّهِ). This sentence indicates that Allah has placed monotheism, the worship of the One, and every praiseworthy trait and disposition within the original nature (Fitrah) of man. However, Satanic whispers and whims divert man from this correct path and lead him into bypasses. The evidence for this is verse 30 of Surah Ar-Rum:

“So set your face steadily to the pure faith—the nature (Fitrah) of Allah upon which He has created mankind. There is no change in the creation of Allah. That is the upright religion.”

[^1]: This word is derived from the root Mana (on the weight of Man’), which means estimation and measurement, but it is often used to mean imaginary measurements and illusory desires. If semen is called Mani, it is because the initial measurement of living beings begins from it.

It is also narrated from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that what is meant by it is changing the nature of monotheism and the command of Allah [1]. This is an irreparable harm that Satan inflicts upon the foundation of human happiness, for he distorts truths and realities with a series of illusions and whispers, and subsequently, happiness is turned into wretchedness.

In the end, He states a general principle, saying: "Whoever takes Satan as a guardian and protector instead of Allah has surely suffered a manifest loss." (وَ مَنْ يَتَّخِذِ الشَّيْطانَ وَلِيًّا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ فَقَدْ خَسِرَ خُسْراناً مُبِيناً).

In the following verse, several points are mentioned which serve as evidence for the previous statement: "Satan constantly gives them false promises and keeps them occupied with long desires, but he performs nothing for them except deceit and trickery." (يَعِدُهُمْ وَ يُمَنِّيهِمْ وَ ما يَعِدُهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ إِلَّا غُرُوراً) [2]. And in the final verse of the verses under discussion, the ultimate fate of Satan's followers is stated as such: "Their abode is Hell, and they shall find no escape from it." (أُولئِكَ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَ لا يَجِدُونَ عَنْها مَحِيصاً) [3].

[^1]: Tafsir al-Tibyan, Vol. 3, p. 334. [^2]: Ghurur (deception) originally means the apparent effect of something, but it is mostly applied to effects that have a deceptive appearance and an unpleasant reality. Anything—including wealth, property, power, and status—that deceives a person and turns them away from the path of truth is called a source of Ghurur. [^3]: Mahis is from the root Hays, meaning to deviate or turn away. Therefore, Mahis means a means of deviation or a way of escape.

Ayah

وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۖ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقًّا ۚ وَمَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ قِيلًا (١٢٢)
But those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We will admit them into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them forever—a true promise of Allah, and who is truer in speech than Allah? (122)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, we read that those who take Satan as their guardian are in manifest loss; Satan gives them false promises and occupies them with vain desires, and the promise of Satan is nothing but deception and delusion. In contrast to them, this verse describes the ultimate end of the people of faith: "Those who believe and do righteous deeds, We shall soon admit them into gardens of Paradise beneath which rivers flow."

(وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ).

This blessing is not fleeting and unstable like the blessings of this world; rather, "the believers shall abide therein forever" (خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً).

This promise is not like the false promises of Satan; rather, it is "a true promise from Allah" (وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقًّا).

It is evident that no one can be more truthful than Allah in His promises and His words.

(وَ مَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ اللَّهِ قِيلًا) because breaking a promise is due to either inability, ignorance, or need—all of which are far removed from His Holy Presence.

Ayahs

لَّيْسَ بِأَمَانِيِّكُمْ وَلَآ أَمَانِىِّ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ۗ مَن يَعْمَلْ سُوٓءًا يُجْزَ بِهِۦ وَلَا يَجِدْ لَهُۥ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (١٢٣)
It will be neither after your hopes nor the hopes of the People of the Book: whoever commits evil shall be requited for it, and he will not find for himself any guardian or helper besides Allah. (123)
وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ مِن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ نَقِيرًا (١٢٤)
And whoever does righteous deeds, whether male or female, should he be faithful—such shall enter paradise and they will not be wronged [so much as] the speck on a date-stone. (124)

Occasion of Revelation

In Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and other commentaries, it is mentioned that the Muslims and the People of the Book were each boasting over the other. The People of the Book would say, "Our Prophet came before your Prophet, and our Book is more ancient than your Book." The Muslims would respond, "Our Prophet is the Seal of the Prophets, and His Book is the final and most complete of the Heavenly Books; therefore, we have superiority over you."

According to another narration, the Jews would say: "We are the chosen nation, and the fire of Hell shall not touch us except for a few numbered days." (وَ قالُوا لَنْ تَمَسَّنَا النَّارُ إِلَّا أَيَّاماً مَعْدُودَةً) [1] And the Muslims would say, "We are the best of nations, for Allah has said regarding us: (كُنْتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ‌)" [2] The above verse was revealed and drew a line of cancellation through these claims, identifying the value of every individual by their actions.

Commentary

Real and False Privileges

In these two verses, one of the most fundamental pillars of Islam is expressed: that the existential value of individuals and their reward or punishment has no connection to their claims and desires, but rather depends solely on action and faith. This is a fixed principle, an unchangeable Sunnah, and a law before which all nations are equal. Therefore, in the first verse, He says: "Virtue and superiority are not according to your desires nor the desires of the People of the Book." (لَيْسَ بِأَمانِيِّكُمْ وَ لا أَمانِيِّ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ‌).

Then He adds: "Whoever does an evil deed will be recompensed for it, and he will not find for himself besides Allah any protector or helper." (مَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً يُجْزَ بِهِ وَ لا يَجِدْ لَهُ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَ لا نَصيراً).

"And likewise, those who perform righteous deeds and are believers, whether male or female, they shall enter Paradise and not the least injustice shall be done to them." (وَ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِنَ الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى‌ وَ هُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ وَ لا يُظْلَمُونَ نَقِيراً) [3]

In this way, the Quran—to use a common expression—has "washed the hands" of everyone regarding such claims, considering alleged, imaginary, social, or racial affiliations to a religion alone to be useless. It introduces faith in the foundations of that school of thought and acting upon its programs as the basis.

Under the first verse, a hadith has been recorded in both Shi'a and Sunni sources stating that after this verse was revealed, some Muslims fell into such a state of terror that they wept out of fear. They knew that man is prone to error and that eventually, sins might be committed by him. If there were to be no pardon or forgiveness, and one were to see the punishment for all their evil deeds, the matter would become very difficult. Thus, they said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, "This verse has left nothing for us." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "By Him in Whose hand is my soul, the matter is exactly as revealed in this verse. However, let me give you this glad tiding so that it may bring you closer to Allah and encourage you to do good deeds: the calamities that befall you are an atonement for your sins, even the thorn that pricks your foot!" [4]

Question

Some might argue from the phrase: (وَ لا يَجِدْ لَهُ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَ لا نَصِيراً) "he will not find for himself besides Allah any protector or helper" against sins, that with the existence of this sentence, the issue of intercession (Shafa'at) and the like is completely negated, taking the verse as proof for the absolute denial of intercession.

Answer

As we have previously pointed out, the meaning of intercession is not that the intercessors—such as the Prophets, Imams عليهم السلام, and the righteous—possess an independent authority against Allah. Rather, their intercession is also by the command of Allah and without His permission, and without the merit and worthiness of those being interceded for, they will never undertake intercession. Therefore, such intercession ultimately returns to Allah and is considered a branch of the guardianship (Wilayah), assistance, and help of Allah.

[^1]: Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 80. [^2]: Surah Al-Imran, Verse 110. [^3]: The explanation of "Naqir" and its original root was mentioned under Verse 53 of this same Surah. [^4]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 1, Page 553.

Ayahs

وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ دِينًا مِّمَّنْ أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُۥ لِلَّهِ وَهُوَ مُحْسِنٌ وَٱتَّبَعَ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ حَنِيفًا ۗ وَٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ خَلِيلًا (١٢٥)
Who has a better religion than him who submits his will to Allah, being virtuous, and follows the creed of Abraham, a Hanif? And Allah took Abraham for a dedicated friend. (125)
وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّحِيطًا (١٢٦)
To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and Allah comprehends all things. (126)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the impact of faith and action, and how mere affiliation with any religion or creed, by itself, carries no weight. At the same time, in the verse under discussion, to prevent any misunderstanding from the previous discourse, the superiority of the religion of Islam over all other religions is expressed in this manner: "Who is better in religion than one who submits himself entirely to Allah, and does not cease from doing good, and follows the pure and sincere creed of Abraham?"

(وَ مَنْ أَحْسَنُ دِيناً مِمَّنْ أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُ لِلَّهِ وَ هُوَ مُحْسِنٌ وَ اتَّبَعَ مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ حَنِيفاً).

Of course, the verse is phrased as a question, but its purpose is to gain an admission from the listener regarding this reality.

In this verse, three things are considered the criteria for the best religion:

First, absolute submission to Allah: "أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُ لِلَّهِ" [^1]. Second, being a doer of good (وَ هُوَ مُحْسِنٌ). The meaning of "doing good" (Ihsan) here encompasses every kind of goodness through the heart, tongue, and action. In a Hadith narrated in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn regarding this verse from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, in response to the question of what is meant by Ihsan, we read:

"ان تعبد اللَّه كانك تراه فان لم تكن تراه فانه يراك"

"Ihsan (in this verse) is that you perform every act in the path of servitude to Allah as if you see Him; for if you do not see Him, He surely sees you and is a witness and observer over you."

And third, following the pure creed of Abraham.

(وَ اتَّبَعَ مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ حَنِيفاً) [^1]. At the end of the verse, the reason for emphasizing the creed of Abraham is stated: that Allah chose Abraham as His Khalil (intimate friend).

(وَ اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ إِبْراهِيمَ خَلِيلًا).

What does Khalil mean?

"Khalil" may be derived from the root khullah (rhyming with hujjah), meaning "friendship," or from the root khallah (rhyming with darbah), meaning "need and necessity." There is discussion among commentators as to which of these two meanings is closer to the concept of the above verse. A group believes that the second meaning is closer to the reality of the verse, because Abraham felt deeply that he was in need of the Lord in everything without exception.

However, since the verse says: "Allah took (granted this status to) Abraham," it is understood that the intended meaning is friendship. This is because if we say Allah

[^1]: Wajh literally means "face," but since the face is the mirror of the human soul and heart, and almost all the senses that connect a human to the outside world are located there, it sometimes also means the soul or the essence (dhat). As we read in verse 88 of Surah Al-Qasas: "كُلُّ شَيْ‌ءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ": Everything shall perish except His Pure Essence.

[^1]: Millah means "religion and creed." The only difference between them is that the word Millah is not attributed to Allah—they do not say "Millat-Allah"—rather it is attributed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, whereas the word "Din" is attributed to Allah, to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and to individuals. "Hanif" refers to one who has inclined away from false religions toward the Truth. The detailed commentary on this word was provided in the second volume of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 461.

chose Abraham as His friend, it seems very appropriate; whereas saying Allah chose Abraham as "needing Him" is less so, especially since the neediness of Allah's creatures is not exclusive to Abraham.

(يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ أَنْتُمُ الْفُقَراءُ إِلَى اللَّهِ—Fatir: 15) "O mankind, you are the ones in need of Allah," unlike the friendship of Allah, in which not everyone is equal.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "If Allah chose Abraham as a Khalil (friend), it was not because of a need for his friendship, but because Abraham was a beneficial servant of the Lord and diligent in seeking His pleasure." [^1] This narration also serves as evidence that Khalil here means friend.

As for what merits Abraham possessed that Allah granted him this status, various reasons have been mentioned in narrations, all of which could be the reason for this choice.

Among them: in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is narrated:

"انما اتخذ اللَّه ابراهيم خليلا لانه لم يرد احدا و لم يسئل احدا قط غير اللَّه"

"Allah chose Abraham as His Khalil because he never turned away a petitioner and never asked anyone for anything except Allah." [^2] From some other narrations, it is understood that this status was due to his frequent prostrations, feeding the hungry, praying in the heart of the night, or due to his diligence in the path of obeying the Lord.

Then, in the following verse, it points to the absolute ownership of the Lord and His encompassment of all things, saying: "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, for Allah encompasses all things."

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the noble verse.

[^2]: 'Uyun Akhbar al-Rida, as cited in Tafsir al-Safi under the verse, and Tafsir al-Burhan, vol. 1, p. 417.

(وَ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ مُحِيطاً).

This is an indication that if Allah chose Abraham as His friend, it was not out of a need for him, for Allah is self-sufficient from all, but rather it was because of the extraordinary and outstanding virtues and attributes of Abraham.

Ayah

وَيَسْتَفْتُونَكَ فِى ٱلنِّسَآءِ ۖ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِيهِنَّ وَمَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ فِى يَتَـٰمَى ٱلنِّسَآءِ ٱلَّـٰتِى لَا تُؤْتُونَهُنَّ مَا كُتِبَ لَهُنَّ وَتَرْغَبُونَ أَن تَنكِحُوهُنَّ وَٱلْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ ٱلْوِلْدَٰنِ وَأَن تَقُومُوا۟ لِلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۚ وَمَا تَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمًا (١٢٧)
They seek your ruling concerning women. Say, ‘Allah gives you a ruling concerning them and what is announced to you in the Book concerning girl orphans—whom you do not give what has been prescribed for them, and yet you desire to marry them—and about the weak among children: that you should maintain the orphans with justice, and whatever good you do, indeed Allah knows it well. (127)

Commentary

Once Again, the Rights of Women

The above verse responds to some of the questions and inquiries raised by the people regarding women (especially orphan girls) and states: "O Prophet, they ask you for a legal ruling concerning the laws related to the rights of women; say, Allah gives you a ruling and a response in this regard."

(وَ يَسْتَفْتُونَكَ فِي النِّساءِ قُلِ اللَّهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِيهِنَّ).

Then it adds: "That which is recited to you in the Holy Quran regarding orphan girls—whose wealth you used to take possession of, yet you would neither marry them yourselves nor hand over their property to them so they could marry others—responds to another part of your questions and reveals the ugliness of this oppressive act."

(وَ ما يُتْلى‌ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ فِي يَتامَى النِّساءِ اللَّاتِي لا تُؤْتُونَهُنَّ ما كُتِبَ لَهُنَّ وَ تَرْغَبُونَ أَنْ تَنْكِحُوهُنَّ) [^1] Then, He advises regarding minor boys who, according to the customs of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), were deprived of inheritance, saying: "Allah commands you to observe the rights of the oppressed children."

(وَ الْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ الْوِلْدانِ).

Once again, He emphasizes the rights of orphans in general and says: "And Allah commands you to maintain justice for the orphans."

(وَ أَنْ تَقُومُوا لِلْيَتامى‌ بِالْقِسْطِ).

And in the end, He draws attention to the fact that "whatever good deed you perform, especially regarding orphans and the weak, it shall not remain hidden from the view of Allah's knowledge, and you shall find its appropriate reward."

(وَ ما تَفْعَلُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ بِهِ عَلِيماً).

Furthermore, it should be noted that the phrase yastaftunaka is originally derived from the root fatwa and futya, which means providing an answer to difficult problems. Since the original root of this word is fata (meaning a fresh youth), it may have first been used for issues where a person selects interesting, fresh, and new answers, and was subsequently adopted for responding to all types of questions.

[^1]: According to the interpretation we mentioned above regarding this sentence of the verse, it becomes clear that "وَ ما يُتْلى‌..." is the "subject" (mubtada), and its "predicate" (khabar) is the phrase yuftikum fihinna, which has been omitted due to the context of the preceding part of the verse. Also, targhabuna here means "disinclination," because we know that if the root raghaba is made transitive with the preposition 'an, it means "disinclination," and if it is made transitive with fi, it means "inclination." From the contextual clues, it is understood that 'an is implied here.

Ayah

وَإِنِ ٱمْرَأَةٌ خَافَتْ مِنۢ بَعْلِهَا نُشُوزًا أَوْ إِعْرَاضًا فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَآ أَن يُصْلِحَا بَيْنَهُمَا صُلْحًا ۚ وَٱلصُّلْحُ خَيْرٌ ۗ وَأُحْضِرَتِ ٱلْأَنفُسُ ٱلشُّحَّ ۚ وَإِن تُحْسِنُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا (١٢٨)
If a woman fears from her husband misconduct or desertion, there is no sin upon the couple if they reach a reconciliation between themselves; and reconcilement is better. The souls are prone to greed; but if you are virtuous and Godwary, Allah is indeed well aware of what you do. (128)

Occasion of Revelation

In many Islamic commentaries and books of Hadith, the occasion of revelation for this verse is narrated as follows: Rafi' ibn Khadij had two wives, one elderly and the other young. Due to certain disagreements, he divorced his elderly wife. Before her waiting period ('iddah) had expired, he said to her: "If you wish, I will reconcile with you, but on the condition that if I prefer my other wife over you, you must remain patient. Or, if you prefer, I will wait until the waiting period ends and we shall part ways." The woman accepted the first proposal, and they reconciled. Then, this noble verse was revealed and explained the ruling for such an action.

Commentary

Peace is Better

As we mentioned under the commentary of verses 34 and 35 of this same Surah [^1], nushuz (rebellion) originally stems from the root nashz, meaning "elevated ground." When used in the context of a husband and wife, it refers to disobedience and rebellion. In the aforementioned verses, the rulings regarding the nushuz of the wife were explained; however, here, the issue of the husband's nushuz is addressed, stating: "Whenever a woman senses that her husband intends to be rebellious or indifferent, there is no harm if she waives some of her rights to preserve the sanctity of the marriage and they make peace with one another."

(وَ إِنِ امْرَأَةٌ خافَتْ مِنْ بَعْلِها نُشُوزاً أَوْ إِعْراضاً فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِما أَنْ يُصْلِحا بَيْنَهُما صُلْحاً)

Since the woman's waiving of a portion of her rights is done out of consent and goodwill, and no compulsion was involved, there is no sin. The expression la junaha (there is no blame/sin) refers to this very fact.

Furthermore, based on the occasion of revelation, two jurisprudential issues can be derived from the verse: First,

[^1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 3, page 372.

that rulings such as the division of nights between two wives are considered a "right" rather than a "decree"; therefore, the wife can voluntarily waive this right entirely or partially. Second, the consideration for a settlement (sulh) does not necessarily have to be property or wealth; rather, the "waiving of a right" can serve as the consideration for the settlement.

Then, to emphasize the matter, He says: "In any case, making peace is better" (وَ الصُّلْحُ خَيْرٌ).

Although this short and profound sentence is mentioned in the context of family disputes in the above verse, it clearly expresses a general, universal, and public law: that in all matters, the primary principle is peace, harmony, friendship, and reconciliation. Conflict, struggle, and separation are contrary to the sound nature of man and his tranquil life; therefore, they should not be resorted to except in necessary and exceptional cases. This is contrary to what some materialists imagine—that the primary principle in human life, like other living beings, is the "struggle for survival" and conflict, and that evolution occurs through this path. This very way of thinking has perhaps been the source of many wars and bloodsheds in recent centuries. However, because man possesses intellect and intelligence, his account is separate from predatory animals, and his evolution takes place under the shadow of cooperation, not conflict [^1]. Fundamentally, the struggle for survival is not an acceptable principle for evolution even among animals.

Following this, He points to the source of many disputes and lack of forgiveness, saying: "People, by nature and according to the instinct of self-love, are encompassed by waves of stinginess, and everyone tries to receive all their rights in full without the slightest reduction, and this is the source of disputes and struggles."

[^1]: A further explanation on this subject was provided in Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 180, under the title "Struggle for Survival."

(وَ أُحْضِرَتِ الْأَنْفُسُ الشُّحَّ) Therefore, if a man and woman realize that the source of many disagreements is stinginess—and stinginess is one of the blameworthy traits—and then strive to reform themselves and practice forgiveness, not only will the roots of family disputes be eliminated, but many social conflicts will also come to an end.

Yet, at the same time, so that men do not abuse the aforementioned ruling, at the end of the verse, He addresses them and recommends righteousness and piety, reminding them to be mindful of their deeds and actions and not to deviate from the path of truth and justice, for Allah is aware of all their actions.

(وَ إِنْ تُحْسِنُوا وَ تَتَّقُوا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيراً)

Ayahs

وَلَن تَسْتَطِيعُوٓا۟ أَن تَعْدِلُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ ۖ فَلَا تَمِيلُوا۟ كُلَّ ٱلْمَيْلِ فَتَذَرُوهَا كَٱلْمُعَلَّقَةِ ۚ وَإِن تُصْلِحُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١٢٩)
You will not be able to be fair between wives, even if you are eager to do so. Yet do not turn away from one altogether, leaving her as if in a suspense. But if you are conciliatory and Godwary, Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (129)
وَإِن يَتَفَرَّقَا يُغْنِ ٱللَّهُ كُلًّا مِّن سَعَتِهِۦ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعًا حَكِيمًا (١٣٠)
But if they separate, Allah will suffice each of them out of His bounty, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-wise. (130)

Commentary

Justice: The Condition for Polygamy

From the phrase at the end of the previous verse, which commanded excellence (Ihsan) and piety (Taqwa), a type of warning to husbands is inferred—that they must be careful not to allow the slightest deviation from the path of justice regarding their wives. Here, the misconception arises that observing justice is impossible even regarding affection and heart-felt inclination; therefore, what should be done in the case of multiple wives?

The verse under discussion answers this question: "Justice in terms of affection among wives is not possible, no matter how much effort is exerted in this regard."

(وَ لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَ لَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ‌) "And you will never be able to be [perfectly] just between wives, even if you should strive [to do so]."

From the phrase "even if you should strive" (وَ لَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ‌), it is understood that among the Muslims, there were individuals who strove hard in this matter. Perhaps the reason for their striving was the absolute command for justice in verse 3 of this same Surah, where it says:

فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا فَواحِدَةً "But if you fear that you will not be just, then [marry only] one."

It is obvious that a Divine law cannot be contrary to human nature, nor can it impose a "duty beyond capacity" (Taklif ma la yutaq). Since heart-felt affections have various factors, some of which are outside of human control, the command to observe justice has not been given regarding them. However, regarding actions, behavior, and the fulfillment of rights among wives—which is possible for a human—justice has been emphasized.

At the same time, so that men do not abuse this ruling, following this phrase, He says: "Now that you cannot observe complete equality in terms of affection among your wives, at least do not direct all your heart-felt inclination toward one of them, such that the other is left in a state of limbo and her rights are practically violated."

(فَلا تَمِيلُوا كُلَّ الْمَيْلِ فَتَذَرُوها كَالْمُعَلَّقَةِ) "So do not incline with total inclination and leave her suspended."

At the end of the verse, He warns those who, prior to the revelation of this ruling, were negligent in observing justice among their wives: "If you choose the path of reform and piety and compensate for the past, Allah will include you in His mercy and forgiveness."
