(وَ إِنْ تُصْلِحُوا وَ تَتَّقُوا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً) "And if you amend [your affairs] and fear Allah - then indeed, Allah is ever Forgiving and Merciful."

In Islamic narrations, matters regarding the observance of justice among wives have been reported that clarify the greatness of this law. Among them: we read in a Hadith that Ali عليه السلام, on the day belonging to one of his two wives, would not even perform his Wudu (ablution) in the house of the other [1]. Regarding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that even during his illness, he would not remain [exclusively] in the house of only one of his wives [2]. Regarding Mu'adh ibn Jabal, it is narrated that he had two wives and both passed away together during a plague; he even used lots (drawing straws) to determine which one to bury first, so as not to have done anything contrary to justice [3].

Answer to a Necessary Question

As we noted under verse 3 of this same Surah, some uninformed individuals conclude from combining that verse with the verse under discussion that polygamy is conditional upon justice, and justice is impossible; therefore, polygamy is forbidden in Islam.

Coincidentally, it appears from Islamic narrations that the first person to raise this objection was Ibn Abi al-Awja, one of the materialists contemporary with Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. He presented this objection to Hisham ibn al-Hakam, the scholarly Islamic mujahid. Hisham, not finding an answer to this question, traveled from his city (apparently Kufa) toward Medina to find the answer. He came to Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. The Imam was surprised by his arrival in Medina outside the time of Hajj or Umrah, but Hisham explained that such a question had arisen. The Imam عليه السلام replied:

"The meaning of justice in the third verse of Surah an-Nisa is justice in maintenance (and observing marital rights and manner of conduct), but the meaning of justice in verse 129 (the verse under discussion), which is considered an impossible matter, is justice in heart-felt inclinations." (Therefore, polygamy, while maintaining Islamic conditions, is neither forbidden nor impossible). When Hisham returned from his journey and provided this answer to Ibn Abi al-Awja, the latter swore: "This answer is not from yourself" [1]. It is clear that if we interpret the word 'justice' in the two verses with two different meanings, it is because of the clear context (Qarinah) present in both verses. For at the end of the verse under discussion, it explicitly says: "do not direct all your heart-felt inclination toward one wife," and in this way, choosing two wives is considered permissible, provided that practically no injustice is done to one of them, even if there is a difference in heart-felt inclination toward them. Furthermore, at the beginning of verse 3 of this same Surah, He explicitly granted permission for polygamy.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Tibyan, Vol. 3, p. 350.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 420.

Then, in the following verse, He points to this reality: if continuing the marriage is unbearable for both parties, and circumstances have arisen where the horizon of life is dark and gloomy for them and is in no way reformable, they are not forced to continue such a marriage and be imprisoned in such a bitter family life until the end of their lives. Rather, they can separate, and at this time, they must decide courageously and not fear the future, because: "If they separate under such conditions, the Great Allah will enrich both of them from His bounty and mercy, and it is hoped that better spouses and a brighter life await them."

(وَ إِنْ يَتَفَرَّقا يُغْنِ اللَّهُ كُلًّا مِنْ سَعَتِهِ‌) "But if they separate [by divorce], Allah will enrich each [of them] from His abundance."

For Allah has vast bounty and mercy joined with wisdom.

(وَ كانَ اللَّهُ واسِعاً حَكِيماً) "And Allah is ever All-Encompassing and Wise."

Ayahs

وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَلَقَدْ وَصَّيْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَإِيَّاكُمْ أَنِ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ وَإِن تَكْفُرُوا۟ فَإِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَنِيًّا حَمِيدًا (١٣١)
To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. We have certainly enjoined those who were given the Book before you, and you, that you should be wary of Allah. But if you are faithless, [you should know that] to Allah indeed belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and Allah is all-sufficient, all-laudable. (131)
وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا (١٣٢)
To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and Allah suffices as trustee. (132)
إِن يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ أَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ وَيَأْتِ بِـَٔاخَرِينَ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ قَدِيرًا (١٣٣)
If He wishes, He will take you away, O mankind, and bring others [in your place]; Allah has the power to do that. (133)
مَّن كَانَ يُرِيدُ ثَوَابَ ٱلدُّنْيَا فَعِندَ ٱللَّهِ ثَوَابُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ سَمِيعًۢا بَصِيرًا (١٣٤)
Whoever desires the reward of this world, [should know that] with Allah is the reward of this world and the Hereafter, and Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing. (134)

Commentary

In the previous verse, reference was made to the fact that if a necessity arises for two spouses to separate, and there is no other remedy, taking this action is permissible, and they should not fear the future, for Allah will enrich them from His grace and bounty. In this verse, it adds: We have the power to enrich them because "to Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth."

(وَ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ‌) One who possesses such infinite kingdom and endless power will not be incapable of enriching His servants. Then, to emphasize piety (Taqwa) in this matter and every other matter, He says: "We have commanded those who were given the Scripture before you, and you as well, to fear Allah [and be pious]."

(وَ لَقَدْ وَصَّيْنَا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَ إِيَّاكُمْ أَنِ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ‌).

Then, addressing the Muslims, He says: implementing the command of Taqwa is for your own benefit, and Allah has no need for it, "and if you disbelieve [and disobey], then surely to Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and Allah is Self-Sufficient, Praiseworthy."

(وَ إِنْ تَكْفُرُوا فَإِنَّ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ غَنِيًّا حَمِيداً).

In reality, the truly Self-Sufficient and Independent is Allah, for He is "Self-Sufficient by Essence" (Ghani bi al-Dhat), and the independence of anyone other than Him is through His help; otherwise, essentially, all are needy and dependent.

Likewise, He is the One who is inherently worthy of "Praise," because His perfections, which grant Him the merit of being praised, are within His Essence, unlike the perfections of others which are borrowed and come from another source.

In the next verse, for the third time, He emphasizes this statement: "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and Allah is sufficient as a Guardian and Disposer of affairs."

(وَ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ وَكِيلًا).

Here, the question arises: why has one point been repeated three times within this short interval? Is it solely for emphasis, or are there other indications hidden within it? A careful look at the content of the verses shows that each time, there was a specific point. The first time, when He promises the two spouses that Allah will enrich them after separation, He mentions His ownership over the expanse of the earth and heaven to prove His ability to fulfill this promise.

The second time, after the recommendation to Taqwa and piety, He repeats this phrase so that it is not imagined that obeying this command benefits Allah, or that opposing it causes Him harm.

In fact, this statement is similar to what the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said in the Nahj al-Balagha at the beginning of the "Hammam" sermon:

(ان اللَّه سبحانه و تعالى خلق الخلق حين خلقهم غنيا عن طاعتهم آمنا من معصيتهم لانه لا تضره معصية من عصاه و لا تنفعه طاعة من اطاعه‌)

"Verily, Allah the Glorified and Exalted created the creation when He created them, being independent of their obedience and safe from their disobedience, for the disobedience of those who disobey Him does not harm Him, nor does the obedience of those who obey Him benefit Him." [1] And the third time, as a prelude to the discussion mentioned in verse 133, He reminds of His ownership over the entire universe.

Then He says: "It is no obstacle for Allah to do away with you and bring others [more ready and determined] to succeed you, who would be more diligent in His obedience, and Allah is capable of doing that."

(إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ وَ يَأْتِ بِآخَرِينَ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ ذلِكَ قَدِيراً) In the commentaries of Al-Tibyan and Majma' al-Bayan, it is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that when this verse was revealed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله struck his hand on the back of Salman and said: "Those people are these: the people of Ajam and Persia." This statement is, in fact, a prophecy of the great services that Muslim Iranians would provide to Islam.

In the final verse, mention is made of those who claim faith in Allah, participate in the fields of Jihad, and implement the commands of Islam without having a Divine goal, but rather their intention is to obtain material results such as war booty and the like. He says: "Those who desire only the reward of this world are in error, for with your Lord is the reward of both this world and the Hereafter."

(مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ ثَوابَ الدُّنْيا فَعِنْدَ اللَّهِ ثَوابُ الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ).

So why do they not pursue both?

"And Allah is aware of everyone's intentions; He hears every voice and sees every scene, and He is informed of the actions of the hypocritical."

(وَ كانَ اللَّهُ سَمِيعاً بَصِيراً) This verse once again expresses the reality that Islam is not only concerned with spiritual and otherworldly aspects, but rather desires both material and spiritual prosperity for its followers.

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 193.

Ayah

۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُونُوا۟ قَوَّٰمِينَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ شُهَدَآءَ لِلَّهِ وَلَوْ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوِ ٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ ۚ إِن يَكُنْ غَنِيًّا أَوْ فَقِيرًا فَٱللَّهُ أَوْلَىٰ بِهِمَا ۖ فَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ ٱلْهَوَىٰٓ أَن تَعْدِلُوا۟ ۚ وَإِن تَلْوُۥٓا۟ أَوْ تُعْرِضُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا (١٣٥)
O you who have faith! Be maintainers of justice and witnesses for the sake of Allah, even if it should be against yourselves or [your] parents and near relatives, and whether it be [someone] rich or poor, for Allah has a greater right over them. So do not follow [your] desires, lest you should be unfair, and if you distort [the testimony] or disregard [it], Allah is indeed well aware of what you do. (135)

Commentary

Social Justice

In proportion to the instructions given in the preceding verses regarding the implementation of justice specifically concerning orphans and wives, this verse mentions a fundamental principle and a general law regarding the implementation of justice in all cases without exception, commanding all people of faith to stand firm for justice.

(يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُونُوا قَوَّامِينَ بِالْقِسْطِ) "O you who have believed, be persistently standing firm in justice."

It should be noted that qawwāmīn is the plural of qawwām (an intensive form), meaning "one who stands much." This means you must stand for justice in every state, in every action, and in every era and time, such that this practice becomes your character and disposition, and deviating from it becomes contrary to your nature and spirit.

The expression "standing" (qiyām) here may be because a person usually must stand up and pursue matters to accomplish tasks. Therefore, "standing for a task" is a metaphor for firm resolve, determination, and serious action regarding it, even if that task—like a judge's ruling—does not physically require standing or movement. It is also possible that the expression "standing" is used because a "standing" (qā’im) object is usually something perpendicular to the ground without the slightest inclination or deviation toward any side; meaning, you must implement justice in such a way that not the slightest deviation occurs in any direction.

Then, to emphasize the matter, He raises the issue of "testimony" and says: "Especially in the case of testimony, you must set aside all considerations and testify to the truth solely for the sake of Allah, even if it ends up being to the detriment of your own self, or your parents, or your relatives."

(شُهَدَاءَ لِلَّهِ وَلَوْ عَلَىٰ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوِ الْوَالِدَيْنِ وَالْأَقْرَبِينَ) "As witnesses for Allah, even if it be against yourselves or parents and relatives."

This issue has existed and continues to exist in all societies, especially in ignorant (jahili) societies, where usually the criteria for giving testimony are likes, dislikes, and the nature of the relationship between individuals and the witness, while truth and justice hold no weight for them. Specifically, it is understood from a hadith narrated by Ibn Abbas that the new Muslims, even after entering Medina, would refrain from giving testimonies that were detrimental to their relatives due to kinship considerations. The above verse was revealed and warned them in this regard [1]. However—as the verse indicates—this action is not compatible with the spirit of faith. A true believer is one who has no considerations in the face of truth and justice and even ignores his own interests and those of his relatives for its implementation.

Furthermore, it is understood from this phrase that relatives can testify for or against one another while maintaining the principles of justice (unless there are indications of accusation regarding favoritism or the exercise of prejudice).

Then, He points to another factor of deviation from the principle of justice, saying: "Neither the consideration of the wealth of the rich should prevent testifying to the truth, nor the emotions arising from the consideration of the poverty of the poor. For if the one against whom the testimony is given is rich or poor, Allah is more aware of their state. Neither the owners of gold and power can cause harm to the witnesses of truth in the face of the Protection of the Lord, nor will the poor remain hungry by the implementation of 'justice'."

(إِنْ يَكُنْ غَنِيًّا أَوْ فَقِيرًا فَاللَّهُ أَوْلَىٰ بِهِمَا) "Whether he is rich or poor, Allah is more worthy of both."

Again, for emphasis, He commands: "Do not follow desire, lest you deviate from justice."

(فَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا الْهَوَىٰ أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا) [2]

[1] Al-Manar, Vol. 5, p. 455. [2] The word ta’dilū may originate from the root of ‘adālat (justice) or from the root of ‘udūl (deviation). If it is from the root of justice, the meaning of the phrase would be: "Do not follow desire so that you may [be able to] implement justice." If it is from the root of deviation, the meaning is: "Do not follow desire in a way that leads to deviating [from the truth]."

From this phrase, it is clearly understood that the source of oppressions and tyrannies is following one's desires (hawā-parastī), and if a society is not a follower of desires, oppression and tyranny will find no way into it! Once again, because of the importance of the implementation of justice, He emphasizes this instruction, saying: "If you prevent the truth from reaching its rightful owner, or distort the truth, or turn away from it after the truth has become clear, Allah is aware of your deeds."

(وَإِنْ تَلْوُوا [1] أَوْ تُعْرِضُوا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا) "And if you distort [your testimony] or refuse [to give it], then indeed Allah is ever, of what you do, Acquainted."

In fact, the phrase an talwū refers to the distortion and changing of the truth, while the phrase tu’ridū refers to refraining from judging by the truth; this is exactly what has been narrated in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام [2]. It is interesting to note that at the end of the verse, the term Khabīr (Acquainted/All-Aware) is used rather than ‘Alīm (All-Knowing), because Khabīr is usually said of one who is aware of the details and subtleties of a matter. This points to the fact that Allah knows your smallest deviation from truth and justice, by whatever excuse or pretext it may be—even when you clothe it in a righteous appearance—and He will give its punishment! The above verse clearly illustrates Islam's extraordinary attention to the issue of social justice in every shape and form. The various types of emphasis used in these few phrases show to what extent Islam is sensitive toward this important human and social issue, even though, most unfortunately, the distance between the practice of Muslims and this sublime Islamic instruction is as far as the earth is from the sky! And this is one of the secrets of their backwardness.

[1] Talwū is from the root layy (rhyming with tayy), meaning "prevention and delay," and originally it means "twisting and winding." [2] Tafsir al-Tibyan, Vol. 5, p. 356.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِٱللَّهِ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتِهِۦ وَكُتُبِهِۦ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا (١٣٦)
O you who have faith! Have faith in Allah and His Apostle and the Book that He has sent down to His Apostle and the Book He had sent down earlier. Whoever disbelieves in Allah and His angels, His Books and His apostles and the Last Day, has certainly strayed into far error. (136)

Occasion of Revelation

It has been narrated from Ibn Abbas that this verse was revealed regarding a group of the leaders of the People of the Book, such as Abdullah bin Salam, Asad bin Ka'b, his brother Asid bin Ka'b, and several others. This is because at the beginning of their service to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they came and said:

"We believe in you, your Heavenly Book, Moses, the Torah, and Ezra (Uzair), but we do not believe in other Heavenly Books nor in other Prophets." The verse was revealed and taught them that they must believe in all of them. [^1]

Commentary

Considering the occasion of revelation, the address in the verse is directed toward a group of believers from the People of the Book

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and al-Manar.

who, after accepting Islam, due to specific prejudices, expressed faith only in their previous religion and the religion of Islam, while refusing to accept the rest of the Prophets and Heavenly Books. However, the Quran advises them to recognize all Prophets and Heavenly Books, for they all pursue a single truth, follow a single goal, and were sent by a single Source (even if they had hierarchical levels, similar to different classes of education and training, where each brought a more complete code than the previous one).

Therefore, it makes no sense to accept some and reject others. Can a single truth be subject to discrimination? And can prejudices block out realities? Thus, the above verse says: "O you who have believed, believe in Allah and His Messenger (the Prophet of Islam) and the Book which He has sent down to His Messenger and the Heavenly Books that were sent down before."

(يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا آمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَالْكِتَابِ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِ وَالْكِتَابِ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ مِنْ قَبْلُ)

Regardless of the aforementioned occasion of revelation, there is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that the address is to all believers—those who have accepted Islam outwardly, but it has not yet penetrated the depths of their souls. It is here that they are invited to become believers from the bottom of their hearts and within their souls. Furthermore, it is possible that the address is to all believers who have believed in Allah and the Prophet in general terms but have not become acquainted with the details and specifics of Islamic beliefs. Here, the Quran commands: true believers must have faith in all Prophets, previous Books, and the Divine angels, because lack of faith in these is equivalent to denying the Wisdom of Allah. Is it possible for the Wise Allah to have left previous human beings without a leader and guide to wander aimlessly in the field of life?! Does faith in angels refer only to the angels of revelation—faith in whom is inseparable from faith in the Prophets and Heavenly Books—or all angels?

Since just as some of them are involved in the matter of revelation and legislation (Tashri'), a group is also commissioned with the administration of the world of creation (Takwin), and faith in them is, in reality, a facet of faith in the Wisdom of Allah.

At the end of the verse, He describes the fate of those who neglect these realities, stating: "And whoever disbelieves in Allah, His angels, His Books, His Messengers, and the Last Day has certainly strayed into far-reaching error."

(وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِاللَّهِ وَمَلَائِكَتِهِ وَكُتُبِهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلَالًا بَعِيدًا)

In fact, faith in five principles is considered necessary in this verse; that is, in addition to faith in the Origin (Allah) and the Hereafter, faith in the Heavenly Books, the Prophets, and the angels is also required.

The expression "far-reaching error" (ضلال بعيد) is a subtle expression, meaning that such individuals have been thrown so far off the main path that their return to the main highway is not easily possible.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا۟ ثُمَّ ءَامَنُوا۟ ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا۟ ثُمَّ ٱزْدَادُوا۟ كُفْرًا لَّمْ يَكُنِ ٱللَّهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ وَلَا لِيَهْدِيَهُمْ سَبِيلًۢا (١٣٧)
As for those who believe and then disbelieve, then believe [again] and then disbelieve and then increase in disbelief, Allah shall never forgive them, nor shall He guide them to any way. (137)
بَشِّرِ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ بِأَنَّ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٣٨)
Inform the hypocrites that there is a painful punishment for them (138)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّخِذُونَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۚ أَيَبْتَغُونَ عِندَهُمُ ٱلْعِزَّةَ فَإِنَّ ٱلْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا (١٣٩)
—those who take the faithless for allies instead of the faithful. Do they seek honour with them? [If so,] indeed all honour belongs to Allah. (139)

Commentary

The Fate of Stubborn Hypocrites

In connection with the discussion in the previous verse regarding the disbelievers and their far-reaching straying, these verses point to the state of a group among them who take on a new form every day. One day they are in the ranks of the believers, and another day in the ranks of the disbelievers; again they join the ranks of the believers, and then they join the ranks of the fanatical and dangerous disbelievers. In short, like a "fickle idol," they appear in a different shape every moment and a different color every day.

Ultimately, they pass away in a state of disbelief and lack of faith! The first of the above verses says regarding the fate of such people: "Indeed, those who believed then disbelieved, then believed and again disbelieved, and then increased in disbelief—never will Allah forgive them, nor will He guide them to a [right] way."

(إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ ازْدادُوا كُفْراً لَمْ يَكُنِ اللَّهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ وَ لا لِيَهْدِيَهُمْ سَبِيلًا).

These successive changes in conduct and changing colors every day were either born of instability and a lack of sufficient investigation into the foundations of Islam, or they were a plot designed and executed by the hypocrites and the fanatical disbelievers of the People of the Book to unsettle the true believers. By these continuous comings and goings, they sought to make the true believers waver in their faith, as was explained in the commentary of Surah Al-Imran, verse 72.

Of course, the above verse does not in any way imply the non-acceptance of the repentance of such individuals; rather, the subject of the verse is only those who eventually close their eyes to the world in a state of intense disbelief. Such individuals, by virtue of their faith and actions, are worthy of neither forgiveness nor guidance, unless they reconsider their conduct.

Then, in the next verse, it says: "Give tidings to the hypocrites that there is for them a painful punishment."

(بَشِّرِ الْمُنافِقِينَ بِأَنَّ لَهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً).

The use of the term "tidings" (basharat) in a context where the discussion is about "painful punishment" is either as a form of mockery toward their vain and baseless thoughts, or it is because the word basharat, which is originally derived from bashar (meaning skin/face), has a broad meaning. It includes any news that affects the human countenance, whether it makes it joyful or sorrowful.

In the final verse, this group of hypocrites is described as follows: "Those who take disbelievers as allies instead of the believers."

(الَّذِينَ يَتَّخِذُونَ الْكافِرِينَ أَوْلِياءَ مِنْ دُونِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

Then it asks: what is their goal in this choice? "Do they truly seek honor and status through this friendship?!" (أَ يَبْتَغُونَ عِنْدَهُمُ الْعِزَّةَ).

While all honor belongs to Allah.

(فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً).

This is because honor always stems from "knowledge" and "power," and these [disbelievers], whose power is insignificant and whose knowledge is as meager as their power, can do nothing to be a source of honor.

This verse warns all Muslims not to seek their honor in any aspect of life—be it economic, cultural, political, or otherwise—through friendship with the enemies of Islam. Instead, they should make their reliance the Pure Essence of Allah, who is the source of all honor. Those other than God among the enemies of Islam neither possess honor to bestow upon anyone, nor would they be trustworthy if they did. For whenever their interests dictate, they immediately abandon their closest allies and go about their own business as if they had never known one another, as contemporary history is a very eloquent witness to this reality!

Ayah

وَقَدْ نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَنْ إِذَا سَمِعْتُمْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ يُكْفَرُ بِهَا وَيُسْتَهْزَأُ بِهَا فَلَا تَقْعُدُوا۟ مَعَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَخُوضُوا۟ فِى حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا مِّثْلُهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ جَامِعُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ فِى جَهَنَّمَ جَمِيعًا (١٤٠)
Certainly He has sent down to you in the Book that when you hear Allah’s signs being disbelieved and derided, do not sit with them until they engage in some other discourse, or else you [too] will be like them. Indeed Allah will gather the hypocrites and the faithless in hell all together (140)

Occasion of Revelation

It has been narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding the revelation of this verse that a group of hypocrites used to sit in the gatherings of Jewish scholars—gatherings in which the verses of the Quran were mocked. The above verse was revealed to clarify the dire consequences of this action.

Commentary

Do Not Sit in a Gathering of Sin

In Surah Al-An'am, which is one of the Meccan Surahs of the Quran, verse 68 explicitly commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "If you see those who mock the verses of the Quran and speak inappropriately, turn away from them." It is certain that this ruling is not exclusive to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; rather, it is a general command expressed in the form of an address to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Its philosophy is also perfectly clear, as this is a form of practical struggle in a negative manner against such actions.

The verse under discussion once again emphasizes this Islamic ruling and warns the Muslims: "It has already been revealed to you in the Book that when you hear the signs of Allah being denied and mocked, do not sit with them until they engage in a different conversation."

(وَ قَدْ نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ أَنْ إِذا سَمِعْتُمْ آياتِ اللَّهِ يُكْفَرُ بِها وَ يُسْتَهْزَأُ بِها فَلا تَقْعُدُوا مَعَهُمْ حَتَّى يَخُوضُوا فِي حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِ‌).

Then, it states the consequence of this action: "If you participate in such gatherings, you will be like them, and your fate will be their fate" (إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً مِثْلُهُمْ‌).

To further emphasize this point, it adds that participating in such sessions is a sign of the spirit of hypocrisy: "And Allah will gather the hypocrites and the disbelievers all together in Hell."

(إِنَّ اللَّهَ جامِعُ الْمُنافِقِينَ وَ الْكافِرِينَ فِي جَهَنَّمَ جَمِيعاً).

Several points can be derived from this verse:

1- Participating in such "gatherings of sin" is equivalent to participating in the "sin" itself, even if the participant remains silent; because such silence is a form of tacit approval and practical endorsement.

2- If "enjoining what is good and forbidding what is evil" is not possible in a "positive" (active) form, it must at least be carried out in a "negative" (passive) form, by distancing oneself from the environment and gathering of sin.

3- Those who, through their silence and participation in such gatherings, practically encourage the sinners, shall face a punishment similar to that of the perpetrators of the sin.

4- Socializing with disbelievers is permissible provided they do not insult Divine verses and there is no other danger, as the phrase "until they engage in a different conversation" (حَتَّى يَخُوضُوا فِي حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِ‌) deems this permissible.

5- Compromising with such sinners is a sign of the spirit of hypocrisy, for a true Muslim can never participate in a gathering where Divine verses and laws are insulted without protesting, or at the very least, manifesting their disapproval by leaving that gathering.

Ayah

ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَرَبَّصُونَ بِكُمْ فَإِن كَانَ لَكُمْ فَتْحٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ قَالُوٓا۟ أَلَمْ نَكُن مَّعَكُمْ وَإِن كَانَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ نَصِيبٌ قَالُوٓا۟ أَلَمْ نَسْتَحْوِذْ عَلَيْكُمْ وَنَمْنَعْكُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۚ فَٱللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ وَلَن يَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ سَبِيلًا (١٤١)
—those who lie in wait for you: if there is a victory for you from Allah, they say, ‘Were we not with you?’ But if the faithless get a share [of victory], they say, ‘Did we not prevail upon you and defend you against the faithful?’ Allah will judge between you on the Day of Resurrection, and Allah will never provide the faithless any way [to prevail] over the faithful. (141)

Commentary

Characteristics of the Hypocrites

This verse and the subsequent verses recount another portion of the characteristics of the hypocrites and their disordered thoughts, stating: "The hypocrites are those who always want to exploit every occurrence for their own benefit; if a victory is granted to you, they immediately insert themselves into the ranks of the believers, saying: 'Were we not with you? And were not our valuable contributions effective in your triumph and victory? Therefore, we are partners and participants in all these successes and their spiritual and material results.'"

(الَّذِينَ يَتَرَبَّصُونَ بِكُمْ فَإِنْ كانَ لَكُمْ فَتْحٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ قالُوا أَ لَمْ نَكُنْ مَعَكُمْ‌).

But if a share of victory falls to the enemies of Islam, they immediately draw close to them, declaring their satisfaction to them and saying: "It was we who encouraged you to fight the Muslims and not to surrender to them; therefore, we have a share in these victories!" (وَ إِنْ كانَ لِلْكافِرِينَ نَصِيبٌ قالُوا أَ لَمْ نَسْتَحْوِذْ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ نَمْنَعْكُمْ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌) [^1] In this way, with their peculiar opportunism, this group wants to share in the honors and even the spoils in the event of a victory for the believers, while also placing a burden of obligation upon them. Conversely, in the event of a victory for the disbelievers, they are happy, and by strengthening them in their disbelief and spying for them, they prepare the grounds for such a victory. Sometimes they are "companions of the caravan" and sometimes "partners of the thief," spending their lives in this double-dealing!

However, the Quran describes their ultimate end in one short sentence, saying:

Finally, a day will come when the curtains are lifted and the mask is removed from their ugly faces; yes, "On the Day of Resurrection, Allah will judge between you."

(فَاللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ). And so that the true believers are not intimidated by them, He adds at the end of the verse: "Allah has never ordained a way for the disbelievers to achieve victory and dominance over the believers."

(وَ لَنْ يَجْعَلَ اللَّهُ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ سَبِيلًا).

Is the aim of this sentence only the lack of victory for the disbelievers over the people of faith in terms of "logic," or does it include "military" victories and the like?

Since the word "Sabil" (way/path) is technically an "indefinite noun in the context of negation" and conveys a general meaning, it is inferred from the verse that the disbelievers will not prevail over the people of faith—not only in terms of logic but also in military, political, cultural, and economic terms; in short, from no perspective whatsoever.

And if we see their victory over Muslims in various fields with our own eyes, it is because many Muslims are not true believers and have completely forgotten the ways of faith and their duties, responsibilities, and missions. There is neither a trace of Islamic unity and brotherhood among them, nor do they perform Jihad in the true sense of the word, nor do they possess the necessary knowledge and awareness that Islam has made obligatory for everyone from the moment of birth until the moment of death. And because they are as such, they are naturally treated as such! A group of jurists (Fuqaha) has argued from this verse in various matters regarding the lack of legal and jurisdictional dominance of disbelievers over believers, and given the generality seen in the verse, this broad application does not seem very far-fetched (Reflect on this).

It is noteworthy that in this verse, the victory of the Muslims is described as "Fath" (a clear opening/triumph), while the victory of the disbelievers is referred to as "Nasib" (a mere share/portion). This is an indication that if any victories fall to them, they are limited, temporary, and unstable, and the final "Fath" and victory belong to the people of faith.

[^1]: "Istahwadha" (استحوذ) is originally derived from the root "Hawdh" (حوذ), meaning the back part of the thighs. Since a camel driver, when wanting to drive a camel quickly, stands behind it and strikes its thighs and back to make it move fast, the word "Istihwadh" has come to mean driving, inciting, accompanied by dominance and mastery. In the above verse, it carries this same meaning.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ يُخَـٰدِعُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَهُوَ خَـٰدِعُهُمْ وَإِذَا قَامُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ قَامُوا۟ كُسَالَىٰ يُرَآءُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ وَلَا يَذْكُرُونَ ٱللَّهَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (١٤٢)
The hypocrites indeed seek to deceive Allah, but it is He who outwits them. When they stand up for prayer, they stand up lazily, showing off to the people and not remembering Allah except a little, (142)
مُّذَبْذَبِينَ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ لَآ إِلَىٰ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَلَآ إِلَىٰ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ۚ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ سَبِيلًا (١٤٣)
wavering in between: neither with these, nor with those. And whomever Allah leads astray, you will never find any way for him. (143)

Commentary

In these two verses, five other characteristics of the hypocrites are mentioned in short phrases:

1- To achieve their sinister goals, they resort to deception and trickery, and even seek to: "deceive Allah, while at the very moment they intend to do so, they are caught in a kind of deception themselves; for in order to gain meager assets, they lose the great capital of their own existence."

(إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ يُخادِعُونَ اللَّهَ وَ هُوَ خادِعُهُمْ‌).

The above interpretation is derived from the "Waw" in "وَ هُوَ خادِعُهُمْ‌" which is a Waw al-Haliyah (circumstantial particle). This is exactly like the famous story narrated from certain great scholars who said to a group of tradesmen: "Fear that foreign travelers might deceive you." Someone replied: "On the contrary, they are uninformed and simple-hearted people; we are the ones who can deceive them!" The great man replied: "That is exactly what I mean; you acquire a meager profit through this path, but you lose the great capital of faith!"

2- They are far from Allah and do not find pleasure in intimate prayer with Him; for this reason: "When they stand up for prayer, they are completely immersed in laziness and lethargy" (وَ إِذا قامُوا إِلَى الصَّلاةِ قامُوا كُسالى‌).

3- Since they do not believe in Allah and His great promises, if they perform an act of worship or a good deed, it is also out of showing off (Riya) and not for the sake of Allah! (يُراؤُنَ النَّاسَ‌).

4- If they utter any remembrance (dhikr) or mention Allah, it is not from the depths of their hearts or out of awareness and mindfulness; and if it occurs, it is very little.

(وَ لا يَذْكُرُونَ اللَّهَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا).

5- They are wandering, aimless individuals lacking a specific plan or path; they are neither among the believers nor in the ranks of the disbelievers! (مُذَبْذَبِينَ بَيْنَ ذلِكَ لا إِلى‌ هؤُلاءِ وَ لا إِلى‌ هؤُلاءِ).

It should be noted that the word Mudhabdhab is the passive participle from the root dhabdhaba. Originally, it refers to the specific sound heard when a hanging object moves due to colliding with air waves. Subsequently, it has been applied to moving objects and to people who are wandering, bewildered, and lacking a plan. This is one of the most subtle expressions used in the Quran regarding the hypocrites. It contains an implicit indication that it is not the case that hypocrites cannot be recognized; rather, this wavering of theirs is accompanied by a specific "tone" by which they are identified. Furthermore, one can infer from this expression the reality that they, like a suspended and hanging object, inherently lack a direction of movement; rather, it is the winds that move them in every direction, carrying them wherever they blow!

At the end of the verse, He describes their fate as such: they are individuals from whom Allah has withdrawn His support due to their actions and has left them to stray in the wilderness, "and whomsoever Allah leaves to stray, you will never find for him a way [of salvation]."

(وَ مَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللَّهُ فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لَهُ سَبِيلًا).

(Regarding the meaning of Allah’s "leading astray" (idlal) and how it does not contradict free will and choice, we discussed this in the first volume of this Tafsir under the commentary of verse 26 of Surah Al-Baqarah).

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۚ أَتُرِيدُونَ أَن تَجْعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطَـٰنًا مُّبِينًا (١٤٤)
O you who have faith! Do not take the faithless for friends instead of the faithful. Do you wish to give Allah a clear sanction against yourselves? (144)
إِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ فِى ٱلدَّرْكِ ٱلْأَسْفَلِ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ وَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُمْ نَصِيرًا (١٤٥)
Indeed the hypocrites will be in the lowest reach of the Fire, and you will never find any helper for them, (145)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ وَٱعْتَصَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَأَخْلَصُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ لِلَّهِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَعَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۖ وَسَوْفَ يُؤْتِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (١٤٦)
except for those who repent and reform, and hold fast to Allah and dedicate their religion [exclusively] to Allah. Those are with the faithful, and soon Allah will give the faithful a great reward. (146)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, some of the characteristics of the hypocrites and disbelievers were mentioned. In these verses, believers are first warned not to choose disbelievers (and hypocrites) as their support and guardians instead of believers.

(يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَتَّخِذُوا الْكَافِرِينَ أَوْلِيَاءَ مِنْ دُونِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ)

This is because such an act is an explicit crime, a violation of the law, and a form of polytheism toward Allah. Considering the principle of Divine Justice, it warrants severe punishment. Therefore, He follows by saying: "Do you wish to provide Allah with a clear proof against yourselves?"

(أَتُرِيدُونَ أَنْ تَجْعَلُوا لِلَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطَانًا مُبِينًا) [^1]

In the next verse, to clarify the state of the hypocrites—whom this group of heedless Muslims take as friends—or the state of those who, while professing Islam, follow the path of hypocrisy and enter into friendship with hypocrites, He says: "Indeed, the hypocrites are in the lowest and deepest depths of the Fire, and you will never find for them any helper."

(إِنَّ الْمُنَافِقِينَ فِي الدَّرْكِ الْأَسْفَلِ مِنَ النَّارِ وَلَنْ تَجِدَ لَهُمْ نَصِيرًا) [^2]

From this verse, it is clearly understood that from the perspective of Islam, hypocrisy is the worst form of disbelief, and hypocrites are the people furthest from Allah. For this reason, their place is the worst and lowest point of Hell. And it must be so, because the dangers posed by hypocrites to human societies are incomparable to any other danger.

[^1]: Sultan is originally derived from the root salatah (on the pattern of maqalah), meaning the power to overcome another. The word sultan itself carries the meaning of a verbal noun and is applied to any kind of "dominion" or "authority." For this reason, a "proof" or "evidence" is also called sultan because it enables a person to prevail over another. Sometimes, those in power are also called sultan, but in the above verse, sultan carries the meaning of proof and evidence.

[^2]: Dark (rhyming with marg) refers to the deepest point of the seabed. The final ropes that are joined together to reach the bottom of the sea are called darak (rhyming with falak). It appears that all these are derived from the meaning of "perceiving" (dark) and reaching something. Sometimes, steps that lead downward (like the stairs of a cellar) are called dark, in contrast to steps that lead upward (like those to a roof), which are referred to as darajah.

By utilizing the immunity they gain under the guise of professing faith, they cowardly and freely attack defenseless individuals, stabbing them in the back. Certainly, the state of such cowardly and dangerous enemies—who appear in the guise of friends—is far worse than that of enemies who explicitly declare their enmity and make their position clear. In reality, "hypocrisy" is the way and method of characterless, base, mysterious, and cowardly individuals who are polluted in every sense of the word.

However, to make it clear that even these extremely polluted individuals have a way back to Allah and can reform their situation, He adds: "Except those who repent and reform their actions (and compensate for the past) and hold fast to the grace of their Lord and purify their religion and faith for Allah."

(إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تَابُوا وَأَصْلَحُوا وَاعْتَصَمُوا بِاللَّهِ وَأَخْلَصُوا دِينَهُمْ لِلَّهِ)

Such people will ultimately be among the saved and will be joined with the believers (فَأُولَئِكَ مَعَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

And Allah will grant a great reward to all those who have faith.

(وَسَوْفَ يُؤْتِ اللَّهُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا)

It is noteworthy that at the end of the verse, He says: "they will be with the believers," indicating that the status of the "steadfast believers" is superior and higher than theirs. The steadfast believers are the core, and these [repentant ones] are the branches; they find light and purity through the presence of the true believers.

Another point to consider is that the fate of the hypocrites is described specifically and considered the lowest level of Hell, whereas for the believers, He suffices with "a great reward" (ajran 'azima), which has no boundaries and depends on the greatness of the Lord's grace.

Ayah

مَّا يَفْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ بِعَذَابِكُمْ إِن شَكَرْتُمْ وَءَامَنتُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ شَاكِرًا عَلِيمًا (١٤٧)
Why should Allah punish you if you give thanks and be faithful? And Allah is appreciative, all-knowing. (147)

Commentary

God's Punishments Are Not Out of Revenge

Following the previous verses which reflected the severe punishment of the disbelievers and hypocrites, this verse points to an "important reality." That is, the painful divine punishments are not because Allah wishes to take "revenge" on rebellious servants, or to "display His power," or to "compensate" for a loss incurred through their disobedience. All of these are characteristics of deficiencies and shortcomings, from which the Pure Essence of Allah is free. Rather, these punishments are all reflections and the evil consequences of the actions and beliefs of humans themselves. Therefore, He says: "What would Allah do with your punishment if you are grateful and believe?"

(ما يَفْعَلُ اللَّهُ بِعَذابِكُمْ إِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ وَ آمَنْتُمْ‌).

Considering that the reality of "shukr" (gratitude) is to utilize every blessing in the way for which it was created, it becomes clear that the meaning of the above sentence is:

If you possess faith and righteous deeds, and employ divine favors in their proper place and do not misuse them, undoubtedly, not the slightest punishment will afflict you.

To emphasize this matter, He adds: "Allah is both Aware of your deeds and intentions, and He is Grateful and the Rewarder of your good deeds."

(وَ كانَ اللَّهُ شاكِراً عَلِيماً).

In the above verse, the subject of "gratitude" is placed before "faith." This is because until a human recognizes His blessings and favors and reaches the station of gratitude, he cannot recognize Him, as His blessings are a means for recognition. In books of Islamic theology, regarding the discussion on the necessity of knowing God (wujub ma'rifat Allah), a group of researchers argue through the "necessity of thanking the Benefactor" (wujub shukr al-mun'im). They establish the innate necessity of gratitude toward the "Bestower of Blessings" as a path toward the necessity of knowing Him (Reflect carefully).

Ayahs

۞ لَّا يُحِبُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْجَهْرَ بِٱلسُّوٓءِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ إِلَّا مَن ظُلِمَ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ سَمِيعًا عَلِيمًا (١٤٨)
Allah does not like the disclosure of [anyone’s] evil [conduct] in speech except by someone who has been wronged, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (148)
إِن تُبْدُوا۟ خَيْرًا أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ أَوْ تَعْفُوا۟ عَن سُوٓءٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَفُوًّا قَدِيرًا (١٤٩)
Whether you disclose a good [deed that you do] or hide it, or excuse an evil [deed], Allah is indeed all-excusing, all-powerful. (149)

Commentary

In these two verses, reference is made to a portion of the ethical instructions of Islam. First, it states: "Allah does not like that evil should be mentioned or that the faults and ugly deeds of individuals be exposed through speech."

(لَا يُحِبُّ اللَّهُ الْجَهْرَ بِالسُّوءِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ) Because just as Allah is the "Veiler of Faults" (Sattar al-Uyub), He does not like for human beings to tear away the veils of others, reveal people's faults, and destroy their reputation. Furthermore, we know that every human usually has hidden weak points; if these faults were to be exposed, a strange spirit of pessimism would cast a shadow over the entire society, making cooperation with one another difficult. Therefore, for the sake of strengthening social bonds and out of respect for human dignity, it is necessary not to tear the veil of privacy without a valid and correct objective.

Additionally, it should be noted that the word "Su’" (evil/bad) refers to any kind of evil or ugliness, and "al-jahr... min al-qawl" (public utterance) refers to any kind of verbal expression or manifestation, whether it be in the form of a complaint, a narration, a curse, a reproach, or backbiting (Ghibah). For this reason, this verse is among those cited in discussions regarding the prohibition of "backbiting," although the concept of the verse is not limited to backbiting and includes every type of speaking ill of others.

Then, the verse points to certain matters that serve as a justification for such speaking ill and tearing of veils, stating: "Except for one who has been wronged" (إِلَّا مَنْ ظُلِمَ).

Such individuals have the right to take action by lodging a complaint to defend themselves against the oppression of the oppressor, or to openly criticize, condemn, and speak of the injustices and tyrannies committed against them. They should not rest until they have secured their rights and repelled the oppression.

In reality, the mention of this exception is so that the aforementioned ethical rule is not exploited by tyrants and oppressors, nor does it become an excuse for submitting to oppression.

It is clear that in such cases, one must limit themselves only to that portion which pertains to the oppression of the oppressor and the defense of the oppressed.

At the end of the verse—as is the method of the Quran—to ensure that individuals do not abuse this exception and use the excuse of being wronged to reveal people's faults without cause, it states: "Allah is All-Hearing and All-Knowing."

(وَ كَانَ اللَّهُ سَمِيعاً عَلِيماً).

In the following verse, it points to the opposite of this ruling, stating: "If you manifest good deeds or hide them, there is no objection (unlike evil deeds which must be concealed absolutely except in exceptional cases); and also, if you choose the path of pardon and forgiveness regarding the evils people have done to you, it is better. For this act is, in truth, a type of Divine action, as He pardons His worthy servants despite having the power to take any kind of revenge."

(إِنْ تُبْدُوا خَيْراً أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ أَوْ تَعْفُوا عَنْ سُوءٍ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَفُوّاً قَدِيراً).

In fact, the second verse stands in contrast to the first verse in two ways: first, "manifesting good" as opposed to "manifesting evil," and second, "pardon and forgiveness" as opposed to those who have been oppressed.

Does forgiving an oppressor lead to his empowerment?!

A question arises here: Does pardon and forgiveness of an oppressor not effectively signify the endorsement of his tyranny? Does this act not encourage him to continue his oppression? And will this instruction not create a kind of sedative negative reaction among the oppressed?

The answer to this question is that the case of "pardon and forgiveness" is distinct from the case of "seeking justice and fighting the oppressor." For this reason, in Islamic instructions, we read on one hand: neither oppress nor submit to oppression (لَا تَظْلِمُونَ وَ لَا تُظْلَمُونَ) [1] and be an enemy to the oppressor and a helper to the oppressed.

(كونا للظالم خصما و للمظلوم عونا) [2] Fight against the oppressors until they submit to the command of Allah.

(فَقاتِلُوا الَّتِي تَبْغِي حَتَّى تَفِي‌ءَ إِلى‌ أَمْرِ اللَّهِ) [3] And on the other hand, instructions for pardon, forgiveness, and overlooking have been given, as He says:

(وَ أَنْ تَعْفُوا أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوى) [4] (وَ لْيَعْفُوا وَ لْيَصْفَحُوا أَ لا تُحِبُّونَ أَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ) [5] Although some uninformed individuals might see a contradiction between these two rulings at first glance,

[1] Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 279. [2] Nahj al-Balagha, Will No. 47 (Note: original text says 48). [3] Surah Al-Hujurat, Verse 9. [4] Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 237. [5] Surah Al-Nur, Verse 22.

it becomes clear upon considering what has been recorded in Islamic sources that the place for pardon and forgiveness is a specific context where it will not be exploited, while the place for fighting and crushing oppression is another.

To explain: pardon and forgiveness are specifically for instances of power and victory over the enemy and his final defeat—that is, in a situation where no new danger is felt from the enemy's side. Rather, pardoning and forgiving him is considered a type of reform and education for him, prompting him to reconsider his path, as we encounter such individuals in many instances of Islamic history. The famous Hadith:

"اذا قدرت على عدوك فاجعل العفو عنه شكرا للقدرة عليه" "When you gain power over your enemy, make forgiveness the zakat (gratitude) for the power gained over him" [1] is a testament to this claim.

However, in cases where the danger of the enemy has not yet been removed and forgiveness would likely make him more audacious and prepared, or where pardon and forgiveness are considered a type of surrender and satisfaction with oppression, Islam never permits such forgiveness, and the leaders of Islam never chose the path of pardon and forgiveness in such instances.

[1] Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, Saying 11 (Note: original text says 10).

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَن يُفَرِّقُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَيَقُولُونَ نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَنَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَن يَتَّخِذُوا۟ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ سَبِيلًا (١٥٠)
Those who disbelieve in Allah and His apostles and seek to separate Allah from His apostles, and say, ‘We believe in some and disbelieve in some’ and seek to take a way in between (150)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ حَقًّا ۚ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا (١٥١)
—it is they who are truly faithless, and We have prepared for the faithless a humiliating punishment. (151)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَلَمْ يُفَرِّقُوا۟ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ سَوْفَ يُؤْتِيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١٥٢)
But those who have faith in Allah and His apostles and make no distinction between any of them—them He will soon give their rewards, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (152)

Commentary

No Discrimination Among the Prophets

In these few verses, a description of the state of a group of disbelievers and believers and their destiny is provided, completing the previous verses which were about the hypocrites.

First, it refers to the state of those who differentiate between the Divine Prophets, considering some to be on the path of truth and others on the path of falsehood, stating: "Those who disbelieve in Allah and His Messengers and wish to create a division between Allah and His Messengers, declaring that we believe in some of them while we do not recognize others, and in their own imagination, they wish to find a way in between; they are the true disbelievers."

(إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَ رُسُلِهِ وَ يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُفَرِّقُوا بَيْنَ اللَّهِ وَ رُسُلِهِ وَ يَقُولُونَ نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَ نَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ وَ يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذُوا بَيْنَ ذلِكَ سَبِيلًا أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكافِرُونَ حَقًّا).

In reality, this sentence clarifies the state of the Jews and the Christians; the Jews did not recognize Christ (peace be upon him), and both groups did not recognize the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, even though the prophethood of these Messengers had been proven to them according to their own Heavenly Books. This discrimination in accepting realities—which stems from whims, desires, ignorant prejudices, and occasionally envy and baseless narrow-mindedness—is a sign of a lack of faith in the Prophets and Allah. For faith is not that a person accepts whatever conforms to his desires and rejects whatever goes against his whims and desires; that is a form of "worshipping one's desires," not faith. True faith is for a person to accept the truth whether it conforms to his desires or goes against them. Therefore, in the above verses, the Quran considers such individuals—despite their claims of faith in Allah and some of the Prophets—to be disbelievers in general, saying:

إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَ رُسُلِهِ‌ ...

Consequently, their faith is deemed worthless even in the instances where they express faith, because it does not originate from a spirit of seeking the truth.

At the end, He threatens them, saying: "We have prepared for the disbelievers a humiliating and debasing punishment."

(وَ أَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ عَذاباً مُهِيناً).

The description of the punishment in this verse as "humiliating" (muhīn) may be due to the fact that by creating division among Allah's Prophets, they have in fact insulted a group of them, and thus their punishment must be proportionate to their action.

Proportionality of Sin and Punishment

To explain: punishment is sometimes "painful" (ʿadhāb alīm), such as flogging and physical torment; sometimes it is "humiliating" (ʿadhāb muhīn), such as splashing mud on someone's clothes and the like; sometimes it is "monumental/loud" (ʿadhāb ʿaẓīm), such as punishment in the presence of a crowd; and sometimes its effect on the human being is deep and remains for a long time (ʿadhāb shadīd), such as long-term imprisonment with hard labor... and the like.

It is clear that describing the punishment with one of these attributes has a proportionality with the type of "sin." Therefore, in many verses of the Quran, the punishment for oppressors is mentioned as a "painful punishment," because it is proportionate to the painful nature of oppression against Allah's servants. Those whose sin is insulting, as well as those who commit severe or high-profile sins, have a punishment similar to it. However, the purpose of mentioning the above examples is to bring the concept closer to the mind; otherwise, the punishments of that world are not comparable to the punishments of this world.

Then, He refers to the state of the believers and their destiny, saying: "Those who have believed in Allah and all His Messengers and did not create a division between any of them, and by this act, proved their 'submission and sincerity' before the truth and their struggle against any kind of misplaced 'prejudice,' Allah will soon give them their rewards."

(وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رُسُلِهِ وَ لَمْ يُفَرِّقُوا بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ أُولئِكَ سَوْفَ يُؤْتِيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ‌).

Of course, believing in the Prophets and recognizing them does not contradict the fact that we consider some to be superior to others, for the difference among them is as certain as the difference in their missions. The point is that we should not create a division among the true Prophets in terms of faith and recognition.

At the end of the verse, it is pointed out that if this group of believers committed such prejudices, divisions, and other sins in the past, if they purify their faith and return to Allah, He will forgive them: "And Allah has always been All-Forgiving, Most Merciful."

(وَ كانَ اللَّهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً).

It is noteworthy that in the above verses, those who create division among the Prophets are introduced as "true disbelievers," but those who believe in all of them are not introduced as "true believers"; they are only described as "believers." Perhaps this difference is because true believers are those who, in addition to faith, are also completely pure and righteous in terms of "action." Evidence for this is found in the verses at the beginning of Surah Al-Anfal, which describe believers—after faith in Allah—with a series of positive and vital actions such as moral, social, and spiritual growth, prayer, zakat, and trust in Allah, and subsequently says:

أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ حَقًّا (Al-Anfal: 4)

Ayahs

يَسْـَٔلُكَ أَهْلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَن تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ كِتَـٰبًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۚ فَقَدْ سَأَلُوا۟ مُوسَىٰٓ أَكْبَرَ مِن ذَٰلِكَ فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَرِنَا ٱللَّهَ جَهْرَةً فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّـٰعِقَةُ بِظُلْمِهِمْ ۚ ثُمَّ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ ٱلْعِجْلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ فَعَفَوْنَا عَن ذَٰلِكَ ۚ وَءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ سُلْطَـٰنًا مُّبِينًا (١٥٣)
The People of the Book ask you to bring down for them a Book from the sky. Certainly they asked Moses for [something] greater than that, for they said, ‘Show us Allah visibly,’ whereat a thunderbolt seized them for their wrongdoing. Then they took up the Calf [for worship], after all the manifest proofs that had come to them. Yet We excused that, and We gave Moses a manifest authority. (153)
وَرَفَعْنَا فَوْقَهُمُ ٱلطُّورَ بِمِيثَـٰقِهِمْ وَقُلْنَا لَهُمُ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْبَابَ سُجَّدًا وَقُلْنَا لَهُمْ لَا تَعْدُوا۟ فِى ٱلسَّبْتِ وَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُم مِّيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا (١٥٤)
And We raised the Mount above them for the sake of their covenant, and We said to them, ‘Enter the gate prostrating’ and We said to them, ‘Do not violate the Sabbath,’ and We took from them a solemn covenant. (154)

Occasion of Revelation

In the commentaries of Al-Tibyan, Majma' al-Bayan, and Ruh al-Ma'ani, it is mentioned regarding the occasion of the revelation of these verses that a group of Jews came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "If you are a Prophet of Allah, present your heavenly book to us all at once, just as Moses brought the Torah all at once." Consequently, the above verses were revealed and provided them with an answer.

Commentary

The Pretext-Seeking of the Jews

These verses first refer to the request of the People of the Book (the Jews) and state: "The People of the Book ask you to bring down a book from heaven (all at once) upon them."

(يَسْئَلُكَ أَهْلُ الْكِتابِ أَنْ تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ كِتاباً مِنَ السَّماءِ).

There is no doubt that they did not have good intentions in this request, for the purpose of the revelation of heavenly books is guidance, direction, and cultivation. Sometimes this goal is achieved by the revelation of a heavenly book all at once, and sometimes its gradual nature assists this goal further. Therefore, they should have asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله for proof and for sublime and valuable teachings, rather than dictating the manner in which heavenly books should be revealed.

Thus, following this request, Allah points to their lack of good intentions and, while comforting His Prophet, recounts the history of the Jews' obstinacy, defiance, and pretext-seeking against their great Prophet, Moses son of Amram (Musa ibn Imran) عليه السلام.

First, He says: "They demanded of Moses something even greater and more strange than this, saying: 'Show us Allah plainly!'" (فَقَدْ سَأَلُوا مُوسى‌ أَكْبَرَ مِنْ ذلِكَ فَقالُوا أَرِنَا اللَّهَ جَهْرَةً).

This strange and irrational request, which reflected a type of belief held by idolaters and characterized Allah as a physical and limited being, and which undoubtedly stemmed from obstinacy and defiance, caused a thunderbolt from the sky to seize them because of this wrongdoing and injustice.

(فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّاعِقَةُ بِظُلْمِهِمْ‌) Then, He refers to another of their foul deeds, which was the issue of "calf-worship," and says: "They took the calf [for worship] even after clear signs and evidences had come to them!" (ثُمَّ اتَّخَذُوا الْعِجْلَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ‌).

Yet, despite all this, so that they might return to the right path and descend from the steed of obstinacy and defiance, "We forgave them for that, and We gave Moses a clear authority and manifest power, and We dismantled the disgraceful display of Samiri and the calf-worshippers."

(فَعَفَوْنا عَنْ ذلِكَ وَ آتَيْنا مُوسى‌ سُلْطاناً مُبِيناً).

Still, they did not wake up from the sleep of negligence and did not descend from the steed of pride. For this reason, "We raised Mount Tur above them [as a canopy] and at that same time took a covenant from them, and We told them to enter the gate of Jerusalem with humility and prostration as a sign of repentance for your sins. We also emphasized to them that they must cease work and trade on the Sabbath and not transgress, and not to utilize the fish of the sea whose fishing was forbidden on that day; and We took from them a solemn covenant regarding all of this." But they did not fulfill any of these emphasized covenants! [1] (وَ رَفَعْنا فَوْقَهُمُ الطُّورَ بِمِيثاقِهِمْ وَ قُلْنا لَهُمُ ادْخُلُوا الْبابَ سُجَّداً وَ قُلْنا لَهُمْ لا تَعْدُوا فِي السَّبْتِ وَ أَخَذْنا مِنْهُمْ مِيثاقاً غَلِيظاً).

[1]: Regarding the meaning of Mount Tur being placed above the heads of the Jews—whether this was due to an earthquake or another factor—as well as regarding calf-worship and other parts of the Jews' poor history, we have discussed this in the first volume of this Tafsir on pages 170 to 186 and 201 to 205.

Is this group, with such a dark history, sincere and truthful in this request they make of you?! If they are telling the truth, why do they not act upon the explicit text of their own heavenly books regarding the signs of the Final Prophet, and why do they ignore all these clear signs of yours?

Here, two points seem necessary to mention:

First, if it is asked: "These actions pertained to the ancestors of the Jews; what connection do they have to the Jews contemporary with the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله?"

In response, it must be said that they never objected to the actions of their ancestors; rather, they showed approval toward them, and thus they are all placed in the same rank.

Second, what was mentioned in the occasion of revelation—that the Jews claimed the Torah was revealed all at once—is not a settled matter. Perhaps what caused this misconception is that the Ten Commandments (Decalogue) were revealed to Moses all at once on tablets; however, regarding the other laws of the Torah, there is no evidence that they were revealed all at once.

Ayahs

فَبِمَا نَقْضِهِم مِّيثَـٰقَهُمْ وَكُفْرِهِم بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَقَتْلِهِمُ ٱلْأَنۢبِيَآءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَقَوْلِهِمْ قُلُوبُنَا غُلْفٌۢ ۚ بَلْ طَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهَا بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (١٥٥)
Then because of their breaking their covenant, their defiance of Allah’s signs, their killing of the prophets unjustly and for their saying, ‘Our hearts are uncircumcised’… Indeed, Allah has set a seal on them for their unfaith, so they do not have faith except a few. (155)
وَبِكُفْرِهِمْ وَقَوْلِهِمْ عَلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ بُهْتَـٰنًا عَظِيمًا (١٥٦)
And for their faithlessness, and their uttering a monstrous calumny against Mary, (156)
وَقَوْلِهِمْ إِنَّا قَتَلْنَا ٱلْمَسِيحَ عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ وَمَا صَلَبُوهُ وَلَـٰكِن شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ ۚ مَا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِلَّا ٱتِّبَاعَ ٱلظَّنِّ ۚ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ يَقِينًۢا (١٥٧)
and for their saying, ‘We killed the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, the apostle of Allah’—though they did not kill him nor did they crucify him, but so it was made to appear to them. Indeed those who differ concerning him are surely in doubt about him: they do not have any knowledge of that beyond following conjectures, and certainly, they did not kill him. (157)
بَل رَّفَعَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْهِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا (١٥٨)
Indeed, Allah raised him up toward Himself, and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (158)

Commentary

Another Aspect of the Transgressions of the Jews

In these verses, reference is made to other parts of the transgressions of the Children of Israel, their obstructions, and their animosity and hostility toward the Prophets of Allah.

In the first verse, referring to the breach of covenant and the disbelief of a group of them, and the killing of Prophets by their hands, it states: "We removed them from Our mercy or prohibited some of the pure blessings for them because of their breaking of the covenant."

(فَبِما نَقْضِهِمْ مِيثاقَهُمْ‌ ...) [1] Following this breach of covenant, they denied the signs of the Lord and took the path of opposition (وَ كُفْرِهِمْ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ‌).

And they did not stop at this, but committed another great crime, which was the murder and killing of the guides and leaders of the path of truth—namely, the Prophets—and they eliminated them without any justification.

(وَ قَتْلِهِمُ الْأَنْبِياءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍ‌).

They were so audacious and reckless in their wrongful acts that they would mock the words of the Prophets and explicitly say to them: "Our hearts are wrapped in covers that prevent us from hearing and accepting your call!"

[1]: From a grammatical perspective, "فبما نقضهم" is a prepositional phrase (jar wa majrur) and must have a governing agent (amil). It is possible that its agent is a deleted and implied "لعناهم" (We cursed them), or the sentence "حرمنا عليهم..." (We forbade them...) which will appear in verse 160. Therefore, what appears in between acts as a parenthetical sentence, which in such cases adds to the eloquence of the speech.

(وَ قَوْلِهِمْ قُلُوبُنا غُلْفٌ‌).

Here, the Quran adds: "Indeed, their hearts are completely sealed and no truth penetrates them; however, the cause of that disbelief and lack of faith is they themselves, and for this reason, they do not believe except for a few individuals who have kept themselves away from such obstinacy."

(بَلْ طَبَعَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْها بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا).

Their transgressions are not limited to these; they raced so swiftly on the path of disbelief that they cast a great slander against the chaste Mary, the mother of the great Prophet of God, who had become pregnant by Divine command without a husband.

(وَ بِكُفْرِهِمْ وَ قَوْلِهِمْ عَلى‌ مَرْيَمَ بُهْتاناً عَظِيماً) They even boasted about killing the Prophets "and said: We have killed the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, the Messenger of Allah."

(وَ قَوْلِهِمْ إِنَّا قَتَلْنَا الْمَسِيحَ عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولَ اللَّهِ‌).

And perhaps the use of the term "Messenger of Allah" regarding the Messiah was said out of mockery and ridicule.

While they were also liars in this claim of theirs, "They never killed the Messiah, nor did they crucify him, but rather another who resembled him was crucified by mistake."

(وَ ما قَتَلُوهُ وَ ما صَلَبُوهُ وَ لكِنْ شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ‌) Then the Quran says: "Those who differed concerning the Messiah were themselves in doubt, and none of them believed in what they said, following nothing but conjecture and guesswork."

(وَ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ لَفِي شَكٍّ مِنْهُ ما لَهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِلَّا اتِّباعَ الظَّنِ‌) Regarding what they differed about, there is discussion among commentators. It is possible that this difference related to the original status and rank of the Messiah (peace be upon him), as a group of Christians considered him the son of God, while others, like the Jews, did not consider him a Prophet at all, and all were in error.

It is also possible that the difference was regarding the manner of his killing, as some claimed to have killed him and some said he was not killed, and none were certain of their words.

Or that those claiming the killing of the Messiah (peace be upon him), due to their lack of familiarity with him, were in doubt whether the one they killed was the Messiah himself or someone else in his place.

Then the Quran says by way of emphasizing the matter: "Certainly they did not kill him, but Allah raised him to Himself, and Allah is Almighty, All-Wise."

(وَ ما قَتَلُوهُ يَقِيناً بَلْ رَفَعَهُ اللَّهُ إِلَيْهِ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزاً حَكِيماً)

The Messiah Was Not Killed—The Legend of the Cross

In the above verse, the Quran says: "The Messiah was neither killed nor crucified, but the matter was made to appear so to them, and they thought they had crucified him, and certainly they did not kill him!" However, the current four Gospels all mention the issue of the crucifixion of the Messiah (peace be upon him) and his killing, and this subject is explained in detail in the final chapters of all four Gospels (Matthew, Luke, Mark, John), and the general belief of Christians today is also based on this issue.

In fact, in one sense, the issue of the killing and crucifixion of the Messiah constitutes one of the most important foundational issues of current Christianity, because we know that current Christians do not consider the Messiah (peace be upon him) as a Prophet who came for the guidance, education, and direction of the people, but rather they consider him "the Son of God!" and "one of the three gods!" whose main purpose in coming to this world was to be sacrificed and to redeem the sins of humanity. They say: He came to be a sacrifice for our sins; he was crucified and killed to wash away the sins of humanity and save the world from punishment. Therefore, they consider the way of salvation exclusively in connection with the Messiah and belief in this subject! For this reason, Christianity is sometimes called the religion of "Salvation" or "Atonement," and the Messiah is given the title of "Savior" and "Redeemer." The fact that we see Christians emphasizing the issue of the cross excessively and their symbol being the "cross" is from this point of view.

This was a summary of the Christian belief regarding the fate of Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him).

However, none of the Muslims have any doubt about the falsehood of this belief, because:

Firstly: The Messiah (peace be upon him) was a Prophet like other Prophets of God; he was neither God nor the son of God. Allah is One and Unique, and He has no peer, likeness, equal, spouse, or child.

Secondly: "Atonement" and becoming a sacrifice for the sins of others is a completely illogical matter. Everyone is a hostage to their own deeds, and the way of salvation is only through one's own faith and righteous deeds.

Thirdly: The belief in "Atonement" is sin-fostering and encourages corruption, destruction, and impurity.

And if we see that the Quran specifically emphasizes the issue of the Messiah (peace be upon him) not being crucified—even though it seemingly appears to be a simple matter—it is for this reason: to severely strike the superstitious belief of atonement and the redemption of the sins of the Ummah, and to deter Christians from this superstitious belief so that they see salvation as dependent on their own deeds, not in seeking refuge in the cross.

Fourthly: There are indications at hand that weaken the issue of the crucifixion of Jesus (peace be upon him).

1- We know that the current four Gospels that testify to the crucifixion of Jesus (peace be upon him) were all written years after the Messiah (peace be upon him) by his disciples or the disciples of his disciples, and this is a statement that Christian historians admit.

We also know that the disciples of the Messiah (peace be upon him) fled when the enemies attacked him, and the Gospels also testify to this fact [1]. Therefore, they took the issue of the crucifixion of Jesus (peace be upon him) from the rumors of the people, and as we will mention later, circumstances developed such that the situation was ripe for mistaking another person for the Messiah (peace be upon him).

2- Another factor that makes mistaking Jesus for another person possible is that those who went to the Garden of "Gethsemane" outside the city to arrest Jesus were a group of Roman soldiers who were busy with military duties in the camps. This group neither knew the Jews, nor did they know their customs, language, and traditions, nor could they distinguish the disciples of Jesus from their master.

3- The Gospels say: The attack on the location of Jesus (peace be upon him) was carried out at night, and how easy it is in such a commotion for the intended person to escape and another to be captured in his place.

4- From the writings of all the Gospels, it is understood that the captured person chose silence in the presence of "Pilate" (the Roman governor in Jerusalem) and spoke very little in response to their words or to defend himself. It seems very unlikely that Jesus (peace be upon him) would see himself in danger and not defend himself with his eloquent and expressive speech and with the specific courage and bravery he possessed. Is there not the possibility that another (most likely Judas Iscariot, who betrayed the Messiah (peace be upon him) and played the role of a spy, and it is said he had a complete resemblance to the Messiah (peace be upon him)) was arrested in his place and was so overwhelmed by terror and anxiety that he could not even defend himself or say a word—especially since we read in the Gospels that "Judas Iscariot" was not seen again after this event and, according to the Gospels, committed suicide! [2]

5- As we said: The disciples of the Messiah (peace be upon him) fled when they felt danger, according to the testimony of the Gospels, and naturally, other friends also went into hiding that day and observed the situation from afar. Therefore, the captured person was within the circle of the Roman military siege and none of his friends were around him. In this way, what wonder is there that a mistake occurred?

6- In the Gospels, we read that the person condemned on the cross complained to God as to why He had left him alone and handed him over to the enemy to be killed! [3] If the Messiah (peace be upon him) was born for this—to be crucified and become a sacrifice for the sins of humanity—such an inappropriate statement from him would be in no way correct. This sentence clearly shows that the crucified person was a weak, fearful, and helpless individual from whom such a statement was possible, and he cannot be the Messiah. [4]

[1]: ...At that time all the disciples forsook him and fled (Gospel of Matthew, Chapter 26, Verse 56). [2]: Gospel of Matthew, Chapter 27, Verse 5. [3]: ...Jesus cried with a loud voice, saying, Eli, Eli, lama sabachthani? that is to say, My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me? (Gospel of Matthew, Chapter 27, Verse 46). [4]: Several parts of the above indications are taken from the book "The Hero of the Cross."

7- Some of the existing Gospels (other than the four Gospels accepted by Christians), such as the Gospel of Barnabas, officially deny the crucifixion of Jesus (peace be upon him), and also some Christian sects have doubted the crucifixion of Jesus (peace be upon him) [1]. And even some researchers believe in the existence of two Jesuses in history: one "the crucified Jesus" and the other "the non-crucified Jesus," with five hundred years between the two! [2]

The sum of what has been said above are indications that clarify the statement of the Quran regarding the mistake in the killing and crucifixion of the Messiah.

Ayah

وَإِن مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِۦ ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا (١٥٩)
There is none among the People of the Book but will surely believe in him before his death; and on the Day of Resurrection, he will be a witness against them. (159)

Commentary

Regarding the interpretation of the above verse, there are two possibilities, each of which is noteworthy for several reasons:

1- The verse states: "There is none from the People of the Book but that they will believe in Christ عليه السلام before 'their death'."

(وَ إِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ‌) This occurs when a person is on the threshold of death and their connection with

[^1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 6, p. 34.

[^2]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 3, p. 345.

this world weakens while their connection with the world after death grows strong. The veils are removed from before their eyes, and they see many realities and gain awareness of them. It is at this moment that their truth-seeking eye observes the station of Christ عليه السلام and they submit to him. Those who denied him will believe in him, and those who considered him God will realize their mistake.
