However, this faith—much like the faith of Pharaoh and other nations who believed at the moment they witnessed the punishment and the precursors of destruction and death—will be of no benefit to them. Therefore, it is better that instead of believing at that critical moment when faith is of no use, they believe now while faith is beneficial. (According to this interpretation, the pronoun in "before his death" refers to the "Person of the Book").

2- The meaning is that all the People of the Book will believe in Prophet Christ عليه السلام before "his death." The Jews will accept him as a Prophet, and the Christians will abandon their belief in his divinity. This will occur when Christ عليه السلام, according to Islamic narrations, descends from heaven at the time of the appearance of the Mahdi (may Allah hasten his reappearance) and prays behind him. The Jews and Christians will see him and believe in him and the Mahdi عليه السلام. It is clear that since Christ عليه السلام belongs to a previous dispensation, he is obligated at that time to follow the existing religion, namely the religion of Islam, which the Mahdi عليه السلام implements. (According to this interpretation, the pronoun in "before his death" refers to "Christ," not the People of the Book).

In many Islamic books, this hadith is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله who said:

"How will you be when the son of Mary descends among you while your Imam is from among yourselves?" [^1] Of course, according to this interpretation, the "People of the Book" refers to the population of Jews and Christians existing at that time.

In the commentary of "Ali ibn Ibrahim," it is narrated from "Shahr ibn Hawshab" that one day Hajjaj said to him: "There is a verse in the Quran that has exhausted me and I am stuck regarding its meaning." Shahr asked: "Which verse is it, O Amir?" Hajjaj said:

The verse "وَ إِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ‌ ..." because I execute Jews and Christians and I observe no sign of such faith in them. Shahr said: "You have not interpreted the verse correctly." Hajjaj asked: "Why? What is the interpretation of the verse?" Shahr said: "The meaning is that Jesus عليه السلام will descend before the end of the world, and no Jew or non-Jew will remain except that they believe in Jesus عليه السلام before his death. He will pray behind the Mahdi عليه السلام." When Hajjaj heard this, he said: "Woe to you! From where did you bring this interpretation?" He replied: "I heard it from Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Husayn ibn Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام." Hajjaj said: "By Allah, you have brought it from a pure and clear spring!" [^2]

At the end of the verse, He says: "And on the Day of Resurrection, Christ عليه السلام will be a witness against them."

(وَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً).

The meaning of Christ's عليه السلام testimony against them is that he will testify

[^1]: From Musnad Ahmad, Sahih Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and Sunan al-Bayhaqi (as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan).

[^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 426.

that he conveyed the message and never invited them to his own godhood or divinity, but rather invited them to the Lordship of the Creator.

Question

A question arises here: according to verse 117 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, Christ عليه السلام limits his testimony on the Day of Resurrection to the time he lived among his community, and as for the period after that, he disclaims this testimony.

وَ كُنْتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً ما دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنْتَ أَنْتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ أَنْتَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ شَهِيدٌ:

"I was a witness over them as long as I was among them, but when You took me up, You were the Observer over them, and You are Witness over all things."

Whereas in the verse under discussion, we read that Christ عليه السلام will testify on the Day of Resurrection regarding all of them, whether they were in his era or not.

Answer

Careful reflection on the content of the two verses shows that the verse under discussion is about testimony regarding the conveyance of the message and the negation of divinity from Christ, whereas verse 117 of Al-Ma'idah relates to testimony regarding their actions. To explain: the verse under discussion says that Jesus عليه السلام will testify against all those who accepted him as a deity—whether they were in his time or came after—that he never invited them to such a thing.

However, verse 117 of Surah Al-Ma'idah says: In addition to the fact that I conveyed the message correctly and sufficiently, as long as I was among them, I practically prevented their deviation; it was after me that they raised the issue of my divinity and followed the path of deviation, and I was not among them that day to be a witness to their actions and prevent them.

Ayahs

فَبِظُلْمٍ مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ حَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّبَـٰتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ وَبِصَدِّهِمْ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ كَثِيرًا (١٦٠)
Due to the wrongdoing of the Jews, We prohibited them certain good things that were permitted to them [earlier], and for their barring many [people] from the way of Allah, (160)
وَأَخْذِهِمُ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ وَقَدْ نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَأَكْلِهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ ۚ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٦١)
and for their taking usury—though they had been forbidden from it—and for eating up the wealth of the people wrongfully. And We have prepared for the faithless among them a painful punishment. (161)
لَّـٰكِنِ ٱلرَّٰسِخُونَ فِى ٱلْعِلْمِ مِنْهُمْ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلِكَ ۚ وَٱلْمُقِيمِينَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۚ وَٱلْمُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ سَنُؤْتِيهِمْ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (١٦٢)
But as for those who are firmly grounded in knowledge from among them, and the faithful, they believe in what has been sent down to you, and what was sent down before you—those who maintain the prayer, give the zakat, and believe in Allah and the Last Day—them We shall give a great reward. (162)

Commentary

The Fate of the Righteous and Unrighteous Among the Jews

In the preceding verses, several examples of the transgressions of the Jews were mentioned. In the above verses, after citing a few more instances of their improper actions, Allah describes the punishments that have overtaken them—and will continue to do so—in this world and the Hereafter as a result of these deeds.

First, He states: "For the wrongdoing of the Jews, and for their hindering many from the way of Allah, We forbade them certain good things that had been lawful for them, and We deprived them of utilizing them":

(فَبِظُلْمٍ مِنَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّباتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ وَ بِصَدِّهِمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ كَثِيراً).

Furthermore, because "they took usury while they had been forbidden from it, and also because they consumed the property of people unjustly; all of these caused them to be afflicted with that deprivation" (وَ أَخْذِهِمُ الرِّبَوا وَ قَدْ نُهُوا عَنْهُ وَ أَكْلِهِمْ أَمْوالَ النَّاسِ بِالْباطِلِ‌).

Beyond this worldly punishment, We shall afflict them with punishments in the Hereafter, "and We have prepared for the disbelievers among them a painful punishment":

(وَ أَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ مِنْهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً). Here, several points must be noted:

1- The "forbidding of good things" (طيبات) refers to what is mentioned in Surah Al-An'am, verse 146, where it says: "Because of the wrongdoing of the Jews, We forbade them every animal with undivided hooves (such as the camel), and We forbade them the fat of oxen and sheep—which they were fond of—except what adheres to their backs or the entrails, or what is joined to the bone."

Therefore, the aforementioned prohibition was a type of legislative and legal prohibition (tashri'i), not a primordial or existential one (takwini). That is, these blessings were naturally available to them, but they were religiously prohibited from consuming them.

Of course, in the current Torah, in the Book of Leviticus, Chapter 11, there is a reference to the prohibition of some of what we mentioned above, but the idea that this prohibition had a punitive aspect is not reflected there. [^1]

2- Did this prohibition have a general scope, including those who were not wrongdoers, or was it specific to the wrongdoers? From the apparent meaning of the above verse and verse 146 of Al-An'am, the prohibition was general (as it uses the expression "لهم" [for them], unlike the matter of the Hereafter's punishment where it uses the expression "لِلْكافِرِينَ مِنْهُمْ‌" [for the disbelievers among them]). Therefore, for those who were oppressors, this deprivation served as a punishment, and for the righteous who were in the minority, it served as a trial and discipline.

However, some commentators believe that this prohibition was specific to the oppressors, and some narrations also hint at this. In Tafsir al-Burhan, under verse 146 of Surah Al-An'am, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "The rulers of the Children of Israel used to prevent the poor and low-income people from eating the meat of birds and animal fats; thus, Allah forbade these things to them because of this oppression." [^2]

3- It is also understood from this verse that the prohibition of "usury" (riba) was not exclusive to Islam and was also forbidden among previous nations, even though in the current corrupted Torah, its prohibition is limited only to "religious brothers." [^1]

In the last of the three verses above, an important reality is mentioned which the Quran repeatedly emphasizes: the Quran's blame and reproach of the Jews is by no means based on racial or ethnic conflict. Islam does not condemn any race as a "race"; rather, its reproaches and attacks are directed only at the corrupted and the deviant. Therefore, in this verse, it exempts the faithful and virtuous individuals among the Jews, praises them, and promises them a great reward, saying: "But those of them who are well-grounded in knowledge and the believers, who believe in what has been revealed to you and what was revealed before you... We shall soon give them a great reward."

(لكِنِ الرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ مِنْهُمْ وَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَ ما أُنْزِلَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ وَ الْمُقِيمِينَ الصَّلاةَ وَ الْمُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ أُولئِكَ سَنُؤْتِيهِمْ أَجْراً عَظِيماً) [^2]

For this reason, we see that a group of Jewish leaders, upon the emergence of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and witnessing the proofs of his truthfulness, embraced Islam, supported it with all their heart and soul, and were respected by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and other Muslims.

[^1]: Refer to Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 7. [^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Volume 1, page 559. [^1]: Torah, Book of Deuteronomy, Chapter 23, verses 19 and 20. [^2]: Regarding the meaning of "الرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ" (those well-grounded in knowledge), more explanations are provided in Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 325 and 326.

Ayahs

۞ إِنَّآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ كَمَآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ نُوحٍ وَٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ ۚ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطِ وَعِيسَىٰ وَأَيُّوبَ وَيُونُسَ وَهَـٰرُونَ وَسُلَيْمَـٰنَ ۚ وَءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ زَبُورًا (١٦٣)
We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him, and [as] We revealed to Abraham and Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the Tribes, Jesus and Job, Jonah, Aaron, and Solomon—and We gave David the Psalms— (163)
وَرُسُلًا قَدْ قَصَصْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَيْكَ مِن قَبْلُ وَرُسُلًا لَّمْ نَقْصُصْهُمْ عَلَيْكَ ۚ وَكَلَّمَ ٱللَّهُ مُوسَىٰ تَكْلِيمًا (١٦٤)
and apostles We have recounted to you earlier and apostles We have not recounted to you—and to Moses Allah spoke directly— (164)
رُّسُلًا مُّبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ حُجَّةٌۢ بَعْدَ ٱلرُّسُلِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا (١٦٥)
apostles, as bearers of good news and warners, so that mankind may not have any argument against Allah, after the [sending of the] apostles; and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (165)
لَّـٰكِنِ ٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ إِلَيْكَ ۖ أَنزَلَهُۥ بِعِلْمِهِۦ ۖ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَشْهَدُونَ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًا (١٦٦)
But Allah bears witness to what He has sent down to you—He sent it down with His knowledge—and the angels bear witness [too], and Allah quite suffices as witness. (166)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, we read that the Jews created divisions among the Prophets of Allah, believing in some and rejecting others. The verses under discussion respond to them once again, stating: "Indeed, We have revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him, and We revealed to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the descendants [of Jacob], Jesus, Job, Jonah, Aaron, and Solomon, and We gave David the Psalms [Zabur]" (إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ كَما أَوْحَيْنا إِلى‌ نُوحٍ وَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ وَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى‌ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ إِسْماعِيلَ وَ إِسْحاقَ وَ يَعْقُوبَ وَ الْأَسْباطِ وَ عِيسى‌ وَ أَيُّوبَ وَ يُونُسَ وَ هارُونَ وَ سُلَيْمانَ وَ آتَيْنا داوُدَ زَبُوراً).

So why do you create division among these great Prophets when they all walked the same path?

It is also possible that the above verse addresses the words of the Arab polytheists and idolaters who were astonished by the descent of revelation upon the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). The verse asks: what is there to wonder at? Was revelation not sent down to the previous Prophets?! It then adds that the Prophets to whom revelation was sent were not limited to these; rather, "other messengers whom We have already told you about, and messengers We have not told you about—all had the same mission and divine revelation was sent down to them":

(وَ رُسُلًا قَدْ قَصَصْناهُمْ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَ رُسُلًا لَمْ نَقْصُصْهُمْ عَلَيْكَ‌).

And even higher than this, Allah spoke directly to Moses:

(وَ كَلَّمَ اللَّهُ مُوسى‌ تَكْلِيماً).

Therefore, the chain of revelation has always existed among humanity. How could it be possible for Us to leave human beings without a guide and leader, yet still hold them responsible and duty-bound? Thus, "We made these messengers bringers of good tidings and warners, so that people might be hopeful of Allah's mercy and reward and fearful of His punishments, so that the proof would be completed against them and they would have no excuse."

(رُسُلًا مُبَشِّرِينَ وَ مُنْذِرِينَ لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى اللَّهِ حُجَّةٌ بَعْدَ الرُّسُلِ‌).

Allah has precisely organized and implemented the program of sending these leaders. Why should it be otherwise, considering that: "He is All-Mighty and All-Wise."

(وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزاً حَكِيماً). "His Wisdom" necessitates that this task be carried out, and "His Power" paves the way. This is because the failure to execute a correct plan is either due to a lack of wisdom and knowledge or a lack of power, while neither of these deficiencies exists in His Holy Essence.

In the final verse, He comforts and strengthens the heart of the Prophet, stating that if this group denies your prophethood and mission, it is of no consequence, because: "Allah bears witness to what He has revealed to you."

(لكِنِ اللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ بِما أَنْزَلَ إِلَيْكَ‌). And certainly, your selection for this position was not without reason; rather, "He sent down these verses based on His knowledge of your merit and worthiness for this mission" (أَنْزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ‌).

This phrase may also point to another meaning: that what has been revealed to you originates from the infinite ocean of Divine Knowledge, and its content is a clear witness that it stems from His knowledge. Therefore, the evidence for the truth of your claim is recorded within the text of these verses and requires no other proof. How could an unlettered person, without relying on Divine Knowledge, bring a book containing the highest teachings, philosophies, laws, moral instructions, and social programs?! At the end, it adds that not only does Allah bear witness to your truthfulness, but "the angels of the Lord also bear witness, though Allah is sufficient as a witness."

(وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ يَشْهَدُونَ وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيداً).

Here, several points should be noted:

Key Points

1- Some commentators have inferred from the phrase "Indeed, We have revealed to you as We revealed..." that the Quran intends to announce to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) that in your religion, all the merits found in previous religions are gathered; "What all the beauties possess, you possess alone." Some narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) also allude to this meaning, and the inspiration of the commentators in this section was indeed aided by such narrations. [^1]

2- In the above verses, we read that the Psalms (Zabur) is among the heavenly books that Allah gave to David. This does not contradict the well-known and established fact that the "Arch-Prophets" (Ulul-'Azm) who possessed a heavenly book and a new law were only five. As understood from Quranic verses and Islamic narrations, the heavenly books revealed to the Prophets were of two types: first, books containing legislative rulings that announced a new religion—these were only five books revealed to the five Arch-Prophets. The second type were books that did not contain new laws but rather consisted of advice, exhortations, guidance, recommendations, and supplications.

[^1]: Refer to Tafsir al-Safi, vol. 1, p. 139; Tafsir al-Burhan, vol. 1, p. 427; and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 1, p. 573.

The "Zabur" belonged to this second category. Even now, the "Psalms of David" (Mazamir) mentioned within the books of the "Old Testament" testify to this fact. Although this book, like other books of the New and Old Testaments, has not remained immune to alteration (tahrif), it can be said that it has preserved its form to some extent. This book consists of 150 chapters, each called a Psalm, and its entirety takes the form of advice, prayer, and supplication.

In a narration from Abu Dharr, it is reported that he asked the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family): "How many were the Prophets?" He replied: "One hundred and twenty-four thousand." I asked: "How many among them were Messengers (Rasul)?" He replied: "Three hundred and thirteen, and the rest were only Prophets (Nabi)..."

Abu Dharr says: I asked: "How many heavenly books were revealed to them?"

The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said: "One hundred and four books: ten books to Adam, fifty books to Seth (Shith), thirty books to Enoch (Idris), and ten books to Abraham (making a total of one hundred books), plus the Torah, the Gospel, the Psalms, and the Quran." [^1]

3- Asbat is the plural of Sibt, meaning the tribes of the Children of Israel, but here it refers to the Prophets who were commissioned from those tribes. [^2]

4- The manner of revelation varied among different Prophets: sometimes through the descent of the Angel of Revelation, sometimes through inspiration to the heart, and sometimes through hearing a voice. In the latter case, Allah would create sound waves in the atmosphere or objects and speak to His Prophet through this means. Among those who clearly held this distinction was Moses son of Amram (peace be upon him), who sometimes heard sound waves from within the "Tree of the Right Valley" and sometimes on Mount "Tur." For this reason, the title Kalim-Allah (the one spoken to by Allah) was given to Moses.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, vol. 10, p. 476.

[^2]: More explanations regarding the Asbat are provided in Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 345.

Perhaps the separate mention of Moses in the above verses was due to this very distinction.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا (١٦٧)
Indeed those who are faithless and bar [others] from the way of Allah, have certainly strayed into far error. (167)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَظَلَمُوا۟ لَمْ يَكُنِ ٱللَّهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ وَلَا لِيَهْدِيَهُمْ طَرِيقًا (١٦٨)
Indeed those who are faithless and do wrong, Allah shall never forgive them, nor shall He guide them to any way, (168)
إِلَّا طَرِيقَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا (١٦٩)
except the way to hell, to remain in it forever, and that is easy for Allah. (169)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, discussions were mentioned regarding the disbelievers and the believers. In these verses, reference is made to another group who have chosen the worst form of disbelief. They are those who, in addition to their own deviation, strive to lead others astray. They are individuals who commit injustice both against themselves and against others, for they have neither traversed the path of guidance themselves nor do they allow others to traverse this path.

Therefore, in the first verse, He says: "Indeed, those who disbelieve and avert [people] from the way of Allah have certainly strayed into far-reaching error."

(إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ صَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ قَدْ ضَلُّوا ضَلالًا بَعِيداً).

Why is this group the furthest of people from the path of truth? Because it seems very unlikely that individuals who are promoters of misguidance would abandon the path they themselves are inviting others toward. They have mixed disbelief with stubbornness and obstinacy, and have stepped into a wilderness that is very distant from the path of truth.

In the next verse, He adds: "Indeed, those who disbelieve and commit injustice (both injustice toward the Truth by not performing what was worthy of Him, injustice toward themselves by depriving themselves of salvation and falling into the valley of misguidance, and injustice toward others by barring them from the path of truth), such people will never be subject to the forgiveness of the Lord, and Allah will not guide them to any path except the path of Hell."

(إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ ظَلَمُوا لَمْ يَكُنِ اللَّهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ وَ لا لِيَهْدِيَهُمْ طَرِيقاً إِلَّا طَرِيقَ جَهَنَّمَ‌).

And "they will abide therein forever" (خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً).

They must know that this divine threat will be carried out, because: "And that is ever easy for Allah, and He has the power over it."

(وَ كانَ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ يَسِيراً).

As we observe, the above verses place a special emphasis regarding this group of disbelievers and their punishment. On one hand, their deviation is described as a "far-reaching error" (ضلال بعيد), and on the other hand, with the phrase "Allah would never..." (لَمْ يَكُنِ اللَّهُ‌ ...), it conveys that their forgiveness is never befitting of Allah's station. Furthermore, the expression of eternal dwelling (khulud) and its reinforcement with the word "forever" (ابدا) are all because they, in addition to being lost, strive for the misguidance of others, and this is a tremendous responsibility.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَكُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ بِٱلْحَقِّ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ خَيْرًا لَّكُمْ ۚ وَإِن تَكْفُرُوا۟ فَإِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١٧٠)
O mankind! The Apostle has certainly brought you the truth from your Lord. So have faith! That is better for you. And if you are faithless, [you should know that] to Allah indeed belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (170)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, the fate of the faithless was explained. Here, the invitation toward faith is presented alongside its results, and through various expressions that stir human longing and interest, all people are encouraged toward this lofty goal.

First, it says: "O people, the very Prophet whom you were awaiting and who was mentioned in the previous Heavenly Books has come to you with the religion of truth."

(يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ قَدْ جاءَكُمُ الرَّسُولُ [^1] بِالْحَقِّ [^2])

[^1]: It appears that the "Alif and Lam" in "al-Rasul" is the "Alif and Lam of Designation" (al-ahd), referring to the Prophet whom they were awaiting. Not only the Jews and Christians, but even the polytheists—having heard matters regarding this from the People of the Book—held such an expectation.

[^2]: In some narrations transmitted through the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام, the word "Haqq" (Truth) has been interpreted as the Wilayah (guardianship) of Ali عليه السلام. As we have repeatedly stated, such interpretations are of the nature of identifying a clear instance (misdaq) and do not imply exclusivity.

Then He says: "This Prophet has come from the One who has undertaken your upbringing and nourishment" (مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ).

Afterward, He adds: "If you believe, it is for your own good; you have not served another, but rather you have served yourselves" (فَآمِنُوا خَيْراً لَكُمْ).

And at the end, He says: Do not think that if you choose the path of disbelief, any harm will reach Allah; it is not so, for Allah is the Owner of whatever is in the heavens and the earth.

(وَ إِنْ تَكْفُرُوا فَإِنَّ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

Furthermore, since Allah is All-Knowing and All-Wise, the commands He has given you and the programs He has organized are all based on a philosophy and wisdom, and are for your own benefit.

(وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً).

Therefore, if He has sent Prophets and programs, it was not due to His own need, but rather because of His Knowledge and Wisdom. Considering all these aspects, is it fitting to abandon the path of faith and step onto the path of disbelief?

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُۥٓ أَلْقَىٰهَآ إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌ مِّنْهُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ ثَلَـٰثَةٌ ۚ ٱنتَهُوا۟ خَيْرًا لَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱللَّهُ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥٓ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ ۘ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا (١٧١)
O People of the Book! Do not exceed the bounds in your religion, and do not attribute anything to Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, was only an apostle of Allah, and His Word that He cast toward Mary and a spirit from Him. So have faith in Allah and His apostles, and do not say, ‘[God is] a trinity.’ Relinquish [such a creed]! That is better for you. Allah is but the One God. He is far too immaculate to have any son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and Allah suffices as trustee. (171)

Commentary

Trinity is Illusory

In this verse and the following one, in connection with the discussions regarding the People of the Book and the disbelievers, reference is made to one of the most significant deviations of the Christian community—namely, "the issue of the Trinity and the triple gods"—and with short, reasoned sentences, it warns them against this great deviation.

First, it cautions them not to follow the path of exaggeration (Ghalw) in their religion and to speak nothing but the truth regarding Allah:

(يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لا تَغْلُوا فِي دِينِكُمْ وَ لا تَقُولُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ إِلَّا الْحَقَّ) "O People of the Book! Do not exceed the limits in your religion, and do not say about Allah except the truth."

The issue of "Ghalw" (extremism/exaggeration) regarding leaders has been one of the most significant sources of deviation in heavenly religions. Since humans have self-love, they tend to portray their leaders and guides as greater than they actually are to increase their own perceived greatness. Sometimes, the notion that exaggerating about leaders is a sign of faith, love, and devotion toward them causes people to step into this perilous abyss. "Ghalw" always carries a major flaw: it destroys the primary root of religion, which is the worship of God and Monotheism (Tawhid). For this reason, Islam has taken a very strict stance against the "Ghulat" (extremists), and in books of "Creed" (Aqa'id) and "Jurisprudence" (Fiqh), they are introduced as the worst of disbelievers.

Then, it points to several points, each of which serves as a proof for the invalidation of the Trinity and the divinity of Christ (peace be upon him):

1- Jesus (peace be upon him) was only the son of Mary (peace be upon her) (إِنَّمَا الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ). This expression (mentioning the name of Jesus's mother alongside his name), which appears in sixteen places in the Holy Quran, reminds us that Christ (peace be upon him), like all other human beings, was in a mother's womb, passed through the embryonic stage, and was born like any other human. He drank milk and was nurtured in his mother's arms; that is, all human attributes were present in him. How is it possible for such a person, who is subject to and governed by the laws of nature and the changes of the material world, to be the Pre-Eternal and Everlasting God? Especially the word Innama (only/verily) in the verse under discussion answers the misconception that if Jesus (peace be upon him) had no father, it does not mean he was the son of God, but rather he was only the son of Mary!

2- Jesus (peace be upon him) was a messenger of Allah (رَسُولُ اللَّهِ). This status is also incompatible with divinity. It is noteworthy that the various sayings of Christ (peace be upon him), parts of which are still present in the current Gospels, all indicate his prophethood and mission for the guidance of humanity, not his divinity or godhood.

3- Jesus (peace be upon him) was a "Word" from Allah bestowed upon Mary (وَ كَلِمَتُهُ أَلْقاها إِلى‌ مَرْيَمَ). In several verses of the Quran, Jesus (peace be upon him) is referred to as a "Word" (Kalimah). This expression is intended to point to the created nature of Christ (peace be upon him). Just as words are our creations, the beings of the world of creation are also created by Allah. Furthermore, just as words express our inner secrets and are signs of our attributes and spirit, the creatures of this world also clarify the attributes of Allah's Beauty and Majesty. For this reason, in several Quranic verses, the term "Word" is applied to all creatures (such as verses 109 of Al-Kahf and 27 of Luqman). However, these words differ from one another; some are very prominent, and some are relatively simple and small. Jesus (peace be upon him) had a particular prominence, especially in terms of creation (in addition to the rank of messengership), because he was created without a father.

4- Jesus is a spirit created by Allah (وَ رُوحٌ مِنْهُ). This expression, which is also used in the Quran regarding the creation of Adam and, in a sense, the creation of all humanity, refers to the greatness of that spirit which Allah created and placed within human beings in general, and Christ and the prophets in particular.

Although some have sought to misuse this expression to claim that Jesus (peace be upon him) was a part of Allah, using the term "minhu" (from Him) as evidence, we know that "min" (from) in such cases does not denote division or part-ship (tab'id). Rather, it is what is called "min al-nushuiyyah," which describes the source and origin of something's emergence.

It is interesting to read in history that Harun al-Rashid had a Christian physician who one day debated with Ali ibn Husayn al-Waqidi, a Muslim scholar. The physician said: "In your Heavenly Book, there is a verse that introduces Christ (peace be upon him) as a part of God," and then he recited the above verse. Waqidi immediately recited this verse from the Quran in response:

وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً مِنْهُ "And He has subjected to you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, all [as a favor] from Him." [^1]

He added that if the word "min" (from) implied being a part, then all creatures in the heavens and the earth must be parts of God according to this verse. Upon hearing this, the Christian physician became a Muslim. Harun al-Rashid was pleased with this event and gave Waqidi a significant reward. [^2] Furthermore, it is astonishing that Christians take the birth of Jesus (peace be upon him) from a mother without a father as proof of his divinity, while they forget that Adam (peace be upon him) came into existence without a father or a mother, yet no one considers this unique creation as proof of his divinity!

Then the Quran follows this explanation by saying: "Now that this is the case, believe in the One God and His messengers, and do not say 'Three' (gods). Desist—it is better for you."

(فَآمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رُسُلِهِ وَ لا تَقُولُوا ثَلاثَةٌ انْتَهُوا خَيْراً لَكُمْ)

Once again, it emphasizes that Allah alone is the only Deity (إِنَّمَا اللَّهُ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ). That is, you admit that despite the Trinity, God is One, whereas if He had a son, the son would be like Him, and in such a state, oneness has no meaning.

[^1]: Surah Al-Jathiyah, Verse 13. [^2]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 6, p. 84.

How is it possible for Allah to have a son when He is free from the deficiency of needing a spouse or child, and free from the deficiency of physicality and the accidents of being a body?

(سُبْحانَهُ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ وَلَدٌ) "Glory be to Him! [Far exalted is He] above having a son."

Furthermore, He owns whatever is in the heavens and the earth; all are His creatures and He is their Creator. Christ (peace be upon him) is also one of these creatures. How can an exceptional status be claimed for him? Can the owned and the created be the son of their Owner and Creator?

(لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ)

Allah is not only their Creator and Owner but also their Disposer, Protector, Provider, and Guardian. (وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ وَكِيلًا) "And sufficient is Allah as a Disposer of affairs."

Fundamentally, why would a God who is Pre-Eternal and Everlasting, and who oversees all beings from eternity to eternity, need a son? Is He like us, needing a son to succeed Him after death?!

Trinity: The Greatest Deviation of Christianity

Among the deviations that the Christian world has fallen into, none is worse than the deviation of the Trinity. For they explicitly say: God is three, and they also explicitly say that He is simultaneously one! That is, they consider both the unity to be real and the triplicity to be actual. This subject has created a massive problem for Christian researchers.

If they were willing to consider the oneness of God as "metaphorical" and the Trinity as "real," it would be one thing; and if they were willing to consider the Trinity as "metaphorical" and Monotheism as "real," the issue would still be simple. But the strange thing is that they consider both to be real and actual! If we see in some recent promotional writings given to uninformed individuals that they speak of a metaphorical triplicity, it is hypocritical talk that in no way aligns with the primary sources of Christianity and the actual beliefs of their scholars.

This is where Christians find themselves faced with an irrational concept, for even a primary school child cannot accept the equation "1 = 3." For this reason, they usually say that this issue should not be accepted by the scale of reason, but rather by the scale of submission (ta'abbud) and the heart! It is from here that the separation of "religion" from "logical reasoning" begins, leading Christianity into the dangerous valley where religion is seen as having no rational aspect but being purely emotional and submissive. It is also from here that the alienation between science and religion, and the conflict between the two, becomes apparent from the logic of modern Christianity. For science says: the number 3 is never equal to one, but modern Christianity says it is! Regarding this belief, several points should be noted:

1- In none of the current Gospels is there any mention of the issue of the Trinity. For this reason, Christian researchers believe that the source of the Trinity in the Gospels is hidden and unapparent. Mr. Hawkes, an American, says: "But the issue of the Trinity in the Old and New Testaments is hidden and unclear" (Bible Dictionary, p. 345, Beirut edition).

As some historians have written, the issue of the Trinity became apparent among Christians from around the third century onwards. This was an innovation that entered true Christianity due to exaggeration on one hand and the mixing of Christians with other nations on the other. Some suggest that the "Christian Trinity" was originally taken from the "Hindu Triad" (the triple-worship of Hindus). [^1]

[^1]: Refer to the 20th Century Encyclopedia (Farid Wajdi) under the entry "Triad"—the Hindu triple gods were: Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva.

2- The Trinity, especially in the form of "Trinity in Unity" (triplicity while being one), is a completely irrational matter and contrary to the self-evidence of reason. We know that religion can never be separated from reason and science. True science is always in harmony with real religion, and they move forward side by side. The statement that religion must be accepted purely out of submission is a very incorrect statement. For if reason is set aside in accepting the principles of a religion and the issue of "blind and deaf submission" arises, no difference will remain between religions. At that point, what reason is there for a person to be a God-worshiper and not an idol-worshiper? And what reason is there for Christians to propagate their religion and not others? Therefore, the advantages they imagine for Christianity and their insistence on drawing people toward it are themselves proof that religion must be known through the logic of reason. This is exactly contrary to the claim they make regarding the Trinity, where they separate "religion" from "reason."

In any case, no statement is worse for destroying the foundation of religion than saying religion has no rational or logical aspect but is purely submissive!

3- The numerous proofs brought in the discussion of Monotheism for the oneness of God's Essence negate any kind of duality, triplicity, or plurality from Him. Allah is a Being who is infinite in all respects; He is Pre-Eternal, Everlasting, and unlimited in terms of knowledge, power, and ability. We know that in infinity, plurality and duality cannot be imagined. For if we assume two infinites, both become finite and limited, because the first being lacks the power, ability, and existence of the second being, and likewise, the second being lacks the existence and distinctions of the first. Therefore, both the first and second beings are limited. To put it more clearly, if we assume two "infinites" in all respects, the "first infinite" must end where it reaches the boundary of the "second infinite," and the second infinite must end where it reaches the boundary of the first. Therefore, both are limited and finite.

The conclusion is: the Essence of Allah, which is an infinite Being, can never have plurality.

Furthermore, if we believe that the Essence of God is composed of "three hypostases" (three principles or three essences), it necessitates that all three be limited, not unlimited and infinite.

Additionally, every "composite" is in need of its "parts," and its existence is the effect of their existence. The implication of composition in the Essence of God is that He would be needy and an effect, whereas we know He is Self-Sufficient and the First Cause of the universe.

4- Beyond all this, how is it possible for the Essence of God to appear in a human form and become in need of a body, place, food, clothing, and the like?

Limiting the Pre-Eternal and Everlasting God within the body of a human, and placing Him in a mother's womb, is among the worst slanders that can be attributed to His Holy Essence. Likewise, attributing a son to God, which entails various physical accidents, is considered an illogical and completely irrational attribution. This is evidenced by the fact that anyone who has not been raised in a Christian environment and has not become accustomed to these illusory and false teachings from early childhood is repulsed by hearing these expressions that contradict the inspiration of nature and reason. If Christians themselves are not bothered by expressions like "God the Father" and "God the Son," it is because they have been familiarized with these false concepts since childhood!

5- Recently, it has been observed that a group of Christian missionaries, in order to deceive uninformed individuals regarding the issue of the Trinity, have resorted to fallacious analogies. Among them: that unity in Trinity (oneness despite triplicity) can be compared to the "mass of the sun," its "light," and its "heat," which are three things and yet one reality. Or it is compared to an object whose reflection falls into three mirrors; although there is only one object, three objects appear! Or they compare it to a triangle that has three angles from the outside, but if we extend the angles from the inside, they meet at one point.

With a little reflection, it becomes clear that these examples have no connection to the issue under discussion. For the "mass of the sun" is certainly distinct from its "light," and "light" (which consists of electromagnetic waves) is scientifically completely different from "heat" (infrared waves). If it is occasionally said these three things are a single personal unit, it is nothing more than a loose and metaphorical expression.

Even clearer is the example of the "object" and the "mirrors," because the image in the mirror is nothing but the reflection of light. The reflection of light is certainly other than the object itself. Therefore, no real or personal union exists between them, and this is something anyone who has studied high school physics knows.

In the example of the triangle, the matter is the same: the angles of the triangle are definitely multiple, and the extension of the angle bisectors to reach a point inside the triangle has nothing to do with the angles themselves.

Surprisingly, some Eastern Christians, inspired by the "Unity of Existence of the Sufis," [^1] have tried to reconcile Monotheism in Trinity with the logic of "Unity of Existence." But it goes without saying that if someone accepts the incorrect and deviant belief of Unity of Existence, they must consider all creatures of this world as part of God's Essence, or rather, imagine them to be Him. In that case, triplicity has no meaning; rather, all beings, small and large, become a part or a manifestation of Him. Therefore, the Christian Trinity can have no connection to Unity of Existence, even though the Sufi Unity of Existence has also been invalidated in its own place.

[^1]: What is meant by the Sufi Unity of Existence is the "Unity of the Existent," which says that existence is nothing but a single unit that has appeared in different forms, and that unit is God!

6- Sometimes some Christians say: "If we call Christ (peace be upon him) the Son of Allah, it is just as you call Imam Husayn (peace be upon him) Thar Allah and Ibn Tharih (the Blood of Allah and the son of His Blood), or as in some narrations, Ali (peace be upon him) is referred to as Yad Allah (the Hand of Allah)."

However, it must be said: Firstly, it is a great mistake that some have translated Thar as "blood," for Thar in the Arabic language never means blood; rather, it means "blood-wit" or "the one whose blood must be avenged" (in Arabic, blood is called dam). Therefore, Thar Allah means: "O you whose blood-wit belongs to Allah, and He is the one who will take your blood-wit." That is, you do not belong to a family such that the head of the family takes your blood-wit, nor do you belong to a tribe such that the tribal chief takes it; you belong to the world of humanity and mankind, you belong to the universe and the Pure Essence of God; therefore, He must take your blood-wit. Likewise, you are the son of Ali ibn Abi Talib, who was a martyr in the way of Allah, and whose blood-wit Allah must also take.

Secondly, if in a phrase regarding men of God, an expression like Yad Allah is used, it is certainly a type of simile, metaphor, and figurative speech. But is any real Christian willing to consider the Sonship of Christ as a type of metaphor or figure of speech? Certainly not, because the authentic sources of Christianity consider "Son" as a "real son" and say: this attribute is specific to Christ (peace be upon him) and no one else. The fact that in some superficial Christian promotional writings, "Son of Allah" is taken as a metaphor or simile is mostly for the purpose of deceiving the masses. To clarify this point, pay close attention to the following statement by the author of the Bible Dictionary under the entry "God": "And the term 'Son of God' is one of the titles of our Savior and Redeemer which is not applied to any other person, except in places where it is known from context that a real son of God is not intended." [^1]

Ayahs

لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ أَن يَكُونَ عَبْدًا لِّلَّهِ وَلَا ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ ۚ وَمَن يَسْتَنكِفْ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَيَسْتَكْبِرْ فَسَيَحْشُرُهُمْ إِلَيْهِ جَمِيعًا (١٧٢)
The Messiah would never disdain being a servant of Allah, nor would the angels brought near [to Him]. And whoever disdains His worship and is arrogant, He will gather them all toward Him. (172)
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَيَزِيدُهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ ۖ وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَنكَفُوا۟ وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فَيُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا وَلَا يَجِدُونَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (١٧٣)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, He will pay them in full their rewards, and He will enhance them out of His grace. But those who are disdainful and arrogant, He will punish them with a painful punishment, and they will not find besides Allah any guardian or helper. (173)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators have narrated regarding the occasion of revelation for this verse that a group of Christians from Najran came to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "Why do you find fault with our leader?" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked: "What fault have I

[^1]: Holy Dictionary, Beirut edition, page 345.

attributed to him?" They replied: "You say that he was a servant of Allah and His Prophet." The above verse was revealed and provided them with an answer.

Commentary

Messiah Was a Servant of Allah

Although the above verses have a specific occasion of revelation, their connection and relationship with the previous verses—which were regarding the negation of the divinity of the Messiah عليه السلام and the invalidation of the issue of the Trinity—is evident.

First, through another explanation, it invalidates the issue of the divinity of the Messiah عليه السلام and says: How can you believe in the divinity of Jesus عليه السلام while "neither the Messiah disdained to be a servant of the Lord, nor do the near-stationed angels of the Lord disdain [it]."

(لَنْ يَسْتَنْكِفَ الْمَسِيحُ أَنْ يَكُونَ عَبْداً لِلَّهِ وَ لَا الْمَلائِكَةُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ‌).

And it is certain that one who is himself a worshiper cannot logically be the object of worship. Is it possible for someone to worship himself? Or for the worshiper and the worshiped, the servant and God, to be one and the same?! It is interesting that we read in a hadith that Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, in order to condemn the deviant Christians who claimed his divinity, said to the "Catholicos" (the great leader of the Christians): "Everything about Jesus عليه السلام was good, he had only one fault, and that was that he did not worship much." The Christian man became upset and said to the Imam: "What a great mistake you are making! On the contrary, he was among the most worshipful of people." The Imam immediately replied: "Whom did he worship? Did he worship anyone other than Allah? Therefore,

by your own admission, the Messiah was a servant, a creature, and a worshiper of Allah, not the worshiped or God." The Christian man fell silent and had no answer. [^1] Then the Quran adds: "Those who refrain from the worship and servitude of the Lord, and if this refusal stems from arrogance and self-conceit, Allah will gather them all before Him on the Day of Resurrection" and will give each a suitable punishment.

(وَ مَنْ يَسْتَنْكِفْ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَ يَسْتَكْبِرْ فَسَيَحْشُرُهُمْ إِلَيْهِ جَمِيعاً).

"On that day, those who possessed faith and performed righteous deeds, He will give them their rewards in full, and will increase for them from His grace and mercy; but those who disdained the servitude of Allah and took the path of arrogance, He will punish them with a painful torment, and they shall find for themselves besides Allah no guardian, supporter, or helper."

(فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَيُوفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَ يَزِيدُهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَ أَمَّا الَّذِينَ اسْتَنْكَفُوا وَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فَيُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً وَ لا يَجِدُونَ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَ لا نَصِيراً).

Here, two points should be noted:

1- Istinkaf (استنكاف) means to refrain from or feel aversion toward something; therefore, it has a broad meaning that is limited by the mention of the phrase istakbaru (استكبروا) following it. This is because refusal to worship Allah sometimes stems from ignorance and lack of knowledge, and sometimes it is due to arrogance, self-conceit, and rebellion. Although both are wrong, the second is far worse.

2- The mention of the angels' lack of disdain for the servitude of the Lord is either because the Christians believed in three deities (the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit—or in other words, God the Father, God the Son, and the intermediary between them). Therefore, in this verse, He wants to

[^1]: Manaqib Ibn Shahr Ashub, Volume 4, page 352.

negate both of the other deities, namely "the Messiah" and "the Angel of the Holy Spirit," so that the Oneness of the Essence of the Lord is established.

Or it is because the verse, while responding to the polytheism of the Christians, also alludes to the polytheism of the Arab idolaters who considered the angels to be the daughters of God and a part of the Lord, and it responds to them as well.

Given these two explanations, there remains no room for the debate of whether the above verse is evidence for the superiority of angels over prophets or not; for the verse is only in the position of negating the "third person" [of the Trinity] or the "deities of the Arab polytheists," and is not intent on stating the superiority of angels over the Messiah عليه السلام.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَكُم بُرْهَـٰنٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ نُورًا مُّبِينًا (١٧٤)
O mankind! Certainly, a proof has come to you from your Lord, and We have sent down to you a manifest light. (174)
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱعْتَصَمُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَسَيُدْخِلُهُمْ فِى رَحْمَةٍ مِّنْهُ وَفَضْلٍ وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَيْهِ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا (١٧٥)
As for those who have faith in Allah, and hold fast to Him, He will admit them to His mercy and grace, and He will guide them on a straight path to Him. (175)

Commentary

The Manifest Light

Following the discussions in the preceding verses regarding the deviations of the People of the Book from the principle of Monotheism and the core teachings of the Prophets, these two verses deliver the final word and identify the path of salvation. First, addressing all the people of the world, it says: "O people, a Prophet has come to you from your Lord who possesses manifest proofs and evidences, and likewise, a manifest light called the Quran has been sent with him, which illuminates the path to your prosperity."

(يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ بُرْهانٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ نُوراً مُبِيناً) According to some scholars, the word burhan (proof) is derived from the root baraha (on the weight of faraha), meaning "to become white." Since clear arguments make the face of truth bright, manifest, and "white" for the listener, they are called burhan.

The intended meaning of burhan in the above verse—as stated by a group of commentators and supported by contextual evidence—is the person of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and the intended meaning of nur (light) is the Holy Quran, which has been referred to as light in other verses as well.

In several hadiths transmitted to us from the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Tafsir Ali bin Ibrahim, and Majma' al-Bayan, burhan is interpreted as the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and nur as Ali عليه السلام. This interpretation does not contradict the one mentioned above, for it is possible that a broad meaning is intended for "light" that encompasses both the Quran and the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, who was the protector, commentator, and defender of the Quran.

In the following verse, the result of following this proof and light is explained as follows: "As for those who believed in Allah and held fast to this Heavenly Book, He will soon admit them into His vast mercy, and will increase their reward from His grace and mercy, and guide them toward Himself via a straight path."

(فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ اعْتَصَمُوا بِهِ فَسَيُدْخِلُهُمْ فِي رَحْمَةٍ مِنْهُ وَ فَضْلٍ وَ يَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَيْهِ صِراطاً مُسْتَقِيماً) [^1]

Ayah

يَسْتَفْتُونَكَ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِى ٱلْكَلَـٰلَةِ ۚ إِنِ ٱمْرُؤٌا۟ هَلَكَ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ وَلَهُۥٓ أُخْتٌ فَلَهَا نِصْفُ مَا تَرَكَ ۚ وَهُوَ يَرِثُهَآ إِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّهَا وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن كَانَتَا ٱثْنَتَيْنِ فَلَهُمَا ٱلثُّلُثَانِ مِمَّا تَرَكَ ۚ وَإِن كَانُوٓا۟ إِخْوَةً رِّجَالًا وَنِسَآءً فَلِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ ۗ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ أَن تَضِلُّوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌۢ (١٧٦)
They ask you for a ruling. Say, ‘Allah gives you a ruling concerning the kalalah: If a man dies and has no children [or parents], but has a sister, for her shall be a half of what he leaves, and he shall inherit from her if she has no children. If there be two sisters, then they shall receive two-thirds of what he leaves. But if there be [several] brothers and sisters, then for the male shall be the like of the share of two females. Allah explains [the laws] for you lest you should go astray, and Allah has knowledge of all things.’ (176)

Occasion of Revelation

Many commentators have narrated the occasion of the revelation of the above verse from "Jabir bin Abdullah al-Ansari," who says: "I was severely ill. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله came to visit me; there, He performed ablution (wudu) and sprinkled some of the water from His wudu upon me. Since I was thinking about death, I said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: 'My only heirs are my sisters; how shall their inheritance be?' This verse, which is known as the Verse of Obligations (Fara'id), was revealed and clarified their inheritance." (The above narration appears with slight variations in Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, al-Tibyan, al-Manar, al-Durr al-Manthur, and others).

According to some, this is the final verse regarding the rulings of Islam that was revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. [^1]

Commentary

The above verse explains the amount of inheritance for brothers and sisters. As we mentioned at the beginning of this Surah in the commentary of verse 12, two verses were revealed in the Quran regarding the inheritance of brothers and sisters: one is verse 12, and the other is the verse under discussion, which is the final verse of Surah an-Nisa. Although these two verses differ in the amounts of inheritance they specify, as we explained at the start of the Surah, each refers to a specific group of brothers and sisters. Verse 12 refers to "maternal" brothers and sisters, whereas the verse under discussion speaks about "full" (paternal and maternal) or "paternal-only" brothers and sisters.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verse.

The evidence for this is that usually, those who are related to the deceased through an intermediary receive an inheritance proportional to that intermediary. That is, maternal brothers and sisters receive a share based on the mother's portion, which revolves around one-third, while paternal or full brothers and sisters receive the share of the father, which revolves around two-thirds. Since verse 12 centers on one-third for the inheritance of brothers and sisters, and the verse under discussion centers on two-thirds, it becomes clear that the former verse concerns those brothers and sisters related to the deceased only through the mother, while the current verse concerns those related through the father or both parents. Furthermore, narrations transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام also confirm this reality. In any case, once one-third or two-thirds of the inheritance is assigned to the brother or sister, the remainder is distributed among other heirs according to Islamic law. Now that the lack of contradiction between the two verses is clear, we proceed to the commentary of the rulings mentioned in the verse:

First, it should be noted that the verse was revealed as a response to a question regarding Kalalah (brothers and sisters). [^1] Therefore, He says: "They ask you for a ruling; say: Allah gives you a ruling concerning the Kalalah (brothers and sisters)."

(يَسْتَفْتُونَكَ قُلِ اللَّهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِي الْكَلالَةِ).

Then, He points to several rulings:

1- Whenever a man dies and has no child, but has a sister, half of his legacy belongs to that one sister.

[^1]: Regarding the literal meaning of "Kalalah" and why brothers and sisters are called Kalalah, we have discussed this in detail under verse 12 of Surah an-Nisa in Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 296.

(إِنِ امْرُؤٌ هَلَكَ لَيْسَ لَهُ وَلَدٌ وَ لَهُ أُخْتٌ فَلَها نِصْفُ ما تَرَكَ‌).

2- And if a woman dies and has no child, and leaves behind one brother (full or paternal), the entire inheritance goes to that one brother.

(وَ هُوَ يَرِثُها إِنْ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَها وَلَدٌ).

3- If someone dies and leaves behind two sisters, they receive two-thirds of his legacy.

(فَإِنْ كانَتَا اثْنَتَيْنِ فَلَهُمَا الثُّلُثانِ مِمَّا تَرَكَ‌)

4- If the heirs of the deceased are several brothers and sisters (more than two), they divide the entire inheritance among themselves such that the share of each brother is twice the share of a sister.

(وَ إِنْ كانُوا إِخْوَةً رِجالًا وَ نِساءً فَلِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ‌).

At the end of the verse, He says: "Allah makes these truths clear to you so that you do not go astray and that you may find the path of happiness (and surely the path God shows is the correct and true path), for He is All-Knowing of all things."

(يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَضِلُّوا وَ اللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيمٌ‌) [^1]

It should not remain unsaid that the above verse explains the inheritance of brothers and sisters in the event that there are no children, and it does not mention the presence or absence of parents. However, considering that according to the verses at the beginning of this Surah, parents are always in the same rank as children—meaning the first tier of inheritance—it becomes clear that the above verse refers to a situation where there are neither children nor parents.

(End of the Commentary of Surah an-Nisa)

[^1]: The phrase "أَنْ تَضِلُّوا" means "lest you go astray" (an la tadillu); that is, the word "la" (not) is implied. This type of expression is frequent in the Quran and Arabic speech.
